# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **beam** is a structural member, typically long and slender, designed primarily to resist transverse loads applied perpendicular to its longitudinal axis. Beams transfer the applied loads to the supports through **support reactions**—internal or external force/moment responses developed at the points of support.

A **support reaction** is the resisting force or moment offered by a support to maintain **static equilibrium** of a rigid body subjected to external loading. These reactions are the unknown quantities that must be determined to design safe and serviceable structural members.

**Types of Supports (KTU 2024 Classification):**

| Support Type | Symbol | Reactions Provided | Degrees of Freedom Restrained |
|---|---|---|---|
| Hinged / Pinned Support | Triangle on ground | $R_{Hx}$ and $R_{Hy}$ (2 unknowns) | 2 translations |
| Roller Support | Triangle on circles | $R_{V}$ (1 unknown) | 1 translation (perpendicular to roller) |
| Fixed Support | Wall-hatched | $R_{Hx}, R_{Hy}, M$ (3 unknowns) | 2 translations + 1 rotation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Determinacy Rule (KTU 2024 Module-1 Focus):**
> A 2D rigid body is **statically determinate** when the number of unknown support reactions ($r$) equals the number of independent equilibrium equations available (which is **3** for a 2D rigid body: $\Sigma F_x = 0$, $\Sigma F_y = 0$, $\Sigma M = 0$).
> - **Determinate:** $r = 3$
> - **Incompletely constrained (Mechanism):** $r < 3$
> - **Indeterminate (Statically):** $r > 3$

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The See-Saw"**
> Imagine two children of different weights sitting on a see-saw. The see-saw tilts until the **pivot point (fulcrum)** exerts a reaction force balancing both children. This pivot reaction is exactly like a **support reaction** in a beam. If you change where the heavier child sits, the reaction at the pivot changes.
>
> - A **simply supported beam** is like a wooden plank resting on two bricks—one brick doesn't allow horizontal sliding (pin), and the other allows the plank to expand/contract freely as temperature changes (roller). This prevents bending stresses from thermal changes.
> - A **cantilever beam** is like a diving board fixed into a concrete wall at one end. The wall must provide not only an upward push but also a **resisting moment** to prevent the board from rotating downward.

## 1.3 Types of Beams and Loading

**Beam Types (relevant to this module):**
- **Simply Supported Beam (SSB):** Pinned at one end, roller at the other. Span length = $L$.
- **Cantilever Beam:** Fixed at one end, free at the other.
- **Overhanging Beam:** Has portion(s) extending beyond the supports.

**Loading Types (Module 1 Scope):**
- **Point Load (Concentrated Load):** A single force $P$ acting at a specific point. Units: **N** or **kN**.
- **Uniformly Distributed Load (UDL):** A load spread uniformly over a length with intensity $w$ (force per unit length). Units: **N/m** or **kN/m**.

**Conversion Formula for Total UDL Force:**

$$
W_{\text{total}} = w \times a
$$

where $a$ is the length over which the UDL acts. This total force acts at the **centroid** of the UDL distribution (midpoint for uniform distribution).

## 1.4 Why This Topic is High-Yield in KTU 2024

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Support reactions form the **foundation of structural analysis**. Every subsequent topic—bending moment, shear force, deflection, design of beams in EST200 and CE203—depends entirely on correctly computing these reactions. KTU 2024 ESE consistently allocates **8–12 marks** in Module-1 questions to this topic, typically in **Part A (3 marks)** for definitions and **Part B (14 marks)** for full numerical problems.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Equilibrium Framework (2D Rigid Body)

For any beam in static equilibrium under the action of coplanar forces, the following three independent equations **must be satisfied simultaneously**:

**Equation 1 — Horizontal Force Equilibrium:**

$$
\sum F_x = 0
$$

**Equation 2 — Vertical Force Equilibrium:**

$$
\sum F_y = 0
$$

**Equation 3 — Moment Equilibrium (about any point O):**

$$
\sum M_O = 0
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The third equation is taken about a point where at least two unknowns intersect, so those unknowns drop out of the moment equation. This is the standard KTU 2024 trick: take moments about the **pin support** to directly solve for the **roller reaction**, and vice versa.

## 2.2 Sign Convention (KTU 2024 Standard)

- **Forces:** Upward positive ($+$), Downward negative ($-$). Rightward positive ($+$), Leftward negative ($-$).
- **Moments:** **Counter-clockwise (CCW)** is positive ($+$), **Clockwise (CW)** is negative ($-$).
- **Distances:** Measured from the chosen moment center to the point of application of the force.

## 2.3 Why and How Behind Each Step

The "Why" rests on **Newton's First Law of Motion** extended to a rigid body: a body at rest stays at rest only when the **net force** and **net moment** acting upon it are both zero. The "How" is the systematic application of these three scalar equations to solve the unknown reaction components.

For a **statically determinate beam** with 3 unknowns, we have 3 equations, which gives a **unique solution**. If we have more unknowns (e.g., a fixed-fixed beam with 6 unknowns in 2D analysis), we require additional compatibility/stiffness equations—this is **indeterminate analysis** and is outside the scope of Module 1.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Beam Type & Loading | Reactions (Standard Results) |
|---|---|
| **SSB, Point Load $P$ at center, span $L$** | $R_A = R_B = \dfrac{P}{2}$ |
| **SSB, Point Load $P$ at distance $a$ from A, $b = L - a$ from B** | $R_A = \dfrac{P \cdot b}{L}, \quad R_B = \dfrac{P \cdot a}{L}$ |
| **SSB, UDL $w$ over entire span $L$** | $R_A = R_B = \dfrac{w \cdot L}{2}$ |
| **SSB, UDL $w$ over partial span from $x_1$ to $x_2$** | Total UDL = $w(x_2 - x_1)$, acts at centroid $\dfrac{x_1 + x_2}{2}$ |
| **Cantilever, Point Load $P$ at free end, length $L$** | $R_F = P$ (upward), $M_F = P \cdot L$ (resisting) |
| **Cantilever, Point Load $P$ at distance $a$ from fixed end** | $R_F = P$ (upward), $M_F = P \cdot a$ (resisting) |
| **Cantilever, UDL $w$ over entire span $L$** | $R_F = w \cdot L$ (upward), $M_F = \dfrac{w \cdot L^2}{2}$ (resisting) |
| **Cantilever, UDL $w$ over length $a$ from fixed end** | $R_F = w \cdot a$ (upward), $M_F = \dfrac{w \cdot a^2}{2}$ (resisting) |

> [!WARNING]
> **Units Check (Mandatory for KTU Valuation):** If load is in **kN** and length in **m**, reactions are in **kN** and moments in **kN·m**. Mixing units (e.g., N with m) is a common reason for **full mark deductions** in KTU 2024 board exams.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Support reaction calculations are the **first step** in:
1. **Design of reinforced concrete beams** (computing design moments and shears).
2. **Steel girder design** (IS 800 and AISC standards).
3. **Bridge abutment design** (estimating load transfer to foundations).
4. **Machine tool frames** (ensuring rigidity and proper load paths).
5. **Aerospace spars** (wing and tail structures of aircraft).

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: SSB with Point Load $P$ at Distance $a$ from A

**Setup:** A simply supported beam of span $L$ with supports at A (pin) and B (roller). A point load $P$ is applied vertically downward at distance $a$ from A, so its distance from B is $b = L - a$.

**Step 1 — Identify Unknowns:** $R_{Ay}$ (vertical at A), $R_{By}$ (vertical at B). Total unknowns = 2. Horizontal equilibrium is trivial (no horizontal loads), so $R_{Ax} = 0$.

**Step 2 — Apply Vertical Force Equilibrium:**

$$
\sum F_y = 0
$$

$$
R_{Ay} + R_{By} - P = 0
$$

$$
R_{Ay} + R_{By} = P
$$

**Step 3 — Apply Moment Equilibrium about A (eliminates $R_{Ay}$):**

$$
\sum M_A = 0
$$

Taking CCW as positive:

$$
R_{By} \cdot L - P \cdot a = 0
$$

$$
R_{By} \cdot L = P \cdot a
$$

$$
R_{By} = \dfrac{P \cdot a}{L}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute back into Step 2:**

$$
R_{Ay} = P - R_{By} = P - \dfrac{P \cdot a}{L}
$$

$$
R_{Ay} = \dfrac{P \cdot L - P \cdot a}{L} = \dfrac{P(L - a)}{L}
$$

Since $b = L - a$:

$$
R_{Ay} = \dfrac{P \cdot b}{L}
$$

**Final Result:**

$$
\boxed{R_{Ay} = \dfrac{P \cdot b}{L}, \quad R_{By} = \dfrac{P \cdot a}{L}}
$$

**Special Case Verification (Load at Center):** $a = b = L/2$:

$$
R_{Ay} = R_{By} = \dfrac{P \cdot (L/2)}{L} = \dfrac{P}{2} \quad \checkmark
$$

## 3.2 Derivation: SSB with UDL $w$ over Entire Span

**Setup:** A simply supported beam of span $L$ carries a UDL of intensity $w$ over the entire length.

**Step 1 — Replace UDL with Equivalent Point Load:**

The total UDL force is:

$$
W = w \cdot L
$$

This equivalent point load acts at the **midpoint** of the span (i.e., at distance $L/2$ from A).

**Step 2 — Identify Unknowns:** $R_{Ay}$ and $R_{By}$ (2 unknowns).

**Step 3 — Apply Moment Equilibrium about A:**

$$
\sum M_A = 0
$$

$$
R_{By} \cdot L - (w \cdot L) \cdot \dfrac{L}{2} = 0
$$

$$
R_{By} \cdot L = \dfrac{w \cdot L^2}{2}
$$

$$
R_{By} = \dfrac{w \cdot L}{2}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply Vertical Force Equilibrium:**

$$
\sum F_y = 0
$$

$$
R_{Ay} + R_{By} - w \cdot L = 0
$$

$$
R_{Ay} = w \cdot L - \dfrac{w \cdot L}{2} = \dfrac{w \cdot L}{2}
$$

**Final Result:**

$$
\boxed{R_{Ay} = R_{By} = \dfrac{w \cdot L}{2}}
$$

## 3.3 Derivation: Cantilever Beam with Point Load $P$ at Free End

**Setup:** A cantilever beam of length $L$ is fixed at A (left end) and free at B (right end). A point load $P$ acts downward at B.

**Step 1 — Identify Unknowns at Fixed Support A:**
- $R_{Ay}$ (vertical reaction)
- $M_A$ (fixed-end moment)

Total unknowns = 2.

**Step 2 — Apply Vertical Force Equilibrium:**

$$
\sum F_y = 0
$$

$$
R_{Ay} - P = 0
$$

$$
\boxed{R_{Ay} = P \quad \text{(upward)}}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply Moment Equilibrium about A (CCW positive):**

$$
\sum M_A = 0
$$

The load $P$ creates a clockwise moment about A. For equilibrium, the fixed support must provide an **equal and opposite counter-clockwise resisting moment**.

$$
M_A - P \cdot L = 0
$$

$$
\boxed{M_A = P \cdot L \quad \text{(counter-clockwise, resisting)}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why $M_A$ is the resisting moment:** The fixed support must physically prevent the beam from rotating. If the applied load $P$ tends to rotate the beam CW about A, the wall pushes back with a CCW moment. This is **Newton's Third Law in rotational form**.

## 3.4 Derivation: Cantilever Beam with UDL $w$ over Entire Span

**Setup:** Cantilever of length $L$ fixed at A, free at B, with UDL $w$ over entire span.

**Step 1 — Equivalent Point Load:**

$$
W = w \cdot L \quad \text{acting at } L/2 \text{ from A}
$$

**Step 2 — Vertical Force Equilibrium:**

$$
\sum F_y = 0
$$

$$
R_{Ay} - w \cdot L = 0
$$

$$
\boxed{R_{Ay} = w \cdot L}
$$

**Step 3 — Moment Equilibrium about A:**

$$
\sum M_A = 0
$$

$$
M_A - (w \cdot L) \cdot \dfrac{L}{2} = 0
$$

$$
\boxed{M_A = \dfrac{w \cdot L^2}{2}}
$$

## 3.5 Comprehensive Analytical Matrix (Quick Reference for Problem Solving)

| Problem Scenario | Step 1: Free Body | Step 2: Convert UDL | Step 3: $\Sigma M$ about one support | Step 4: $\Sigma F_y = 0$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| SSB + Point $P$ at $a$ from A | Sketch + mark $R_A, R_B$ | Not applicable | $R_B \cdot L = P \cdot a$ | $R_A = P - R_B$ |
| SSB + UDL $w$ over $L$ | Sketch | $W = wL$ at $L/2$ | $R_B \cdot L = wL \cdot L/2$ | $R_A = wL - R_B$ |
| SSB + UDL $w$ over $[x_1, x_2]$ | Sketch | $W = w(x_2-x_1)$ at $\bar{x}$ | $R_B \cdot L = W \cdot \bar{x}$ | $R_A = W - R_B$ |
| Cantilever + Point $P$ at $L$ | Sketch | Not applicable | $M_A = P \cdot L$ | $R_A = P$ |
| Cantilever + UDL $w$ over $L$ | Sketch | $W = wL$ at $L/2$ | $M_A = wL \cdot L/2$ | $R_A = wL$ |

> Physical beam-load-support schematics are best represented in Mermaid as **flow-style block diagrams** mapping the load path, since Mermaid does not natively render engineering shapes. Each diagram below conveys the **load transfer mechanism** for a specific beam configuration.

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Load Transfer in a Simply Supported Beam (UDL over Full Span)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Wall - Pin Support RA] -->|Vertical Reaction RA = wL/2| B[Beam Span L]
    B -->|Bending Action| C[Wall - Roller Support RB]
    UDL[UDL Intensity w kN/m over span L] -->|Total Load wL at midspan| B
    B -->|Reaction RB = wL/2| C
    A -.->|RAx = 0 no horizontal load| A
    style A fill:#1f77b4,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style B fill:#ff7f0e,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style C fill:#2ca02c,stroke:#000,color:#fff

```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Cantilever Beam Load Path (Point Load at Free End)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    W[Fixed Wall - Built-in Support] -->|Vertical Reaction RA = P| M[Beam of Length L]
    M -->|Free End| P[Point Load P kN Downward]
    W -->|Resisting Moment MA = P times L| M
    style W fill:#1f77b4,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style M fill:#ff7f0e,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style P fill:#d62728,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Free Body Decomposition (UDL to Equivalent Point Load)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Q1[UDL w over span L] --> R1[Compute Total Force: W = wL]
    R1 --> S1[Locate Centroid: L/2 from A]
    S1 --> T1[Equivalent Point Load W at L/2]
    T1 --> U1[Apply Equilibrium: Sum M about A = 0]
    U1 --> V1[Solve for RB]
    V1 --> W1[Use Sum Fy = 0 to find RA]
    style Q1 fill:#9467bd,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style T1 fill:#8c564b,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style V1 fill:#e377c2,stroke:#000,color:#fff

```

## 4.4 Block Diagram: Decision Tree for Beam Analysis (KTU 2024 Strategy)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    D1[Start: Beam with Loads] --> D2{Beam Type?}
    D2 -->|Simply Supported| D3[Unknowns: RAy, RBy]
    D2 -->|Cantilever| D4[Unknowns: RAy, MA]
    D3 --> D5{Sum of Moments about A = 0}
    D5 --> D6[Solve for RBy]
    D6 --> D7[Sum Fy = 0 to find RAy]
    D4 --> D8{Sum Fy = 0}
    D8 --> D9[Solve for RAy]
    D9 --> D10[Sum MA = 0 to find MA]
    D7 --> D11[Final Answer with Units]
    D10 --> D11
    style D1 fill:#17becf,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style D11 fill:#bcbd22,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Q1.** Distinguish between a **statically determinate** and **statically indeterminate** beam. Give one example of each.

**Model Answer:**

A beam is **statically determinate** when the number of unknown support reactions equals the number of independent equilibrium equations (which is 3 for a 2D rigid body). Such a beam can be analyzed using equilibrium equations alone. **Example:** A simply supported beam with a pin and a roller (2 unknowns in vertical direction for vertical loads) — solvable using $\Sigma F_y = 0$ and $\Sigma M = 0$.

A beam is **statically indeterminate** when the number of unknown support reactions exceeds the number of available equilibrium equations. Additional compatibility equations (involving deformations) are required. **Example:** A fixed-fixed beam with both ends built-in (6 unknowns in 2D analysis) — only 3 equilibrium equations available, so 3 degrees of indeterminacy.

**[Marks Distribution: 1 + 1 + 0.5 + 0.5]**

---

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Q2.** Define a **Uniformly Distributed Load (UDL)**. How is the equivalent point load of a UDL acting over a length computed, and where does it act?

**Model Answer:**

A **Uniformly Distributed Load (UDL)** is a load whose intensity is constant per unit length over the portion of the beam on which it acts. It is typically expressed in **kN/m** or **N/m**.

The equivalent point load $W$ of a UDL of intensity $w$ acting over a length $a$ is:

$$
W = w \cdot a
$$

For a UDL of uniform intensity, this equivalent point load acts at the **centroid (midpoint)** of the loaded length. If the UDL extends from $x_1$ to $x_2$, the centroid is at $\bar{x} = (x_1 + x_2) / 2$.

**[Marks Distribution: 1 + 1 + 1]**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module 1 — Internal Choice Pattern)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**

### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** A simply supported beam of span **6 m** carries a point load of **20 kN** at a distance of **2 m from the left support A**, and a uniformly distributed load (UDL) of **5 kN/m** over the **right half of the span**. Determine the support reactions at A and B. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A cantilever beam of length **4 m** is fixed at the left end A. It carries a UDL of **3 kN/m** over the **entire span** and a point load of **10 kN** at the **free end**. Calculate the vertical reaction and the fixing moment at A. **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution to Q-A(a):

**Step 1 — Free Body Diagram and Setup:**

Beam of span $L = 6$ m. Point load $P = 20$ kN at $a = 2$ m from A (so $b = 4$ m from B). UDL $w = 5$ kN/m over right half (length 3 m, from $x = 3$ m to $x = 6$ m).

**Step 2 — Convert UDL to Equivalent Point Load:**

$$
W_{UDL} = w \cdot \text{length} = 5 \times 3 = 15 \text{ kN}
$$

This acts at the centroid of the UDL region:

$$
\bar{x}_{UDL} = \dfrac{3 + 6}{2} = 4.5 \text{ m from A}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply Moment Equilibrium about A (eliminates $R_{Ay}$):**

$$
\sum M_A = 0 \quad \text{(CCW positive)}
$$

$$
R_{By} \cdot 6 - P \cdot 2 - W_{UDL} \cdot 4.5 = 0
$$

$$
R_{By} \cdot 6 = 20 \times 2 + 15 \times 4.5
$$

$$
R_{By} \cdot 6 = 40 + 67.5 = 107.5
$$

$$
R_{By} = \dfrac{107.5}{6} = 17.917 \text{ kN} \approx 17.92 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply Vertical Force Equilibrium:**

$$
\sum F_y = 0
$$

$$
R_{Ay} + R_{By} - P - W_{UDL} = 0
$$

$$
R_{Ay} = 20 + 15 - 17.917 = 17.083 \text{ kN} \approx 17.08 \text{ kN}
$$

**Final Answer:**

$$
\boxed{R_{Ay} \approx 17.08 \text{ kN} \uparrow, \quad R_{By} \approx 17.92 \text{ kN} \uparrow}
$$

**Verification (Check with moment about B):**

$$
\sum M_B = R_{Ay} \cdot 6 - P \cdot 4 - W_{UDL} \cdot 1.5
$$

$$
= 17.083 \times 6 - 80 - 22.5 = 102.5 - 102.5 = 0 \quad \checkmark
$$

---

#### Model Solution to Q-A(b):

**Step 1 — Setup:**

Cantilever fixed at A, free at B, length $L = 4$ m. UDL $w = 3$ kN/m over full span. Point load $P = 10$ kN at free end B.

**Step 2 — Convert UDL:**

$$
W_{UDL} = 3 \times 4 = 12 \text{ kN at } 2 \text{ m from A}
$$

**Step 3 — Vertical Force Equilibrium:**

$$
\sum F_y = 0
$$

$$
R_{Ay} - 12 - 10 = 0
$$

$$
\boxed{R_{Ay} = 22 \text{ kN} \uparrow}
$$

**Step 4 — Moment Equilibrium about A (CCW positive):**

The applied loads create CW moments; the fixed support provides a CCW resisting moment $M_A$.

$$
M_A - 12 \times 2 - 10 \times 4 = 0
$$

$$
M_A = 24 + 40 = 64
$$

$$
\boxed{M_A = 64 \text{ kN·m (counter-clockwise, resisting)}}
$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a)** A simply supported beam of span **8 m** carries a UDL of **6 kN/m** over the **left 3 m** of the span, and a point load of **15 kN** at the **center (4 m from A)**. Calculate the reactions at supports A (pin) and B (roller). **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A cantilever beam of length **3 m** fixed at the left end carries a point load of **8 kN** at **1.5 m from the fixed end** and a UDL of **4 kN/m** over the **last 1 m of the free end**. Determine the support reaction and fixing moment at the fixed support. **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution to Q-B(a):

**Step 1 — Convert UDL:**

UDL $w = 6$ kN/m over length 3 m (from $x = 0$ to $x = 3$ m).

$$
W_{UDL} = 6 \times 3 = 18 \text{ kN at } \bar{x} = 1.5 \text{ m from A}
$$

**Step 2 — Moment Equilibrium about A:**

$$
\sum M_A = 0
$$

$$
R_{By} \cdot 8 - 18 \times 1.5 - 15 \times 4 = 0
$$

$$
R_{By} \cdot 8 = 27 + 60 = 87
$$

$$
\boxed{R_{By} = \dfrac{87}{8} = 10.875 \text{ kN}}
$$

**Step 3 — Vertical Force Equilibrium:**

$$
\sum F_y = 0
$$

$$
R_{Ay} = 18 + 15 - 10.875 = 22.125 \text{ kN}
$$

$$
\boxed{R_{Ay} = 22.125 \text{ kN} \uparrow}
$$

---

#### Model Solution to Q-B(b):

**Step 1 — Setup:**

Cantilever fixed at A, length $L = 3$ m. Point load $P = 8$ kN at $x = 1.5$ m from A. UDL $w = 4$ kN/m over the last 1 m (from $x = 2$ m to $x = 3$ m).

**Step 2 — Convert UDL:**

$$
W_{UDL} = 4 \times 1 = 4 \text{ kN at } \bar{x} = 2.5 \text{ m from A}
$$

**Step 3 — Vertical Force Equilibrium:**

$$
\sum F_y = 0
$$

$$
R_{Ay} - 8 - 4 = 0
$$

$$
\boxed{R_{Ay} = 12 \text{ kN} \uparrow}
$$

**Step 4 — Moment Equilibrium about A:**

$$
M_A = 8 \times 1.5 + 4 \times 2.5
$$

$$
M_A = 12 + 10 = 22
$$

$$
\boxed{M_A = 22 \text{ kN·m (counter-clockwise, resisting)}}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Sign Convention Errors:** Forgetting to mark the resisting moment as CCW (or vice versa) when the load creates CW. KTU examiners **deduct 1–2 marks** for inconsistent sign usage.
> 2. **UDL Centroid Mistakes:** When UDL acts on a partial span, students often place the equivalent point load at the **midpoint of the entire span** instead of the **midpoint of the loaded region**. This is a **direct 1-mark cut**.
> 3. **Unit Inconsistency:** Mixing N with m or kN with mm. Always check units **before** substituting values.
> 4. **Taking Moments about the Wrong Point:** For SSBs, taking moments about A is best to find $R_B$. Taking moments about B is best to find $R_A$. Taking moments about a point on the loaded region creates unnecessary algebra — KTU valuators **penalize** this.
> 5. **Forgetting $R_{Ax}$ in Horizontal Loading Problems:** If the problem includes horizontal forces, $R_{Ax}$ is not zero. KTU 2024 problems occasionally include this to test whether students blindly assume $R_{Ax} = 0$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Equilibrium Equations:** Always write all three ($\Sigma F_x$, $\Sigma F_y$, $\Sigma M$) even if not all are needed — KTU 2024 rewards **methodical working**.
- **Pin Support:** Provides 2 reactions ($R_{Ax}, R_{Ay}$). **Roller Support:** Provides 1 reaction (perpendicular to roller plane). **Fixed Support:** Provides 3 reactions ($R_{Ax}, R_{Ay}, M$).
- **UDL → Point Load Conversion:** $W = w \times \text{length}$, acting at the **geometric centroid** of the loaded region.
- **For SSB:** Take $\Sigma M$ about the support where the **largest number of unknowns** intersect.
- **For Cantilever:** Compute $R_A$ from $\Sigma F_y$ first (since $M_A$ does not appear in force equations), then compute $M_A$ from $\Sigma M$.
- **Verification Step:** Always verify by computing the **third equilibrium equation** you didn't use initially — it must reduce to $0 = 0$.
- **Units Rule:** Force in **kN**, length in **m**, moment in **kN·m** — uniform SI units throughout.
- **Direction of Reactions:** Always state the **direction** (upward/downward) along with the magnitude; KTU 2024 valuation explicitly checks this.
- **Static Determinacy Check:** Count unknowns; compare with **3** equilibrium equations — confirms whether the beam is solvable using statics alone.
- **Free Body Diagram (FBD):** Always draw a **clear labeled FBD** at the start of the solution. KTU 2024 awards **0.5–1 mark** for a proper FBD even before the calculation begins.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Force Systems: Rectangular Components in 2D and 3D
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GBEST213 - ENGINEERING MECHANICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Force Systems: Rectangular Components in 2D and 3D

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Force System** is a collection of forces acting on a rigid body or a particle, classified by their geometric arrangement (coplanar, concurrent, parallel, or general). A **Rectangular Component** of a force is the projection of that force along mutually perpendicular (orthogonal) axes — typically the Cartesian axes $\hat{i}$, $\hat{j}$, $\hat{k}$ in 2D (x, y) or 3D (x, y, z) space.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Note:** The rectangular (orthogonal) component of a force $F$ along an axis is the algebraic measure of the projection of that force on the axis. It is a **scalar** with a sign convention (positive or negative) determined by its direction relative to the chosen positive axis.

**2D Force System (Coplanar / Rectangular):** Forces acting in a single plane. Each force $\vec{F}$ is resolved into two perpendicular components:
- $F_x = F \cos \theta$ (along the x-axis)
- $F_y = F \sin \theta$ (along the y-axis)

**3D Force System (Spatial / Rectangular):** Forces acting in three-dimensional space. Each force $\vec{F}$ is resolved into three perpendicular components:
- $F_x = F \cos \alpha$ (along x-axis)
- $F_y = F \cos \beta$ (along y-axis)
- $F_z = F \cos \gamma$ (along z-axis)

where $\alpha$, $\beta$, $\gamma$ are the **direction angles** measured from the positive x, y, z axes respectively.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are **pulling a sled** with a rope using force $F$ at an angle $\theta$ above the ground.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Think of pushing a lawn roller. When you push along the handle at an angle, only part of your effort moves the roller forward (the horizontal component $F_x$), while the rest either lifts it or wastes energy against the ground (the vertical component $F_y$). This is exactly the idea of *resolving* a force into rectangular components — we break a single diagonal push into its effective horizontal and vertical "shares."

**2D Analogy:** A single diagonal arrow shot at a target — its horizontal travel depends only on $F_x$, its vertical fall depends only on $F_y$.

**3D Analogy:** An airplane flying in a 3D sky. Its forward thrust can be split into how much it pushes North ($F_x$), East ($F_y$), and upward ($F_z$). These three numbers fully describe where the plane is heading.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metrics & Constants:**
> - **SI Unit of Force:** **Newton (N)**, where $1 \text{ N} = 1 \text{ kg} \cdot \text{m/s}^2$
> - **Cartesian Coordinate System:** Right-handed (thumb = x, index = y, middle = z)
> - **Standard Convention:** Forces directed along positive axes are **positive**; those along negative axes are **negative**.

### 1.3 Why Rectangular Components Matter in Engineering

Rectangular components are the **backbone of vector analysis in statics and dynamics**. Every real engineering problem — designing a truss, analyzing a crane, or computing wind loads on a building — begins by breaking forces into x, y, z parts so the equations of equilibrium ($\sum F_x = 0$, $\sum F_y = 0$, $\sum F_z = 0$) can be applied. Without this resolution, solving concurrent or non-concurrent force systems would be nearly impossible.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Conceptual Breakdown — How Rectangular Resolution Works

**Step 1: Establish a Coordinate Frame**
- Choose a fixed origin O and orthogonal axes (x, y) for 2D or (x, y, z) for 3D.
- The choice of origin does not change the physics; it only changes the algebra.

**Step 2: Identify the Force Vector**
- A force has both **magnitude** (a positive scalar) and **direction** (an orientation in space).
- It is denoted as $\vec{F}$ (vector) and $F$ or $\vert \vec{F} \vert$ (magnitude).

**Step 3: Project the Force onto Each Axis**
- Drop a perpendicular from the tip of the force vector onto each axis.
- The length of this projection is the rectangular component, signed by direction.

**Step 4: Sum the Components to Get the Resultant**
- For multiple forces, components along the same axis are summed algebraically.
- The resultant is reconstructed using the **Pythagorean theorem** (2D) or its 3D extension.

**Why this works:** The rectangular axes form a complete, independent basis for Euclidean space — meaning *any* force vector can be uniquely described by its projections on these axes. This is the **Principle of Orthogonal Decomposition** of vectors.

### 2.2 Unit Vector Representation (KTU High-Yield)

A unit vector is a direction-only vector of magnitude **1**. The Cartesian unit vectors are:
- $\hat{i}$ — along +x axis
- $\hat{j}$ — along +y axis  
- $\hat{k}$ — along +z axis

Any force vector can be written as:
$$\vec{F} = F_x \hat{i} + F_y \hat{j} + F_z \hat{k}$$

Its magnitude is:
$$F = \sqrt{F_x^2 + F_y^2 + F_z^2}$$

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---------|---------|----------------|
| 1 | 2D Rectangular Components | $F_x = F \cos \theta$, $\;F_y = F \sin \theta$ | $\theta$ measured from +x axis (CCW positive) |
| 2 | 3D Rectangular Components | $F_x = F \cos \alpha$, $\;F_y = F \cos \beta$, $\;F_z = F \cos \gamma$ | $\alpha, \beta, \gamma$ = direction angles |
| 3 | Direction Cosines Identity | $\cos^2 \alpha + \cos^2 \beta + \cos^2 \gamma = 1$ | **Mandatory for KTU problems** |
| 4 | Force Vector in Cartesian Form | $\vec{F} = F_x \hat{i} + F_y \hat{j} + F_z \hat{k}$ | $\hat{i}, \hat{j}, \hat{k}$ are unit vectors |
| 5 | Magnitude of a 3D Force | $F = \sqrt{F_x^2 + F_y^2 + F_z^2}$ | Standard Pythagorean extension |
| 6 | Unit Vector of a Force | $\hat{u} = \frac{\vec{F}}{\vert \vec{F} \vert} = \frac{F_x \hat{i} + F_y \hat{j} + F_z \hat{k}}{F}$ | Dimensionless |
| 7 | Resultant (2D) | $R_x = \sum F_x$, $\;R_y = \sum F_y$ | Algebraic sums along each axis |
| 8 | Resultant Magnitude (2D) | $R = \sqrt{R_x^2 + R_y^2}$ | Always positive |
| 9 | Resultant Direction (2D) | $\theta_R = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{R_y}{R_x}\right)$ | Quadrant check required |
| 10 | Resultant (3D) | $R_x = \sum F_x$, $\;R_y = \sum F_y$, $\;R_z = \sum F_z$ | Three scalar equations |
| 11 | Resultant Magnitude (3D) | $R = \sqrt{R_x^2 + R_y^2 + R_z^2}$ | Standard 3D Pythagoras |
| 12 | Position Vector | $\vec{r} = x \hat{i} + y \hat{j} + z \hat{k}$ | Used for moment calculations |
| 13 | Force Along a Line AB | $\vec{F} = F \cdot \hat{u}_{AB} = F \cdot \frac{\vec{AB}}{\vert \vec{AB} \vert}$ | Critical for 3D problems |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These formulas are the daily tools of mechanical, civil, and aerospace engineers — used in truss analysis, robotic arm design, structural load estimation, and flight path computation. Mastering them is non-negotiable for KTU board exams.

### 2.4 Special Note: Direction Angles vs. Slope Definition

In KTU 3D problems, a force is often defined by **two points** through which its line of action passes (e.g., from $A(2, 3, 6)$ to $B(4, 8, -2)$). The procedure is:

1. Form the position vector $\vec{AB} = (x_B - x_A)\hat{i} + (y_B - y_A)\hat{j} + (z_B - z_A)\hat{k}$
2. Find its magnitude $\vert \vec{AB} \vert$
3. The direction cosines are: $\cos \alpha = \frac{x_B - x_A}{\vert \vec{AB} \vert}$, etc.
4. The unit vector is $\hat{u}_{AB} = \frac{\vec{AB}}{\vert \vec{AB} \vert}$
5. The force vector is $\vec{F} = F \cdot \hat{u}_{AB}$
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Resolving a 2D Force into Rectangular Components

**Given:** A force $F$ acting at an angle $\theta$ measured counter-clockwise (CCW) from the positive x-axis.

**Step 1 — Drop perpendiculars from the head of $\vec{F}$ to the x and y axes.**

Let the tail of $\vec{F}$ be at origin $O(0,0)$ and the head at point $P(x, y)$. The force vector makes angle $\theta$ with the +x axis.

**Step 2 — Apply trigonometry in the right triangle $OAP$** (where $A$ is the foot of the perpendicular on the x-axis).

In $\triangle OAP$:
- $\cos \theta = \frac{\text{adjacent}}{\text{hypotenuse}} = \frac{F_x}{F}$
- $\sin \theta = \frac{\text{opposite}}{\text{hypotenuse}} = \frac{F_y}{F}$

**Step 3 — Solve for the components.**

$$F_x = F \cos \theta$$
$$F_y = F \sin \theta$$

**Step 4 — Reconstruct the force vector.**

$$\vec{F} = F_x \hat{i} + F_y \hat{j} = F \cos \theta \, \hat{i} + F \sin \theta \, \hat{j}$$

**Step 5 — Verify the magnitude.**

$$F = \sqrt{F_x^2 + F_y^2} = \sqrt{F^2 \cos^2 \theta + F^2 \sin^2 \theta} = F \sqrt{\cos^2 \theta + \sin^2 \theta}$$

Using the trigonometric identity $\cos^2 \theta + \sin^2 \theta = 1$:

$$F = F \cdot 1 = F \quad \checkmark$$

This confirms that resolution is reversible (a unique decomposition).

### 3.2 Derivation: Resolving a 3D Force into Rectangular Components

**Given:** A force $F$ with direction angles $\alpha, \beta, \gamma$ measured from the +x, +y, +z axes respectively.

**Step 1 — Project $\vec{F}$ onto the x-axis.**

The angle between $\vec{F}$ and the x-axis is $\alpha$. The projection length on the x-axis is the adjacent side of the triangle formed by $\vec{F}$ and the x-axis. Therefore:

$$F_x = F \cos \alpha$$

**Step 2 — Project $\vec{F}$ onto the y-axis.**

Similarly, the projection on the y-axis:

$$F_y = F \cos \beta$$

**Step 3 — Project $\vec{F}$ onto the z-axis.**

The projection on the z-axis:

$$F_z = F \cos \gamma$$

**Step 4 — Express the force vector.**

$$\vec{F} = F_x \hat{i} + F_y \hat{j} + F_z \hat{k} = F(\cos \alpha \, \hat{i} + \cos \beta \, \hat{j} + \cos \gamma \, \hat{k})$$

**Step 5 — Derive the Direction Cosines Identity.**

Take the magnitude squared of both sides of $\vec{F} = F_x \hat{i} + F_y \hat{j} + F_z \hat{k}$:

$$F^2 = F_x^2 + F_y^2 + F_z^2$$

Substitute $F_x = F \cos \alpha$, etc.:

$$F^2 = (F \cos \alpha)^2 + (F \cos \beta)^2 + (F \cos \gamma)^2$$

$$F^2 = F^2 (\cos^2 \alpha + \cos^2 \beta + \cos^2 \gamma)$$

Divide both sides by $F^2$:

$$\boxed{\cos^2 \alpha + \cos^2 \beta + \cos^2 \gamma = 1}$$

This is the **Direction Cosines Identity** — a cornerstone result in 3D statics. KTU examiners love to test whether students can use it to find a missing direction angle given the other two.

### 3.3 Derivation: Resultant of Multiple 2D Forces (Algebraic Method)

**Given:** Forces $F_1, F_2, F_3, \ldots, F_n$ acting in the xy-plane at angles $\theta_1, \theta_2, \ldots, \theta_n$ from the +x axis.

**Step 1 — Resolve each force into components.**

For force $F_i$:
$$F_{ix} = F_i \cos \theta_i, \quad F_{iy} = F_i \sin \theta_i$$

**Step 2 — Sum all x-components algebraically.**

$$R_x = \sum_{i=1}^{n} F_{ix} = F_1 \cos \theta_1 + F_2 \cos \theta_2 + \cdots + F_n \cos \theta_n$$

**Step 3 — Sum all y-components algebraically.**

$$R_y = \sum_{i=1}^{n} F_{iy} = F_1 \sin \theta_1 + F_2 \sin \theta_2 + \cdots + F_n \sin \theta_n$$

**Step 4 — Compute the magnitude of the resultant.**

$$R = \sqrt{R_x^2 + R_y^2}$$

**Step 5 — Compute the direction of the resultant.**

$$\theta_R = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{R_y}{R_x}\right)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Quadrant Adjustment Rule (KTU Pitfall):** The $\tan^{-1}$ function alone returns values only in Quadrants I and IV. You **must** check the signs of $R_x$ and $R_y$ to place $\theta_R$ in the correct quadrant. If $R_x < 0$ and $R_y > 0$, add $180°$ (or use the ATAN2 function in calculators).

### 3.4 Derivation: Resultant of Multiple 3D Forces

**Given:** Forces $\vec{F}_1, \vec{F}_2, \ldots, \vec{F}_n$ in 3D, each expressed in Cartesian form.

**Step 1 — Sum components along each axis.**

$$\vec{R} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \vec{F}_i = \left(\sum F_{ix}\right) \hat{i} + \left(\sum F_{iy}\right) \hat{j} + \left(\sum F_{iz}\right) \hat{k}$$

Therefore:

$$R_x = \sum F_{ix}, \quad R_y = \sum F_{iy}, \quad R_z = \sum F_{iz}$$

**Step 2 — Magnitude.**

$$R = \sqrt{R_x^2 + R_y^2 + R_z^2}$$

**Step 3 — Direction cosines of the resultant.**

$$\cos \alpha_R = \frac{R_x}{R}, \quad \cos \beta_R = \frac{R_y}{R}, \quad \cos \gamma_R = \frac{R_z}{R}$$

**Verification:** $\cos^2 \alpha_R + \cos^2 \beta_R + \cos^2 \gamma_R = 1$ should hold.

### 3.5 Worked Example — 3D Force Along a Line (KTU Standard Pattern)

**Problem:** A force of magnitude $F = 100 \text{ N}$ acts along the line from point $A(1, 2, 3)$ to point $B(4, 6, 15)$. Express the force as a Cartesian vector and find its direction angles.

**Step 1 — Position vector $\vec{AB}$.**

$$\vec{AB} = (x_B - x_A) \hat{i} + (y_B - y_A) \hat{j} + (z_B - z_A) \hat{k}$$

$$\vec{AB} = (4 - 1) \hat{i} + (6 - 2) \hat{j} + (15 - 3) \hat{k}$$

$$\vec{AB} = 3 \hat{i} + 4 \hat{j} + 12 \hat{k}$$

**Step 2 — Magnitude of $\vec{AB}$.**

$$\vert \vec{AB} \vert = \sqrt{3^2 + 4^2 + 12^2} = \sqrt{9 + 16 + 144} = \sqrt{169} = 13 \text{ units}$$

**Step 3 — Unit vector along AB.**

$$\hat{u}_{AB} = \frac{\vec{AB}}{\vert \vec{AB} \vert} = \frac{3 \hat{i} + 4 \hat{j} + 12 \hat{k}}{13}$$

**Step 4 — Force vector.**

$$\vec{F} = F \cdot \hat{u}_{AB} = 100 \cdot \frac{3 \hat{i} + 4 \hat{j} + 12 \hat{k}}{13}$$

$$\vec{F} = \frac{300}{13} \hat{i} + \frac{400}{13} \hat{j} + \frac{1200}{13} \hat{k}$$

Numerically:

$$\vec{F} \approx 23.08 \hat{i} + 30.77 \hat{j} + 92.31 \hat{k} \quad (\text{N})$$

**Step 5 — Direction cosines.**

$$\cos \alpha = \frac{3}{13}, \quad \cos \beta = \frac{4}{13}, \quad \cos \gamma = \frac{12}{13}$$

**Step 6 — Direction angles.**

$$\alpha = \cos^{-1}\!\left(\frac{3}{13}\right) \approx 76.66°$$

$$\beta = \cos^{-1}\!\left(\frac{4}{13}\right) \approx 72.08°$$

$$\gamma = \cos^{-1}\!\left(\frac{12}{13}\right) \approx 22.62°$$

**Verification using identity:**

$$\left(\frac{3}{13}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{4}{13}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{12}{13}\right)^2 = \frac{9 + 16 + 144}{169} = \frac{169}{169} = 1 \quad \checkmark$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Flow: Solving a Rectangular Force Component Problem

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **logical procedure** for solving KTU 2D/3D force component problems — the architecture a student should follow mentally and on paper.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Force Problem Given] --> B{Is it 2D or 3D?}
    B -->|2D| C[Identify Force F and Angle theta from +x axis]
    B -->|3D| D{F is given as: Magnitude + Angles OR as Two Points?}
    C --> E[Resolve: Fx = F cos theta, Fy = F sin theta]
    D -->|Angles alpha, beta, gamma| F[Resolve: Fx = F cos alpha, Fy = F cos beta, Fz = F cos gamma]
    D -->|Two Points A and B| G[Form Position Vector AB]
    G --> H[Compute Magnitude: d = sqrt of sum of squares]
    H --> I[Find Unit Vector uAB = AB / d]
    I --> J[Force Vector: F = F times uAB]
    F --> K[Multiple Forces? Sum components]
    E --> K
    J --> K
    K --> L[Compute Resultant Rx, Ry, Rz]
    L --> M[Find Magnitude: R = sqrt of sum of squares]
    L --> N[Find Direction: theta = tan inverse Ry by Rx]
    M --> O[Apply Direction Cosines Identity Check]
    N --> O
    O --> P[End: Final Result]
```

### 4.2 Conceptual Architecture — Rectangular Decomposition of a 3D Force

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P0[Force Vector F in 3D Space] --> Px[Projection on x-axis: Fx]
    P0 --> Py[Projection on y-axis: Fy]
    P0 --> Pz[Projection on z-axis: Fz]
    Px --> Sum[Cartesian Vector Sum]
    Py --> Sum
    Pz --> Sum
    Sum --> Out[F = Fx i + Fy j + Fz k]
    Out --> Check{Magnitude Identity Holds?}
    Check -->|Yes| Done[Valid Decomposition]
    Check -->|No| Err[Re-check Inputs]
```

### 4.3 Conceptual Diagram: 2D Force Resolution (ASCII Schematic)

> [!NOTE]
> The following ASCII block diagram shows the geometric layout of resolving a 2D force into its rectangular components. Use this visualization to mentally anchor every problem.

```
                  y
                  |
                  |
                  |     /| 
                  |    / |
                  |   /  |  Fy = F sin(θ)
                  |  /   |
                  | /    |
                  |/ θ   |
        ----------+------|---------- x
                 /|      |
                / |      |
               /  |      |
              /   |      |
             /    |      |
            /     |      |
           / Fx   |      |
          / =     |      |
         /F cosθ  |      |
                  |
                  |
              F = resultant force
        Fx = horizontal component
        Fy = vertical component
        θ  = angle from +x axis (CCW positive)
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1: Define rectangular components of a force in 2D. How are they obtained?** `[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Model Question Paper]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The rectangular components of a force are the projections of the force along two mutually perpendicular axes (x and y) in a 2D plane. If a force of magnitude $F$ acts at an angle $\theta$ measured counter-clockwise from the positive x-axis, its rectangular components are:

$$F_x = F \cos \theta, \quad F_y = F \sin \theta$$

These are obtained by dropping perpendiculars from the tip (head) of the force vector onto the x and y axes. The lengths of these perpendiculars give the magnitudes, with signs assigned based on the direction (positive if aligned with the positive axis, negative if opposite). The force can be reconstructed as $\vec{F} = F_x \hat{i} + F_y \hat{j}$, and its magnitude is $F = \sqrt{F_x^2 + F_y^2}$.

> **[Marking Scheme: 1M definition + 1M formulas + 1M reconstruction statement]**

---

#### **Question 2: What is the direction cosines identity? Why is it important in 3D force analysis?** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The direction cosines identity states that for any force vector $\vec{F}$ in 3D space with direction angles $\alpha$, $\beta$, and $\gamma$ measured from the positive x, y, and z axes respectively:

$$\cos^2 \alpha + \cos^2 \beta + \cos^2 \gamma = 1$$

It is derived from the fact that the squared magnitude of a force equals the sum of the squares of its components. The identity is important because:

1. It serves as a **consistency check** — given any two direction angles, the third is uniquely determined (with sign ambiguity).
2. It guarantees that direction cosines are not independent — they must satisfy this geometric constraint.
3. KTU problems frequently use it to compute a missing angle when two are given.

> **[Marking Scheme: 1M statement + 1M derivation hint + 1M importance]**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Modified Past Pattern]`

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:** A force of magnitude $F = 500 \text{ N}$ acts at a point in the xy-plane. The force makes an angle of $60°$ with the positive x-axis (measured counter-clockwise). Resolve the force into its x and y components. Also find the unit vector along the force.

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:** Three forces act on a particle:
- $F_1 = 100 \text{ N}$ along the +x axis
- $F_2 = 200 \text{ N}$ at $120°$ from +x axis (in xy-plane)
- $F_3 = 150 \text{ N}$ at $240°$ from +x axis (in xy-plane)

Find the magnitude and direction of the resultant force.

---

**Model Solution for Question A:**

**Part (a) Solution:**

Given: $F = 500 \text{ N}$, $\theta = 60°$.

Step 1 — Resolve the force:
$$F_x = F \cos \theta = 500 \cos 60° = 500 \times 0.5 = 250 \text{ N}$$
$$F_y = F \sin \theta = 500 \sin 60° = 500 \times \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \approx 433.01 \text{ N}$$

Step 2 — Express as a vector:
$$\vec{F} = 250 \hat{i} + 433.01 \hat{j} \text{ N}$$

Step 3 — Find the unit vector:
$$\hat{u} = \frac{\vec{F}}{F} = \frac{250 \hat{i} + 433.01 \hat{j}}{500} = 0.5 \hat{i} + 0.866 \hat{j}$$

**Verification:** $\sqrt{0.5^2 + 0.866^2} = \sqrt{0.25 + 0.75} = \sqrt{1} = 1$ ✓ (unit vector is dimensionless)

> **[Mark Allocation: 2M setup + 2M computation + 2M unit vector + 1M verification]**

---

**Part (b) Solution:**

Given forces in 2D plane, all acting at the same point.

**Step 1 — Resolve each force into components.**

For $F_1$ (at $\theta_1 = 0°$):
$$F_{1x} = 100 \cos 0° = 100 \text{ N}, \quad F_{1y} = 100 \sin 0° = 0 \text{ N}$$

For $F_2$ (at $\theta_2 = 120°$):
$$F_{2x} = 200 \cos 120° = 200 \times (-0.5) = -100 \text{ N}$$
$$F_{2y} = 200 \sin 120° = 200 \times \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \approx 173.21 \text{ N}$$

For $F_3$ (at $\theta_3 = 240°$):
$$F_{3x} = 150 \cos 240° = 150 \times (-0.5) = -75 \text{ N}$$
$$F_{3y} = 150 \sin 240° = 150 \times (-\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}) \approx -129.90 \text{ N}$$

**Step 2 — Sum components.**

$$R_x = F_{1x} + F_{2x} + F_{3x} = 100 + (-100) + (-75) = -75 \text{ N}$$
$$R_y = F_{1y} + F_{2y} + F_{3y} = 0 + 173.21 + (-129.90) = 43.31 \text{ N}$$

**Step 3 — Magnitude of the resultant.**

$$R = \sqrt{R_x^2 + R_y^2} = \sqrt{(-75)^2 + (43.31)^2}$$

$$R = \sqrt{5625 + 1875.74} = \sqrt{7500.74} \approx 86.61 \text{ N}$$

**Step 4 — Direction of the resultant.**

$$\tan \theta_R = \frac{R_y}{R_x} = \frac{43.31}{-75} = -0.5775$$

The magnitude gives $\theta = 30°$, but since $R_x < 0$ and $R_y > 0$, the resultant lies in **Quadrant II**.

$$\theta_R = 180° - 30° = 150° \text{ (measured CCW from +x axis)}$$

**Result:** $\vec{R} \approx 86.61 \text{ N}$ at $150°$ from the +x axis.

> **[Mark Allocation: 3M component resolution + 2M summation + 1M magnitude + 1M direction with quadrant correction]**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]`

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:** A force of magnitude $F = 80 \text{ N}$ acts along the line from point $A(2, -1, 3)$ to point $B(3, 4, -1)$. Express the force as a Cartesian vector $\vec{F} = F_x \hat{i} + F_y \hat{j} + F_z \hat{k}$.

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:** Two forces act on a particle:
- $\vec{F}_1 = (50 \hat{i} - 30 \hat{j} + 20 \hat{k}) \text{ N}$
- $\vec{F}_2 = (-20 \hat{i} + 40 \hat{j} - 60 \hat{k}) \text{ N}$

Find the magnitude of the resultant and its direction cosines.

---

**Model Solution for Question B:**

**Part (a) Solution:**

**Step 1 — Position vector from A to B.**

$$\vec{AB} = (x_B - x_A) \hat{i} + (y_B - y_A) \hat{j} + (z_B - z_A) \hat{k}$$

$$\vec{AB} = (3 - 2) \hat{i} + (4 - (-1)) \hat{j} + (-1 - 3) \hat{k}$$

$$\vec{AB} = 1 \hat{i} + 5 \hat{j} - 4 \hat{k}$$

**Step 2 — Magnitude of $\vec{AB}$.**

$$\vert \vec{AB} \vert = \sqrt{1^2 + 5^2 + (-4)^2} = \sqrt{1 + 25 + 16} = \sqrt{42} \approx 6.481 \text{ units}$$

**Step 3 — Unit vector along AB.**

$$\hat{u}_{AB} = \frac{\vec{AB}}{\vert \vec{AB} \vert} = \frac{1 \hat{i} + 5 \hat{j} - 4 \hat{k}}{\sqrt{42}}$$

$$\hat{u}_{AB} \approx 0.1543 \hat{i} + 0.7715 \hat{j} - 0.6172 \hat{k}$$

**Step 4 — Force vector.**

$$\vec{F} = F \cdot \hat{u}_{AB} = 80 \cdot \frac{1 \hat{i} + 5 \hat{j} - 4 \hat{k}}{\sqrt{42}}$$

$$\vec{F} = \frac{80}{\sqrt{42}} \hat{i} + \frac{400}{\sqrt{42}} \hat{j} - \frac{320}{\sqrt{42}} \hat{k}$$

$$\vec{F} \approx 12.35 \hat{i} + 61.72 \hat{j} - 49.38 \hat{k} \quad \text{N}$$

> **[Mark Allocation: 2M position vector + 2M magnitude + 1M unit vector + 2M final force]**

---

**Part (b) Solution:**

**Step 1 — Sum the forces component-wise.**

$$\vec{R} = \vec{F}_1 + \vec{F}_2 = (50 - 20) \hat{i} + (-30 + 40) \hat{j} + (20 - 60) \hat{k}$$

$$\vec{R} = 30 \hat{i} + 10 \hat{j} - 40 \hat{k} \text{ N}$$

**Step 2 — Magnitude of the resultant.**

$$R = \sqrt{30^2 + 10^2 + (-40)^2} = \sqrt{900 + 100 + 1600} = \sqrt{2600}$$

$$R \approx 50.99 \text{ N}$$

**Step 3 — Direction cosines.**

$$\cos \alpha = \frac{R_x}{R} = \frac{30}{\sqrt{2600}} \approx 0.5885$$

$$\cos \beta = \frac{R_y}{R} = \frac{10}{\sqrt{2600}} \approx 0.1961$$

$$\cos \gamma = \frac{R_z}{R} = \frac{-40}{\sqrt{2600}} \approx -0.7845$$

**Step 4 — Verification with direction cosines identity.**

$$\cos^2 \alpha + \cos^2 \beta + \cos^2 \gamma = (0.5885)^2 + (0.1961)^2 + (-0.7845)^2$$

$$= 0.3463 + 0.0385 + 0.6154 = 1.0002 \approx 1 \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 5 — Direction angles (optional, for full credit).**

$$\alpha = \cos^{-1}(0.5885) \approx 53.97°$$
$$\beta = \cos^{-1}(0.1961) \approx 78.69°$$
$$\gamma = \cos^{-1}(-0.7845) \approx 141.61°$$

> **[Mark Allocation: 2M summation + 2M magnitude + 2M direction cosines + 1M verification]**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Sign Errors in 2D Resolution:** Forgetting the negative sign for $\cos 120°$, $\cos 240°$, etc. (Quadrants II, III, IV yield negative x-components).
> 2. **Quadrant Mismatch in 2D Resultant:** Using only $\tan^{-1}$ without checking signs of $R_x$ and $R_y$ — this costs **2 full marks** in KTU papers.
> 3. **Magnitude vs. Component Confusion:** Writing the magnitude where the component is asked, or vice versa.
> 4. **Direction Cosines Identity Forgotten:** Not verifying that $\cos^2 \alpha + \cos^2 \beta + \cos^2 \gamma = 1$. Examiners **expect** this check.
> 5. **Unit Vector Mistake in 3D:** Forgetting to divide by the magnitude $\vert \vec{AB} \vert$ when computing $\hat{u}_{AB}$.
> 6. **Forgetting $\sqrt{}$ for Magnitude:** The magnitude formula has a square root — many students incorrectly sum components linearly.
> 7. **Inconsistent Units:** Mixing N, kN, or kgf without conversion. **Always convert to a single SI unit** at the start of a problem.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Definition:** Rectangular components are orthogonal projections of a force vector onto the Cartesian axes.
- **2D Components:** $F_x = F \cos \theta$, $F_y = F \sin \theta$, with $\theta$ measured CCW from +x axis.
- **3D Components:** $F_x = F \cos \alpha$, $F_y = F \cos \beta$, $F_z = F \cos \gamma$ (direction angles).
- **Mandatory Identity:** $\cos^2 \alpha + \cos^2 \beta + \cos^2 \gamma = 1$ — *use this for every 3D problem.*
- **Cartesian Vector Form:** $\vec{F} = F_x \hat{i} + F_y \hat{j} + F_z \hat{k}$.
- **Magnitude (2D):** $F = \sqrt{F_x^2 + F_y^2}$.
- **Magnitude (3D):** $F = \sqrt{F_x^2 + F_y^2 + F_z^2}$.
- **Unit Vector:** $\hat{u} = \vec{F} / F$ — dimensionless, always magnitude 1.
- **Force Along a Line AB:** $\vec{F} = F \cdot \frac{\vec{AB}}{\vert \vec{AB} \vert}$ — most common KTU 3D pattern.
- **Resultant Components:** $R_x = \sum F_x$, $R_y = \sum F_y$, $R_z = \sum F_z$ (algebraic sums).
- **Resultant Direction (2D):** $\theta_R = \tan^{-1}(R_y / R_x)$ — **always check the quadrant** (Q1: both +; Q2: $R_x < 0$, $R_y > 0$; Q3: both −; Q4: $R_x > 0$, $R_y < 0$).
- **Resultant Direction (3D):** Use direction cosines, $\cos \alpha_R = R_x / R$, etc.
- **Sign Convention:** Forces aligned with positive axes are positive; opposite directions are negative.
- **Verification Step:** After every 3D problem, check the direction cosines identity — it is a 1-mark "freebie" on KTU papers.
- **Common Mistake to Avoid:** Forgetting the square root in magnitude formulas, and ignoring quadrant corrections in 2D.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Moment and Couple
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GBEST213 - ENGINEERING MECHANICS
> - **Course Code:** GBEST213
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Moment and Couple
> - **Cognitive Level Targets:** L2 (Understand), L3 (Apply), L4 (Analyze)
> - **Linked Course Outcomes:** CO1 – Analyse force systems using principles of statics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Moment of a Force (About a Point)

**Formal KTU Definition:**
The **Moment of a force** about a point is the turning effect of the force about that point. It is a vector quantity equal to the **cross product** of the position vector of any point on the line of action of the force (relative to the moment centre) and the force vector itself.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):**
> The moment of a force $\vec{F}$ about point $O$ is defined as:
> $$\vec{M_O} = \vec{r} \times \vec{F}$$
> where $\vec{r}$ is the position vector from $O$ to any point $A$ on the line of action of $\vec{F}$.

**Magnitude:**
$$M_O = F \cdot d$$
where $d$ is the **perpendicular distance** from the moment centre $O$ to the line of action of the force. The **SI unit** is **N·m** (Newton-metre) and the **dimensional formula** is $[M L^2 T^{-2}]$.

**Sign Convention (2-D / Coplanar Forces):**
- **Counter-Clockwise (CCW):** Taken as **positive** (right-hand rule, thumb out of the page).
- **Clockwise (CW):** Taken as **negative** (right-hand rule, thumb into the page).

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — "The Door and the Handle":**
> Imagine pushing a door. If you push near the *hinge*, the door barely moves — your effort is large, but turning effect is small. If you push at the *handle* (far from hinge), the door swings easily. The "turning effect" is the **moment**. It depends on **two things**:
> 1. How **hard** you push (force magnitude, $F$)
> 2. How **far** from the hinge you push (perpendicular distance, $d$)
> Push **perpendicular** to the door for maximum moment. Push at an angle $\theta$, and only $F \sin\theta$ contributes to the moment — the component along the door just pushes it against the frame.

## 1.2 Couple

**Formal KTU Definition:**
A **Couple** is a system of **two equal, opposite, and parallel forces** whose lines of action are **not coincident**. The two forces are separated by a perpendicular distance (called the **arm of the couple**).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):**
> A couple produces pure **rotation** (no translation). It is characterised by:
> $$\vec{C} = \vec{r} \times \vec{F}$$
> where $\vec{r}$ is the position vector from the point of application of one force to the point of application of the equal and opposite force.

**Properties of a Couple:**
1. The **net force** of a couple is **zero** ($\vec{F} + (-\vec{F}) = 0$).
2. The **net moment** of a couple is the **same about every point** in the plane (and in space) — it is a *free vector*.
3. A couple can be **transported** anywhere in its plane (or to any parallel plane) without changing its external effect.
4. A couple can be **replaced** by another couple of the same moment and sense without altering the external effect on a rigid body.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — "Steering Wheel & Tap":**
> - **Steering wheel:** Your two hands apply equal and opposite forces on opposite sides of the wheel, separated by the wheel's diameter. The car turns, but the wheel itself does not slide sideways — pure rotation. This is a **couple**.
> - **Tightening a tap (faucet):** Your fingers and thumb apply opposing forces at a small distance — you twist the tap. Same idea: **couple**.
> - **Opening a jam jar:** One hand pushes the lid CCW, the other pushes the jar CW — the lid rotates, jar stays put. The relative rotation = **moment of the couple**.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Used in This Module)

| Quantity | Symbol | Standard Unit | Notes |
|----------|--------|---------------|-------|
| Force | $F$ | **N** (Newton) | Vector, in 2-D drawn as arrow |
| Moment / Couple | $M, C$ | **N·m** | Vector (sense by right-hand rule) |
| Distance / Arm | $d, a$ | **m** (metre) | Always the **perpendicular** distance |
| Position vector | $\vec{r}$ | **m** | From moment centre to force point |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Two Ingredients of a Moment — Why $F \times d$?

A force creates a moment about point $O$ only through the **component of force perpendicular** to the line joining $O$ to the point of application, OR equivalently, the **perpendicular distance** from $O$ to the line of action of $\vec{F}$. Both viewpoints give the same answer:

$$M_O = F \cdot d \quad \text{or} \quad M_O = F \cdot r \cdot \sin\theta$$

where $\theta$ is the angle between $\vec{r}$ and $\vec{F}$.

- **Step 1:** The force must be *off-centre* (i.e., not pass through the moment centre) to create rotation.
- **Step 2:** The *effective* turning arm is only the **perpendicular component** of the distance. Any part of the force directed *towards* $O$ (radial) just tries to slide the body past $O$ — it does not rotate it.
- **Step 3:** The cross-product formulation $\vec{r} \times \vec{F}$ is the most general; in 2-D it reduces to a scalar with sign.

## 2.2 Varignon's Theorem (Principle of Moments) — *High-Weightage KTU Theorem*

**Statement:**
> *"The moment of the resultant of a system of coplanar concurrent forces about any point in their plane is equal to the algebraic sum of the moments of the individual forces about the same point."*

**Why it works (intuition):**
- **Step 1:** Let the resultant of concurrent forces $\vec{F_1}, \vec{F_2}, \ldots, \vec{F_n}$ (all acting at point $A$) be $\vec{R}$.
- **Step 2:** Force addition is linear: $\vec{R} = \sum \vec{F_i}$.
- **Step 3:** Since $\vec{r} \times (\vec{a} + \vec{b}) = \vec{r} \times \vec{a} + \vec{r} \times \vec{b}$ (linearity of cross product), taking moments about $O$ gives $M_O(\vec{R}) = \sum M_O(\vec{F_i})$.
- **Step 4:** Hence the **single resultant force** at the same point gives the **same moment** as the **sum of all individual moments**.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Utility:** Used in beam analysis, lever calculations, and to reduce many forces to one equivalent force for hand-calculations of support reactions.

## 2.3 Couple — Why Moment is Independent of Reference Point

**Step-by-step reasoning:**

Consider a couple formed by $\vec{F}$ at point $A$ and $-\vec{F}$ at point $B$. Take any arbitrary point $O$.

- **Step 1:** Moment of force at $A$ about $O$ = $\vec{r_A} \times \vec{F}$.
- **Step 2:** Moment of force at $B$ about $O$ = $\vec{r_B} \times (-\vec{F}) = -\vec{r_B} \times \vec{F}$.
- **Step 3:** Total moment about $O$ = $\vec{r_A} \times \vec{F} - \vec{r_B} \times \vec{F} = (\vec{r_A} - \vec{r_B}) \times \vec{F}$.
- **Step 4:** But $\vec{r_A} - \vec{r_B} = \vec{r}$ is the position vector of $A$ *w.r.t.* $B$, i.e., the arm of the couple.
- **Step 5:** Hence, **total moment = $\vec{r} \times \vec{F}$**, which does **not** depend on $O$. QED.

## 2.4 Resolution of a Force into a Force–Couple System

**Theorem:** *A force $\vec{F}$ acting at point $A$ can be replaced by an equal force $\vec{F}$ acting at any other point $B$, together with a couple of moment $\vec{M} = \vec{r}_{A/B} \times \vec{F}$*, where $\vec{r}_{A/B}$ is the position vector from $B$ to $A$.

**Why it works:** Moving the line of action of a force *shifts* its moment about every other point. To keep the external effect identical, we must "compensate" by adding a couple equal to that shift.

**Special Case — 2-D Force Shift:**
If a force $F$ is shifted parallel to its original line of action by a perpendicular distance $d$, the equivalent system at the new line of action is:
- Force $F$ (same magnitude and direction)
- A couple of magnitude $M = F \cdot d$, with the **same sense** as the original moment of $F$ about the new point of application.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility —** This is the basis of moving loads on beams, locating equivalent point loads on distributed loads, and simplifying complex 2-D/3-D force systems in structural analysis.

## 2.5 Resolution of a Couple into Two Forces (Inverse of Above)

A pure couple of moment $C$ can be represented as **any two equal, opposite, parallel forces** whose product of magnitude and perpendicular separation equals $C$. This is used to **transfer** a moment into a system of forces compatible with other force diagrams (e.g., screw-jack analysis, gear-train analysis).

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Vector Form | Units | When to Use |
|---|---------|---------|-------------|-------|-------------|
| 1 | Moment of force about point $O$ | $M_O = F \cdot d$ | $\vec{M_O} = \vec{r} \times \vec{F}$ | **N·m** | Single force, 2-D |
| 2 | Moment using angle | $M_O = F \cdot r \cdot \sin\theta$ | $\vec{r} \times \vec{F}$ | **N·m** | When angle $\theta$ is given |
| 3 | Varignon's Theorem | $M_O(\vec{R}) = \sum M_O(\vec{F_i})$ | $\vec{r} \times \vec{R} = \sum \vec{r} \times \vec{F_i}$ | **N·m** | Concurrent force system |
| 4 | Moment of a couple | $C = F \cdot a$ | $\vec{C} = \vec{r} \times \vec{F}$ | **N·m** | Two equal, opposite forces |
| 5 | Couple = Force × arm | $\vert C \vert = F \cdot a$ | — | **N·m** | Compute from geometry |
| 6 | Force → Force + Couple (shift) | $C = F \cdot d$ | $\vec{C} = \vec{r}_{A/B} \times \vec{F}$ | **N·m** | Move force from $A$ to $B$ |
| 7 | Couple → Two forces | $F = C / a$ | — | **N** | Split a couple for analysis |
| 8 | Sense of moment (RHR) | CCW $\oplus$, CW $\ominus$ | Right-Hand Rule | — | Sign convention in 2-D |

> [!TIP]
> **Always remember:** the *distance* in $F \cdot d$ is **always the perpendicular distance** from the moment centre to the *line of action* of the force — never the slant distance.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation — Moment of a Force using Position Vector

**Given:** Force $\vec{F}$ acting at point $A$ with position vector $\vec{r}$ from moment centre $O$.

**Setup (vector diagram):**
- $\vec{r}$ = position vector of $A$ from $O$ (magnitude = $OA = r$)
- $\vec{F}$ = force vector at $A$
- Angle between $\vec{r}$ and $\vec{F}$ is $\theta$.

**Derivation:**

By the definition of the cross product in 2-D (or 3-D with the $z$-component rule):

$$
\vec{M_O} = \vec{r} \times \vec{F}
$$

Magnitude:

$$
\vert \vec{M_O} \vert = \vert \vec{r} \vert \cdot \vert \vec{F} \vert \cdot \sin\theta = r \cdot F \cdot \sin\theta
$$

Now consider the geometry: if we drop a perpendicular from $O$ to the line of action of $\vec{F}$, the foot is point $P$. The triangle $OAP$ is right-angled at $P$. So:

$$
\sin\theta = \frac{OP}{OA} = \frac{d}{r}
$$

Substitute:

$$
M_O = r \cdot F \cdot \left( \frac{d}{r} \right) = F \cdot d
$$

Hence the **moment magnitude = Force $\times$ Perpendicular distance**, and the **direction** is given by the right-hand rule (or $\pm$ sign convention in 2-D). $\blacksquare$

## 3.2 Derivation — Varignon's Theorem (Principle of Moments)

**Given:** A system of concurrent coplanar forces $\vec{F_1}, \vec{F_2}, \ldots, \vec{F_n}$ all meeting at point $A$. Their resultant is $\vec{R}$. We need to show that $M_O(\vec{R}) = \sum M_O(\vec{F_i})$ for any point $O$ in the plane.

**Step 1 — Define the resultant:**
By the parallelogram law (or principle of force composition):

$$
\vec{R} = \vec{F_1} + \vec{F_2} + \ldots + \vec{F_n} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \vec{F_i}
$$

**Step 2 — Take moment of both sides about $O$ (using cross product):**

$$
\vec{r} \times \vec{R} = \vec{r} \times \left( \vec{F_1} + \vec{F_2} + \ldots + \vec{F_n} \right)
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the distributive property of cross product:**

$$
\vec{r} \times \vec{R} = \vec{r} \times \vec{F_1} + \vec{r} \times \vec{F_2} + \ldots + \vec{r} \times \vec{F_n}
$$

**Step 4 — Identify moments:**
- Left-hand side: $M_O(\vec{R})$
- Right-hand side: $M_O(\vec{F_1}) + M_O(\vec{F_2}) + \ldots + M_O(\vec{F_n})$

**Step 5 — Conclude:**

$$
\boxed{M_O(\vec{R}) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} M_O(\vec{F_i})}
$$

Hence, the moment of the resultant about $O$ equals the algebraic sum of the moments of the individual forces about $O$. $\blacksquare$

## 3.3 Derivation — Couple's Moment is Independent of Reference Point

**Given:** Couple formed by $\vec{F}$ at $A$ and $-\vec{F}$ at $B$. Let $O$ be an arbitrary point.

**Step 1 — Write the moment of each force about $O$:**

- Moment of $\vec{F}$ at $A$: $\vec{M_1} = \vec{r_A} \times \vec{F}$
- Moment of $-\vec{F}$ at $B$: $\vec{M_2} = \vec{r_B} \times (-\vec{F}) = -\vec{r_B} \times \vec{F}$

**Step 2 — Total moment about $O$:**

$$
\vec{M_O} = \vec{M_1} + \vec{M_2} = \vec{r_A} \times \vec{F} - \vec{r_B} \times \vec{F}
$$

**Step 3 — Factor out $\vec{F}$:**

$$
\vec{M_O} = (\vec{r_A} - \vec{r_B}) \times \vec{F}
$$

**Step 4 — Recognise the difference vector:**
The vector $\vec{r_A} - \vec{r_B}$ is the position vector of $A$ *with respect to* $B$. Call it $\vec{r}$, which is the perpendicular arm of the couple (in magnitude, equal to the perpendicular distance between the two lines of action).

$$
\vec{M_O} = \vec{r} \times \vec{F}
$$

**Step 5 — Interpret:**
- The expression contains **no $\vec{r_A}$ or $\vec{r_B}$** separately, hence **no dependence on the choice of $O$**.
- The result is **always** $\vec{r} \times \vec{F}$ — a *free vector* — no matter where we measure it from.

**Step 6 — Magnitude and sense:**
- Magnitude: $\vert C \vert = F \cdot a$ (where $a$ = arm of couple = perpendicular distance between the two forces).
- Sense: given by the right-hand rule (CCW = positive).

Hence, the **moment of a couple is a free vector** and is the same about any point. $\blacksquare$

## 3.4 Derivation — Resolution of a Force into a Force + Couple

**Given:** A force $\vec{F}$ acting at point $A$. We want to move it to a parallel line of action passing through point $B$, where $B$ is at perpendicular distance $d$ from the original line of action.

**Step 1 — Original moment of $\vec{F}$ about $B$:**
Let $\vec{r}_{A/B}$ be the position vector from $B$ to $A$. The moment of $\vec{F}$ (at $A$) about $B$ is:

$$
\vec{M_B} = \vec{r}_{A/B} \times \vec{F}
$$

Magnitude: $M_B = F \cdot d$ (where $d = \vert \vec{r}_{A/B} \vert \sin\theta$ = perpendicular distance).

**Step 2 — Original system at $B$ before shift:**
A free body at $B$ experiences the original force $\vec{F}$ at $A$, giving moment $M_B = F \cdot d$ about $B$ (sense: CCW if $\vec{F}$ tends to rotate CCW about $B$).

**Step 3 — Equivalent system at $B$ after shift:**
Replace $\vec{F}$ (at $A$) with $\vec{F}$ (at $B$) plus a couple $\vec{C}$.

- The translated force $\vec{F}$ at $B$ creates **zero moment** about $B$ (zero arm).
- To **preserve** the moment of $F \cdot d$ about $B$, we must add a couple $C = F \cdot d$ with the **same sense** as the original moment.

**Step 4 — Conclude:**

$$
\boxed{\text{Force at } A \;\; \equiv \;\; \text{Force at } B \; + \; \text{Couple of magnitude } F \cdot d}
$$

with the couple in the **same rotational sense** as the original moment of $\vec{F}$ about $B$. $\blacksquare$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reverse Operation (Couple → Two Forces):**
> A couple of moment $C$ can be represented by any two equal, opposite, parallel forces $\pm \vec{F}$ separated by a perpendicular distance $a$ such that $F \cdot a = C$. This representation is **not unique** — many $(F, a)$ pairs are valid.

## 3.5 Numerical Worked Examples (KTU Pattern)

### Example 1 — Single Force, Find Moment

A force of $200$ N is applied at a point on a rigid body. The line of action of the force passes at a perpendicular distance of $0.75$ m from point $O$. Determine the moment of the force about $O$ and state its sense.

**Solution:**

$$
M_O = F \cdot d = 200 \times 0.75 = 150 \; \text{N·m}
$$

By the given figure, the rotation is **counter-clockwise**, so $M_O = +150$ N·m (CCW).

### Example 2 — Varignon's Theorem Application

Two forces act at a point: $F_1 = 100$ N along $+x$ axis, and $F_2 = 80$ N making $60°$ with $+x$ axis. Find the moment of the resultant about a point $O$ located $0.5$ m above the point of application.

**Solution:**

Position vector $\vec{r} = 0.5 \hat{j}$ m. Force $\vec{F_1} = 100 \hat{i}$ N. Force $\vec{F_2} = 80 \cos 60° \hat{i} + 80 \sin 60° \hat{j} = 40 \hat{i} + 69.28 \hat{j}$ N.

**Moment of $F_1$ about $O$:**

$$
M_O(F_1) = \vec{r} \times \vec{F_1} = (0.5 \hat{j}) \times (100 \hat{i}) = 0.5 \cdot 100 \cdot (\hat{j} \times \hat{i}) = 50 \cdot (-\hat{k}) = -50 \hat{k} \;\text{N·m}
$$

**Moment of $F_2$ about $O$:**

$$
M_O(F_2) = (0.5 \hat{j}) \times (40 \hat{i} + 69.28 \hat{j}) = 0.5 \cdot 40 \cdot (\hat{j} \times \hat{i}) + 0 = -20 \hat{k} \;\text{N·m}
$$

**Resultant moment (by Varignon's Theorem):**

$$
M_O(\vec{R}) = -50 + (-20) = -70 \;\text{N·m} \;\;(\text{i.e., 70 N·m CW})
$$

**Verification by computing $\vec{R}$ first and then its moment:**

$$
\vec{R} = (100 + 40)\hat{i} + 69.28 \hat{j} = 140 \hat{i} + 69.28 \hat{j} \;\text{N}
$$

$$
M_O(\vec{R}) = (0.5 \hat{j}) \times (140 \hat{i} + 69.28 \hat{j}) = 0.5 \cdot 140 \cdot (-\hat{k}) + 0 = -70 \hat{k} \;\text{N·m} \;\;\checkmark
$$

### Example 3 — Resolution of Force into Force + Couple

A horizontal force of $500$ N acts along a line $0.3$ m above the base of a column. Find the equivalent force-couple system at the base.

**Solution:**

- **Force:** $F = 500$ N (horizontal, same direction).
- **Couple:** $C = F \cdot d = 500 \times 0.3 = 150$ N·m.
- **Sense:** The force tries to rotate the column about the base — depends on direction. If force points to the right, it tries to rotate the column **CCW** about the base → couple is **+150 N·m (CCW)**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Concept Map — Force → Moment → Couple (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Force System] --> B{Acting at a point?}
    A --> C{Two equal, opposite, parallel forces?}
    B -->|Yes, single force| D[Moment about reference point]
    D --> D1[M = r x F or F x d]
    D --> D2[Sign: CCW positive, CW negative]
    B -->|Yes, multiple concurrent| E[Varignon Theorem Applicable]
    E --> E1[Compute R = sum of Fi]
    E --> E2[Mo of R = sum of Mo of Fi]
    C -->|Yes| F[Couple Formed]
    F --> F1[Net force = 0]
    F --> F2[Moment = F x arm = constant]
    F --> F3[Free vector, can be moved in plane]
    D --> G[Shift force to new line of action]
    G --> G1[Add compensating couple]
    G1 --> G2[Couple magnitude = F x perpendicular shift]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Force–Couple Equivalence Operations

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    OP1[Original Force at Point A] --> PROC1{Shift operation}
    PROC1 -->|Translate to B| OP2[Force at Point B]
    OP1 -->|Moment about B| OP3[Couple of value F x d]
    OP2 -->|Plus| OP3
    OP2 --> COMB[Equivalent System at B]
    OP3 --> COMB
    COMB --> VAL[Same External Effect on Rigid Body]
    OP1 --> VAL
    OP2 -.->|Can be replaced by| COUP[Pure Couple]
    OP3 -.->|No residual force| COUP
    COUP --> VAL2[Translates anywhere in plane without effect change]
```

## 4.3 Decision Flow — Choosing the Right Tool in a Problem

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Given: Forces and Geometry] --> Q1{Force through moment centre?}
    Q1 -->|Yes| A1[Moment = 0]
    Q1 -->|No| A2[Compute perpendicular distance d]
    A2 --> A3[M = F x d]
    A2 --> A4[M = r x F sin theta]
    START --> Q2{Equal opposite parallel forces?}
    Q2 -->|Yes| B1[Identify as Couple]
    B1 --> B2[Moment = F x a, independent of point]
    START --> Q3{Multiple concurrent forces?}
    Q3 -->|Yes| C1[Apply Varignon Theorem]
    C1 --> C2[Sum of moments = moment of resultant]
    Q3 -->|No| C3[Compute each moment separately and add]
    START --> Q4{Force to be moved to new point?}
    Q4 -->|Yes| D1[Equivalent = Force at new point + couple F x d]
```

## 4.4 Schematic — Couple as a Free Vector

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    N1[Force F upward at point A] --> N2[Force F downward at point B]
    N2 --> N3[Perpendicular distance between forces = arm a]
    N3 --> N4[Moment of couple C = F x a]
    N4 --> N5[Vector C is perpendicular to plane of forces]
    N5 --> N6[Couple can be transported to ANY point in plane]
    N6 --> N7[External effect on rigid body remains IDENTICAL]
    N1 -.->|No net force| N7
    N2 -.->|No net force| N7
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Mermaid Block Diagrams?** The topics *Moment of a Force* and *Couple* are inherently geometric. Because Mermaid cannot render free-body sketches with arrows in 3-D, the block diagrams above map the **logical operations** (shift, combine, transport) that govern the physics. These flows match the KTU 2024 Scheme evaluation rubric for stepwise problem solving.
