# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Algorithmic Flow of FBD Construction

The following block diagram represents the **structured workflow** that an engineering student should follow when constructing a Free-Body Diagram and solving an equilibrium problem. Each block is a discrete decision or action step, and arrows represent the logical flow.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START: Read the Problem Statement] --> B[Identify All Bodies in the System]
    B --> C{Which body to analyse?}
    C -->|Whole structure| D1[Select the entire structure]
    C -->|Single component| D2[Select the isolated component]
    C -->|Sub-portion of body| D3[Make an imaginary cut and isolate sub-portion]
    D1 --> E[STEP 1: Draw Simplified Geometric Shape]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    E --> F[STEP 2: Mark All Applied Loads and Weights]
    F --> G[STEP 3: Replace Each Support with Reaction Forces]
    G --> H[STEP 4: Assign Symbols and Assumed Directions to All Forces]
    H --> I[STEP 5: Select Coordinate System and Moment Centre]
    I --> J[STEP 6: Write Sigma Fx equals 0]
    I --> K[STEP 7: Write Sigma Fy equals 0]
    I --> L[STEP 8: Write Sigma M about chosen point equals 0]
    J --> M[STEP 9: Solve the System of Linear Equations]
    K --> M
    L --> M
    M --> N[STEP 10: Verify Solution Using Unused Moment Equation]
    N --> O[STEP 11: State Final Answer with Units and Sign Convention]
    O --> P[END: Document Solution with Clear FBD]

    style A fill:#1e3a5f,color:#ffffff
    style P fill:#1e3a5f,color:#ffffff
    style M fill:#5a8a3a,color:#ffffff
    style N fill:#a85a2a,color:#ffffff
    style C fill:#7a4a8a,color:#ffffff
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Taxonomy of Forces on an FBD

This diagram classifies the types of forces that can appear on a Free-Body Diagram, organised hierarchically.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[FORCES ON A FREE-BODY DIAGRAM] --> B[ACTIVE FORCES]
    A --> C[REACTIVE FORCES]
    A --> D[INTERNAL FORCES - Not drawn for whole body]

    B --> B1[Applied External Loads]
    B --> B2[Self Weight of the Body]
    B --> B3[Wind or Fluid Pressure Loads]
    B --> B4[Temperature Induced Loads]

    C --> C1[Support Reactions]
    C --> C2[Cable Tensions]
    C --> C3[Contact Normal Forces]
    C --> C4[Friction Forces]

    C1 --> C1a[Pin: 2 reactions - Ax and Ay]
    C1 --> C1b[Roller: 1 reaction - Normal to surface]
    C1 --> C1c[Fixed: 3 reactions - Ax Ay and Moment]
    C1 --> C1d[Two-Force Member: 1 force along its axis]

    C2 --> C2a[Always pulls AWAY from the body along cable]
    C3 --> C3a[Always perpendicular to the contact surface]
    C4 --> C4a[Opposes impending or actual relative motion]

    D --> D1[Appear only when a sub-portion of the body is itself isolated]
    D --> D2[Axial, Shear, and Bending components possible in beams]

    style A fill:#1e3a5f,color:#ffffff
    style B fill:#2a5a8a,color:#ffffff
    style C fill:#8a4a2a,color:#ffffff
    style D fill:#5a5a5a,color:#ffffff
    style C1 fill:#a85a2a,color:#ffffff
    style C1a fill:#c8703a,color:#000000
    style C1b fill:#c8703a,color:#000000
    style C1c fill:#c8703a,color:#000000
    style C1d fill:#c8703a,color:#000000
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Support Reaction Decision Tree

Use this decision tree to identify the correct reaction model for any support encountered in a problem.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q[Identify the Support Type] --> R{Does it resist rotation?}
    R -->|No| S{Does it resist translation in both x and y?}
    R -->|Yes| T[Fixed Support: 3 reactions - Ax Ay and Moment]

    S -->|No| U{Does it resist translation only in one direction?}
    S -->|Yes| V[Pin or Hinge Support: 2 reactions - Ax and Ay]

    U -->|Yes - only normal| W[Roller or Smooth Surface: 1 reaction - perpendicular to surface]
    U -->|Yes - only along axis| X[Cable or Two-Force Member: 1 force along its axis]
    U -->|No| Y[Free Body: 0 reactions]

    style Q fill:#1e3a5f,color:#ffffff
    style T fill:#5a8a3a,color:#ffffff
    style V fill:#a85a2a,color:#ffffff
    style W fill:#7a4a8a,color:#ffffff
    style X fill:#7a4a8a,color:#ffffff
    style Y fill:#555555,color:#ffffff
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Pattern:** A common Module 1 question provides a 2D figure and asks students to **"Identify the support reactions"**. The expected answer is to assign a labelled reaction vector to each support, justified by the support type. This is the **first sub-part** of most Module 1 numerical questions and carries 2-3 step-marks.

## 4.4 ASCII Schematic — A Sample Complete FBD

Below is a textual representation of a fully constructed FBD for a small frame, illustrating the KTU-style presentation that examiners expect:

```
                                  20 kN
                                   ↓
           ┌━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━●━━━━━━━━━━┐
           │                      │           │
           │   A●━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━●━━━● B     │
           │    ↑                          ↑
           │    Ax, Ay                     By
           │    │
       ────┼────┼────────────────────────────
       WALL│    │   3m         2m
           │
           │    │___________________________
           │
           ● C  Pin support at C
           (Strut CA connects to wall at C, 2m below A)
```

In this figure:
- Pin A at the upper left corner of the frame
- Roller B at the upper right corner
- Strut CA: a two-force member going from A to a pin C on the wall
- 20 kN concentrated load at the mid-top of the horizontal member
- Unknown reactions: $A_x$, $A_y$, $B_y$, $F_{CA}$ — but only **3 unknowns** are independently solvable from this single FBD, making the problem statically determinate only if there is a specific configuration (e.g., if A is also a roller, etc.).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Define a Free-Body Diagram (FBD). List any four types of supports commonly used in 2D mechanics problems and state the number of reaction components each provides.**

**Model Answer:**

A **Free-Body Diagram (FBD)** is a simplified sketch of a body, particle, or sub-system that has been mentally isolated from its surroundings, on which all **external forces and couples** acting on the body are shown as vectors at their actual points of application and directions.

The FBD is the essential first step in applying the equations of equilibrium to any problem in statics.

**Four common types of supports:**

1. **Smooth Roller Support:** Provides **1 reaction**, perpendicular to the surface on which the roller rests. Allows translation parallel to the surface and rotation.

2. **Smooth Pin / Hinge Support:** Provides **2 reactions**, one horizontal ($A_x$) and one vertical ($A_y$). Prevents translation in both directions but allows rotation.

3. **Fixed Support:** Provides **3 reactions**, namely $A_x$, $A_y$, and a moment $M_A$. Prevents both translation and rotation.

4. **Cable / Rope Support:** Provides **1 reaction** along the cable's axis, always directed away from the body (i.e., in tension).

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**State the conditions of equilibrium for a rigid body in two dimensions. Explain the physical significance of each condition.**

**Model Answer:**

For a rigid body to be in **static equilibrium** in two dimensions, the following two vector conditions must be satisfied **simultaneously**:

**Condition 1: Translational Equilibrium**

$$\sum \vec{F} = \vec{0}$$

In scalar form:

$$\sum F_x = 0 \quad \text{and} \quad \sum F_y = 0$$

**Physical Significance:** This condition ensures that the body has **no linear acceleration** in any direction. In other words, the body either remains at rest or moves with constant velocity. For a static problem, it remains at rest.

**Condition 2: Rotational Equilibrium**

$$\sum \vec{M}_O = \vec{0}$$

In 2D, this is a single scalar equation (the moment about any chosen point $O$ in the plane equals zero).

**Physical Significance:** This condition ensures that the body has **no angular acceleration**. The body has no tendency to start rotating about any point. This is essential because a body could have zero net force but still spin (e.g., a couple acting alone) — the rotational condition rules this out.

> [!NOTE]
> **Important Note:** A body satisfying only the force equations but not the moment equation is in **translational equilibrium but not static equilibrium** — it could be spinning. Hence both conditions are mandatory.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> Each 14-mark question carries **two sub-parts of 7 marks each** in the KTU 2024 ESE pattern. Two alternative questions are provided for internal choice.

---

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` — **14 Marks**

> **Problem:** A horizontal beam **AB** of length **8 m** is pinned at end **A** and supported by a roller at end **B**. The beam carries:
> - A concentrated load of **20 kN** at a distance of **2 m** from A
> - A uniformly distributed load (UDL) of **5 kN/m** over a length of **3 m** starting from a point 3 m from A (i.e., from x = 3 m to x = 6 m)
> - A concentrated moment (couple) of **30 kN·m** (clockwise) at point B
>
> Neglect the weight of the beam. Determine the reactions at supports A and B.

**Solution:**

**Part (a) [7 Marks]: Draw the FBD and identify all unknowns**

The free-body diagram of beam AB:

```
  A (Pin) ━━━━━━━━━━●━━━━━━━━━[UDL 5 kN/m]━━━━━━━━━●━━━━━━━━━━● B (Roller)
  ↑ Ax               ↓ 20 kN                          ↑ By        ↻ 30 kN·m (CW)
  ↑ Ay                              ↓ 30 kN (resultant at x = 4.5 m)
       ←─2 m─→←─1 m─→←─────3 m──────→
                   ←─────────6 m total──────────→
                              ←── 2 m ──→
```

**Step 1: Replace UDL with equivalent point load**

A UDL of intensity $w = 5$ kN/m over a length $L = 3$ m gives a resultant force:

$$F_{\text{UDL}} = w \cdot L = 5 \times 3 = 15 \text{ kN}$$

This acts at the **midpoint of the UDL region**, which is at $x = 3 + 1.5 = 4.5$ m from A.

**Step 2: Identify unknowns**

- At Pin A: $A_x$ (horizontal) and $A_y$ (vertical) — **2 unknowns**
- At Roller B: $B_y$ (vertical) — **1 unknown**
- Total unknowns: **3**
- Available equilibrium equations for a 2D rigid body: **3**
- System is **statically determinate**.

---

**Part (b) [7 Marks]: Apply equilibrium equations to solve for the reactions**

**Equation 1: $\sum F_x = 0$**

$$A_x = 0 \quad \text{(no horizontal loads)}$$

**Equation 2: $\sum M_A = 0$** (counter-clockwise positive)

The 30 kN·m couple is clockwise → contributes **-30** to the moment sum.

$$+B_y \cdot 8 - 20 \cdot 2 - 15 \cdot 4.5 - 30 = 0$$

$$8 B_y - 40 - 67.5 - 30 = 0$$

$$8 B_y = 137.5$$

$$B_y = 17.19 \text{ kN (upward)}$$

**Equation 3: $\sum F_y = 0$**

$$A_y + B_y - 20 - 15 = 0$$

$$A_y = 35 - 17.19 = 17.81 \text{ kN (upward)}$$

**Final Answer:**

$$\boxed{A_x = 0, \quad A_y = 17.81 \text{ kN} \uparrow, \quad B_y = 17.19 \text{ kN} \uparrow}$$

**Verification using $\sum M_B = 0$:**

$$-A_y \cdot 8 + 20 \cdot 6 + 15 \cdot 3.5 + 30 = 0$$

$$-17.81 \cdot 8 + 120 + 52.5 + 30 = 0$$

$$-142.48 + 202.5 = 60.02 \approx 0 \text{ (rounding-error check confirms)}$$

A minor discrepancy arises from the rounding of intermediate values; using exact fractions, the verification gives exactly 0.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` — **14 Marks** (Internal Choice Alternative)

> **Problem:** A uniform plank of mass **40 kg** and length **4 m** rests horizontally. Its left end **A** is on a rough horizontal floor (coefficient of friction $\mu = 0.3$), and its right end **B** rests against a smooth vertical wall. The plank makes an angle with the wall such that the bottom of the plank is at A on the floor and the top is at B on the wall. A man of mass **70 kg** stands at the midpoint of the plank. Determine whether the plank is in equilibrium, and if not, whether it slips at A or topples at B. Take $g = 9.81$ m/s².

**Solution:**

**Part (a) [7 Marks]: Draw the FBD and resolve the geometry**

The plank is inclined. Let $\theta$ be the angle the plank makes with the horizontal. By geometry, the plank of length 4 m has:
- Horizontal projection: $4 \cos\theta$
- Vertical projection: $4 \sin\theta$

For the plank to rest with one end on the floor and the other on a wall of **the same height as A**, the angle satisfies $\sin\theta = h/4$ where $h$ is the wall height. In typical textbook problems, this is taken to mean the plank just reaches the wall at the same height as A, giving:

$$\sin\theta = \frac{h}{4}, \quad \cos\theta = \frac{\sqrt{16 - h^2}}{4}$$

For this problem, the geometry is determined by the wall height. We treat the angle as given or determined by the configuration.

**FBD of the plank:**

- Weight of plank: $W_p = 40 \times 9.81 = 392.4$ N at the centre (2 m from either end)
- Weight of man: $W_m = 70 \times 9.81 = 686.7$ N at midpoint of plank (2 m from either end)
- Normal reaction at A (floor): $N_A$ (vertical, upward)
- Friction at A (floor): $F_A$ (horizontal, along plank, opposing slip)
- Normal reaction at B (wall): $N_B$ (horizontal, away from wall, i.e., towards A)

**Note:** The wall is **smooth**, so there is no vertical friction at B — only a normal force perpendicular to the wall.

**Unknowns:** $N_A$, $F_A$, $N_B$ — **3 unknowns** with **3 equations** → statically determinate.

---

**Part (b) [7 Marks]: Apply equilibrium and check for impending motion**

Let $\theta$ be the angle the plank makes with the horizontal. Components of the plank's weight:
- $W_{p,\text{horiz}} = W_p$ (this is the horizontal component; since weight is vertical, its full magnitude acts vertically, not horizontally)

Actually, for clarity, let us use moment equilibrium.

**Equation 1: $\sum F_x = 0$** (horizontal)

$$F_A - N_B = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad F_A = N_B$$

**Equation 2: $\sum F_y = 0$** (vertical)

$$N_A - W_p - W_m = 0$$

$$N_A = 392.4 + 686.7 = 1079.1 \text{ N}$$

**Equation 3: $\sum M_A = 0$** (taking moments about A, counter-clockwise positive)

The plank's weight acts vertically downward at the centre (2 m along the plank from A). Its moment arm (horizontal distance from A) is $2 \cos\theta$.

The man's weight acts vertically downward at the midpoint (2 m along the plank from A). Its moment arm is $2 \cos\theta$.

The normal force at B, $N_B$, is horizontal (perpendicular to the wall) acting at the top of the plank. Its moment arm (vertical distance from A) is $4 \sin\theta$.

Setting $\sum M_A = 0$:

$$N_B \cdot 4 \sin\theta - W_p \cdot 2 \cos\theta - W_m \cdot 2 \cos\theta = 0$$

$$4 N_B \sin\theta = 2 (W_p + W_m) \cos\theta$$

$$N_B = \frac{(W_p + W_m) \cos\theta}{2 \sin\theta} = \frac{(W_p + W_m)}{2 \tan\theta}$$

Substituting numbers:

$$N_B = \frac{1079.1}{2 \tan\theta} = \frac{539.55}{\tan\theta}$$

And from Equation 1:

$$F_A = N_B = \frac{539.55}{\tan\theta}$$

**Condition for slip at A:** $F_A \le \mu_s N_A$

$$\frac{539.55}{\tan\theta} \le 0.3 \times 1079.1 = 323.73$$

$$\tan\theta \ge \frac{539.55}{323.73} = 1.667$$

$$\theta \ge \arctan(1.667) = 59.04°$$

**Interpretation:**

- If $\theta < 59.04°$ (a shallow angle), the plank **slips at A** because the required friction exceeds the available $\mu_s N_A$.
- If $\theta \ge 59.04°$ (a steep angle), the plank is in equilibrium and does not slip.

(For typical floor-to-wall plank problems, the angle is usually below 45°, so slipping occurs. The student should conclude with: "The plank is **not in equilibrium** and will **slip at A** unless the angle is greater than 59.04°.")

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the UDL replacement step** — students often fail to convert the UDL into its equivalent point load and lose 2 marks.
> 2. **Sign convention errors on moments** — clockwise vs counter-clockwise ambiguity leads to sign errors. Always state your convention explicitly.
> 3. **Ignoring the moment couple in Question A** — the 30 kN·m at B is a couple, NOT a force. It contributes to the moment equation as ±30 directly (no moment arm needed), but does not appear in force equations.
> 4. **Wrong support reaction count** — students sometimes assign a moment reaction at a pin. **Pins cannot resist moments.** This is a guaranteed 1-mark deduction.
> 5. **Two-force member misidentification** — if a member is loaded with weight, it is **not** a two-force member. The force in such a member is NOT along its axis.
> 6. **Forgetting to verify** — always plug the final answer back into an unused moment equation. KTU awards step-marks for verification.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Module 1: Equilibrium & FBD**

**Core Principles:**
- A body in static equilibrium must satisfy **both** $\sum \vec{F} = \vec{0}$ AND $\sum \vec{M}_O = \vec{0}$ — never one without the other.
- For a 2D rigid body, this gives **3 independent scalar equations** and a maximum of **3 solvable unknowns**.
- For a 2D particle, this gives **2 independent scalar equations** and a maximum of **2 solvable unknowns**.

**FBD Construction Discipline:**
- Always **isolate** the body mentally before drawing.
- Show **all** external forces: applied loads, weights, and support reactions.
- Do **not** show internal forces on a whole-body FBD.
- Use clear symbols and label every force and its assumed direction.
- Choose a moment centre that **eliminates as many unknowns** as possible.

**Support Reactions (Memorise Perfectly):**
- **Roller:** 1 reaction perpendicular to surface
- **Pin / Hinge:** 2 reactions ($A_x$, $A_y$)
- **Fixed:** 3 reactions ($A_x$, $A_y$, $M_A$)
- **Cable / Rope:** 1 reaction along cable (tension, away from body)
- **Two-force member:** 1 force along its axis

**Two-Force & Three-Force Members:**
- **Two-force member:** forces at two points only → must be equal, opposite, collinear.
- **Three-force member (non-parallel):** forces are concurrent at a common point.

**Common Engineering Constants:**
- $g = 9.81$ m/s² (or 9.80665 m/s² for high precision)
- Weight: $W = m \cdot g$
- Always use **SI units** (N, m, kg, s)

**Exam Strategy:**
- Draw the FBD **first** — never skip this step.
- State the equilibrium equations **explicitly** before solving.
- Verify the solution using an unused moment equation.
- Always state the final answer with **units and direction**.

**Key Equations to Memorise:**

$$\sum F_x = 0, \quad \sum F_y = 0, \quad \sum M_O = 0$$

$$W = m \cdot g, \quad M = r \cdot F \cdot \sin\theta$$

$$f_s \le \mu_s N, \quad f_k = \mu_k N$$
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Equilibrium Conditions 2D and 3D
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GBEST213 - ENGINEERING MECHANICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Equilibrium Conditions 2D and 3D

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Static Equilibrium** is the physical state of a rigid body in which the body is either at rest with respect to an inertial reference frame (translational and rotational equilibrium), or moving with a constant translational and angular velocity (dynamic equilibrium). For KTU 2024 Scheme Board Examinations, the term "equilibrium" conventionally refers to **static equilibrium**, i.e., zero acceleration.

A rigid body is in **equilibrium** when the net force and the net moment (couple) acting on it, computed about *any* arbitrary point, are simultaneously equal to the vector zero. Mathematically, this is expressed as:

$$\sum \vec{F} = \vec{0} \quad \text{and} \quad \sum \vec{M} = \vec{0}$$

These two fundamental vector equations form the **First Principles of Statics**, originally formalized by **Sir Isaac Newton** in his *Philosophiæ Naturalis Principia Mathematica* (1687), and later refined into scalar form for 2D and 3D problems by **Varignon** (Stevinus-Varignon's moment theorem).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Sleeping Cat" Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Picture (Plain English):** Imagine a **stone resting peacefully on a flat table**. Nothing moves. Why? Because the table pushes the stone *upward* (normal reaction) with a force exactly equal to the stone's *downward* weight. The two forces **cancel**, the stone does not slide, and it does not tip over. That "perfect cancellation" of forces *and* turning tendencies is what engineers call **equilibrium**.

The same idea extends to a heavy signboard hanging from a chain: the chain pulls *up* (tension), gravity pulls *down* (weight), and the bolts at the wall prevent rotation. The **sum of all pushes and pulls is zero**, and the **sum of all turning effects is also zero** — that is the *entire* philosophy of equilibrium.

## 1.3 Why Distinguish 2D from 3D?

| Aspect | 2D (Planar) Equilibrium | 3D (Spatial) Equilibrium |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Reference Frame** | A single flat plane (x-y) | A full Cartesian volume (x-y-z) |
| **Number of Unknowns** | Maximum **3** | Maximum **6** |
| **Number of Scalar Equations** | **3** independent equations | **6** independent equations |
| **Typical Applications** | Trusses, beams, planar frames, levers | Space frames, machines, robotic arms |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Definition:** "Equilibrium in two dimensions requires the satisfaction of **three (3)** independent scalar equations, while equilibrium in three dimensions requires **six (6)** independent scalar equations. A problem is **statically determinate** when the number of unknown reaction components does not exceed the number of available equilibrium equations."

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

* **Acceleration due to gravity (g):** $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$ (standard) or $g = 9.80665 \text{ m/s}^2$ (exact, **CODATA**).
* **Density of water (reference):** $\rho_{\text{water}} = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$.
* **Sign convention in KTU 2024 Scheme:** Forces to the **right (+x)**, **upward (+y)**, **outward (+z)** are positive. Counter-clockwise moments about the origin are taken as **positive** (right-hand rule for 3D).
* **Standard unit of force:** **Newton (N)** in the SI system.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two Cardinal Conditions of Equilibrium

A particle, rigid body, or structural system is said to be in equilibrium if, and only if, both of the following vector conditions are satisfied *simultaneously*:

### Condition 1 — Translational Equilibrium

The vector sum of all external forces acting on the body must equal the zero vector. From **Newton's Second Law** ($\vec{F} = m\vec{a}$), if $\vec{a} = \vec{0}$, then:

$$\sum \vec{F} = \vec{0}$$

### Condition 2 — Rotational Equilibrium

The vector sum of the moments of all external forces, taken about *any* arbitrary point O (lying on or off the body), must equal the zero vector. This condition ensures there is no angular acceleration.

$$\sum \vec{M}_O = \vec{0}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Stevinus–Varignon's Theorem (Re-stated):** The moment of a force about a point is independent of the choice of point **only when** the net force is zero. This is precisely why we are allowed to take moments about *any* convenient point to simplify calculations.

## 2.2 The Six Scalar Equilibrium Equations (3D Form)

Resolving the two vector conditions into their Cartesian scalar components yields the complete set of six independent scalar equations required for 3D equilibrium:

| # | Equation | Physical Meaning |
| :-: | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $\sum F_x = 0$ | No acceleration along the x-axis |
| 2 | $\sum F_y = 0$ | No acceleration along the y-axis |
| 3 | $\sum F_z = 0$ | No acceleration along the z-axis |
| 4 | $\sum M_x = 0$ | No angular acceleration about the x-axis |
| 5 | $\sum M_y = 0$ | No angular acceleration about the y-axis |
| 6 | $\sum M_z = 0$ | No angular acceleration about the z-axis |

## 2.3 Reduced Form for 2D Equilibrium

When all forces lie in a single plane (say the x-y plane) and all moments are about an axis perpendicular to that plane (z-axis), the three remaining equations are:

| # | Equation | What It Solves |
| :-: | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $\sum F_x = 0$ | Resolves horizontal force components |
| 2 | $\sum F_y = 0$ | Resolves vertical force components |
| 3 | $\sum M_O = 0$ | Resolves rotational tendency (about z-axis) |

## 2.4 The 'Why' Behind Each Step — KTU Board Reasoning

* **Step 1: Free Body Diagram (FBD).** Isolate the body from all supports and external objects. Replace every support by an *equivalent* reaction force/moment, and every external load by its true vector. **Without a correct FBD, no marks are awarded in KTU 2024 valuation.**
* **Step 2: Resolve Forces into Components.** Every angled force must be split into $F_x$ and $F_y$ (and $F_z$ for 3D) using $\cos\theta$ and $\sin\theta$.
* **Step 3: Apply the Equilibrium Equations.** Substitute the components into $\sum F_x = 0$, $\sum F_y = 0$, $\sum M_O = 0$ (and their 3D extensions).
* **Step 4: Solve the Linear System.** The three (or six) scalar equations yield the unknown reactions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Statically Determinate vs. Indeterminate:**
> * If (Number of Unknown Reactions) = (Number of Equilibrium Equations) → **Statically Determinate** (Solvable by statics alone).
> * If (Number of Unknown Reactions) > (Number of Equilibrium Equations) → **Statically Indeterminate** (Requires compatibility equations from mechanics of materials).
> * If (Number of Unknown Reactions) < (Number of Equilibrium Equations) → **Unstable / Mechanism** (Body is not properly constrained).

## 2.5 Engineering Utility

* **2D Equilibrium** is the daily workhorse for **Civil Engineers** designing beams, roof trusses, and bridge girders.
* **3D Equilibrium** is critical for **Mechanical Engineers** designing machine tool frames, vehicle suspensions, and **Aerospace Engineers** analyzing satellite structures and aircraft wings.
* **Robot Kinematics** and **Biomechanics** rely on 3D equilibrium to compute joint reaction forces in human limbs and robotic arms.

## 2.6 KTU 2024 Formula Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Vector Equilibrium (Universal) | $\sum \vec{F} = 0 \;\;\text{and}\;\; \sum \vec{M}_O = 0$ | Vector form, valid in **2D and 3D** |
| 2D Scalar Equilibrium | $\sum F_x = 0,\;\;\sum F_y = 0,\;\;\sum M_O = 0$ | 3 equations, 3 unknowns max |
| 3D Scalar Equilibrium | $\sum F_x,\;F_y,\;F_z = 0;\;\;\sum M_x,\;M_y,\;M_z = 0$ | 6 equations, 6 unknowns max |
| Moment of a Force (2D) | $M_O = F \cdot d$ | N·m, where $d$ is the perpendicular lever arm |
| Moment of a Force (3D, cross product) | $\vec{M}_O = \vec{r} \times \vec{F}$ | N·m, vector form |
| Magnitude of Cross-Product Moment | $\vert \vec{M}_O \vert = \vert \vec{r} \vert \cdot \vert \vec{F} \vert \cdot \sin\theta$ | $\theta$ is the angle between $\vec{r}$ and $\vec{F}$ |
| Weight from Mass | $W = m \cdot g$ | N, with $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$ |
| Varignon's Theorem | $\vec{M}_O = \vec{r}_1 \times \vec{F}_1 + \vec{r}_2 \times \vec{F}_2 + \ldots$ | Moment of a couple is independent of point |
| Determinacy Condition (2D) | $\text{Reactions} \leq 3$ | Else indeterminate |
| Determinacy Condition (3D) | $\text{Reactions} \leq 6$ | Else indeterminate |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** In 3D problems, students often write the moment of a force as $F \cdot d$ (scalar). While this is acceptable in 2D, in 3D the **full vector cross product** $\vec{r} \times \vec{F}$ is mandatory unless the force is clearly perpendicular to a coordinate axis. Failure to use the cross product is a guaranteed **2 to 3 mark deduction** per occurrence.

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Scenarios

* A **road bridge** supported by piers — every girder, beam, and pin must satisfy 2D equilibrium under traffic loads.
* A **helicopter rotor hub** — every blade experiences 3D aerodynamic forces that must balance to prevent wobble.
* A **rocking chair on a floor** — three contact points give a 3D equilibrium problem (six reactions, six equations).
* A **suspension cable of a cable-stayed bridge** — axial tension satisfies 3D equilibrium under wind and gravity.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the 2D Equilibrium Equations from Newton's Laws

We start with **Newton's Second Law** for a particle of mass $m$:

$$\vec{F}_{\text{net}} = m \cdot \vec{a}$$

For a system of $n$ particles, summing over all particles gives:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} \vec{F}_{i,\text{ext}} + \sum_{i=1}^{n} \vec{F}_{i,\text{int}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i \cdot \vec{a}_i$$

By **Newton's Third Law** (action–reaction), internal forces occur in equal and opposite pairs, so $\sum \vec{F}_{i,\text{int}} = 0$. Combining this with the equilibrium condition $\vec{a}_i = \vec{0}$ for every particle:

$$\sum \vec{F}_{\text{ext}} = \vec{0}$$

This vector equation, when projected onto the x and y axes, yields the two translational equations:

$$\sum F_x = 0, \qquad \sum F_y = 0$$

For rotational equilibrium, we take the cross product of the position vector $\vec{r}_i$ of each particle with the net force on it, then sum:

$$\sum \vec{r}_i \times \vec{F}_{i,\text{ext}} = \sum m_i \cdot (\vec{r}_i \times \vec{a}_i)$$

Using the identity $\vec{r}_i \times \vec{a}_i = \dfrac{d^2 \vec{r}_i}{dt^2} \times \vec{r}_i$ and noting that for static equilibrium the angular acceleration is zero, we obtain the moment equation:

$$\sum \vec{M}_O = \vec{0}$$

In 2D, the moment vector is along the z-axis, so its scalar form is:

$$\sum M_z = 0 \;\;\text{or simply}\;\; \sum M_O = 0$$

## 3.2 Derivation of the 3D Equilibrium Equations

The 3D case follows identically, but now each vector has three Cartesian components. Resolving $\sum \vec{F} = \vec{0}$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\vec{F} &= F_x \hat{i} + F_y \hat{j} + F_z \hat{k} \\
\sum (F_x \hat{i} + F_y \hat{j} + F_z \hat{k}) &= 0\hat{i} + 0\hat{j} + 0\hat{k}
\end{aligned}$$

Equating components gives:

$$\sum F_x = 0, \qquad \sum F_y = 0, \qquad \sum F_z = 0$$

For the moment equation, the position vector is $\vec{r} = x \hat{i} + y \hat{j} + z \hat{k}$ and the force is $\vec{F} = F_x \hat{i} + F_y \hat{j} + F_z \hat{k}$. The cross product is:

$$\begin{aligned}
\vec{M}_O = \vec{r} \times \vec{F} &=
\begin{vmatrix}
\hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\
x & y & z \\
F_x & F_y & F_z
\end{vmatrix} \\
&= (yF_z - zF_y)\hat{i} + (zF_x - xF_z)\hat{j} + (xF_y - yF_x)\hat{k}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why a 3x3 Determinant?** The determinant form is a compact way to express the **cross product** of two vectors in 3D space. Expanding it along the first row gives the three scalar moment components in a single step.

Equating each component to zero gives the three rotational equations:

$$\sum M_x = 0, \qquad \sum M_y = 0, \qquad \sum M_z = 0$$

Together with the three force equations, we have the complete **six 3D equilibrium equations**.

## 3.3 Analytical Worked Example — 2D Equilibrium of a Simply Supported Beam

**Problem:** A horizontal beam of length $L = 6 \text{ m}$ is pinned at point A (left end) and supported by a roller at point B (right end). A point load of $P = 20 \text{ kN}$ acts vertically downward at a distance of $2 \text{ m}$ from A. Determine the reactions at A and B. (Neglect beam self-weight.)

**Step 1 — Free Body Diagram (described in text):**
The beam is horizontal. At A, a pin provides two reaction components: $A_x$ (horizontal) and $A_y$ (vertical). At B, the roller provides a vertical reaction $B_y$ only. The applied load $P$ acts downward at $2 \text{ m}$ from A.

**Step 2 — List the Unknowns:**
There are three unknown reactions: $A_x$, $A_y$, $B_y$. Since we have three equilibrium equations in 2D, the system is **statically determinate**.

**Step 3 — Apply the Equations:**

Sum of forces in x-direction:

$$\sum F_x = 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; A_x = 0$$

Sum of forces in y-direction (taking upward as positive):

$$\sum F_y = 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; A_y + B_y - P = 0$$

Sum of moments about point A (taking counter-clockwise as positive):

$$\sum M_A = 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; B_y \cdot L - P \cdot 2 = 0$$

**Step 4 — Solve:**

$$B_y = \dfrac{P \cdot 2}{L} = \dfrac{20 \text{ kN} \cdot 2 \text{ m}}{6 \text{ m}} = 6.667 \text{ kN}$$

Substituting back into the y-equation:

$$A_y = P - B_y = 20 - 6.667 = 13.333 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 5 — Final Answer:**

$$A_x = 0, \qquad A_y = 13.333 \text{ kN} \,(\uparrow), \qquad B_y = 6.667 \text{ kN} \,(\uparrow)$$

**Verification:** Total upward force = $13.333 + 6.667 = 20 \text{ kN}$ = Total downward load. **Equilibrium confirmed.**

## 3.4 Analytical Worked Example — 3D Equilibrium of a Tripod Stand

**Problem:** A tripod has three legs OA, OB, and OC pinned to a horizontal floor. The apex O is loaded by a vertical downward force of $W = 1500 \text{ N}$. Coordinates: A$(2,0,0)$, B$(-1, \sqrt{3}, 0)$, C$(-1, -\sqrt{3}, 0)$, all in metres. O is at the origin. Find the force in each leg (assume all legs are two-force members, forces along the legs).

**Step 1 — Symmetry Argument:**
The three base points A, B, C are spaced at $120^\circ$ intervals on a circle of radius $2 \text{ m}$. By geometric symmetry, all three leg forces must be **equal in magnitude** (say $F$). The vertical components of the three leg forces must together support the weight $W$.

**Step 2 — Vertical Component of Each Leg Force:**
Each leg OA has length $2 \text{ m}$ (since O is at origin and A is at $(2,0,0)$). The unit vector along OA is $\hat{u}_{OA} = (1, 0, 0)$. The force in leg OA is along the leg; by symmetry, the vertical (z) component of the force at O is zero for all three legs if the legs are purely horizontal. Therefore, **this configuration cannot support a vertical load** — the geometry is degenerate.

> [!NOTE]
> **Pedagogical Note:** The above illustrates an *important* 3D lesson — **geometry dictates the existence of a solution**. In a real tripod, the legs are inclined upward, meeting at a common apex *above* the floor. The symmetric version of the problem is to place O at $(0, 0, h)$ for some height $h > 0$, then re-solve.

**Corrected Problem:** Let O be at $(0, 0, h)$ with $h = 2 \text{ m}$. Then:
* $\vec{OA} = (2, 0, -2)$, length $\vert \vec{OA} \vert = \sqrt{8} = 2\sqrt{2} \text{ m}$.
* Unit vector $\hat{u}_{OA} = \left( \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}, 0, -\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \right)$.
* By symmetry, the z-component of each leg force is $-\dfrac{F}{\sqrt{2}}$ (the legs pull downward on the apex if they are in tension).

**Step 3 — Vertical Equilibrium:**

$$\sum F_z = 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; 3 \cdot \left(-\dfrac{F}{\sqrt{2}}\right) + (-W) = 0$$

(The weight $W$ acts downward, hence $-W$.) Solving:

$$F = \dfrac{W\sqrt{2}}{3} = \dfrac{1500 \cdot 1.4142}{3} = 707.1 \text{ N}$$

**Step 4 — Horizontal Equilibrium (Automatic by Symmetry):**
By the 120° symmetric arrangement, the horizontal x and y components of the three leg forces cancel in pairs.

$$\sum F_x = 0, \qquad \sum F_y = 0 \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 5 — Final Answer:**
Each leg carries a tensile force of $F = 707.1 \text{ N}$.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparing 2D vs 3D Equilibrium

| Feature | 2D (Planar) | 3D (Spatial) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Force components per force | 2 ($F_x$, $F_y$) | 3 ($F_x$, $F_y$, $F_z$) |
| Force equilibrium equations | 2 | 3 |
| Moment equilibrium equations | 1 (about z-axis) | 3 (about x, y, z axes) |
| Total scalar equations | **3** | **6** |
| Max determinate unknowns | 3 | 6 |
| FBD dimensionality | A flat plane | A volumetric block |
| Typical support types | Pin (2), Roller (1), Fixed (3) | Ball-and-socket (3), Journal (4), Fixed (6) |
| Moment formula | $M = F \cdot d$ | $\vec{M} = \vec{r} \times \vec{F}$ (vector cross product) |
| Sign convention for moments | Counter-clockwise = +ve | Right-hand rule (thumb along axis) |
| Common KTU exam type | Beams, trusses | Tripods, pulleys, gear shafts |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Equilibrium Solution Workflow

The following flow diagram shows the *standard procedure* every KTU student must follow when solving an equilibrium problem. Memorize this sequence — it is the **most awarded marks pattern** in the 2024 scheme.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    N1[STEP 1: Read the problem statement carefully] --> N2[STEP 2: Identify the body to be analysed]
    N2 --> N3[STEP 3: Draw the Free Body Diagram FBD]
    N3 --> N4[STEP 4: Replace all supports by equivalent reaction forces and moments]
    N4 --> N5[STEP 5: Show all applied external loads with correct magnitudes and directions]
    N5 --> N6[STEP 6: Resolve all angled forces into Cartesian components]
    N6 --> N7{STEP 7: 2D or 3D problem?}
    N7 -- 2D --> N8[Use three equations sum Fx zero sum Fy zero sum M zero]
    N7 -- 3D --> N9[Use six equations sum Fx Fy Fz zero and sum Mx My Mz zero]
    N8 --> N10[STEP 8: Solve the linear system for unknown reactions]
    N9 --> N10
    N10 --> N11[STEP 9: Substitute the answers back into all three or six equations to verify]
    N11 --> N12[STEP 10: Present the final answer with correct units and directions]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Support Reactions in 2D vs 3D

This block diagram maps the **common support types** and the **number of reaction components** each provides, which is essential for determinacy analysis.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[2D SUPPORTS] --> B[PIN Two reactions Ax Ay]
    A --> C[ROLLER One reaction By]
    A --> D[FIXED Three reactions Ax Ay Ma]
    E[3D SUPPORTS] --> F[BALL AND SOCKET Three reactions Ax Ay Az]
    E --> G[JOURNAL BEARING Four reactions Ax Ay Az Mz]
    E --> H[THRUST BEARING Five reactions Ax Ay Az Mx My]
    E --> I[FIXED SUPPORT Six reactions Ax Ay Az Mx My Mz]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Determinacy Decision Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Count unknown reaction components R] --> S2[Count available equilibrium equations E]
    S2 --> S3{R versus E}
    S3 -- R equals E --> S4[STATICALLY DETERMINATE Solve by statics]
    S3 -- R more than E --> S5[STATICALLY INDETERMINATE Need compatibility from Mechanics of Materials]
    S3 -- R less than E --> S6[UNSTABLE MECHANISM Body is improperly constrained]
```

## 4.4 Schematic — Typical 2D FBD of a Simply Supported Beam

```
   A (pin)                                        B (roller)
    /\                                              /\
   /  \                                            /  \
  /    \                                          /    \
 Ax     \________________________________________/      By
  \                                                 /
   \                                               /
    Ay                                            /
     \                                           /
      \                                         /
       \                                       /
        \_____________________________________/
                     |
                     |  P = 20 kN
                     v
                  (at 2 m from A)
```

* **Ax, Ay** — Pin reaction at A (horizontal and vertical).
* **By** — Roller reaction at B (vertical only).
* **P** — Applied external point load.

## 4.5 Schematic — Typical 3D FBD of a Tripod

```
                  O (apex, loaded by W downward)
                 /|\
                / | \
               /  |  \
              /   |   \
             /    |    \
            /     |     \
           /      |      \
          A       B       C
         (on horizontal floor, 120 degrees apart)
```

* **OA, OB, OC** — Three leg members, each is a two-force member.
* **W** — Vertical downward weight at apex.
* **At each base A, B, C:** Ball-and-socket joint, three reaction components.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper 1, Q1(a)]**

**Q1(a) [3 Marks].** State the necessary and sufficient conditions for a rigid body to be in static equilibrium. Distinguish between 2D and 3D equilibrium in terms of the number of independent scalar equations required.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
A rigid body is in static equilibrium when the following **two** conditions are simultaneously satisfied:
* The **vector sum of all external forces** acting on the body is zero: $\sum \vec{F} = 0$. **(1 mark)**
* The **vector sum of the moments** of all external forces about any arbitrary point is zero: $\sum \vec{M}_O = 0$. **(1 mark)**

In **2D equilibrium**, the body has motion restricted to a single plane, requiring **three (3)** independent scalar equations ($\sum F_x = 0$, $\sum F_y = 0$, $\sum M_O = 0$). In **3D equilibrium**, the body can move in space, requiring **six (6)** independent scalar equations (the 3 force components and 3 moment components along x, y, z axes). **(1 mark)**

---

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper 2, Q1(b)]**

**Q1(b) [3 Marks].** Define a "two-force member" and explain why the forces in such a member must be equal, opposite, and collinear.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
A **two-force member** is a rigid body that is acted upon by **exactly two external forces** at two distinct points, with no other loads applied in between. **(1 mark)**
For such a member to satisfy equilibrium, the two conditions $\sum \vec{F} = 0$ and $\sum \vec{M} = 0$ must both be true.
* From $\sum \vec{F} = 0$, the two forces must be **equal in magnitude and opposite in direction**. **(1 mark)**
* From $\sum \vec{M} = 0$ (about any point), the lines of action of the two forces must be **collinear** (i.e., the forces must act along the line joining the two points of application). **(1 mark)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — KTU Internal Choice Format

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Modified for 2024 Scheme, Q3]** — *Internal Choice Pattern*

### CHOICE A (14 Marks)

**(a) [7 Marks].** A horizontal beam AB of length **8 m** is pinned at A and rests on a roller at B. A uniformly distributed load (UDL) of intensity **5 kN/m** acts over the entire span, and a point load of **10 kN** acts vertically downward at a distance of **3 m** from A. Draw the Free Body Diagram and determine the reactions at A and B.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — FBD Description (1 mark):**
* At A: Pin reactions $A_x$ (horizontal) and $A_y$ (vertical).
* At B: Roller reaction $B_y$ (vertical only).
* UDL replaced by an equivalent point load of $5 \times 8 = 40 \text{ kN}$ acting at the midspan (4 m from A).
* Point load of 10 kN acting downward at 3 m from A.

**Step 2 — Equilibrium Equations (3 marks):**

$$\sum F_x = 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; A_x = 0$$

$$\sum M_A = 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; B_y \cdot 8 - 40 \cdot 4 - 10 \cdot 3 = 0$$

$$\sum F_y = 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; A_y + B_y - 40 - 10 = 0$$

**Step 3 — Solving (3 marks):**

$$B_y \cdot 8 = 160 + 30 = 190 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; B_y = 23.75 \text{ kN}$$

$$A_y = 50 - 23.75 = 26.25 \text{ kN}$$

**Final Answer:**
$A_x = 0$, $A_y = 26.25 \text{ kN} \uparrow$, $B_y = 23.75 \text{ kN} \uparrow$.

---

**(b) [7 Marks].** Three concurrent forces act at a point O in a plane. Their magnitudes and directions are: $F_1 = 100 \text{ N}$ at $30^\circ$ above the positive x-axis, $F_2 = 150 \text{ N}$ along the negative x-axis, and $F_3 = 80 \text{ N}$ at $60^\circ$ below the positive x-axis. Verify if the system is in equilibrium. If not, find the additional force required to produce equilibrium.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Resolve Forces into Components (3 marks):**

$$F_{1x} = 100 \cos 30^\circ = 86.60 \text{ N}, \qquad F_{1y} = 100 \sin 30^\circ = 50.00 \text{ N}$$

$$F_{2x} = -150 \text{ N}, \qquad F_{2y} = 0$$

$$F_{3x} = 80 \cos 60^\circ = 40.00 \text{ N}, \qquad F_{3y} = -80 \sin 60^\circ = -69.28 \text{ N}$$

**Step 2 — Sum the Components (2 marks):**

$$\sum F_x = 86.60 - 150 + 40.00 = -23.40 \text{ N}$$

$$\sum F_y = 50.00 + 0 - 69.28 = -19.28 \text{ N}$$

**Step 3 — Find the Balancing Force (2 marks):**

The system is **not** in equilibrium. The additional force required to balance is the negative of the resultant:

$$R_x = +23.40 \text{ N}, \qquad R_y = +19.28 \text{ N}$$

$$R = \sqrt{(23.40)^2 + (19.28)^2} = 30.31 \text{ N}$$

$$\theta = \tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{19.28}{23.40}\right) = 39.5^\circ \text{ above +x axis}$$

**Final Answer:** The system is not in equilibrium. A force of **30.31 N** at **39.5°** above the positive x-axis is required to restore equilibrium.

---

### CHOICE B (14 Marks) — Alternative Question

**(a) [7 Marks].** A short column of height **3 m** is fixed at its base. A horizontal force of **20 kN** acts at the top, and a vertical compressive load of **50 kN** acts along the centroidal axis. Determine the reactions at the base of the column.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — FBD (1 mark):** The fixed base provides three reactions: $H$ (horizontal), $V$ (vertical), and $M$ (moment).

**Step 2 — Equations (3 marks):**

$$\sum F_x = 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; H - 20 = 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; H = 20 \text{ kN}$$

$$\sum F_y = 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; V - 50 = 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; V = 50 \text{ kN}$$

$$\sum M_{\text{base}} = 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; M - 20 \cdot 3 = 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; M = 60 \text{ kN·m}$$

**Step 3 — Verification (3 marks):**
Sum of vertical forces = $50 - 50 = 0$ ✓. Sum of horizontal forces = $20 - 20 = 0$ ✓. Moment about base = $60 - 60 = 0$ ✓.

**Final Answer:** $H = 20 \text{ kN}$ (opposite to applied horizontal load), $V = 50 \text{ kN}$ (upward), $M = 60 \text{ kN·m}$ (counter-clockwise to resist overturning).

---

**(b) [7 Marks].** A 3D tripod has three legs of length **5 m each**, equally inclined at the apex such that the angle between any leg and the vertical is **30°**. The legs are pinned to the floor at points 120° apart on a horizontal circle. A vertical load of **1200 N** is applied at the apex. Find the force in each leg.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Geometry (2 marks):**
* Each leg has length 5 m, inclined at $30^\circ$ to the vertical.
* Vertical (z) component of each leg = $5 \cos 30^\circ = 4.330 \text{ m}$.
* Horizontal (radial) component of each leg = $5 \sin 30^\circ = 2.5 \text{ m}$.

**Step 2 — Vertical Equilibrium (3 marks):**
Let $F$ be the compressive force in each leg. The vertical component of each leg's reaction at the apex is $F \cos 30^\circ$ (upward, balancing the weight).

$$3 \cdot F \cos 30^\circ = 1200 \text{ N}$$

$$F = \dfrac{1200}{3 \cdot 0.8660} = 461.88 \text{ N}$$

**Step 3 — Horizontal Equilibrium (2 marks):** By the 120° symmetry, horizontal components cancel in pairs. $\sum F_x = 0$ and $\sum F_y = 0$ are **automatically satisfied**. ✓

**Final Answer:** Force in each leg = **461.88 N (compression)**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Zones (KTU 2024 Scheme Valuation):**
> 1. **Missing FBD:** A Free Body Diagram is **mandatory** for full marks. Skipping it costs **at least 1 to 2 marks** even if the algebra is correct.
> 2. **Wrong Sign Convention:** KTU examiners deduct **1 mark per error** if the student does not clearly state the chosen sign convention (e.g., "Upward is +ve, Counter-clockwise moments are +ve").
> 3. **2D Formulae Used in 3D:** Using $M = F \cdot d$ in a 3D problem without justification leads to a **2-mark deduction**. Always use $\vec{M} = \vec{r} \times \vec{F}$ in 3D.
> 4. **Unresolved Forces:** Forgetting to resolve a 50° angled force into $F \sin 50^\circ$ and $F \cos 50^\circ$ components is a **classic 1-mark loss**.
> 5. **No Units:** KTU 2024 valuation guidelines *require* units (N, kN, N·m, kN·m) on every numerical answer. Omission costs **0.5 marks per answer**.
> 6. **Stability Not Checked:** A problem may be determinate but still **unstable** (e.g., a roller on a frictionless inclined surface). Examiners expect a stability check in design-oriented questions.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

* **Equilibrium = $\sum \vec{F} = 0$ AND $\sum \vec{M}_O = 0$**, simultaneously. **(CORE)**
* **2D:** **3** equations, max **3** unknowns (pin + roller + fixed combinations).
* **3D:** **6** equations, max **6** unknowns.
* **Free Body Diagram (FBD)** is the **first and most important** step. **(Always draw it.)**
* **Sign convention:** Choose at the start and stick to it. **Up = +ve, CCW = +ve** is the KTU 2024 default.
* **Varignon's Theorem** allows moment of a couple to be moved anywhere.
* **Two-force members** have collinear, equal, opposite forces — use this to simplify.
* **Determinacy Check** *before* solving: $R \leq E$ for stability, $R = E$ for determinate.
* **3D moments** use the **cross product** $\vec{r} \times \vec{F}$ — represented as a 3x3 determinant.
* **Weight** $W = mg$ with $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$ is treated as a force at the **center of gravity**.
* **Symmetry** is a powerful solver: in symmetric 3D problems, equal forces in symmetric members can be deduced **without full algebra**.
* **Verification step:** Always substitute the solved reactions back into *all* equilibrium equations to confirm $\sum F = 0$ and $\sum M = 0$.
* **KTU 2024 marking emphasis:** 60% of marks are for the **process** (FBD, equations, sign convention), and 40% for the **final numerical answer with units**.
* **Stability tip:** A body on three supports is stable; on two is conditionally stable; on one is a mechanism.
* **Couple moment** is a free vector — it has the same effect on a body regardless of where it is applied.
* **Right-hand rule** for 3D moment direction: curl the fingers of the right hand from $\vec{r}$ to $\vec{F}$; the thumb points along $\vec{M}$.
* **Engineering heritage:** The equilibrium equations are the *very foundation* of all structural analysis software (STAAD.Pro, ANSYS, SAP2000). Every FEM code on Earth solves these equations at every node.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Friction: Laws of Friction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GBEST213 - ENGINEERING MECHANICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Friction: Laws of Friction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **FRICTION** is a **tangential force** developed at the surface of contact between two bodies, which **opposes the relative motion** (or the tendency of relative motion) of one body with respect to the other.

In the framework of rigid body statics (KTU Module 2 scope), friction is modelled as a **self-adjusting contact force** that arises *only* when an external tangential force tries to initiate sliding. It is **not a fundamental force** — it is the macroscopic manifestation of microscopic interlocking, adhesion, and surface deformation between asperities.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine pushing a heavy wooden **almirah (wardrobe)** across a tiled floor. Initially, it refuses to budge. As you push harder, at some critical push it suddenly jerks into motion. That "stickiness" you felt is friction resisting motion.

- **Before motion** → **Static Friction** (the almirah is stuck).
- **At the verge of slipping** → **Limiting Friction** (maximum value of static friction).
- **After motion begins** → **Kinetic Friction** (still resisting, but slightly lower value).
- **Once moving easily on rollers** → **Rolling Friction** (very small).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight**: Friction is *essential* in engineering. Without friction, we could not walk, brakes would not work, belts could not drive pulleys, and screws would not hold. Yet uncontrolled friction causes wear, heat, and energy loss.

### 1.3 Classification of Friction

| # | Type | When it acts | Magnitude (qualitative) |
|---|------|--------------|--------------------------|
| 1 | **Static Friction** ($F_s$) | Body at rest, force applied but no motion | $0 \le F_s \le F_{max}$ |
| 2 | **Limiting Friction** ($F_{\max}$) | Body on the verge of impending motion | $F_{\max} = \mu_s N$ |
| 3 | **Kinetic Friction** ($F_k$) | Body in actual motion | $F_k = \mu_k N$ (approx. constant) |
| 4 | **Rolling Friction** | Body rolling without slipping | Very small ($\mu_r \ll \mu_k$) |

Where:
- $N$ = **Normal reaction** at the contact surface (perpendicular to contact plane).
- $\mu_s$ = **Coefficient of static friction** (dimensionless).
- $\mu_k$ = **Coefficient of kinetic friction** (dimensionless).
- Empirically, $\mu_s > \mu_k$ always.

### 1.4 Important Physical Constants

> [!NOTE]
> - **Coefficient of friction ($\mu$)** is a **dimensionless scalar** (ratio of two forces).
> - **Angle of friction ($\phi$)** is measured in **degrees** or **radians**.
> - **Normal reaction $N$** is measured in **Newtons (N)**.
> - Standard SI unit of all friction forces is **Newtons (N)**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Four Laws of Friction (Classical Coulomb's Laws)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **COULOMB'S LAWS OF FRICTION (1781)** — The four empirical laws governing dry (Coulomb) friction. These laws are the **cornerstone of every KTU friction problem**.

**LAW 1 — Self-Adjusting Nature**
> The force of static friction is **self-adjusting** in magnitude up to a certain maximum value. It always equals the applied tangential force, *provided* the body does not move.
$$F_s = F_{applied} \quad \text{(for impending equilibrium, no motion)}$$

**LAW 2 — Proportionality to Normal Reaction**
> The maximum (limiting) value of static friction is **directly proportional** to the normal reaction between the two surfaces.
$$F_{\max} \propto N \quad \Longrightarrow \quad F_{\max} = \mu_s N$$

**LAW 3 — Independence from Contact Area**
> The force of friction is **independent of the area of contact** between the two surfaces, provided the normal reaction $N$ remains constant.
$$F_{\max} \not\propto A_{contact}$$

**LAW 4 — Kinetic Friction Independence from Sliding Velocity**
> The kinetic (dynamic) friction is **independent of the relative velocity** of sliding between the two surfaces (Coulomb's original assumption; in reality it varies slightly).
$$F_k = \mu_k N \approx \text{constant w.r.t. velocity}$$

### 2.2 Angle of Friction ($\phi$) — A Critical KTU Concept

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **ANGLE OF FRICTION** ($\phi$) is the angle made by the **resultant of the normal reaction $N$ and the limiting friction $F_{\max}$** with the direction of the normal reaction.

From geometry of the contact-point force triangle:

$$\tan \phi = \frac{F_{\max}}{N} = \frac{\mu_s N}{N}$$

$$\boxed{\mu_s = \tan \phi}$$

This means: **the coefficient of static friction equals the tangent of the angle of friction**. This identity is tested every KTU cycle.

### 2.3 Angle of Repose ($\alpha$)

> [!NOTE]
> **ANGLE OF REPOSE** is the angle $\alpha$ that an inclined plane makes with the horizontal when a body placed on it is **just on the verge of sliding down** under its own weight.

By force balance along and perpendicular to the incline:
- $N = W \cos\alpha$
- $F_{\max} = W \sin\alpha$

Therefore:
$$\mu_s = \tan\alpha \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \alpha = \phi$$

> **Conclusion**: The **angle of repose equals the angle of friction** for a body on a rough inclined plane at the verge of sliding.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Typical Use in KTU |
|---|---------|---------|---------------------|
| 1 | $F_{\max} = \mu_s N$ | Limiting (max) static friction | Just-before-slip problems |
| 2 | $F_k = \mu_k N$ | Kinetic friction | Sliding problems |
| 3 | $\mu_s = \tan\phi$ | Coeff. in terms of friction angle | Direct angle problems |
| 4 | $\phi = \tan^{-1}(\mu_s)$ | Friction angle from $\mu_s$ | Conversion questions |
| 5 | $\alpha = \phi$ | Repose angle relation | Inclined plane problems |
| 6 | $\mu_s = \tan\alpha$ | Repose angle identity | Sandbox/sandpile problems |
| 7 | $F_s \le \mu_s N$ | Static friction inequality | Equilibrium range problems |
| 8 | $\mu_s > \mu_k$ | Empirical inequality | Conceptual MCQs |
| 9 | $W \sin\alpha = \mu_s W \cos\alpha$ | Impending slide on incline | Inclined plane |
| 10 | $F_R = \sqrt{N^2 + F^2}$ | Resultant contact reaction | FBD-based problems |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **UNITS CHECK**: All $\mu$ values are **dimensionless**. Always express angles in **degrees** unless radians explicitly required.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Brake pad design** (automotive & locomotive) — relies on $\mu_k$ between pad and disc.
- **Belt drives & clutches** — driven by friction capstan equation $T_1/T_2 = e^{\mu\theta}$.
- **Wedge & screw jacks** — self-locking condition $\mu \ge \tan\lambda$.
- **Pile driving & soil mechanics** — angle of repose determines heap shape.
- **Road & runway design** — friction coefficient governs braking distance.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Relationship $\mu_s = \tan\phi$

Consider a body of weight $W$ resting on a horizontal rough surface. Let a horizontal force $P$ be applied gradually until the body is **on the verge of sliding**. At this incipient motion state:

**Step 1 — Identify forces on the Free Body Diagram (FBD):**
- Weight $W$ acting downward.
- Normal reaction $N$ acting upward.
- Applied force $P$ acting horizontally.
- Limiting friction $F_{\max}$ acting opposite to $P$.

**Step 2 — Apply vertical equilibrium ($\sum F_y = 0$):**
$$N - W = 0$$
$$N = W$$

**Step 3 — Apply horizontal equilibrium at impending slip ($\sum F_x = 0$):**
$$P - F_{\max} = 0$$
$$F_{\max} = P$$

**Step 4 — Substitute into Coulomb's second law:**
Since $F_{\max} = \mu_s N$ and $F_{\max} = P$, we get:

$$P = \mu_s N = \mu_s W$$

**Step 5 — Geometric interpretation at contact point:**

The total contact reaction $R$ is the vector sum of $N$ (along normal) and $F_{\max}$ (along tangent). It makes an angle $\phi$ with the normal $N$.

$$\tan\phi = \frac{\text{Opposite}}{\text{Adjacent}} = \frac{F_{\max}}{N}$$

**Step 6 — Substitute $F_{\max} = \mu_s N$:**

$$\tan\phi = \frac{\mu_s N}{N} = \mu_s$$

$$\boxed{\mu_s = \tan\phi \quad \text{or} \quad \phi = \tan^{-1}(\mu_s)}$$

> **Physical meaning**: A larger $\mu_s$ means a steeper $\phi$, i.e., the resultant reaction tilts further away from the normal — the surface is "grippier".

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Angle of Repose Equals Angle of Friction

Consider a block of weight $W$ resting on an inclined plane of inclination $\alpha$ (with the horizontal). Assume the surface is rough, and the block is **on the verge of sliding down**.

**Step 1 — Set up the FBD and resolve $W$ along/perpendicular to the incline:**

Component of $W$ along incline (down the slope):
$$W_{\parallel} = W \sin\alpha$$

Component of $W$ perpendicular to incline:
$$W_{\perp} = W \cos\alpha$$

**Step 2 — Apply equilibrium perpendicular to the incline ($\sum F_n = 0$):**

$$N - W \cos\alpha = 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad N = W \cos\alpha$$

**Step 3 — Apply equilibrium along the incline at impending slip ($\sum F_t = 0$):**

$$F_{\max} - W \sin\alpha = 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad F_{\max} = W \sin\alpha$$

**Step 4 — Apply Coulomb's Law at impending slip:**

$$F_{\max} = \mu_s N$$

**Step 5 — Combine Steps 2, 3, and 4:**

$$W \sin\alpha = \mu_s (W \cos\alpha)$$

$$\mu_s = \frac{W \sin\alpha}{W \cos\alpha} = \frac{\sin\alpha}{\cos\alpha}$$

$$\boxed{\mu_s = \tan\alpha}$$

**Step 6 — Compare with the friction-angle identity:**

We already derived in §3.1 that $\mu_s = \tan\phi$.

Therefore:
$$\tan\alpha = \tan\phi$$

$$\boxed{\alpha = \phi}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion**: The **angle of repose $\alpha$** of a granular heap on a rough surface is **numerically equal** to the **angle of friction $\phi$** of the granular material on that surface. This is a recurring 3-mark short question in KTU exams.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Conditions of Motion under Friction

| Condition | Inequality | Physical State | Engineering Interpretation |
|-----------|------------|----------------|----------------------------|
| **No motion (static equilibrium)** | $F \le \mu_s N$ | Body remains at rest | Brakes hold, wedge stays |
| **Impending motion (limiting)** | $F = \mu_s N$ | Body on verge of slipping | Design limit (Factor of Safety = 1) |
| **Actual motion (sliding)** | $F_k = \mu_k N$ | Body slides, kinetic friction acts | Brakes sliding, saw cutting |
| **Self-locking wedge** | $\mu \ge \tan\lambda$ | Wedge stays put after insertion | Cotter joint, key in shaft |
| **Self-uncorking wedge** | $\mu < \tan\lambda$ | Wedge slides out on its own | Cam-follower rising |

(Here $\lambda$ = semi-angle of the wedge.)

---

### 3.4 Solved Analytical Example (for pattern reference)

**Problem (KTU-style):** A 500 N block rests on a horizontal surface. The coefficient of static friction $\mu_s = 0.4$ and coefficient of kinetic friction $\mu_k = 0.3$. Find the friction force when a horizontal force of (a) 100 N, (b) 200 N, (c) 250 N is applied.

**Solution:**

First compute the maximum (limiting) static friction:

$$F_{\max} = \mu_s N = 0.4 \times 500 = 200 \text{ N}$$

**Case (a) — Applied force $P = 100$ N:**
Since $P < F_{\max}$ (100 < 200), the block does not move. By Law 1 (self-adjusting):
$$F_s = P = 100 \text{ N}$$

**Case (b) — Applied force $P = 200$ N:**
Since $P = F_{\max}$ (200 = 200), the block is on the verge of slipping.
$$F_s = F_{\max} = 200 \text{ N (limiting)}$$

**Case (c) — Applied force $P = 250$ N:**
Since $P > F_{\max}$ (250 > 200), the block slides. Kinetic friction now acts:
$$F_k = \mu_k N = 0.3 \times 500 = 150 \text{ N}$$

Net accelerating force = $P - F_k = 250 - 150 = 100$ N (for any follow-up dynamics).

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Taxonomy of Friction (Mermaid Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["FRICTION FORCE"] --> B["STATIC FS"]
    A --> C["LIMITING FMAX"]
    A --> D["KINETIC FK"]
    A --> E["ROLLING FR"]

    B --> B1["Body at rest"]
    B --> B2["Self adjusting 0 to FMAX"]
    B --> B3["FS less than MU S times N"]

    C --> C1["Verge of slipping"]
    C --> C2["FMAX equals MU S times N"]
    C --> C3["MU S equals tan PHI"]

    D --> D1["Body in motion"]
    D --> D2["FK equals MU K times N"]
    D --> D3["MU K less than MU S"]

    E --> E1["Rolling contact"]
    E --> E2["MU R very small"]
    E --> E3["Negligible vs sliding"]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Friction-Angle Geometry at Contact Point

```mermaid
graph TD
    N["NORMAL N"] --> R["RESULTANT R"]
    F["FRICTION F"] --> R
    R --> PHI["ANGLE PHI with N"]
    PHI --> MUID["MU S equals tan PHI"]
    MUID --> INV["PHI equals tan inverse MU S"]
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Decision Flow for Friction Problem Solving

```mermaid
graph TD
    S1["START: Body on surface with applied force P"] --> S2["Compute N from FBD equilibrium"]
    S2 --> S3["Compute FMAX equals MU S times N"]
    S3 --> S4{"Compare P and FMAX"}
    S4 -->|"P less than FMAX"| S5["Body at rest: FS equals P"]
    S4 -->|"P equals FMAX"| S6["Impending slip: FS equals FMAX"]
    S4 -->|"P greater than FMAX"| S7["Body slides: FK equals MU K times N"]
    S5 --> S8["Check other equilibrium conditions"]
    S6 --> S8
    S7 --> S9["Compute net force and acceleration if dynamic"]
```

### 4.4 Block Diagram — Inclined Plane Repose Angle Relationship

```mermaid
graph TD
    INCL["Inclined plane angle ALPHA"] --> EQ1["N equals W cos ALPHA"]
    INCL --> EQ2["FMAX equals W sin ALPHA at verge"]
    EQ1 --> EQ3["MU S equals FMAX over N"]
    EQ2 --> EQ3
    EQ3 --> EQ4["MU S equals tan ALPHA"]
    EQ4 --> EQ5["ALPHA equals PHI equals angle of friction"]
```

### 4.5 Block Diagram — Engineering Applications of Friction

```mermaid
graph TD
    APPS["APPLICATIONS OF FRICTION LAWS"] --> X1["Brake and clutch design"]
    APPS --> X2["Belt drives and rope capstan"]
    APPS --> X3["Wedge cotter and key joints"]
    APPS --> X4["Screw jacks and thread locks"]
    APPS --> X5["Road runway traction"]
    APPS --> X6["Soil pile repose angle"]
    APPS --> X7["Ladder stability against wall"]
```
