<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**State and explain the laws of friction.**

**Model Answer (rigid 3-mark structure):**

The laws of dry friction (Coulomb, 1781) are:

1. **Law of self-adjustment:** The force of static friction is self-adjusting in magnitude up to a maximum limiting value, equal to the applied tangential force so long as the body remains in equilibrium. ($F_s = F_{applied}$ for $0 \le F_s \le F_{\max}$.)

2. **Law of proportionality:** The maximum (limiting) static friction is directly proportional to the normal reaction between the surfaces: $F_{\max} = \mu_s N$.

3. **Law of independence from contact area:** For a given normal reaction, the limiting friction is independent of the apparent area of contact.

4. **Law of independence from sliding velocity:** The kinetic friction is independent of the relative velocity of sliding (Coulomb's assumption).

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's tip**: Naming all **four** laws and writing one formula each fetches full 3 marks. Skipping Law 1 (self-adjustment) costs a mark.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define angle of friction and angle of repose. Show that they are equal.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Angle of friction ($\phi$):** The angle made by the resultant of the normal reaction and the limiting friction force with the direction of the normal reaction.
$$\tan\phi = \frac{F_{\max}}{N} = \frac{\mu_s N}{N} = \mu_s \quad \Rightarrow \quad \boxed{\mu_s = \tan\phi}$$

- **Angle of repose ($\alpha$):** The angle of inclination of an inclined plane with the horizontal at which a body placed on it is just on the verge of sliding down under its own weight.

For a body on a rough inclined plane at impending slip:
$$F_{\max} = W \sin\alpha, \qquad N = W \cos\alpha$$
$$\mu_s = \frac{F_{\max}}{N} = \frac{W \sin\alpha}{W \cos\alpha} = \tan\alpha$$

Since $\mu_s = \tan\phi$ and $\mu_s = \tan\alpha$:
$$\tan\phi = \tan\alpha \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \boxed{\phi = \alpha}$$

Hence the angle of friction equals the angle of repose.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks): Friction Equilibrium on an Inclined Plane**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 2]**

**(a)** A block of weight **500 N** rests on a rough inclined plane making an angle of **30°** with the horizontal. The coefficient of static friction between the block and the plane is **0.35**.

   (i) Determine whether the block will remain at rest or slide down.
   (ii) Find the magnitude and direction of the friction force on the block.
   (iii) What is the smallest angle at which the block will begin to slide? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A block of weight **800 N** is on the verge of moving up an inclined plane (angle **20°**) under a force **P** applied parallel to the plane. If $\mu_s = 0.45$, find the magnitude of $P$. What additional force $Q$ applied at 15° above the plane reduces $P$? **(7 marks)**

---

### **Solution to Question A:**

**(a) (i) Check whether block slides down at $\alpha = 30°$:**

Normal reaction:
$$N = W \cos\alpha = 500 \cos 30° = 500 \times 0.8660 = 433.0 \text{ N}$$

Limiting (maximum) friction available:
$$F_{\max} = \mu_s N = 0.35 \times 433.0 = 151.6 \text{ N}$$

Force tending to slide the block down the plane:
$$F_{slide} = W \sin\alpha = 500 \sin 30° = 500 \times 0.5 = 250 \text{ N}$$

**Comparison:**
$$F_{slide} = 250 \text{ N} \quad > \quad F_{\max} = 151.6 \text{ N}$$

Since the driving force (250 N) exceeds the maximum available friction (151.6 N), **the block will slide DOWN** the incline.

**(a) (ii) Direction and magnitude of actual friction:**

Since the block is sliding (kinetic condition), the friction opposes motion — i.e., acts **up the plane**.

If we use $\mu_k = \mu_s = 0.35$ (assumed, otherwise specify in problem):
$$F_k = \mu_k N = 0.35 \times 433.0 = 151.6 \text{ N (up the plane)}$$

(If the problem had specified the block was still at rest but on verge, we would say $F_s = F_{\max} = 151.6$ N, and since $F_{slide} > F_{\max}$, body slides.)

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall**: Students often say "$F = 151.6$ N up the plane, hence block is in equilibrium" — WRONG. If the required friction ($F_{slide} = 250$ N) exceeds available friction ($F_{\max} = 151.6$ N), equilibrium is **not possible**, and the body **slides**.

**(a) (iii) Smallest angle for impending slide:**

At impending slide (any angle $\alpha^*$):
$$W \sin\alpha^* = \mu_s W \cos\alpha^*$$
$$\tan\alpha^* = \mu_s = 0.35$$
$$\alpha^* = \tan^{-1}(0.35) = 19.29° \approx 19.3°$$

This is the **angle of repose** for the given $\mu_s$.

---

**(b) Find $P$ when block is on the verge of moving UP:**

When the body is about to move **up**, friction acts **down** the plane (opposing impending motion).

Force balance along incline (impending up-motion):
$$P = W \sin\alpha + \mu_s N$$
$$P = W \sin\alpha + \mu_s W \cos\alpha$$

$$P = 800 \sin 20° + 0.45 \times 800 \cos 20°$$

$$P = 800 \times 0.3420 + 0.45 \times 800 \times 0.9397$$

$$P = 273.6 + 338.3 = 611.9 \text{ N}$$

$$\boxed{P \approx 611.9 \text{ N}}$$

**With additional force $Q$ at 15° above the plane:**

Now apply $P$ parallel to plane (up the slope) and $Q$ at 15° above the plane. Resolve $Q$:

- Component of $Q$ along plane (up): $Q \cos 15°$
- Component of $Q$ perpendicular to plane (into plane, increases $N$): $Q \sin 15°$

New normal reaction:
$$N' = W \cos\alpha + Q \sin 15°$$
$$N' = 800 \cos 20° + Q \sin 15°$$
$$N' = 751.75 + 0.2588 Q$$

New equilibrium (impending up-motion):
$$P + Q \cos 15° = W \sin\alpha + \mu_s N'$$

$$P + 0.9659 Q = 800 \sin 20° + 0.45 (751.75 + 0.2588 Q)$$

$$P + 0.9659 Q = 273.6 + 338.3 + 0.1165 Q$$

$$P + 0.9659 Q = 611.9 + 0.1165 Q$$

$$P = 611.9 + 0.1165 Q - 0.9659 Q$$

$$P = 611.9 - 0.8494 Q$$

This shows that with $Q$ applied, the required $P$ decreases. If we want $P$ minimized or for a specific reduction, the relation is:
$$\boxed{P = 611.9 - 0.849\,Q}$$

(For example, if $Q = 100$ N, then $P = 611.9 - 84.94 = 526.96$ N.)

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice: Ladder & Friction Angle**

**(a)** A ladder of length **6 m** and weight **400 N** rests against a smooth vertical wall and on a rough horizontal floor ($\mu_s = 0.3$). The ladder is inclined at **60°** with the horizontal. Check the equilibrium of the ladder. If not in equilibrium, find the minimum horizontal force $P$ to be applied at the base to keep it in position. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A block of weight **1000 N** is placed on a rough horizontal surface ($\mu_s = 0.5$). A force $P$ makes an angle of **30°** below the horizontal. Find the magnitude of $P$ when the block is on the verge of moving. Find the angle $\phi$ (friction angle). Also compute the actual force needed if $P$ is applied at **20° above** horizontal. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Solution to Question B:**

**(a) Ladder equilibrium:**

Let $A$ = foot of ladder (on floor), $B$ = top of ladder (against smooth wall). Length $L = 6$ m, weight $W = 400$ N acting at midspan (3 m from each end). Angle with horizontal = 60°.

**Reactions:**
- At $A$: Normal $N_A$ (up) + friction $F_A$ (horizontal). Since wall is smooth, only horizontal reaction at $B$.
- At $B$: Normal $N_B$ (horizontal, away from wall).

**Equilibrium equations:**

Vertical: $N_A - W = 0 \Rightarrow N_A = 400$ N

Horizontal: $N_B - F_A = 0 \Rightarrow F_A = N_B$

Moment about $A$ (taking counter-clockwise positive):
$$N_B \cdot L \sin 60° - W \cdot \frac{L}{2} \cos 60° = 0$$

$$N_B \times 6 \times 0.8660 - 400 \times 3 \times 0.5 = 0$$

$$5.196 \, N_B = 600 \quad \Rightarrow \quad N_B = 115.47 \text{ N}$$

Therefore $F_A = N_B = 115.47$ N.

**Check friction limit:**
$$F_{\max} = \mu_s N_A = 0.3 \times 400 = 120 \text{ N}$$

Required friction $F_A = 115.47$ N $\le F_{\max} = 120$ N.

**The ladder IS in equilibrium** (with a safety margin of 4.53 N).

If instead $F_A > F_{\max}$, we would compute the additional force $P$ at the base.

*(If $P$ is needed to prevent slipping: $P$ acts horizontally toward wall at base A. New horizontal equilibrium:*

$N_B = F_A + P$ and moment still gives $N_B = 115.47$ N, so:

$F_A = 115.47 - P$

For impending slip: $F_A = \mu_s N_A = 120$ N gives:

$120 = 115.47 - P \Rightarrow P = -4.53$ N (negative means already safe — no extra force needed).*)

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall**: Ladder problem — students forget that the **wall is smooth** (only $N_B$, no friction at $B$). Also, the weight acts at **midspan**, not at the bottom or center of mass unless specified.

---

**(b) Block with inclined force $P$:**

$P$ at 30° below horizontal. Components:
- Horizontal: $P \cos 30°$ (pulling forward)
- Vertical (downward): $P \sin 30°$ (adds to $N$)

**Forces on block:**
- Weight $W = 1000$ N (down)
- $P \cos 30°$ (horizontal)
- $N = W + P \sin 30°$ (normal up)
- Friction $F = \mu_s N$ opposing motion (horizontal, opposite to $P \cos 30°$)

**At impending motion:**
$$P \cos 30° = \mu_s N = \mu_s (W + P \sin 30°)$$

$$P \cos 30° = \mu_s W + \mu_s P \sin 30°$$

$$P (\cos 30° - \mu_s \sin 30°) = \mu_s W$$

$$P = \frac{\mu_s W}{\cos 30° - \mu_s \sin 30°}$$

$$P = \frac{0.5 \times 1000}{0.8660 - 0.5 \times 0.5} = \frac{500}{0.8660 - 0.25} = \frac{500}{0.6160}$$

$$\boxed{P \approx 811.7 \text{ N}}$$

**Friction angle:**
$$\phi = \tan^{-1}(\mu_s) = \tan^{-1}(0.5) = 26.57°$$

**If $P$ is applied at 20° ABOVE horizontal instead:**

Now $P \sin 20°$ is **upward** (reduces $N$):
$$N = W - P \sin 20°$$

Equilibrium:
$$P \cos 20° = \mu_s (W - P \sin 20°)$$

$$P \cos 20° + \mu_s P \sin 20° = \mu_s W$$

$$P (\cos 20° + 0.5 \sin 20°) = 0.5 \times 1000$$

$$P (0.9397 + 0.5 \times 0.3420) = 500$$

$$P (0.9397 + 0.1710) = 500$$

$$P (1.1107) = 500$$

$$\boxed{P \approx 450.2 \text{ N}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Direction Pitfall**: When $P$ is **below** horizontal, vertical component adds to $N$ → larger friction → larger $P$ needed. When $P$ is **above** horizontal, vertical component reduces $N$ → smaller friction → smaller $P$ needed. This is the basis of "optimum angle of pull" — pulling above horizontal saves effort!

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **MOST COMMON MARKS-LOSING MISTAKES in KTU Friction Problems:**
> 1. **Confusing static vs kinetic friction**: Use $F_{\max} = \mu_s N$ only at *impending* motion; use $F_k = \mu_k N$ during *actual* sliding.
> 2. **Forgetting the friction angle identity** $\mu_s = \tan\phi$: Tests almost every cycle.
> 3. **Sign errors on inclined planes**: Always resolve $W$ into $W\sin\alpha$ (along plane) and $W\cos\alpha$ (perpendicular).
> 4. **Direction of friction always OPPOSES impending motion** — draw the FBD carefully.
> 5. **Assuming wall is rough when the problem says smooth** (e.g., ladder problems).
> 6. **Using wrong normal reaction**: When force $P$ has a vertical component, $N \ne W$; it becomes $N = W \pm P \sin\theta$.
> 7. **Unit omission**: $\mu$ is dimensionless; angles in degrees.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **HIGH-DENSITY REVISION SUMMARY — "Friction: Laws of Friction"**

- ✅ **Friction is a self-adjusting tangential contact force** opposing relative motion (or its tendency).
- ✅ **Four Laws of Coulomb**: (1) Self-adjusting up to $F_{\max}$, (2) $F_{\max} \propto N$, (3) Independent of contact area, (4) Independent of sliding velocity.
- ✅ **Limiting friction**: $F_{\max} = \mu_s N$ — the maximum static friction available.
- ✅ **Kinetic friction**: $F_k = \mu_k N$ — during actual sliding.
- ✅ **Always**: $\mu_s > \mu_k$ (empirical fact).
- ✅ **Angle of friction** $\phi$: $\tan\phi = \mu_s$, hence $\phi = \tan^{-1}(\mu_s)$.
- ✅ **Angle of repose** $\alpha$: $\tan\alpha = \mu_s$ on an inclined plane at impending slide.
- ✅ **Key identity**: $\boxed{\phi = \alpha}$ (angle of friction = angle of repose).
- ✅ **Condition for no motion**: $F \le \mu_s N$.
- ✅ **Condition for impending slip**: $F = \mu_s N$.
- ✅ **Condition for sliding**: $F > \mu_s N$, and then kinetic friction $F_k = \mu_k N$ applies.
- ✅ **Inclined plane formulas**:
  - $N = W\cos\alpha$
  - Driving force down plane $= W\sin\alpha$
  - At impending slide: $\mu_s = \tan\alpha$
- ✅ **Force $P$ at angle $\theta$ with horizontal**:
  - Below horizontal → increases $N$, increases required $P$.
  - Above horizontal → decreases $N$, decreases required $P$ (**optimum angle of pull** concept).
- ✅ **$\mu$ is dimensionless**; all friction forces in Newtons.
- ✅ **Resultant contact reaction** $R = \sqrt{N^2 + F^2}$ makes angle $\phi$ with the normal.
- ✅ **Engineering apps**: brakes, clutches, belt drives, wedges, screw jacks, ladder stability, soil piles.
- ✅ **Self-locking wedge condition**: $\mu \ge \tan\lambda$ (where $\lambda$ = semi-angle).
- ✅ **Common KTU problem types**: (i) Impending motion on horizontal surface, (ii) Inclined plane (angle of repose), (iii) Force at angle to horizontal, (iv) Ladder against wall, (v) Two-block / wedge problems, (vi) Find smallest force / largest angle.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Analysis of Blocks and Ladder
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** GBEST213 - ENGINEERING MECHANICS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Analysis of Blocks and Ladder # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Analysis of Blocks **Formal KTU Definition:** A **block** in Engineering Mechanics refers to a rigid body resting on a surface (horizontal or inclined), subjected to a system of coplanar concurrent or non-concurrent forces. The "Analysis of Blocks" is the procedure of determining the reaction forces (normal and frictional) and the equilibrium state (stable, impending slip, or kinetic) using the laws of static friction, governed by **Coulomb's Law of Dry Friction**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Scheme):** The block problems in Module 2 of GBEST213 are predominantly built on the principle $F \le \mu N$, where the limiting case $F = \mu_s N$ defines the **impending slip condition**. **Conceptual Analogy (The Intuition):** Imagine you are trying to slide a heavy wooden almirah (wardrobe) across your room. The instant you start pushing, the almirah does not move. Why? Because the floor exerts a tiny "grip" on the almirah's base — this is **static friction**. As you increase your push, the friction increases in equal measure (up to a limit). The instant your push exceeds this limit, the almirah jerks and starts moving. The friction now drops to a slightly smaller value called **kinetic friction**. - The "block" is your almirah. - The "applied force" is your push. - The "friction" is the floor's grip. - The "normal reaction" is the floor pushing the almirah up against gravity. > [!NOTE] > **Key Physical Constants / Metrics (KTU Board Favourites):** > - Coefficient of Static Friction: $\mu_s$ (dimensionless) > - Coefficient of Kinetic Friction: $\mu_k$ (dimensionless) > - Note that **$\mu_s > \mu_k$** always holds. > - Standard value: $\mu \in [0, 1.5]$ for most engineering material pairs (rubber-concrete: $\mu \approx 0.9$, teflon-teflon: $\mu \approx 0.04$). --- ## 1.2 Analysis of Ladder **Formal KTU Definition:** A **ladder** is modelled in statics as a uniform (or non-uniform) slender rigid bar of length $L$ and weight $W$, leaning at an angle $\alpha$ against a vertical wall with its foot resting on a horizontal floor. The **Analysis of Ladder** involves determining the reaction forces at the two contact points (floor and wall) and identifying the **minimum angle** $\alpha_{min}$ below which the ladder slips — this angle is a function of the coefficients of friction at the floor and wall. **Conceptual Analogy:** Think of a painter's ladder propped against the wall of your house. You climb up safely when the ladder is "more vertical" (large $\alpha$). But if you make it too flat (small $\alpha$), the bottom slips outward and the ladder falls. The "magic angle" at which the bottom is *just about* to slip is what the problem asks you to find. The rougher the floor, the more flatly the ladder can be placed safely. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight:** In KTU examinations, the ladder is a **non-concurrent force system** — you *must* use all three equilibrium equations: $\Sigma F_x = 0$, $\Sigma F_y = 0$, and $\Sigma M = 0$. The first two alone are insufficient. **Classification of Ladder Contact Conditions (KTU Standard):** | Case | Floor | Wall | Ladder Type | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **1** | Rough ($\mu_1$) | Smooth ($\mu_2 = 0$) | Most common in exams | | **2** | Rough ($\mu_1$) | Rough ($\mu_2$) | Higher-order problem | | **3** | Smooth | Smooth | Trivially unstable | | **4** | Rough | Rough, with a person/cargo | Applied load problem | The analysis typically involves an additional load (a man, a paint bucket, etc.) at a specific point on the ladder, making the system fully determinate. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Block Analysis — Theoretical Foundation The free body of a block on an inclined plane reveals **three** force families: 1. **Active forces:** Applied force $P$ and self-weight $W = mg$. 2. **Normal reaction $N$:** Perpendicular to the contact surface. 3. **Friction force $F$:** Tangential to the contact surface, always opposing the *impending* (or actual) relative motion. The block is in equilibrium if and only if: $$F \le \mu_s N$$ The three regimes of behaviour are: - **Static equilibrium:** $F < \mu_s N$ — friction is self-adjusting. - **Impending slip (up the incline):** $F = \mu_s N$, motion about to begin upward. - **Impending slip (down the incline):** $F = \mu_s N$, motion about to begin downward. ### Derivation of the Angle of Friction Let a block rest on a horizontal surface with an applied horizontal force $P$ and vertical load $W$. At impending slip: $$P = \mu_s N, \quad N = W$$ The resultant reaction $R$ of the surface is the vector sum of $N$ and $F$: $$R = \sqrt{N^2 + F^2} = N\sqrt{1 + \mu_s^2}$$ The angle that $R$ makes with the normal is called the **angle of friction** $\phi$: $$\tan \phi = \frac{F}{N} = \mu_s$$ $$\boxed{\phi = \tan^{-1}(\mu_s)}$$ ### Derivation of the Angle of Repose For a block on a rough inclined plane (no applied force), at impending slip down the plane: $$W \sin \theta = \mu_s N = \mu_s W \cos \theta$$ $$\tan \theta = \mu_s$$ Comparing with $\tan \phi = \mu_s$, we obtain: $$\boxed{\theta = \phi}$$ The angle of repose is **numerically equal** to the angle of friction. The physical meaning: a block will just begin to slide on an incline equal to its angle of friction. --- ## 2.2 Ladder Analysis — Theoretical Foundation A standard ladder (Case 1: rough floor, smooth wall) is subjected to: | Symbol | Force | Point of Action | Direction | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | $W$ | Weight of ladder | Mid-point ($L/2$ from foot) | Vertically down | | $W_P$ | Weight of person (if any) | Distance $a$ from foot | Vertically down | | $N_A$ | Floor normal | Foot of ladder | Vertically up | | $F_A$ | Floor friction | Foot of ladder | Horizontal (toward wall) | | $N_B$ | Wall normal | Top of ladder | Horizontal (away from wall) | The three equilibrium equations are: $$\Sigma F_x = 0: \quad N_B - F_A = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad F_A = N_B$$ $$\Sigma F_y = 0: \quad N_A - W - W_P = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad N_A = W + W_P$$ $$\Sigma M_A = 0: \quad N_B \cdot L \sin \alpha - W \cdot \frac{L}{2} \cos \alpha - W_P \cdot a \cos \alpha = 0$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why moment equation and not force equations alone?** A ladder is a *non-concurrent* force system. With 3 unknowns ($N_A$, $F_A$, $N_B$) and only 2 force equations, you **must** use the moment equation. This is the most common mistake KTU students make — they set $\Sigma F_x = 0$ and $\Sigma F_y = 0$ and forget the third equation. ### Ladder Slip Condition Substituting the moment equation and using $F_A = N_B$ at impending slip: $$F_A = N_B = \frac{\left(W \cdot \dfrac{L}{2} + W_P \cdot a\right) \cos \alpha}{L \sin \alpha}$$ For no slip, $F_A \le \mu_1 N_A$: $$\frac{\left(W \cdot \dfrac{L}{2} + W_P \cdot a\right) \cos \alpha}{L \sin \alpha} \le \mu_1 (W + W_P)$$ The **minimum safe angle** $\alpha_{min}$ occurs at equality: $$\tan \alpha_{min} = \frac{\left(W \cdot \dfrac{L}{2} + W_P \cdot a\right)}{\mu_1 \cdot L \cdot (W + W_P)}$$ --- ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) > [!NOTE] > **Master these equations — they appear in 90% of KTU 2024 ladder and block problems.** ### Block on a Horizontal Surface | Condition | Formula | Meaning | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Equilibrium (no slip) | $F \le \mu_s N$ | Static balance | | Impending slip | $F = \mu_s N$ | Critical state | | Angle of friction | $\phi = \tan^{-1}(\mu_s)$ | Direction of total reaction | | Angle of repose | $\theta_r = \tan^{-1}(\mu_s) = \phi$ | Self-sliding angle | ### Block on an Inclined Plane (angle $\theta$, smooth contact + applied force $P$ horizontal) | Direction of Impending Motion | $N$ (perpendicular) | Equilibrium along plane | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Up the plane** | $N = W\cos\theta + P\sin\theta$ | $P\cos\theta = W\sin\theta + \mu_s N$ | | **Down the plane** | $N = W\cos\theta - P\sin\theta$ | $P\cos\theta + W\sin\theta = \mu_s N$ | ### Ladder Equations (Uniform, length $L$, weight $W$, angle $\alpha$, rough floor $\mu_1$, smooth wall) | Quantity | Formula | Comment | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Floor normal | $N_A = W$ | $\Sigma F_y = 0$ (no person) | | Wall normal | $N_B = \dfrac{W L \cos\alpha}{2 L \sin\alpha} = \dfrac{W \cot\alpha}{2}$ | $\Sigma M_A = 0$ | | Floor friction | $F_A = N_B = \dfrac{W \cot\alpha}{2}$ | $\Sigma F_x = 0$ | | Min safe angle | $\tan\alpha_{min} = \dfrac{1}{2\mu_1}$ | At impending slip | | Ladder self-weight in moment | $W \cdot (L/2) \cdot \cos\alpha$ | Perpendicular distance from $A$ | | Top contact moment arm | $N_B \cdot L \sin\alpha$ | Perpendicular distance from $A$ | ### Units and Dimensions - $\mu$, $\phi$, $\theta$ are **dimensionless** (radians or degrees). - Forces: **Newtons (N)** in SI; **kgf** in MKS. - Distances: **metres (m)**. - Moments: **N·m**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Real-World Utility:** > Block-on-incline analysis is foundational for **earth dam design** (slope stability), **brake pad engineering** (friction in disc brakes), and **rockfall barriers** in civil engineering. Ladder analysis is directly used in **scaffolding safety design** under IS 4014 and OSHA 1926 standards, and in **fire-truck ladder deployment** by mechanical engineers. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Minimum Angle for Ladder (Case 1: Rough Floor, Smooth Wall) **Problem Setup:** A uniform ladder of length $L$ and weight $W$ leans against a smooth vertical wall, foot on a rough horizontal floor with coefficient of friction $\mu$. Find the **minimum angle** $\alpha$ for equilibrium. ### Step 1: Free Body Diagram Identify all forces acting on the ladder: - Weight $W$ at midspan, vertically downward. - Normal reaction at floor $N_A$ at the foot, vertically upward. - Frictional force $F_A$ at the foot, horizontal (toward wall, opposing slip). - Normal reaction at wall $N_B$ at the top, horizontal (away from wall, smooth ⇒ no friction at top). ### Step 2: Apply Vertical Equilibrium The vertical forces are $N_A$ (up) and $W$ (down): $$\Sigma F_y = 0$$ $$N_A - W = 0$$ $$N_A = W$$ ### Step 3: Apply Moment Equilibrium (about point A — foot of ladder) This eliminates $N_A$ and $F_A$ from the equation, leaving only $N_B$ and $W$ as unknowns. The perpendicular distance from $A$ to the line of action of $W$ is: $$d_W = \frac{L}{2} \cos \alpha$$ The perpendicular distance from $A$ to the line of action of $N_B$ is: $$d_B = L \sin \alpha$$ Taking counter-clockwise as positive: $$\Sigma M_A = 0$$ $$N_B \cdot (L \sin \alpha) - W \cdot \left(\frac{L}{2} \cos \alpha\right) = 0$$ $$N_B \cdot L \sin \alpha = W \cdot \frac{L}{2} \cos \alpha$$ ### Step 4: Solve for $N_B$ Cancel $L$ from both sides: $$N_B \sin \alpha = \frac{W}{2} \cos \alpha$$ $$N_B = \frac{W \cos \alpha}{2 \sin \alpha}$$ $$\boxed{N_B = \frac{W}{2} \cot \alpha}$$ ### Step 5: Apply Horizontal Equilibrium $$\Sigma F_x = 0$$ $$N_B - F_A = 0$$ $$F_A = N_B = \frac{W}{2} \cot \alpha$$ ### Step 6: Apply Friction Inequality (Equilibrium Condition) For the ladder to remain in equilibrium: $$F_A \le \mu N_A$$ $$\frac{W}{2} \cot \alpha \le \mu W$$ The weight $W$ cancels (it is non-zero): $$\frac{1}{2} \cot \alpha \le \mu$$ ### Step 7: Determine the Critical (Minimum) Angle At the **impending slip** condition, the inequality becomes an equality: $$\frac{1}{2} \cot \alpha_{min} = \mu$$ $$\cot \alpha_{min} = 2\mu$$ $$\tan \alpha_{min} = \frac{1}{2\mu}$$ $$\boxed{\alpha_{min} = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{1}{2\mu}\right)}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Physical Interpretation:** The minimum safe angle depends *only* on the friction coefficient — the ladder's weight $W$ and length $L$ are irrelevant for a *uniform* ladder! This is a famous KTU trick question: "If the ladder's weight is doubled, does $\alpha_{min}$ change?" — **No, it does not, for a uniform ladder.** --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Block on Inclined Plane with Horizontal Force **Problem Setup:** A block of weight $W$ rests on a rough inclined plane (angle $\theta$, coefficient of static friction $\mu_s$). A horizontal force $P$ pushes the block *up* the plane. Find the minimum $P$ required to initiate upward motion. ### Step 1: Free Body Diagram The four forces on the block: - Weight $W$ (vertically down). - Horizontal push $P$ (toward the plane, i.e., pointing "into" the incline). - Normal reaction $N$ (perpendicular to plane, away from surface). - Friction $F$ (down the plane, opposing impending motion). ### Step 2: Resolve Forces Along and Perpendicular to the Plane We rotate the coordinate system to align with the incline. The horizontal force $P$ and weight $W$ must be resolved. Decomposition table: | Force | Component along plane (up positive) | Component perpendicular to plane (outward positive) | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | $W$ | $-W \sin \theta$ | $-W \cos \theta$ | | $P$ (horizontal) | $P \cos \theta$ | $-P \sin \theta$ | | $N$ | $0$ | $N$ | | $F$ (down plane) | $-\mu_s N$ | $0$ | ### Step 3: Perpendicular Equilibrium $$\Sigma F_{\perp} = 0$$ $$N - W \cos \theta - P \sin \theta = 0$$ $$N = W \cos \theta + P \sin \theta$$ ### Step 4: Parallel Equilibrium (at impending slip up) $$\Sigma F_{\parallel} = 0$$ $$P \cos \theta - W \sin \theta - \mu_s N = 0$$ $$P \cos \theta = W \sin \theta + \mu_s N$$ ### Step 5: Substitute $N$ from Step 3 $$P \cos \theta = W \sin \theta + \mu_s (W \cos \theta + P \sin \theta)$$ ### Step 6: Expand and Group Terms Containing $P$ $$P \cos \theta = W \sin \theta + \mu_s W \cos \theta + \mu_s P \sin \theta$$ $$P \cos \theta - \mu_s P \sin \theta = W \sin \theta + \mu_s W \cos \theta$$ $$P (\cos \theta - \mu_s \sin \theta) = W (\sin \theta + \mu_s \cos \theta)$$ ### Step 7: Final Formula for $P_{min}$ $$\boxed{P_{min} = \frac{W(\sin \theta + \mu_s \cos \theta)}{\cos \theta - \mu_s \sin \theta}}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Validity Condition:** This formula is *only* valid when $\cos \theta > \mu_s \sin \theta$, i.e., $\tan \theta < \cot \phi = 1/\mu_s$. If the incline is steeper than the angle of friction, the block slides down by itself and no finite $P$ can prevent this. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Quick Reference Table for Common KTU Variants | Configuration | Free-Body Highlight | Critical Equation | Slip Triggered By | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Block, horizontal floor, horizontal push $P$ | $F=P$, $N=W$ | $P = \mu_s W$ | Push exceeds $\mu_s W$ | | Block, incline $\theta$, no applied force | $F=W\sin\theta$, $N=W\cos\theta$ | $\tan\theta = \mu_s$ | Incline steeper than $\theta_r$ | | Block, incline $\theta$, horizontal push | As derived in 3.2 | $P_{min} = W\frac{\sin\theta + \mu_s\cos\theta}{\cos\theta - \mu_s\sin\theta}$ | Push exceeds $P_{min}$ | | Ladder, uniform, rough floor only | 3 unknowns, 3 equations | $\tan\alpha_{min} = \frac{1}{2\mu}$ | $\alpha < \alpha_{min}$ | | Ladder, with man at distance $a$ | Extra weight $W_P$ in moment | $\tan\alpha_{min} = \frac{WL/2 + W_P a}{\mu_1 L (W+W_P)}$ | $\alpha < \alpha_{min}$ | | Ladder, rough floor $\mu_1$ and rough wall $\mu_2$ | $F_B$ at wall too | Need 4th equation: $F_B = \mu_2 N_B$ | Both slips simultaneously | --- ## 3.4 Worked Example (KTU-Style 14-Mark Problem) **Question:** A uniform ladder of length $5\text{ m}$ and weight $300\text{ N}$ rests against a smooth vertical wall with its foot on a rough horizontal floor ($\mu = 0.25$). A man weighing $700\text{ N}$ climbs the ladder to a point $3\text{ m}$ from the foot. Determine: (a) The reactions at the floor and the wall when the man is at $3\text{ m}$. (b) The minimum angle at which the ladder must be placed for safe use when the man is at the top. **Solution (Part a):** Let $\alpha$ be the angle the ladder makes with the horizontal. For a $5\text{ m}$ ladder with foot-to-top $5\text{ m}$: - The vertical height of the top is $h = 5 \sin\alpha$. - The horizontal distance of the foot from the wall is $x = 5 \cos\alpha$. Take moments about the foot $A$ (eliminating $N_A$ and $F_A$): The moment arm of $W_{ladder}$ (acting at the midpoint) about $A$: $$d_W = \frac{L}{2}\cos\alpha = 2.5\cos\alpha$$ The moment arm of $W_{man}$ (acting at $3\text{ m}$ from foot) about $A$: $$d_{man} = 3\cos\alpha$$ The moment arm of $N_B$ (acting at top, horizontally) about $A$: $$d_B = L\sin\alpha = 5\sin\alpha$$ Applying $\Sigma M_A = 0$ (CCW positive): $$N_B \cdot (5\sin\alpha) - 300 \cdot (2.5\cos\alpha) - 700 \cdot (3\cos\alpha) = 0$$ $$5 N_B \sin\alpha = 750\cos\alpha + 2100\cos\alpha$$ $$5 N_B \sin\alpha = 2850 \cos\alpha$$ $$N_B = 570 \cot\alpha \quad \text{(N)}$$ **Apply $\Sigma F_x = 0$:** $$F_A = N_B = 570\cot\alpha$$ **Apply $\Sigma F_y = 0$:** $$N_A = 300 + 700 = 1000\text{ N}$$ **Check safety:** For equilibrium, $F_A \le \mu N_A = 0.25 \times 1000 = 250\text{ N}$. So $570\cot\alpha \le 250$, which gives $\cot\alpha \le 0.4386$, or $\tan\alpha \ge 2.280$. This requires $\alpha \ge 66.32°$. If the ladder is at any smaller angle, it slips. **Solution (Part b):** Man at the top means $a = L = 5\text{ m}$: $$N_B \cdot 5\sin\alpha = 300 \cdot 2.5\cos\alpha + 700 \cdot 5\cos\alpha$$ $$5 N_B \sin\alpha = 750\cos\alpha + 3500\cos\alpha = 4250\cos\alpha$$ $$N_B = 850\cot\alpha$$ For safety, $F_A = N_B \le \mu N_A = 0.25 \times 1000 = 250\text{ N}$: $$850\cot\alpha \le 250$$ $$\cot\alpha \le 0.2941$$ $$\tan\alpha_{min} \ge 3.40$$ $$\boxed{\alpha_{min} = \tan^{-1}(3.40) \approx 73.6°}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Observation:** When the man climbs higher, $\alpha_{min}$ increases. The ladder must be more vertical to remain safe. This is a classic KTU result. # 4.
