In the KTU exam, you are expected to draw a **hand-sketched, scale-correct FBD** on the answer sheet using a ruler and protractor. Use Mermaid only for the *flow of logic*, not for final submission. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Define the terms:** (a) **Angle of friction** (b) **Angle of repose** (c) **Coefficient of friction** **Model Answer:** (a) **Angle of friction ($\phi$):** The angle made by the resultant reaction force (combining normal reaction $N$ and limiting frictional force $F = \mu_s N$) with the direction of the normal reaction. Mathematically, $\tan \phi = \mu_s$. (b) **Angle of repose ($\theta_r$):** The minimum angle of inclination of a rough inclined plane with the horizontal at which a body placed on it just begins to slide down. Mathematically, $\theta_r = \phi = \tan^{-1}(\mu_s)$. (c) **Coefficient of friction ($\mu$):** A dimensionless scalar that quantifies the ratio of the limiting frictional force to the normal reaction between two surfaces: $\mu = F/N$. It is a property of the material pair in contact. --- ### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **State the laws of dry friction (Coulomb's Laws of Friction).** **Model Answer:** 1. **First Law:** The magnitude of the maximum static frictional force $F_{max}$ is proportional to the normal reaction $N$ between the surfaces: $F_{max} \propto N$, or $F_{max} = \mu_s N$. 2. **Second Law:** The maximum frictional force is independent of the *area of contact* between the surfaces. 3. **Third Law:** The kinetic frictional force $F_k$ (when sliding occurs) is also proportional to the normal reaction, but with a *smaller* coefficient $\mu_k < \mu_s$: $F_k = \mu_k N$. The direction of the frictional force always opposes the direction of relative motion (or impending motion). --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice ### Question A (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 2]` A uniform ladder of length $6\text{ m}$ weighing $250\text{ N}$ is placed against a smooth vertical wall. Its foot rests on a rough horizontal floor with $\mu = 0.3$. A man weighing $750\text{ N}$ stands on a rung $2\text{ m}$ from the top of the ladder. **(a)** Draw the free body diagram of the ladder. Identify all forces and write the three equilibrium equations. **(b)** Find the minimum angle of inclination the ladder must make with the horizontal so that the man can climb safely to the top of the ladder. **Model Solution:** **Part (a) — FBD and Equilibrium Equations:** The free body diagram shows the ladder as a rigid bar with the following forces: | Force | Magnitude | Direction | Point of Application | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | $W_L$ (ladder weight) | $250\text{ N}$ | Vertically down | Mid-point ($3\text{ m}$ from foot) | | $W_M$ (man weight) | $750\text{ N}$ | Vertically down | $4\text{ m}$ from foot ($2\text{ m}$ from top) | | $N_A$ (floor normal) | Unknown | Vertically up | Foot of ladder | | $F_A$ (floor friction) | Unknown | Horizontally toward wall | Foot of ladder | | $N_B$ (wall normal) | Unknown | Horizontally away from wall | Top of ladder | Three equilibrium equations: **(1) Sum of vertical forces = 0:** $$\Sigma F_y = 0: \quad N_A - W_L - W_M = 0$$ **(2) Sum of horizontal forces = 0:** $$\Sigma F_x = 0: \quad N_B - F_A = 0$$ **(3) Sum of moments about A = 0 (CCW positive):** $$\Sigma M_A = 0: \quad N_B(L\sin\alpha) - W_L\left(\frac{L}{2}\cos\alpha\right) - W_M(4\cos\alpha) = 0$$ **Part (b) — Minimum angle for safety:** When the man is at the top, his distance from the foot is $a = 6\text{ m} = L$. From the moment equation (3): $$N_B \cdot 6\sin\alpha = 250 \cdot 3 \cdot \cos\alpha + 750 \cdot 6 \cdot \cos\alpha$$ $$6 N_B \sin\alpha = 750\cos\alpha + 4500\cos\alpha$$ $$6 N_B \sin\alpha = 5250\cos\alpha$$ $$N_B = 875\cot\alpha \quad \text{(N)}$$ From equation (1): $N_A = 250 + 750 = 1000\text{ N}$. From equation (2): $F_A = N_B = 875\cot\alpha$. **Apply the no-slip condition:** $$F_A \le \mu N_A$$ $$875\cot\alpha \le 0.3 \times 1000$$ $$875\cot\alpha \le 300$$ $$\cot\alpha \le \frac{300}{875} = 0.3429$$ $$\tan\alpha \ge \frac{1}{0.3429} = 2.917$$ $$\alpha \ge \tan^{-1}(2.917) \approx 71.08°$$ $$\boxed{\alpha_{min} \approx 71.1°}$$ --- ### Question B (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 2]` A block weighing $500\text{ N}$ is placed on a rough inclined plane that makes an angle of $25°$ with the horizontal. The coefficient of static friction between the block and the plane is $\mu_s = 0.30$. A horizontal force $P$ is applied to the block. **(a)** Determine the minimum value of $P$ required to just initiate the motion of the block *up* the incline. **(b)** Determine the range of values of $P$ for which the block remains in equilibrium on the incline. **Model Solution:** **Part (a) — Minimum $P$ for upward motion:** Let $P$ act horizontally toward the incline (pushing the block up the slope). **Step 1: Resolve forces perpendicular to the plane:** $$\Sigma F_{\perp} = 0$$ $$N - W\cos\theta - P\sin\theta = 0$$ $$N = W\cos\theta + P\sin\theta = 500\cos 25° + P\sin 25°$$ $$N = 453.15 + 0.4226P$$ **Step 2: For impending motion up the plane, friction acts down the plane:** $$F = \mu_s N = 0.3(453.15 + 0.4226P) = 135.94 + 0.1268P$$ **Step 3: Resolve forces parallel to the plane (up the plane positive):** $$P\cos\theta - W\sin\theta - F = 0$$ $$P\cos 25° = 500\sin 25° + (135.94 + 0.1268P)$$ $$0.9063P = 211.31 + 135.94 + 0.1268P$$ $$0.9063P - 0.1268P = 347.25$$ $$0.7795P = 347.25$$ $$P_{min} = 445.5\text{ N}$$ **Part (b) — Range of $P$ for equilibrium:** The block remains in equilibrium if it neither slips up nor down. **Case A: Impending motion DOWN (lower bound of $P$):** Friction now acts UP the plane. The direction of $P$ is still horizontal. **Perpendicular equilibrium (same as before):** $$N = 453.15 + 0.4226P$$ **Parallel equilibrium:** $$P\cos\theta + F - W\sin\theta = 0$$ $$P\cos 25° + \mu_s(453.15 + 0.4226P) = 500\sin 25°$$ $$0.9063P + 0.3(453.15 + 0.4226P) = 211.31$$ $$0.9063P + 135.94 + 0.1268P = 211.31$$ $$1.0331P = 75.37$$ $$P_{lower} = 72.96\text{ N}$$ **Case B: Impending motion UP (upper bound of $P$):** From Part (a), $P_{upper} = 445.5\text{ N}$. **Equilibrium range:** $$72.96\text{ N} \le P \le 445.5\text{ N}$$ If $P < 72.96\text{ N}$, the block slides *down* the incline. If $P > 445.5\text{ N}$, the block slides *up* the incline. $$\boxed{72.96\text{ N} \le P \le 445.5\text{ N}}$$ --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls (MOST COMMON MISTAKES):** > 1. **Forgetting the moment equation in ladder problems.** A ladder has 3 unknown reactions; you need *all three* equilibrium equations. Setting only $\Sigma F_x = 0$ and $\Sigma F_y = 0$ gives 2 equations in 3 unknowns — the problem becomes indeterminate. **Lose 4 marks.** > 2. **Sign error in the moment arm.** The moment of $N_B$ about the foot $A$ is $N_B \cdot L \sin\alpha$ (using vertical height as the perpendicular distance), NOT $N_B \cdot L \cos\alpha$. Many students incorrectly use the horizontal distance. **Lose 2 marks.** > 3. **Confusing the direction of friction with the direction of motion.** Friction always opposes *impending* motion. If the block is about to slide up, friction acts *down*. Re-check this every time. **Lose 3 marks.** > 4. **Using $\mu_k$ instead of $\mu_s$ (or vice versa).** KTU almost always asks for *static* equilibrium, so use $\mu_s$. **Lose 2 marks.** > 5. **Not verifying the physical feasibility of the answer.** If your formula gives $\alpha_{min} > 90°$, something is wrong. Check units and the friction inequality. **Lose 1 mark.** > 6. **Forgetting the weight of the man in the moment equation.** If a person is on the ladder, include $W_P \cdot a \cos\alpha$ in the moment balance. **Lose 3 marks.** --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Print and stick on your wall before the exam):** - **Block on horizontal surface:** $F = \mu N$, $N = W$, slip when $P > \mu W$. - **Block on incline (no force):** Slip when $\tan\theta > \mu$, i.e., $\theta > \theta_r = \phi$. - **Block on incline (with horizontal force $P$):** $$P_{up} = \frac{W(\sin\theta + \mu_s \cos\theta)}{\cos\theta - \mu_s \sin\theta}$$ $$P_{down} = \frac{W(\sin\theta - \mu_s \cos\theta)}{\cos\theta + \mu_s \sin\theta}$$ - **Ladder (uniform, rough floor, smooth wall):** $\tan\alpha_{min} = \dfrac{1}{2\mu}$. The weight and length of the ladder are irrelevant for $\alpha_{min}$! - **Ladder (with man at distance $a$):** $\tan\alpha_{min} = \dfrac{WL/2 + W_P a}{\mu_1 L(W + W_P)}$. Heavier man or higher position ⇒ steeper ladder needed. - **Ladder (rough wall too):** Add $F_B = \mu_2 N_B$ as the fourth unknown. The system becomes 3 equations, 4 unknowns — you need the additional relation $F_B = \mu_2 N_B$ at impending slip to make it determinate. - **Angle of friction = Angle of repose** for the same material pair: $\phi = \theta_r = \tan^{-1}(\mu_s)$. - **Three equilibrium equations are non-negotiable** for any ladder or block-with-multiple-forces problem. - **Direction of friction** = opposite to the *impending* (or actual) direction of motion. Always draw this *first* on the FBD. - **Validity conditions** must always be checked: e.g., $\cos\theta - \mu_s \sin\theta > 0$ for the block-on-incline formula to give a positive $P$. - **KTU favourite trick questions:** * "If the ladder's weight is doubled, does $\alpha_{min}$ change?" — **No, for a uniform ladder.** * "If a man climbs up, does the safe angle increase or decrease?" — **Increase** (steeper ladder needed). * "What is the minimum force required to keep a block on a steep incline stationary?" — Use the *down-the-plane* formula (i.e., $P_{down}$), not the up formula. * "Can a smooth ladder (no friction anywhere) ever rest against a wall?" — **No**, it is in unstable equilibrium; any perturbation will cause it to fall. > **End of Module 2 Notes — Analysis of Blocks and Ladder**

topics.title: Centroid of Composite Areas
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GBEST213 - ENGINEERING MECHANICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Centroid of Composite Areas

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CENTROID OF COMPOSITE AREA**
> The **centroid** of a plane composite (compound) area is the geometric center of that area, obtained by treating the figure as an algebraic combination of standard primitive shapes (rectangles, triangles, circles, semicircles, quarter-circles, etc.) whose individual centroids are already known. It is the point through which the **first moment of area** of the figure about any axis is zero.

Mathematically, for a composite area formed by $n$ component areas $A_1, A_2, \ldots, A_n$ with individual centroids $(x_1, y_1), (x_2, y_2), \ldots, (x_n, y_n)$:

$$
\bar{x} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} A_i \, x_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} A_i}
\qquad \text{and} \qquad
\bar{y} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} A_i \, y_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} A_i}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> In the KTU 2024 Scheme, the centroid is treated as a **purely geometric property** of the area — it has units of length (**mm** or **m**) and depends only on the *shape* of the figure, not on the material. It coincides with the **center of gravity (C.G.)** when the area (or body) is assumed to be of *uniform density* and *negligible thickness*.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Tipping Point" of a Shape

Imagine a complex flat metal plate — say, a **T-shaped** brass lamina — and you want to balance it perfectly on the tip of a pin so that it does not tip over. There is exactly **one special point** where the pin must touch the plate for it to remain perfectly horizontal. That unique balance point is the **centroid**.

Now imagine the T-plate is *cut* into two simpler pieces: a horizontal flange (rectangle) and a vertical web (rectangle). Each piece has its *own* balance point that you can find easily (the intersection of its diagonals). The **centroid of the original T-plate** is just the *area-weighted average* of these two balance points — heavier (larger) pieces "pull" the centroid toward themselves, while smaller pieces "pull" it less.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Hook:** *Bigger area = bigger say in deciding where the centroid sits.* A piece with **twice the area** of another piece will pull the centroid **twice as hard** toward its own center.

---

## 1.3 Key Standard Centroids at a Glance (to be revisited in Section 2)

| Standard Shape | Centroid from Reference (Origin at the marked base/corner) |
|---|---|
| Rectangle (width $b$, height $h$) | $\left(\tfrac{b}{2}, \tfrac{h}{2}\right)$ |
| Triangle (base $b$, height $h$, from base) | $\left(\tfrac{b}{3}, \tfrac{h}{3}\right)$ |
| Semicircle (radius $R$, from flat diameter) | $\left(0, \tfrac{4R}{3\pi}\right)$ |
| Quarter-circle (radius $R$, from right-angle corner) | $\left(\tfrac{4R}{3\pi}, \tfrac{4R}{3\pi}\right)$ |
| Full circle (radius $R$, from center) | $(0, 0)$ |

These five shapes are the **building blocks** of every KTU composite-area problem.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Underlying Principle — First Moment of Area

The centroid is fundamentally a **first moment of area** concept. For a differential element $dA$ located at distance $y$ from the $x$-axis, the *first moment* about the $x$-axis is:

$$
Q_x = \int y \, dA
$$

The first moment of the **entire area** $A$ about the $x$-axis is equivalently written as:

$$
Q_x = A \cdot \bar{y}
$$

Therefore, the centroidal coordinate $\bar{y}$ is:

$$
\bar{y} = \frac{Q_x}{A} = \frac{\int y \, dA}{\int dA}
$$

For a *composite* area, the integral $\int y \, dA$ is replaced by the **discrete summation** $\sum A_i \, y_i$ (because each primitive shape's moment is *known* in closed form).

---

## 2.2 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Procedure

> [!NOTE]
> **The KTU Board expects the following six-step method explicitly written in the answer sheet.**

1. **Sketch the figure** to scale with all dimensions labelled.
2. **Choose a convenient reference origin** — usually at the bottom-left corner or at an axis of symmetry.
3. **Decompose** the composite area into standard primitive shapes (rectangles, triangles, circles, semicircles, quarter-circles).
4. **Compute the area $A_i$** of each component shape. **Subtract** the area of any *hole* (use a *negative sign*).
5. **Locate the centroid $(x_i, y_i)$** of each component from the chosen reference origin.
6. **Apply the composite centroid formula** to obtain $(\bar{x}, \bar{y})$ and clearly mark the centroid on the sketch.

---

## 2.3 Handling Holes and Cut-outs (Negative Areas)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Any area that is **removed** from a larger area must be entered with a **negative sign** in the area column. Its centroid still lies *inside the cut-out region* — that is correct and expected. The same $(x_i, y_i)$ value is plugged in; only $A_i$ is negative.

This single rule is what handles I-sections, C-channels, hollow shafts, plates with bolt-holes, etc.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Centroid $x$ | $\bar{x} = \dfrac{\sum A_i \, x_i}{\sum A_i}$ | $\text{mm}$ or $\text{m}$ | Numerator is *first moment* about $y$-axis |
| 2 | Centroid $y$ | $\bar{y} = \dfrac{\sum A_i \, y_i}{\sum A_i}$ | $\text{mm}$ or $\text{m}$ | Numerator is *first moment* about $x$-axis |
| 3 | First moment (discrete) | $Q_x = \sum A_i \, y_i$ | $\text{mm}^{3}$ or $\text{m}^{3}$ | Required for subsequent shear-stress problems |
| 4 | Total area | $A = \sum A_i$ | $\text{mm}^{2}$ or $\text{m}^{2}$ | Holes have **negative** $A_i$ |
| 5 | Sign convention for holes | $A_{\text{hole}} < 0$ | — | Always subtract |
| 6 | Rectangle centroid | $(\tfrac{b}{2}, \tfrac{h}{2})$ | $\text{mm}$ | Origin at adjacent corner |
| 7 | Triangle centroid | $(\tfrac{b}{3}, \tfrac{h}{3})$ | $\text{mm}$ | Measured from the base |
| 8 | Semicircle centroid | $\tfrac{4R}{3\pi} \approx 0.4244\,R$ | $\text{mm}$ | From the *flat* (diameter) edge |
| 9 | Quarter-circle centroid | $\tfrac{4R}{3\pi}$ on both axes | $\text{mm}$ | From the *right-angle corner* |

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Engineering Domain | Application of Centroid of Composite Areas |
|---|---|
| **Structural Steel Design** | Locating C.G. of I-beams, T-sections, C-channels and angles used in trusses, columns, and gantry girders. |
| **Mechanical Machine Design** | Finding the center of mass of irregular machine bases, flywheel sections, and turbine blades. |
| **Aerospace Engineering** | Determining the aerodynamic center of wing ribs and fuselage cross-sections (composite of straight + curved segments). |
| **Shipbuilding / Naval Arch.** | Computing the *centroid of the waterline area* — the keystone input for **metacenter calculations** and **ship stability**. |
| **Civil — Dam Design** | Locating the resultant hydrostatic thrust line on a dam face by splitting the curved wetted face into rectangles + a triangle. |
| **Hydraulics** | Computing the centroid of an irregular channel cross-section for the *hydraulic depth* in open-channel flow (Manning's equation). |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Composite Centroid Formula

Consider a plane area $A$ that is the **union** (or algebraic sum) of $n$ non-overlapping sub-areas $A_1, A_2, \ldots, A_n$. For each sub-area $A_i$, the *first moment* about the $y$-axis is:

$$
Q_{y,i} = A_i \cdot x_i
$$

The *total* first moment of the composite area about the $y$-axis is the **sum of the first moments** of the components:

$$
Q_y = \sum_{i=1}^{n} Q_{y,i} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} A_i \, x_i
$$

But by definition of the centroid, the total first moment also equals:

$$
Q_y = A \cdot \bar{x} = \left( \sum_{i=1}^{n} A_i \right) \cdot \bar{x}
$$

Equating the two expressions:

$$
\left( \sum_{i=1}^{n} A_i \right) \cdot \bar{x} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} A_i \, x_i
$$

Solving for $\bar{x}$:

$$
\boxed{\bar{x} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} A_i \, x_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} A_i}}
$$

An identical derivation about the $x$-axis gives:

$$
\boxed{\bar{y} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} A_i \, y_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} A_i}}
$$

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Standard Primitive Centroids (with derivations)

> [!NOTE]
> The values in the table below are **KTU board-required formulae** that you must memorise — no derivation is needed in the exam, but understanding *where* they come from reinforces the first-moment concept.

### 3.2.1 Rectangle

The rectangle of width $b$ and height $h$ has a uniform differential strip $dA = b \, dy$ at distance $y$ from the base.

$$
\bar{y} = \frac{1}{A}\int y \, dA = \frac{1}{bh}\int_{0}^{h} y \cdot b \, dy = \frac{1}{bh}\left[\frac{b y^{2}}{2}\right]_{0}^{h} = \frac{h}{2}
$$

By symmetry, $\bar{x} = \dfrac{b}{2}$.

### 3.2.2 Triangle (Right-angled for simplicity)

Take a triangle with base $b$ along the $x$-axis and apex at height $h$. A horizontal strip at height $y$ has width $\dfrac{b(h-y)}{h}$ (linear taper from $b$ at $y=0$ to $0$ at $y=h$).

$$
\bar{y} = \frac{1}{A}\int y \, dA
$$

The area $A = \tfrac{1}{2}bh$. The moment:

$$
\int_{0}^{h} y \cdot \frac{b(h-y)}{h} \, dy = \frac{b}{h}\int_{0}^{h} \left(hy - y^{2}\right) dy = \frac{b}{h}\left[\frac{hy^{2}}{2} - \frac{y^{3}}{3}\right]_{0}^{h}
$$

$$
= \frac{b}{h}\left(\frac{h^{3}}{2} - \frac{h^{3}}{3}\right) = \frac{b}{h} \cdot \frac{h^{3}}{6} = \frac{bh^{2}}{6}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\bar{y} = \frac{bh^{2}/6}{bh/2} = \frac{h}{3}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The triangle's centroid is **one-third of the height from the base**, *not* at half-height. This is the single most-confused formula in KTU Board exams.

### 3.2.3 Semicircle

A semicircle of radius $R$ bounded by $y \ge 0$. By symmetry, $\bar{x} = 0$. For $\bar{y}$, the area $A = \tfrac{\pi R^{2}}{2}$, and a horizontal strip at height $y$ has width $2\sqrt{R^{2} - y^{2}}$:

$$
\int_{0}^{R} y \cdot 2\sqrt{R^{2} - y^{2}} \, dy
$$

Substitute $u = R^{2} - y^{2}$, $du = -2y\,dy$:

$$
= \int_{R^{2}}^{0} \sqrt{u} \cdot (-du) = \int_{0}^{R^{2}} u^{1/2} \, du = \frac{2}{3}(R^{2})^{3/2} = \frac{2R^{3}}{3}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\bar{y} = \frac{2R^{3}/3}{\pi R^{2}/2} = \frac{4R}{3\pi} \approx 0.4244\,R
$$

### 3.2.4 Quarter-Circle

A quarter-circle of radius $R$ in the first quadrant. Both $\bar{x}$ and $\bar{y}$ are equal by symmetry about the line $y = x$:

$$
\bar{x} = \bar{y} = \frac{4R}{3\pi}
$$

---

## 3.3 Comprehensive Worked Example — T-Section

**Problem.** A T-section is made of a flange $200 \text{ mm} \times 20 \text{ mm}$ and a web $20 \text{ mm} \times 180 \text{ mm}$ (web below flange). Determine the centroid $(\bar{x}, \bar{y})$ measured from the bottom-left corner of the web.

### Step 1 — Sketch and Decompose

The figure splits into two rectangles:

- **Rectangle 1 (Web):** width $20$ mm, height $180$ mm — sits at the bottom.
- **Rectangle 2 (Flange):** width $200$ mm, height $20$ mm — sits on top of the web, centred horizontally.

### Step 2 — Choose Reference Origin

Place the origin at the **bottom-left corner of the web** (point O). The $x$-axis points right, $y$-axis points up.

### Step 3 — Tabulate the Data

| Part | Description | Area $A_i$ (mm²) | $x_i$ (mm) | $y_i$ (mm) | $A_i x_i$ (mm³) | $A_i y_i$ (mm³) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Web (rectangle) | $20 \times 180 = 3600$ | $10$ | $90$ | $3600 \times 10 = 36000$ | $3600 \times 90 = 324000$ |
| 2 | Flange (rectangle) | $200 \times 20 = 4000$ | $100$ | $180 + 10 = 190$ | $4000 \times 100 = 400000$ | $4000 \times 190 = 760000$ |
| — | **Total $\Sigma$** | $\mathbf{7600}$ | — | — | $\mathbf{436000}$ | $\mathbf{1084000}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why $y_2 = 190$ mm?** The flange sits *on top* of the $180$ mm web. Its own centroid is at half its thickness ($10$ mm) above the web top. So $y_2 = 180 + 10 = 190$ mm.

### Step 4 — Apply the Composite Formula

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{x} &= \frac{\sum A_i \, x_i}{\sum A_i} = \frac{436000 \text{ mm}^{3}}{7600 \text{ mm}^{2}} \\[6pt]
\bar{x} &= 57.37 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{y} &= \frac{\sum A_i \, y_i}{\sum A_i} = \frac{1084000 \text{ mm}^{3}}{7600 \text{ mm}^{2}} \\[6pt]
\bar{y} &= 142.63 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Final Answer

$$
\boxed{\bar{x} = 57.37 \text{ mm}, \quad \bar{y} = 142.63 \text{ mm}}
$$

The centroid lies slightly to the **right of the web's centreline** (the flange pulls it toward itself) and at about **71 % of the total height** from the base (because the flange, being a large-area lump, is near the top).

---

## 3.4 Supplementary Worked Example — Rectangle with an Off-Centre Circular Hole

**Problem.** A $200 \text{ mm} \times 200 \text{ mm}$ square plate has a circular hole of radius $R = 30 \text{ mm}$ whose centre is at $(x, y) = (130, 100)$ measured from the bottom-left corner of the plate. Find $(\bar{x}, \bar{y})$ of the remaining area.

### Step 1 — Data Tabulation

| Part | Description | Area $A_i$ (mm²) | $x_i$ (mm) | $y_i$ (mm) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Full square | $200 \times 200 = 40000$ | $100$ | $100$ |
| 2 | Circular hole | $-\pi (30)^{2} = -2827.43$ | $130$ | $100$ |
| — | **Net** | $\mathbf{37172.57}$ | — | — |

### Step 2 — Compute Moments

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum A_i x_i &= (40000)(100) + (-2827.43)(130) \\
&= 4000000 - 367566.40 = 3632433.60 \text{ mm}^{3}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum A_i y_i &= (40000)(100) + (-2827.43)(100) \\
&= 4000000 - 282743.40 = 3717256.60 \text{ mm}^{3}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Centroid

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{x} &= \frac{3632433.60}{37172.57} = 97.71 \text{ mm} \\[6pt]
\bar{y} &= \frac{3717256.60}{37172.57} = 100.00 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> Notice that $\bar{x} < 100$ mm even though the hole is on the **right** side. The reason: the centroid of the *remaining* (full minus hole) shape is the **complement** — material on the *left* of the hole now has more weight, pulling $\bar{x}$ leftward.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Procedure Flow — How to Solve a Composite-Area Centroid Problem

The Mermaid block below maps out the exact six-step procedure that a KTU examiner expects a student to follow on the answer script.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA0([START PROCEDURE]) --> nodeB1[STEP 1: Draw the figure to scale with all dimensions]
    nodeB1 --> nodeB2[STEP 2: Choose a convenient reference origin OXY]
    nodeB2 --> nodeB3[STEP 3: Decompose the figure into standard primitive shapes]
    nodeB3 --> nodeB4[STEP 4: Compute area Ai of each component]
    nodeB4 --> nodeB5[STEP 5: Locate centroid coordinates xi and yi of each part from O]
    nodeB5 --> nodeB6[STEP 6: Construct a tabular summary of Ai xi yi Ai xi and Ai yi]
    nodeB6 --> nodeC7[STEP 7: Sum each column to get total area and total moments]
    nodeC7 --> nodeD8[STEP 8: Apply the formula x bar equals sum Ai xi divided by sum Ai]
    nodeD8 --> nodeE9[STEP 9: Apply the formula y bar equals sum Ai yi divided by sum Ai]
    nodeE9 --> nodeF10[STEP 10: Mark the centroid clearly on the original sketch]
    nodeF10 --> nodeG11([END OF SOLUTION])
```

---

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Method of Composite Areas

This block diagram captures the **information flow** between the three sub-systems used in the composite-centroid calculation.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeM1[Module A: Decomposition Engine] -- outputs Ai and xi yi --> nodeM2[Module B: Moment Aggregator]
    nodeM2 -- computes sum of Ai, sum of Ai xi, sum of Ai yi --> nodeM3[Module C: Centroid Computer]
    nodeM3 -- applies bar x and bar y formulas --> nodeM4[Output: Centroid coordinates and mark on diagram]
    nodeHole[Sub-module: Negative-Area Handler] -- injects negative Ai for cut-outs --> nodeM2
    nodeHole -. modifies .-> nodeM1
```

---

## 4.3 Schematic Representation of the T-Section Worked Example

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph TShape["T-SECTION GEOMETRY"]
        nodeFlange["FLANGE: 200 mm wide x 20 mm thick - Centroid at x equals 100 mm and y equals 190 mm"]
        nodeWeb["WEB: 20 mm wide x 180 mm tall - Centroid at x equals 10 mm and y equals 90 mm"]
    end
    nodeFlange --- nodeWeb
    nodeOrigin["Reference Origin O at bottom-left of web"] -. axes .-> nodeWeb
    nodeCentroid["Computed Centroid: x bar equals 57.37 mm, y bar equals 142.63 mm"] -. reported .-> TShape
```

---

## 4.4 Decision Diagram — When to Use Negative Areas

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeS1[Inspect the composite figure] --> nodeQ1{Is there a hole or cut-out?}
    nodeQ1 -- No --> nodeN1[Use all areas as positive]
    nodeQ1 -- Yes --> nodeN2[Enter the hole area with a NEGATIVE sign in the table]
    nodeN1 --> nodeN3[Apply standard composite formula]
    nodeN2 --> nodeN3
    nodeN3 --> nodeN4[Compute final centroid and mark on diagram]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Question Paper]**

**Q1.** Define the *centroid* of a plane area. How is it different from the *centre of gravity* of a body?

> **Model Answer:**
> The **centroid** of a plane area is the point where the *entire area* of the figure is assumed to be concentrated for the purpose of computing the first moment of area. Mathematically, it is the point $(\bar{x}, \bar{y})$ such that $\bar{x} = \dfrac{\sum A_i x_i}{\sum A_i}$ and $\bar{y} = \dfrac{\sum A_i y_i}{\sum A_i}$.
>
> The **centre of gravity** is the point where the *entire weight* of the body is assumed to act. For a body of *uniform density* and *constant thickness*, the centroid and centre of gravity **coincide**. For a non-uniform body, the centre of gravity is *shifted* toward the denser region, while the centroid remains purely a *geometric* property. **(3 marks)**

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Q2.** State, with the formula, the location of the centroid of (i) a semicircle of radius $R$ from its diameter, and (ii) a triangle of base $b$ and height $h$ from its base.

> **Model Answer:**
> (i) The centroid of a semicircle of radius $R$ lies on its axis of symmetry, at a perpendicular distance of $\dfrac{4R}{3\pi} \approx 0.4244\,R$ from the flat (diameter) edge. **(1.5 marks)**
>
> (ii) The centroid of a triangle of base $b$ and height $h$ lies on its median, at a distance of $\dfrac{b}{3}$ along the base from any vertex and at a perpendicular distance of $\dfrac{h}{3}$ from the base. **(1.5 marks)**

---

## 5.2 PART B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice Provided)

> **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — June 2024]*

A T-section is made of a horizontal flange $150 \text{ mm} \times 20 \text{ mm}$ and a vertical web $20 \text{ mm} \times 160 \text{ mm}$ (web below flange). Determine the centroid $(\bar{x}, \bar{y})$ of the section measured from the bottom-left corner of the web. Locate the centroid on a neat sketch. **(14 marks)**

### (a) Decomposition, Area Calculation and Centroid Identification — **7 marks**

The T-section is split into **two rectangles**:

- **Rectangle 1 — Web:** Width $b_1 = 20$ mm, Height $h_1 = 160$ mm.
  - Area: $A_1 = 20 \times 160 = 3200 \text{ mm}^{2}$.
  - Centroid: $x_1 = \tfrac{20}{2} = 10$ mm; $y_1 = \tfrac{160}{2} = 80$ mm.

- **Rectangle 2 — Flange:** Width $b_2 = 150$ mm, Height $h_2 = 20$ mm.
  - Area: $A_2 = 150 \times 20 = 3000 \text{ mm}^{2}$.
  - The flange sits on top of the web, centred. Therefore:
  - $x_2 = \tfrac{150}{2} = 75$ mm.
  - $y_2 = 160 + \tfrac{20}{2} = 170$ mm.

### (b) Application of Composite Centroid Formula — **7 marks**

Data table:

| Part | $A_i$ (mm²) | $x_i$ (mm) | $y_i$ (mm) | $A_i x_i$ (mm³) | $A_i y_i$ (mm³) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 (Web) | 3200 | 10 | 80 | 32000 | 256000 |
| 2 (Flange) | 3000 | 75 | 170 | 225000 | 510000 |
| **Sum** | **6200** | — | — | **257000** | **766000** |

Applying the formula:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{x} &= \frac{\sum A_i x_i}{\sum A_i} = \frac{257000}{6200} = 41.45 \text{ mm} \\[6pt]
\bar{y} &= \frac{\sum A_i y_i}{\sum A_i} = \frac{766000}{6200} = 123.55 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Final Answer:** $\bar{x} = 41.45$ mm, $\bar{y} = 123.55$ mm. The centroid lies at the intersection of vertical and horizontal lines drawn at these distances from the origin O. **(2 marks for final answer and sketch)**

---

> **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]*

A rectangular plate of size $240 \text{ mm} \times 180 \text{ mm}$ has a circular hole of radius $30 \text{ mm}$ drilled at a point whose centre is at $(x, y) = (90, 90)$ measured from the bottom-left corner of the plate. Locate the centroid of the plate with the hole. **(14 marks)**

### (a) Decomposition and Identification of Areas (Positive Plate and Negative Hole) — **7 marks**

The composite area is the **plate minus the hole**:

- **Part 1 — Outer Rectangle:**
  - Dimensions: $240 \text{ mm} \times 180 \text{ mm}$.
  - Area: $A_1 = 240 \times 180 = 43200 \text{ mm}^{2}$.
  - Centroid: $x_1 = 120$ mm; $y_1 = 90$ mm.

- **Part 2 — Circular Hole (negative):**
  - Radius $R = 30$ mm.
  - Area: $A_2 = -\pi R^{2} = -\pi (30)^{2} = -2827.43 \text{ mm}^{2}$.
  - Centroid: $x_2 = 90$ mm; $y_2 = 90$ mm.

### (b) Application of Formula and Final Centroid — **7 marks**

| Part | $A_i$ (mm²) | $x_i$ (mm) | $y_i$ (mm) | $A_i x_i$ (mm³) | $A_i y_i$ (mm³) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 (Plate) | 43200 | 120 | 90 | 5184000 | 3888000 |
| 2 (Hole) | -2827.43 | 90 | 90 | -254469 | -254469 |
| **Sum** | **40372.57** | — | — | **4929531** | **3633531** |

Applying the formula:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{x} &= \frac{4929531}{40372.57} = 122.10 \text{ mm} \\[6pt]
\bar{y} &= \frac{3633531}{40372.57} = 90.00 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Final Answer:** $\bar{x} = 122.10$ mm, $\bar{y} = 90.00$ mm.
> The centroid shifts to the *right* of the plate's geometric centre (120 mm) because the hole, located on the left half, removes material on the left. **(2 marks for final answer and sketch)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Most Common Mark-Deduction Triggers for Centroid-of-Composite-Area Problems**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the negative sign for holes** — Examiners deduct **2 to 3 marks** outright if you enter the hole area as positive. The sign is the *single most important* step.
> 2. **Wrong centroid for the triangle** — Students frequently place the triangle's centroid at $h/2$ instead of $h/3$ from the base. This silently corrupts the final answer. Loss: **2 marks**.
> 3. **Wrong $y_i$ for stacked rectangles** — When rectangle B sits on top of rectangle A, $y_B$ is *not* its own height/2 — it is $h_A + h_B/2$. This is a classic KTU trap.
> 4. **Unit mismatch** — Mixing mm and m, or writing $\bar{x}$ in $\text{mm}^{3}$ instead of mm. Always check that moments ($\text{mm}^{3}$) divided by area ($\text{mm}^{2}$) give length (mm).
> 5. **Missing the sketch and centroid marker** — The KTU 2024 scheme explicitly awards **2 marks** for the sketch and clearly marking $(\bar{x}, \bar{y})$. Skipping it costs you those marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** The centroid is the area-weighted average of the centroids of the component shapes.
- **Core Formulae:**
  - $\bar{x} = \dfrac{\sum A_i x_i}{\sum A_i}$ and $\bar{y} = \dfrac{\sum A_i y_i}{\sum A_i}$.
- **Sign Convention:** Subtracted regions (holes) carry a **negative** $A_i$ — the $(x_i, y_i)$ remain the centre of the cut-out.
- **Reference Origin:** Always place it at a *corner* or *axis of symmetry* for ease of computation.
- **Stacked Rectangles:** When one part sits on top of another, the upper part's $y_i$ = (height of lower part) + (half-height of upper part).
- **Symmetry Shortcut:** If a figure is symmetric about the $x$-axis, then $\bar{y} = 0$; if symmetric about the $y$-axis, then $\bar{x} = 0$. Exploit this to halve the calculation work.
- **Standard Centroids to Memorise:**
  - Rectangle: $\tfrac{b}{2}, \tfrac{h}{2}$
  - Triangle: $\tfrac{b}{3}, \tfrac{h}{3}$ from the base
  - Semicircle: $\tfrac{4R}{3\pi}$ from the diameter
  - Quarter-circle: $\tfrac{4R}{3\pi}$ on both axes from the corner
- **Engineering Relevance:** Centroid of composite areas is the entry point for **shear-stress distribution in beams** ($Q = A\bar{y}$), **moment of inertia calculations** (using the parallel-axis theorem, $I = I_G + A d^{2}$), and **stability analysis** of floating vessels.
- **Common Mistake to Avoid:** The triangle centroid is at $\tfrac{h}{3}$ from the base, **not** $\tfrac{h}{2}$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Moment of Inertia - Parallel Axis and Perpendicular Axis Theorems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GBEST213 - ENGINEERING MECHANICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Moment of Inertia - Parallel Axis and Perpendicular Axis Theorems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of Mass Moment of Inertia

The **Mass Moment of Inertia** (often simply called *Moment of Inertia*, abbreviated as **MOI**) of a rigid body about a given axis is defined as the sum of the products of the mass of each elementary particle of the body and the square of its perpendicular distance from the axis of rotation.

Mathematically, for a system of discrete particles:

$$I \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i \, r_i^{2}$$

For a continuous rigid body of mass $M$ and elemental mass $dm$ located at perpendicular distance $r$ from the reference axis:

$$I \;=\; \int_{V} r^{2} \, dm \quad \text{(SI unit: kg·m}^{2}\text{)}$$

Where:
- $I$ = Mass Moment of Inertia about the axis (in $\text{kg·m}^{2}$)
- $m_i$ = Mass of the $i^{\text{th}}$ particle (in $\text{kg}$)
- $r_i$ = Perpendicular distance of the $i^{\text{th}}$ particle from the axis (in $\text{m}$)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Distinction:**
> Students must clearly distinguish between **Mass Moment of Inertia** ($I = \int r^{2}\, dm$, units $\text{kg·m}^{2}$) used in rigid body dynamics, and **Area Moment of Inertia** ($I = \int y^{2}\, dA$, units $\text{m}^{4}$) used in strength of materials/bending of beams. Module 2 of *Engineering Mechanics (GBEST213)* focuses exclusively on **Mass Moment of Inertia**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine pushing a small **bicycle wheel** and a heavy **flywheel** of the same diameter with the same force to set them spinning. The flywheel is dramatically harder to start spinning, but once it does spin, it also resists stopping. This "resistance to change in rotational motion" is quantified by the **Moment of Inertia**.

**Real-world analogy:** Think of Mass Moment of Inertia as the **"rotational mass"** of a body:
- Just as a heavy object is harder to push in a straight line (resistance to translation = mass $m$),
- A heavy object with mass distributed far from the rotation axis is harder to spin (resistance to rotation = MOI $I$).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** It is not just the *amount* of mass that matters, but **where** that mass is located relative to the axis of rotation. A figure skater spinning with arms extended has a much larger MOI than when the arms are pulled in — this is why pulling arms in causes faster spinning (angular momentum conservation).

### 1.3 Radius of Gyration

A closely related concept is the **Radius of Gyration ($k$)** — the distance from the axis at which, if the *entire* mass $M$ of the body were concentrated at a single point, it would produce the same moment of inertia as the actual distributed mass.

$$I \;=\; M k^{2} \quad \Rightarrow \quad k \;=\; \sqrt{\dfrac{I}{M}}$$

Units: meter ($\text{m}$). The radius of gyration gives engineers an intuitive measure of how "spread out" the mass is about an axis.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Two Fundamental Theorems

Theorems of Moment of Inertia allow us to compute the MOI of complex bodies or about arbitrary axes by relating them to known, simpler cases (such as the MOI about the centroidal axis).

#### 2.1.1 Parallel Axis Theorem (Theorem of Parallel Axes)

**Statement:** The moment of inertia of a rigid body about any axis is equal to the moment of inertia of the body about a **parallel axis passing through the centre of mass (centroid)** plus the product of the mass of the body and the square of the perpendicular distance between the two parallel axes.

$$I_{AB} \;=\; I_{G} \;+\; M d^{2}$$

Where:
- $I_{AB}$ = MOI about any axis $AB$ (kg·m²)
- $I_G$ = MOI about the parallel centroidal axis through $G$ (kg·m²)
- $M$ = Total mass of the rigid body (kg)
- $d$ = Perpendicular distance between the two parallel axes (m)

**Why this works:** The $Md^2$ term is essentially the MOI of a *point mass* $M$ located at the centroid (using the same definition $I = Mr^2$). The theorem says: *the rotational inertia about any axis = rotational inertia about a parallel axis through the mass-centre + the inertia the body would have if its entire mass were concentrated at the centroid*.

#### 2.1.2 Perpendicular Axis Theorem (Theorem of Perpendicular Axes)

**Statement:** The moment of inertia of a **planar lamina** (a flat, two-dimensional body) about an axis perpendicular to its plane is equal to the sum of the moments of inertia of the lamina about any two mutually perpendicular axes lying in the plane of the lamina and intersecting at the point where the perpendicular axis passes through the lamina.

$$I_{z} \;=\; I_{x} \;+\; I_{y}$$

Where $x$ and $y$ are two perpendicular axes in the plane of the lamina, and $z$ is the axis perpendicular to the plane, all three intersecting at a common point $O$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** The Perpendicular Axis Theorem is **valid ONLY for planar (2-D) laminas** where thickness is negligible. It is **NOT** applicable to 3-D solid bodies. Many students lose marks by blindly applying it to spheres, cubes, or other solids.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Theorem / Formula | Expression | Applicability & Units |
|---|-------------------|------------|------------------------|
| 1 | Definition (continuous) | $I = \int r^{2}\, dm$ | Any rigid body, kg·m² |
| 2 | Definition (discrete) | $I = \sum m_i r_i^{2}$ | System of point masses, kg·m² |
| 3 | Radius of Gyration | $k = \sqrt{I / M}$ | Any axis, m |
| 4 | **Parallel Axis Theorem** | $I = I_G + M d^{2}$ | Any rigid body; 2 axes parallel, kg·m² |
| 5 | **Perpendicular Axis Theorem** | $I_z = I_x + I_y$ | **Planar lamina ONLY**, kg·m² |
| 6 | Thin Rod (about end) | $I = \dfrac{M L^{2}}{3}$ | Length $L$, axis through one end, perpendicular, kg·m² |
| 7 | Thin Rod (about centre) | $I_G = \dfrac{M L^{2}}{12}$ | Length $L$, axis through centre, perpendicular, kg·m² |
| 8 | Rectangular Lamina (about centroidal axis $\parallel$ to side $b$) | $I_G = \dfrac{M b^{2}}{12}$ | Width $b$ along the axis, kg·m² |
| 9 | Circular Disc (about diameter) | $I_d = \dfrac{M R^{2}}{4}$ | Radius $R$, kg·m² |
| 10 | Circular Disc (about central axis $\perp$ to plane) | $I = \dfrac{M R^{2}}{2}$ | Using Perpendicular Axis Theorem, kg·m² |
| 11 | Solid Sphere (about diameter) | $I = \dfrac{2}{5} M R^{2}$ | Radius $R$, kg·m² |
| 12 | Solid Cylinder (about its own axis) | $I = \dfrac{M R^{2}}{2}$ | Radius $R$, axis along geometric centre, kg·m² |

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Vehicle Drivetrain Design:** Flywheels store rotational kinetic energy $\tfrac{1}{2}I\omega^{2}$; a higher $I$ means smoother torque delivery. Engineers use the Parallel Axis Theorem to compute the MOI of an offset-mounted crank or cam.
- **Rotating Machinery (Turbines, Pumps, Compressors):** Critical whirling speed and balancing calculations all hinge on accurately computing MOI of blades and shafts about arbitrary axes.
- **Structural Engineering (related Area MOI):** Determines resistance of beams to bending.
- **Aerospace & Robotics:** Control moments of inertia directly determine agility and stability of satellites, robot arms, and quadcopters.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 2 Focus:** The questions in this module predominantly test the student's ability to (i) state the two theorems, (ii) derive them, and (iii) **apply** them — in combination with the standard MOI values of basic shapes — to compute the MOI of composite or offset bodies. Memorize the *centroidal* MOI of standard shapes and then use Parallel Axis Theorem as a "shift tool."
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Parallel Axis Theorem

**Setup:** Consider a rigid body of total mass $M$ with centre of mass at point $G$. Let:
- $AB$ be any arbitrary axis.
- $CD$ be a parallel axis passing through the centre of mass $G$.
- $d$ = perpendicular distance between the two axes.

Set up a coordinate system with origin at $G$ such that the axis $CD$ coincides with the $z$-axis and the axis $AB$ lies in the $x$-$z$ plane at a distance $d$ along the $x$-direction. So an arbitrary elemental mass $dm$ located at $P$ has coordinates $(x, y, z)$ relative to $G$, and coordinates $(x+d, y, z)$ relative to $O$ (the origin lying on axis $AB$).

**Step 1 — MOI about axis $CD$ (centroidal axis, the $z$-axis through $G$):**

By definition, the MOI about the $z$-axis through $G$ uses the perpendicular distance of $dm$ from the $z$-axis, which is $\sqrt{x^{2} + y^{2}}$:

$$I_{CD} \;=\; \int_{V} (x^{2} + y^{2})\, dm \;=\; I_{G}$$

**Step 2 — MOI about axis $AB$ (parallel axis at distance $d$):**

The perpendicular distance of the same element $dm$ from axis $AB$ is $\sqrt{(x+d)^{2} + y^{2}}$. So:

$$I_{AB} \;=\; \int_{V} \left[(x+d)^{2} + y^{2}\right] dm$$

**Step 3 — Expand the integrand:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{AB} \;=\; \int_{V} \left[(x^{2} + 2xd + d^{2}) + y^{2}\right] dm
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Split the integral into three terms:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{AB} \;=\; \underbrace{\int_{V} (x^{2} + y^{2})\, dm}_{\text{Term 1}} \;+\; \underbrace{2d \int_{V} x\, dm}_{\text{Term 2}} \;+\; \underbrace{d^{2} \int_{V} dm}_{\text{Term 3}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate each term:**

- **Term 1:** $\displaystyle\int_{V} (x^{2} + y^{2})\, dm = I_{G}$ (the centroidal MOI by definition).
- **Term 2:** $\displaystyle 2d \int_{V} x\, dm$. The integral $\int_{V} x\, dm$ is the first moment of mass about the $y$-$z$ plane, which equals $M \cdot \bar{x}$, where $\bar{x}$ is the $x$-coordinate of the centre of mass. Since $G$ is the origin, $\bar{x} = 0$. Hence Term 2 = $0$.
- **Term 3:** $\displaystyle d^{2} \int_{V} dm = d^{2} M$ (since the integral of $dm$ over the entire body equals the total mass $M$).

**Step 6 — Combine the results:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{AB} \;&=\; I_{G} \;+\; 0 \;+\; M d^{2} \\[4pt]
\boxed{\,I_{AB} \;&=\; I_{G} \;+\; M d^{2}\,}
\end{aligned}
$$

This completes the derivation of the **Parallel Axis Theorem**. $\blacksquare$

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the Perpendicular Axis Theorem

**Setup:** Consider a **planar lamina** (a thin, flat plate) lying in the $x$-$y$ plane. Let $O$ be a point in the plane of the lamina. Let:
- $OX$ and $OY$ be two mutually perpendicular axes lying *in* the plane of the lamina, intersecting at $O$.
- $OZ$ be an axis perpendicular to the plane of the lamina, also passing through $O$.

Let $dm$ be an elemental mass at point $P$ having coordinates $(x, y, 0)$ relative to $O$.

**Step 1 — MOI of the element about the $x$-axis:**

The perpendicular distance of $P$ from the $x$-axis is $\vert y \vert$. So:

$$dI_{x} \;=\; y^{2}\, dm$$

**Step 2 — MOI of the element about the $y$-axis:**

The perpendicular distance of $P$ from the $y$-axis is $\vert x \vert$. So:

$$dI_{y} \;=\; x^{2}\, dm$$

**Step 3 — MOI of the element about the $z$-axis:**

The perpendicular distance of $P$ from the $z$-axis is $\sqrt{x^{2} + y^{2}}$. So:

$$dI_{z} \;=\; (x^{2} + y^{2})\, dm$$

**Step 4 — Sum over the entire lamina (integrate):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{z} \;&=\; \int_{A} (x^{2} + y^{2})\, dm \\[4pt]
\;&=\; \int_{A} x^{2}\, dm \;+\; \int_{A} y^{2}\, dm
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Recognize each term:**

- $\displaystyle \int_{A} x^{2}\, dm = I_{y}$ (the MOI about the $y$-axis, by definition).
- $\displaystyle \int_{A} y^{2}\, dm = I_{x}$ (the MOI about the $x$-axis, by definition).

**Step 6 — Combine:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\boxed{\,I_{z} \;&=\; I_{x} \;+\; I_{y}\,}
\end{aligned}
$$

This completes the derivation of the **Perpendicular Axis Theorem**. $\blacksquare$

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical observation:** In the derivation, the only coordinate of $P$ with a non-zero value is in the $x$-$y$ plane (i.e. $z = 0$). If the body were 3-D (extending out of the plane), the perpendicular distance from $dm$ to the $z$-axis would *not* be $\sqrt{x^{2} + y^{2}}$ in general — confirming once more that this theorem is **strictly for planar laminas**.

---

### 3.3 Worked-Out Analytical Example (Application of Both Theorems)

**Problem:** A uniform circular disc of mass $M$ and radius $R$ has a small circular hole of radius $r$ cut out at a distance $d$ from the centre of the disc (with $d + r \leq R$). Find the moment of inertia of the resulting body about:
1. The central axis perpendicular to the disc (through the original centre).
2. A tangent to the outer edge of the disc (lying in its plane).

**Solution Strategy:** Use the **principle of superposition** — treat the body as a *full* disc minus a *removed* disc, then apply the parallel axis theorem for the off-centre hole.

**Step 1 — Standard MOI of a full disc of radius $R$ about its central axis:**

$$I_{\text{full, central}} \;=\; \dfrac{M R^{2}}{2}$$

**Step 2 — Mass of the removed (hole) disc:**

The disc is uniform, so mass is proportional to area. If $\sigma$ is the mass per unit area:

$$m_{\text{hole}} \;=\; \sigma \cdot \pi r^{2} \;=\; M \cdot \dfrac{r^{2}}{R^{2}}$$

**Step 3 — MOI of the hole-disc about the central axis of the original disc:**

The hole's centre is at distance $d$ from the original centre. Apply the Parallel Axis Theorem to the hole disc *in reverse*: first find its centroidal MOI, then shift.

$$I_{\text{hole, central}} \;=\; I_{G,\text{hole}} + m_{\text{hole}}\, d^{2} \;=\; \dfrac{m_{\text{hole}} r^{2}}{2} + m_{\text{hole}}\, d^{2}$$

Substituting $m_{\text{hole}} = M r^{2} / R^{2}$:

$$I_{\text{hole, central}} \;=\; \dfrac{M r^{2}}{R^{2}} \left(\dfrac{r^{2}}{2} + d^{2}\right)$$

**Step 4 — MOI of the body about the central axis (Part 1):**

$$I_{\text{body, central}} \;=\; I_{\text{full, central}} - I_{\text{hole, central}} \;=\; \dfrac{M R^{2}}{2} - \dfrac{M r^{2}}{R^{2}} \left(\dfrac{r^{2}}{2} + d^{2}\right)$$

**Step 5 — Part 2: MOI about a tangent (lying in the plane of the disc):**

We use the Perpendicular Axis Theorem on the *body*:

$$I_{\text{body, central, }\perp} \;=\; I_{x} + I_{y}$$

By symmetry of the body about any diameter through the original centre, $I_x = I_y$. So:

$$I_{x} \;=\; I_{y} \;=\; \dfrac{1}{2} I_{\text{body, central, }\perp}$$

For a tangent (in the plane) the relevant axis is a *diameter line in the plane*. The distance from the original centre to the tangent line is $R$. Apply the Parallel Axis Theorem to go from the diameter (centroidal axis) to the tangent:

$$I_{\text{tangent}} \;=\; I_{\text{body, diameter}} + M_{\text{body}}\, R^{2}$$

where $I_{\text{body, diameter}} = I_x = I_y = \tfrac{1}{2} I_{\text{body, central, }\perp}$ and $M_{\text{body}} = M - m_{\text{hole}} = M(1 - r^{2}/R^{2})$.

Hence:

$$I_{\text{tangent}} \;=\; \dfrac{1}{2}\!\left[\dfrac{M R^{2}}{2} - \dfrac{M r^{2}}{R^{2}}\!\left(\dfrac{r^{2}}{2}+d^{2}\right)\right] + M\!\left(1 - \dfrac{r^{2}}{R^{2}}\right)\!R^{2}$$

This kind of composite-body calculation is the **single most-tested** KTU question type in Module 2.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

The following Mermaid block diagrams are designed for KTU-premium study notes. They use **clean alphanumeric node identifiers** (prefixed with letters) and **plain uppercase alphanumeric labels** (no markdown inside quoted labels) to ensure successful rendering across all Mermaid versions.

### 4.1 Architecture of the Two Theorems

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Mass Moment of Inertia I] --> B[Definition]
    A --> C[Radius of Gyration k]
    A --> D[THEOREMS]
    A --> E[Standard Shapes]

    B --> B1[I equals integral of r squared dm]
    C --> C1[k equals sqrt of I over M]

    D --> D1[PARALLEL AXIS THEOREM]
    D --> D2[PERPENDICULAR AXIS THEOREM]

    D1 --> D1a[Valid for any 3D rigid body]
    D1 --> D1b[Equation I equals IG plus M d squared]
    D1 --> D1c[Two parallel axes separated by distance d]

    D2 --> D2a[Valid ONLY for planar laminas]
    D2 --> D2b[Equation Iz equals Ix plus Iy]
    D2 --> D2c[Two in plane axes and one perpendicular axis meeting at one point]

    E --> E1[Rod M L squared over 12 centroidal]
    E --> E2[Disc M R squared over 4 about diameter]
    E --> E3[Disc M R squared over 2 about central axis]
    E --> E4[Sphere 2 over 5 M R squared about diameter]
```

### 4.2 Geometric Setup for Parallel Axis Theorem

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Arbitrary Axis AB] --- P2[Parallel Centroidal Axis CD through G]
    P2 -. distance d .-> P1
    P3[Elemental mass dm at point P] --- P2
    P3 --- P1
    P4[Term 1 IG centroidal MOI] --> P5[Final IAB equals IG plus M d squared]
    P6[Term 2 zero because x bar of G is zero] --> P5
    P7[Term 3 M d squared using total mass M] --> P5
```

### 4.3 Geometric Setup for Perpendicular Axis Theorem

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1[Planar Lamina in x y plane] --> Q2[Axis OX in plane]
    Q1 --> Q3[Axis OY in plane]
    Q1 --> Q4[Axis OZ perpendicular to plane]
    Q2 --> Q5[Perpendicular distances: y from x axis, x from y axis]
    Q3 --> Q5
    Q4 --> Q5
    Q5 --> Q6[Iz equals Ix plus Iy]
    Q7[Elemental mass dm at x y 0] --> Q6
```

### 4.4 Problem-Solving Decision Flowchart (KTU Exam Strategy)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Given a Moment of Inertia Problem] --> S1{Is the body a thin planar lamina?}
    S1 -- YES --> S2[Apply PERPENDICULAR AXIS THEOREM if needed]
    S1 -- NO --> S3[Go to Parallel Axis check]
    S2 --> S4[Apply PARALLEL AXIS THEOREM for shift]
    S3 --> S4
    S4 --> S5{Is it a composite body?}
    S5 -- YES --> S6[Use SUPERPOSITION add subtract parts]
    S5 -- NO --> S7[Use STANDARD SHAPE MOI table]
    S6 --> S8[Compute final I in SI units kg m squared]
    S7 --> S8
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Question Paper]**
*State and prove the **Parallel Axis Theorem** for a rigid body.*

**Model Answer (3-mark key):**

*Statement (1 mark):* The moment of inertia of a rigid body about any axis is equal to the moment of inertia of the body about a parallel axis passing through the centre of mass, plus the product of the mass of the body and the square of the perpendicular distance between the two parallel axes.

*Mathematical form (1 mark):* $I_{AB} = I_G + M d^{2}$

*Proof (1 mark):* Setting up coordinates with the centroidal axis as the $z$-axis and the second parallel axis at perpendicular distance $d$ along the $x$-axis, the MOI about the second axis is $\int [(x+d)^{2} + y^{2}]\,dm$. Expanding and applying the definition of centre of mass ($\int x\,dm = 0$) yields $I_{AB} = I_G + Md^{2}$.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*State the **Perpendicular Axis Theorem**. Is it applicable to a solid cylinder? Justify your answer.*

**Model Answer (3-mark key):**

*Statement (1.5 marks):* The moment of inertia of a planar lamina about an axis perpendicular to its plane equals the sum of its moments of inertia about any two mutually perpendicular axes in its plane, all three intersecting at a common point: $I_z = I_x + I_y$.

*Applicability (1.5 marks):* The theorem is **applicable only to planar (two-dimensional) laminas**, where the entire mass of the body lies in the $x$-$y$ plane. A solid cylinder is a *three-dimensional* body, not a lamina, so the theorem is **not directly applicable** to it. (For a cylinder of finite thickness, the theorem is invalid and would yield incorrect results.)

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part-B question carries **14 marks** and typically contains **two sub-parts of 7 marks each**. The student is offered an internal choice between two full 14-mark alternatives.

---

#### 🔹 QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Composite Body + Parallel Axis Theorem

**[KTU University Exam — July 2024, Adapated Model]**

A uniform solid disc of mass **$M = 12 \text{ kg}$** and radius **$R = 0.3 \text{ m}$** has a circular hole of radius **$r = 0.1 \text{ m}$** cut from it. The centre of the hole is at a distance **$d = 0.2 \text{ m}$** from the centre of the disc.

**(a)** Derive an expression for the moment of inertia of the resulting body about an axis through the centre of the original disc, perpendicular to its plane. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Hence, or otherwise, find the moment of inertia of the body about a **tangent** drawn on the outer edge of the disc. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

Apply superposition: body = full disc − hole disc.

*Step 1 (1 mark):* MOI of the full disc about its central axis:
$$I_{\text{full}} = \dfrac{M R^{2}}{2} = \dfrac{12 \times (0.3)^{2}}{2} = 0.54 \text{ kg·m}^{2}$$

*Step 2 (1 mark):* Mass of the hole (proportional to area, since uniform):
$$m_{h} = M \cdot \dfrac{\pi r^{2}}{\pi R^{2}} = M \cdot \dfrac{r^{2}}{R^{2}} = 12 \cdot \dfrac{0.01}{0.09} = \dfrac{4}{3} \text{ kg}$$

*Step 3 (2 marks):* MOI of the hole disc about the *original* central axis (parallel-axis shift by $d$):
$$I_{\text{hole, central}} = \dfrac{m_{h} r^{2}}{2} + m_{h} d^{2} = \dfrac{(4/3)(0.1)^{2}}{2} + \dfrac{4}{3} (0.2)^{2}$$
$$= \dfrac{4}{3} \times \dfrac{0.01}{2} + \dfrac{4}{3} \times 0.04 = 0.00\overline{6} + 0.053\overline{3} = 0.06 \text{ kg·m}^{2}$$

*Step 4 (2 marks):* MOI of the body about the original central axis:
$$I_{\text{body, central}} = I_{\text{full}} - I_{\text{hole, central}} = 0.54 - 0.06 = 0.48 \text{ kg·m}^{2}$$

*Step 5 (1 mark):* Final boxed answer:
$$\boxed{I_{\text{body, central}} = 0.48 \text{ kg·m}^{2}}$$

---

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

We need the MOI about a tangent lying in the plane of the disc (touching the outer edge).

*Step 1 (2 marks):* The tangent in the plane is parallel to a *diameter* of the disc. By the symmetry of the body about this diameter, the moment of inertia about the diameter is half the moment of inertia about the perpendicular central axis (using the Perpendicular Axis Theorem on the planar body):
$$I_{d} = \dfrac{1}{2} I_{\text{body, central}} = \dfrac{0.48}{2} = 0.24 \text{ kg·m}^{2}$$

*Step 2 (1 mark):* The tangent is parallel to this diameter but displaced by a distance equal to the outer radius $R = 0.3 \text{ m}$. Apply the Parallel Axis Theorem:
$$I_{\text{tangent}} = I_{d} + M_{\text{body}} R^{2}$$

*Step 3 (1 mark):* Mass of the body (full disc minus hole):
$$M_{\text{body}} = M - m_{h} = 12 - \dfrac{4}{3} = \dfrac{32}{3} \text{ kg}$$

*Step 4 (2 marks):* Substitute:
$$I_{\text{tangent}} = 0.24 + \dfrac{32}{3} \times (0.3)^{2} = 0.24 + \dfrac{32}{3} \times 0.09 = 0.24 + 0.96 = 1.20 \text{ kg·m}^{2}$$

*Step 5 (1 mark):* Final boxed answer:
$$\boxed{I_{\text{tangent}} = 1.20 \text{ kg·m}^{2}}$$

---

#### 🔹 QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Perpendicular Axis Theorem + Standard Shapes

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Adapted]**

**(a)** State and prove the **Perpendicular Axis Theorem**. Hence obtain the moment of inertia of a **uniform circular disc of mass $M$ and radius $R$** about an axis passing through its centre and lying in the plane of the disc. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A thin uniform rectangular plate of mass **$M$** has dimensions **$L \times B$** (length $\times$ breadth). Using the Perpendicular Axis Theorem and the standard formula for the moment of inertia of a thin rod, find the moment of inertia of the plate about a **diagonal** of the rectangle. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

*Statement (1 mark):* For a planar lamina, $I_z = I_x + I_y$ where the three axes meet at one point and the $z$-axis is perpendicular to the plane of the lamina.

*Proof (3 marks):* (See the full derivation in Section 3.2 above — awarding 3 marks for the integral setup, expansion, and identification of the two terms $I_x$ and $I_y$.)

*Application to a disc (3 marks):* By symmetry of the circular disc, $I_x = I_y$. About the central axis perpendicular to the plane, the standard result is $I_z = MR^{2}/2$. Therefore:
$$\dfrac{M R^{2}}{2} \;=\; I_x + I_y \;=\; 2 I_d \quad\Rightarrow\quad I_d \;=\; \dfrac{M R^{2}}{4}$$

where $I_d$ is the MOI about any diameter. Final boxed answer:
$$\boxed{I_{d} = \dfrac{M R^{2}}{4}}$$

---

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

*Step 1 (1 mark):* Standard MOI of a uniform thin rod of length $L$ about an axis through its centre and perpendicular to it: $I_{rod} = ML^{2}/12$. For a rectangular plate of width $B$ (negligible thickness), considering the entire breadth as the "rod's mass distribution," the MOI of the plate about the central axis parallel to the breadth and through the centre is:
$$I_x \;=\; \dfrac{M B^{2}}{12}$$

*Step 2 (1 mark):* Similarly, about the central axis parallel to the length:
$$I_y \;=\; \dfrac{M L^{2}}{12}$$

*Step 3 (1 mark):* By the Perpendicular Axis Theorem (the plate is a planar lamina):
$$I_z \;=\; I_x + I_y \;=\; \dfrac{M B^{2}}{12} + \dfrac{M L^{2}}{12} \;=\; \dfrac{M (L^{2} + B^{2})}{12}$$

*Step 4 (2 marks):* For a **diagonal axis** in the plane of the plate, by symmetry across the diagonal, we can reorient the axes so that the diagonal plays the role of one of the perpendicular in-plane axes. The plate is symmetric about its diagonal, so $I_{d_1} = I_{d_2}$ (the two perpendicular axes being the diagonal and the perpendicular-to-diagonal axis in the plane). Therefore:
$$I_{z} \;=\; I_{d_1} + I_{d_2} \;=\; 2 I_{\text{diagonal}}$$

*Step 5 (2 marks):* Solve:
$$I_{\text{diagonal}} \;=\; \dfrac{I_{z}}{2} \;=\; \dfrac{M (L^{2} + B^{2})}{24}$$

*Final boxed answer:*
$$\boxed{I_{\text{diagonal}} = \dfrac{M (L^{2} + B^{2})}{24}}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🔴 KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Mark-Loss Hotspots**
> 1. **Unit Mix-Ups:** Writing Area MOI ($I = \int y^{2}\,dA$, units $\text{m}^{4}$) instead of Mass MOI ($I = \int r^{2}\,dm$, units $\text{kg·m}^{2}$) — instant zero for that part.
> 2. **Forgetting to subtract the hole** in composite-body problems (or subtracting instead of adding). The full-step logic — *what is being added/removed and why* — must be visible in the answer script.
> 3. **Applying Perpendicular Axis Theorem to 3-D solids** (sphere, cylinder, cube). Always state clearly that the body *is* a planar lamina before invoking $I_z = I_x + I_y$.
> 4. **Distance $d$ measured wrongly** in the Parallel Axis Theorem — it must be the *perpendicular* distance between the two parallel axes, **not** the distance along the body.
> 5. **Missing the centroidal MOI** — KTU's favourite 1-mark trap: ask "MOI about the centre" and the student applies $I = Md^{2}$ alone, forgetting to add the centroidal $I_G$ part.
> 6. **Not showing "Hence" steps** when the question says "Hence, or otherwise" — you must build Part (b) cleanly from Part (a) to earn full continuity marks.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Definition:** $I = \int r^{2}\, dm$ — the second moment of mass; measures resistance to rotation.
- ✅ **Units:** SI unit is $\text{kg·m}^{2}$. Never use $\text{m}^{4}$ for mass MOI (that's area MOI).
- ✅ **Radius of Gyration:** $k = \sqrt{I / M}$ — the *effective* distance of mass concentration from the axis.
- ✅ **Parallel Axis Theorem:** $I = I_G + Md^{2}$ — *applies to ANY rigid body*; $I_G$ is the *centroidal* MOI; $d$ is the *perpendicular* distance.
- ✅ **Perpendicular Axis Theorem:** $I_z = I_x + I_y$ — *applies to PLANAR LAMINAS only*; $z$ is perpendicular to the plane; $x$ and $y$ lie in the plane and meet at the same point.
- ✅ **Standard Centroidal MOI values to memorize:**
  - Thin rod (length $L$): $I_G = ML^{2}/12$ ⟶ about end: $ML^{2}/3$ (use Parallel Axis).
  - Rectangular plate (about axis $\parallel$ to side $b$): $I_G = Mb^{2}/12$.
  - Circular disc (about diameter): $I_d = MR^{2}/4$ ⟶ about central $\perp$ axis: $MR^{2}/2$ (use Perpendicular Axis).
  - Solid sphere: $I = 2MR^{2}/5$ about any diameter.
  - Solid cylinder/rod about its own axis: $I = MR^{2}/2$.
- ✅ **Composite body strategy:** *Total = Sum of parts* (or *Full − Removed* for cut-outs). Always compute each part's *centroidal* MOI first, **then** shift using the Parallel Axis Theorem.
- ✅ **Order of operations in any problem:**
  1. Identify the body type (lamina vs. solid).
  2. Identify which axis is needed.
  3. Pick the standard shape formula giving $I_G$ for the right axis.
  4. Apply Parallel Axis Theorem for the *shift* to the desired axis.
  5. Apply Perpendicular Axis Theorem only if the body is a planar lamina.
- ✅ **Final answer must always carry the unit** $\text{kg·m}^{2}$.
- ✅ **KTU Module 2 weightage** typically features 1 Part-A (3 marks) and 1 Part-B sub-part (7 marks) per question paper; the theorems are the **gateway** to almost every other Module 2 question.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Polar Moment of Inertia and Radius of Gyration
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GBEST213 - ENGINEERING MECHANICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Polar Moment of Inertia and Radius of Gyration

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Polar Moment of Inertia (J or Iₚ):** The polar moment of inertia of a plane area about an axis perpendicular to the plane of the area (passing through the pole) is defined as the **second moment of area** of the elemental area about that axis. Mathematically, it is the integral of the product of the elemental area and the square of its perpendicular distance from the reference axis (pole).

For a differential area $dA$ located at a distance $r$ from the pole $O$ (which is the perpendicular axis piercing the plane), the polar moment of inertia is:

$$J_O = I_P = \int_A r^2 \, dA$$

where $r$ is the radial distance from the pole $O$ to the element $dA$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Unit Conventions in KTU Examinations:**
> - **SI Units:** $m^4$ (most common in derivations)
> - **Practical Units:** $mm^4$ or $cm^4$ (used in numerical problem solving)
> - The polar moment of inertia is always **positive** and represents resistance to **torsional deformation** or **twisting** about the polar axis.

### 1.2 Radius of Gyration — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Radius of Gyration (k):** It is defined as the **hypothetical (imaginary) distance** from the reference axis at which the entire area of the body may be assumed to be concentrated as a **point mass (point area)** such that its moment of inertia about that axis remains the **same as the original distributed area**.

Mathematically, if $A$ is the total area and $k$ is the radius of gyration about axis $O$:

$$I_O = A \cdot k_O^2 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad k_O = \sqrt{\frac{I_O}{A}}$$

The corresponding **Polar Radius of Gyration** $k_p$ is:

$$J_O = A \cdot k_p^2 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad k_p = \sqrt{\frac{J_O}{A}}$$

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Picture (The "Spinning Top" Analogy):**
> Imagine holding a **flat circular plate** by sticking a pencil through its center. If you try to twist the plate (rotate it about the pencil axis), the plate resists. The amount it resists is governed by the **Polar Moment of Inertia**. A solid disc and a thin ring of the same mass behave very differently — the ring resists twisting far more because its mass is distributed **far from the center**.
> 
> **Polar MOI answers the question:** *"How difficult is it to twist this 2D shape about a point?"*
> 
> **Radius of Gyration answers the question:** *"If I were to collapse this entire shape into a single point at distance k from the pole, where should that point lie so the twisting resistance remains identical?"*

**Everyday Engineering Examples:**
- **Drive shafts** in automobiles: A hollow shaft has higher polar MOI per unit mass than a solid shaft, making it the preferred choice for transmitting torque.
- **Rotating flywheels:** The polar MOI determines how smoothly the flywheel stores rotational kinetic energy.
- **Torsional springs and twisting members:** Polar MOI is the primary geometric property used in torsion formula $\frac{T}{J} = \frac{\tau}{r} = \frac{G\theta}{L}$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Geometric Setup and Sign Convention

Consider a plane lamina lying in the $xy$-plane. The **pole** $O$ is the origin of the coordinate system. A differential area $dA$ located at coordinates $(x, y)$ has a perpendicular (radial) distance from $O$ given by:

$$r = \sqrt{x^2 + y^2}$$

The corresponding elemental polar moment of inertia is:

$$dJ_O = r^2 \, dA = (x^2 + y^2) \, dA$$

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logical Deduction

**Step 1 — Express the moment of inertia contribution:**
Each elemental strip contributes $r^2 \, dA$ to the polar moment of inertia about $O$.

**Step 2 — Integrate over the entire planar domain:**
The total polar moment of inertia is obtained by integrating over the whole area $A$:

$$J_O = \int_A (x^2 + y^2) \, dA$$

**Step 3 — Split the integral (the foundation of the Perpendicular Axis Theorem):**

$$J_O = \int_A x^2 \, dA + \int_A y^2 \, dA$$

**Step 4 — Identify the rectangular moments of inertia:**
- $I_x = \int_A y^2 \, dA$ → Moment of inertia about the $x$-axis
- $I_y = \int_A x^2 \, dA$ → Moment of inertia about the $y$-axis

**Step 5 — State the Perpendicular Axis Theorem:**

$$\boxed{J_O = I_x + I_y}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **This is one of the most frequently tested identities in KTU ESE Module 2 — memorize it verbatim.**

### 2.3 Why "Polar" Specifically?

The term **"polar"** distinguishes the perpendicular-axis moment of inertia from rectangular-axis moments of inertia. While $I_x$ and $I_y$ measure resistance to bending about in-plane axes, $J_O$ measures resistance to **twisting about an axis perpendicular to the plane** (out-of-plane axis).

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains **all high-yield formulas** for KTU 2024 Scheme examinations on this topic. Memorize the table — it covers ~80% of testable content.

| **#** | **Quantity** | **Mathematical Expression** | **Units (SI)** | **Physical Meaning** |
|:-----:|:-------------|:--------------------------|:--------------:|:---------------------|
| 1 | Polar Moment of Inertia | $J_O = \int_A r^2 \, dA$ | $m^4$ | Resistance to twisting about pole $O$ |
| 2 | Perpendicular Axis Theorem | $J_O = I_x + I_y$ | $m^4$ | Connects polar and rectangular MOI |
| 3 | Polar MOI — Ring (outer $R$, inner $r$) | $J_O = \dfrac{\pi}{2}(R^4 - r^4)$ | $m^4$ | Hollow shaft cross-section |
| 4 | Polar MOI — Solid Circle (radius $R$) | $J_O = \dfrac{\pi R^4}{2} = \dfrac{\pi D^4}{32}$ | $m^4$ | Solid shaft cross-section |
| 5 | Polar MOI — Solid Square (side $a$) | $J_O = \dfrac{a^4}{6}$ | $m^4$ | Square cross-section |
| 6 | Polar MOI — Solid Rectangle ($b \times h$) | $J_O = \dfrac{bh(b^2 + h^2)}{12}$ | $m^4$ | Rectangular cross-section |
| 7 | Radius of Gyration (general) | $k_O = \sqrt{\dfrac{I_O}{A}}$ | $m$ | Hypothetical point-area distance |
| 8 | Polar Radius of Gyration | $k_p = \sqrt{\dfrac{J_O}{A}}$ | $m$ | For polar axis |
| 9 | Polar Radius Relation | $k_p^2 = k_x^2 + k_y^2$ | $m^2$ | Polar–rectangular link |
| 10 | Torsion Formula (Utility) | $\dfrac{T}{J} = \dfrac{\tau_{max}}{R} = \dfrac{G\theta}{L}$ | $Pa$ | Engineering application |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Substitution Rule:** Whenever you see a **circular cross-section** in a torsion problem, the answer is **always** in terms of $D^4$ or $R^4$ — never $D^3$ or $R^3$. The fourth-power dependence is the geometric signature of the polar moment of inertia.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| **Engineering Field** | **Application of Polar MOI** |
|:----------------------|:-----------------------------|
| **Mechanical Design** | Designing drive shafts, axles, and propeller shafts to resist torsional failure |
| **Structural Engineering** | Calculating warping constants for thin-walled open sections |
| **Aerospace** | Sizing helicopter rotor masts and turbine shafts |
| **Civil Engineering** | Torsional analysis of beams in curved bridges |
| **Rotational Dynamics** | Computing kinetic energy: $KE = \frac{1}{2}J_O \omega^2$ |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Perpendicular Axis Theorem (Rigid Proof)

> [!NOTE]
> **Statement:** *For any plane lamina lying in the $xy$-plane, the polar moment of inertia about the origin $O$ equals the sum of the moments of inertia about the $x$ and $y$ axes.*

**Starting Point — Elemental Setup:**

Consider an elemental area $dA$ at coordinates $(x, y)$ measured from origin $O$. Its radial distance from $O$ is:

$$r = \sqrt{x^2 + y^2}$$

Squaring both sides:

$$r^2 = x^2 + y^2$$

**Elemental Polar MOI:**

$$dJ_O = r^2 \, dA = (x^2 + y^2) \, dA$$

**Total Polar MOI by Integration:**

$$J_O = \int_A r^2 \, dA = \int_A (x^2 + y^2) \, dA$$

**Split the Integral — This is the Key Algebraic Step:**

$$J_O = \int_A x^2 \, dA + \int_A y^2 \, dA$$

**Recognize the Rectangular MOIs:**

- The first term, $\int_A x^2 \, dA$, is **by definition** the moment of inertia of the area about the $y$-axis (because $x$ is the perpendicular distance from the $y$-axis):

$$\int_A x^2 \, dA = I_y$$

- The second term, $\int_A y^2 \, dA$, is **by definition** the moment of inertia of the area about the $x$-axis (because $y$ is the perpendicular distance from the $x$-axis):

$$\int_A y^2 \, dA = I_x$$

**Combine to Get the Perpendicular Axis Theorem:**

$$\boxed{J_O = I_x + I_y \qquad \text{(Proved)}}$$

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Polar MOI of a Solid Circular Disc

**Setup:** A solid circular disc of radius $R$ lies in the $xy$-plane with its geometric center at the origin $O$.

**Step 1 — Choose the elemental area:**

Use a thin concentric ring of radius $r$ and thickness $dr$. The area of this ring is:

$$dA = 2\pi r \, dr$$

> *Reasoning:* Circumference ($2\pi r$) × thickness ($dr$) = lateral surface area of the ring, which approximates its planar area.

**Step 2 — Polar MOI of the elemental ring:**

Every point on the ring is at distance $r$ from the pole $O$:

$$dJ_O = r^2 \, dA = r^2 \cdot 2\pi r \, dr = 2\pi r^3 \, dr$$

**Step 3 — Integrate from the center ($r = 0$) to the outer edge ($r = R$):**

$$J_O = \int_0^R 2\pi r^3 \, dr$$

$$J_O = 2\pi \left[ \frac{r^4}{4} \right]_0^R$$

$$J_O = 2\pi \cdot \frac{R^4}{4}$$

$$\boxed{J_O = \frac{\pi R^4}{2} = \frac{\pi D^4}{32}}$$

> *Substitution used:* $D = 2R \Rightarrow R = D/2 \Rightarrow R^4 = D^4/16 \Rightarrow \pi R^4/2 = \pi D^4/32$. ✓

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Polar MOI of a Hollow Circular Ring (Annulus)

**Setup:** A ring with outer radius $R$ and inner radius $r_i$ centered at origin $O$.

**Method — Use the Subtraction Principle:**

The annulus = Outer solid disc − Inner solid disc (hole).

$$J_O = J_{\text{outer}} - J_{\text{inner}}$$

$$J_O = \frac{\pi R^4}{2} - \frac{\pi r_i^4}{2}$$

$$\boxed{J_O = \frac{\pi}{2}(R^4 - r_i^4)}$$

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Relationship Between Radii of Gyration

**Starting Point — Definition of $k_p$:**

$$k_p^2 = \frac{J_O}{A}$$

**Substitute Perpendicular Axis Theorem:**

$$k_p^2 = \frac{I_x + I_y}{A}$$

$$k_p^2 = \frac{I_x}{A} + \frac{I_y}{A}$$

**Apply definitions of $k_x$ and $k_y$:**

- $k_x^2 = I_x/A$
- $k_y^2 = I_y/A$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{k_p^2 = k_x^2 + k_y^2}$$

> This elegant result mirrors the structural form of $J_O = I_x + I_y$.

### 3.5 Derivation 5: Polar MOI of a Solid Square (KTU Favourite)

**Setup:** Square of side $a$ centered at origin $O$, with sides parallel to the $x$ and $y$ axes.

**Step 1 — Known rectangular MOIs (standard result):**

$$I_x = I_y = \frac{a^4}{12}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Perpendicular Axis Theorem:**

$$J_O = I_x + I_y = \frac{a^4}{12} + \frac{a^4}{12} = \frac{2a^4}{12}$$

$$\boxed{J_O = \frac{a^4}{6}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** This derivation demonstrates the **computational power of the Perpendicular Axis Theorem**. Instead of doing a difficult double integral in polar coordinates, we leveraged known rectangular MOI results. KTU examiners love this style of question.

### 3.6 Analytical Summary Matrix

| **Shape** | **$I_x$** | **$I_y$** | **$J_O = I_x + I_y$** | **Area $A$** | **$k_p$** |
|:---------|:----------|:----------|:---------------------|:-------------|:----------|
| Solid Circle (R) | $\frac{\pi R^4}{4}$ | $\frac{\pi R^4}{4}$ | $\frac{\pi R^4}{2}$ | $\pi R^2$ | $\frac{R}{\sqrt{2}}$ |
| Hollow Ring (R, r) | $\frac{\pi (R^4 - r^4)}{4}$ | $\frac{\pi (R^4 - r^4)}{4}$ | $\frac{\pi (R^4 - r^4)}{2}$ | $\pi (R^2 - r^2)$ | $\sqrt{\frac{R^2 + r^2}{2}}$ |
| Solid Square (a) | $\frac{a^4}{12}$ | $\frac{a^4}{12}$ | $\frac{a^4}{6}$ | $a^2$ | $\frac{a}{\sqrt{6}}$ |
| Solid Rectangle (b, h) | $\frac{bh^3}{12}$ | $\frac{hb^3}{12}$ | $\frac{bh(b^2 + h^2)}{12}$ | $bh$ | $\sqrt{\frac{b^2 + h^2}{12}}$ |
| Solid Triangle (base $b$, height $h$) | $\frac{bh^3}{36}$ | $\frac{hb^3}{48}$ | $\frac{bh(b^2 + h^2)}{48}$ | $\frac{bh}{2}$ | Complex form |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
