<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Define Polar Moment of Inertia. State the Perpendicular Axis Theorem.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Polar Moment of Inertia ($J_O$):** It is the moment of inertia of a plane area about an axis perpendicular to the plane of the area and passing through the pole. Mathematically:
> $$J_O = \int_A r^2 \, dA$$
> where $r$ is the perpendicular distance of the elemental area $dA$ from the pole $O$. Units: $m^4$ or $mm^4$.

> **Perpendicular Axis Theorem:** *For any plane lamina, the polar moment of inertia about an axis perpendicular to the plane equals the sum of the moments of inertia about any two mutually perpendicular axes lying in the plane and intersecting at the point where the polar axis pierces the plane.*
> $$J_O = I_x + I_y$$

**[3 Marks: 1.5 for definition, 1.5 for theorem statement]**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
**Define Radius of Gyration. What is its physical significance?**

**Model Answer:**

> **Radius of Gyration ($k$):** It is the distance from the reference axis at which the entire area of the body may be assumed to be concentrated as a point such that its moment of inertia about that axis is the same as that of the original distributed area.
> $$k = \sqrt{\frac{I}{A}}$$

> **Physical Significance:** The radius of gyration gives a **single characteristic length** that captures how the area is distributed about an axis. A larger $k$ means the area is spread farther from the axis, increasing resistance to bending or twisting. It is widely used in column buckling ($P_{cr} = \pi^2 EI / (kL)^2$), shaft design, and structural mechanics to express "spread" compactly.

**[3 Marks: 1.5 for definition, 1.5 for significance]**

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**

**(a)** Derive the Perpendicular Axis Theorem for plane areas. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A solid circular shaft of diameter $80\text{ mm}$ is transmitting a torque of $2\text{ kN·m}$. Find: (i) the polar moment of inertia, (ii) the maximum shear stress induced, and (iii) the polar radius of gyration. Take $G = 80\text{ GPa}$. **(7 Marks)**

---

### **Model Solution — Question A**

#### **Part (a): Derivation of Perpendicular Axis Theorem**

**Step 1:** Consider a plane area in the $xy$-plane with origin $O$ as the pole. Let $dA$ be an elemental area at $(x, y)$.

**Step 2:** Radial distance: $r = \sqrt{x^2 + y^2}$, so $r^2 = x^2 + y^2$.

**Step 3:** Polar MOI of the element:
$$dJ_O = r^2 \, dA = (x^2 + y^2) \, dA$$

**Step 4:** Total polar MOI:
$$J_O = \int_A (x^2 + y^2) \, dA = \int_A x^2 \, dA + \int_A y^2 \, dA$$

**Step 5:** By definition, $I_y = \int_A x^2 \, dA$ (since $x$ is distance from $y$-axis) and $I_x = \int_A y^2 \, dA$ (since $y$ is distance from $x$-axis).

**Step 6:** Substituting:
$$\boxed{J_O = I_x + I_y \qquad \text{(Perpendicular Axis Theorem — Proved)}}$$

**[7 Marks: 1 for setup, 2 for algebraic manipulation, 2 for identification of $I_x$ and $I_y$, 2 for final result with units and statement]**

---

#### **Part (b): Solid Circular Shaft Numerical**

**Given:** $D = 80\text{ mm} = 0.08\text{ m}$, $T = 2\text{ kN·m} = 2000\text{ N·m}$, $G = 80\text{ GPa} = 80 \times 10^9\text{ Pa}$.

**(i) Polar Moment of Inertia:**

$$J_O = \frac{\pi D^4}{32} = \frac{\pi (0.08)^4}{32}$$

Computing $(0.08)^4 = 4.096 \times 10^{-5}\text{ m}^4$:

$$J_O = \frac{\pi \times 4.096 \times 10^{-5}}{32} = \frac{1.2868 \times 10^{-4}}{32}$$

$$J_O = 4.021 \times 10^{-6}\text{ m}^4 \approx 4.02 \times 10^6\text{ mm}^4$$

**(ii) Maximum Shear Stress:**

Using torsion formula $\tau_{max} = \dfrac{T \cdot R}{J_O} = \dfrac{T \cdot (D/2)}{J_O}$:

$$\tau_{max} = \frac{2000 \times 0.04}{4.021 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{80}{4.021 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$\boxed{\tau_{max} = 19.895 \times 10^6\text{ Pa} \approx 19.9\text{ MPa}}$$

**(iii) Polar Radius of Gyration:**

Area of shaft: $A = \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4} = \dfrac{\pi (0.08)^2}{4} = 5.0265 \times 10^{-3}\text{ m}^2$

$$k_p = \sqrt{\frac{J_O}{A}} = \sqrt{\frac{4.021 \times 10^{-6}}{5.0265 \times 10^{-3}}}$$

$$k_p = \sqrt{8.0 \times 10^{-4}} = 0.02828\text{ m}$$

$$\boxed{k_p = 28.28\text{ mm}}$$

> **Quick Check:** For a solid circle, $k_p = R/\sqrt{2} = 40/\sqrt{2} = 28.28$ mm. ✓

**[7 Marks: 2 for $J_O$, 2 for $\tau_{max}$, 2 for $k_p$, 1 for correct units throughout]**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — June 2023]**

**(a)** Derive an expression for the polar moment of inertia of a hollow circular section of outer diameter $D$ and inner diameter $d$. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A hollow shaft of outer diameter $100\text{ mm}$ and inner diameter $60\text{ mm}$ is to be replaced by a solid shaft of the same material such that both shafts have the **same polar moment of inertia**. Find the diameter of the solid shaft. Also find the percentage saving in weight (cross-sectional area) by using the hollow shaft. **(7 Marks)**

---

### **Model Solution — Question B**

#### **Part (a): Polar MOI of Hollow Circular Section**

**Step 1:** Consider a hollow circular section with outer diameter $D$ (radius $R = D/2$) and inner diameter $d$ (radius $r = d/2$).

**Step 2:** Treat as a solid disc of radius $R$ **minus** a solid disc of radius $r$ (the hole).

**Step 3:** Polar MOI of solid disc of radius $R$:
$$J_1 = \frac{\pi R^4}{2}$$

**Step 4:** Polar MOI of removed disc of radius $r$:
$$J_2 = \frac{\pi r^4}{2}$$

**Step 5:** Polar MOI of hollow section:
$$J_O = J_1 - J_2 = \frac{\pi R^4}{2} - \frac{\pi r^4}{2}$$

$$\boxed{J_O = \frac{\pi}{2}(R^4 - r^4) = \frac{\pi}{32}(D^4 - d^4)}$$

**[7 Marks: 2 for conceptual setup, 2 for individual MOIs, 2 for subtraction, 1 for final form in both $R, r$ and $D, d$ versions]**

---

#### **Part (b): Equivalent Solid Shaft**

**Given:** Hollow shaft $D = 100\text{ mm}$, $d = 60\text{ mm}$.

**Step 1: Polar MOI of hollow shaft:**

$$J_{\text{hollow}} = \frac{\pi}{32}(D^4 - d^4) = \frac{\pi}{32}(100^4 - 60^4)$$

Computing: $100^4 = 10^8$, $60^4 = 12.96 \times 10^6$

$$J_{\text{hollow}} = \frac{\pi}{32}(100000000 - 12960000) = \frac{\pi \times 87040000}{32}$$

$$J_{\text{hollow}} = \pi \times 2.72 \times 10^6 = 8.545 \times 10^6\text{ mm}^4$$

**Step 2: Equate to solid shaft of diameter $D_s$:**

$$\frac{\pi D_s^4}{32} = 8.545 \times 10^6$$

$$D_s^4 = \frac{32 \times 8.545 \times 10^6}{\pi} = 87.04 \times 10^6$$

$$D_s = (87.04 \times 10^6)^{1/4} = 96.65\text{ mm}$$

$$\boxed{D_s \approx 96.65\text{ mm}}$$

**Step 3: Percentage saving in area:**

$$A_{\text{hollow}} = \frac{\pi}{4}(D^2 - d^2) = \frac{\pi}{4}(100^2 - 60^2) = \frac{\pi}{4}(6400) = 5026.5\text{ mm}^2$$

$$A_{\text{solid}} = \frac{\pi}{4}D_s^2 = \frac{\pi}{4}(96.65)^2 = \frac{\pi}{4}(9341.2) = 7336.0\text{ mm}^2$$

**Percentage saving** = $\dfrac{A_{\text{solid}} - A_{\text{hollow}}}{A_{\text{solid}}} \times 100$:

$$\% \text{ Saving} = \frac{7336.0 - 5026.5}{7336.0} \times 100 = \frac{2309.5}{7336.0} \times 100$$

$$\boxed{\% \text{ Saving} \approx 31.48\%}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The hollow shaft delivers the **same torsional capacity** as a much heavier solid shaft, demonstrating why hollow shafts dominate real-world drive-line design.

**[7 Marks: 2 for hollow $J_O$ computation, 2 for solving $D_s$, 2 for area calculation, 1 for percentage saving]**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting to convert units:** KTU problems often mix $mm$ and $m$ in the same problem. Convert **all quantities to a consistent unit** (preferably $mm$ or $m$) at the very start. A $1000^4$ error is one of the most common ways students lose **3 to 4 marks** in a single part.
> 
> 2. **Confusing $I_x$ and $I_y$ with $J_O$:** Students often quote $J_O = \pi D^4 / 32$ as $I_x$ or $I_y$ — these are **three different quantities**. For a circle: $I_x = I_y = \pi D^4 / 64$ and $J_O = \pi D^4 / 32 = 2 \times I_x$.
> 
> 3. **Radius of Gyration units:** Always in $m$ or $mm$ (linear units), **never** in $m^2$ or $mm^2$. Examiners deduct a full mark for this.
> 
> 4. **Perpendicular Axis Theorem valid only for plane laminae:** Do not apply $J = I_x + I_y$ to 3D bodies — it is strictly a planar area theorem.
> 
> 5. **Squaring vs. fourth power:** When given $D = 80$ mm, remember to raise to the **fourth** power: $D^4 = 80^4 = 4.096 \times 10^7\text{ mm}^4$. Students often mistakenly compute $D^2$ or $D^3$.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Polar MOI & Radius of Gyration**

- **Polar MOI** is the second moment of area about an axis **perpendicular to the plane** of the lamina; symbol: $J_O$ or $I_P$.
- **Fundamental integral:** $J_O = \int_A r^2 \, dA$, with $r$ as the perpendicular distance from the pole to $dA$.
- **Perpendicular Axis Theorem** (must memorize): $J_O = I_x + I_y$ — applies **only to plane areas**.
- **Solid circle:** $J_O = \dfrac{\pi D^4}{32} = \dfrac{\pi R^4}{2}$ — this is the **most-used** formula in KTU problems.
- **Hollow circle:** $J_O = \dfrac{\pi (D^4 - d^4)}{32} = \dfrac{\pi (R^4 - r^4)}{2}$ — derived by subtraction.
- **Radius of Gyration** $k = \sqrt{I/A}$ is a **linear** quantity (units: $m$ or $mm$), **not** an area.
- **Polar Radius of Gyration:** $k_p = \sqrt{J_O/A}$; for a solid circle, $k_p = R/\sqrt{2}$.
- **Linkage identity:** $k_p^2 = k_x^2 + k_y^2$ (mirrors $J_O = I_x + I_y$).
- **Torsion formula** $\dfrac{T}{J} = \dfrac{\tau}{R} = \dfrac{G\theta}{L}$ is the **primary engineering application** of polar MOI.
- **Units check:** MOI in $m^4$ or $mm^4$; Radius of Gyration in $m$ or $mm$; **never confuse them**.
- **Practical tip:** The fourth-power dependence on diameter means doubling the diameter increases the polar MOI by a factor of **16** — this is why shaft sizing is so critical in design.
- **Exam strategy:** When stuck, default to the Perpendicular Axis Theorem — it converts difficult polar integrals into easier rectangular MOI lookups.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Mass Moment of Inertia of Ring and Disc
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GBEST213 - ENGINEERING MECHANICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Mass Moment of Inertia of Ring and Disc

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is Mass Moment of Inertia (MMI)?

The **Mass Moment of Inertia (MMI)** of a rigid body about a given axis is defined as the sum of the products of the mass of each elementary particle and the square of its perpendicular distance from the axis of rotation.

$$I \;=\; \sum m_i \, r_i^{\,2} \quad \text{(Discrete form)} \qquad\qquad I \;=\; \int r^{2}\, dm \quad \text{(Continuous form)}$$

where:
- $I$ = Mass moment of inertia (kg·m²)
- $m_i$ = mass of the $i^{th}$ particle (kg)
- $r_i$ = perpendicular distance of the $i^{th}$ particle from the axis (m)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (GBEST213 – Module 2):** The Mass Moment of Inertia is a **scalar** quantity that quantifies a body's resistance to **angular acceleration** about a specified axis. It plays the same role for rotational motion that mass plays for translational motion.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Two Types of Moments Must NOT be Confused:**
> 1. **Mass Moment of Inertia ($I$)** — Units: **kg·m²** — depends on **mass distribution** (used in dynamics).
> 2. **Area Moment of Inertia ($I_a$)** — Units: **m⁴** — depends on **area distribution** (used in bending/strength of materials).

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy — The Ice Skater:** Imagine an ice skater spinning. When she pulls her arms inward (reducing $r$), her mass moves closer to the spin axis and her spinning speed *increases dramatically*. This is because $I$ decreased. Conversely, when she extends her arms, $I$ increases and she slows down. **The farther the mass sits from the axis, the harder it is to start or stop the rotation.** This is exactly why flywheels, which store rotational energy, are designed as **hollow rings** — the mass is placed as far from the axis as possible.

A thin **ring** has all its mass concentrated at one distance $R$ from the central axis, while a **disc** has its mass spread uniformly from the center outwards. This single geometric difference is what produces the famous factor of **2** between their MMI values.

### 1.3 Standard Assumptions for Ring and Disc

| Body | Mass Distribution | Geometric Property |
|---|---|---|
| **Thin Ring** | All mass at radius $R$ (zero thickness) | Circumference $= 2\pi R$ |
| **Uniform Disc** | Mass uniformly spread across radius $R$ | Area $= \pi R^{2}$ |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic — Why the Integration Works

To find the MMI of a continuous body, we follow this **4-step KTU logic**:

1. **Choose an elemental slice** that has a known, simple geometry (a ring, a strip, a disc element).
2. **Express the mass of the element** ($dm$) in terms of the body's total mass $M$ and the geometry — using the principle of **uniform mass distribution**:

$$dm \;=\; \rho \, dV \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad dm \;=\; \sigma \, dA \quad \text{or} \quad dm \;=\; \lambda \, dL$$

where $\rho$ (kg/m³), $\sigma$ (kg/m²), and $\lambda$ (kg/m) are **mass densities** for volume, area, and length respectively.

3. **Identify the perpendicular distance** $r$ of *every point* in the element from the chosen axis.
4. **Integrate** $r^{2}\, dm$ over the full body:

$$I \;=\; \int r^{2}\, dm$$

### 2.2 The Perpendicular Axis Theorem (PAT)

For any **lamina** (thin, flat plate) lying in the $xy$-plane, if $I_{xx}$ and $I_{yy}$ are the MMIs about two perpendicular axes *in the plane*, and $I_{zz}$ is the MMI about the axis *perpendicular* to the plane through their intersection, then:

$$I_{zz} \;=\; I_{xx} \;+\; I_{yy}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This theorem is the BACKBONE of KTU problems on Ring and Disc.** It lets you compute two MMIs from one — and vice versa. For bodies with **symmetry about both in-plane axes** (such as a ring and a disc), we have $I_{xx} = I_{yy}$, hence:

$$I_{xx} \;=\; I_{yy} \;=\; \frac{I_{zz}}{2}$$

### 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet — Ring & Disc

> [!NOTE]
> **Memorize this table** — it covers **80% of KTU Module-2 questions on this topic**.

| # | Body | Axis of Rotation | MMI Formula | Derivation Method |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Thin Ring / Hoop** (Mass $M$, Radius $R$) | Central axis $\perp$ to plane (through $O$) | $I_{zz} \;=\; M R^{2}$ | All mass at distance $R$ |
| 2 | **Thin Ring** | Any diameter ($x$ or $y$) | $I_{xx} \;=\; I_{yy} \;=\; \dfrac{M R^{2}}{2}$ | Perpendicular Axis Theorem |
| 3 | **Thin Ring** | Tangent in plane of ring | $I \;=\; \dfrac{3 M R^{2}}{2}$ | Parallel Axis Theorem |
| 4 | **Thin Ring** | Tangent $\perp$ to plane | $I \;=\; 2 M R^{2}$ | Parallel Axis Theorem |
| 5 | **Uniform Disc** (Mass $M$, Radius $R$) | Central axis $\perp$ to plane (through $O$) | $I_{zz} \;=\; \dfrac{M R^{2}}{2}$ | Ring-element integration |
| 6 | **Uniform Disc** | Any diameter ($x$ or $y$) | $I_{xx} \;=\; I_{yy} \;=\; \dfrac{M R^{2}}{4}$ | Perpendicular Axis Theorem |
| 7 | **Uniform Disc** | Tangent in plane of disc | $I \;=\; \dfrac{5 M R^{2}}{4}$ | Parallel Axis Theorem |
| 8 | **Uniform Disc** | Tangent $\perp$ to plane | $I \;=\; \dfrac{3 M R^{2}}{2}$ | Parallel Axis Theorem |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Parallel Axis Theorem** (used above): For an axis parallel to a centroidal axis at perpendicular distance $d$:

$$I \;=\; I_{G} \;+\; M d^{2}$$

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Flywheels and Pulleys:** Designed as **rings** to maximize $I$ per unit mass (energy storage in IC engines, punching machines, regenerative braking systems).
- **Automobile Brake Discs:** Solid **discs** that dissipate heat while providing predictable rotational inertia for smooth braking.
- **Turbine Rotors:** The MMI distribution determines critical (whirling) speeds — a vital design parameter in power-plant engineering.
- **Gears and Sprockets:** Engineered as rings with web/arms, balancing strength and low MMI for rapid acceleration.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Notation used in all derivations:**
> - $M$ = total mass of the body (kg)
> - $R$ = outer radius (m)
> - $r$ = radius of an elementary ring inside the body (variable, $0 \le r \le R$)
> - $O$ = geometric center
> - $XOX'$ and $YOY'$ = two perpendicular diameters
> - $ZOZ'$ = central axis perpendicular to the plane

---

### 3.1 DERIVATION 1 — MMI of a Thin Ring about its Central Axis ($ZOZ'$)

**Geometric Setup:** A thin ring of mass $M$ and radius $R$ is centered at $O$. The axis $ZOZ'$ is perpendicular to the plane of the ring, passing through $O$.

**Step 1 — Mass per unit length:**
The ring has length (circumference) $L = 2\pi R$, and total mass $M$.

$$\lambda \;=\; \frac{M}{2 \pi R} \quad \text{(kg/m)}$$

**Step 2 — Elemental mass:**
Take an infinitesimal arc element of length $dl$ on the ring.

$$dm \;=\; \lambda \, dl \;=\; \frac{M}{2 \pi R}\, dl$$

**Step 3 — Distance from axis:**
Every point on the ring is at perpendicular distance $R$ from the central axis $ZOZ'$.

$$r \;=\; R$$

**Step 4 — Integration:**

$$I_{zz} \;=\; \int r^{2}\, dm \;=\; \int_{0}^{2\pi R} R^{2} \cdot \frac{M}{2\pi R}\, dl$$

Factor out the constants (since $R$ does not depend on $dl$):

$$I_{zz} \;=\; \frac{M R^{2}}{2 \pi R} \int_{0}^{2\pi R} dl \;=\; \frac{M R^{2}}{2 \pi R} \cdot \big[ l \big]_{0}^{2\pi R}$$

$$I_{zz} \;=\; \frac{M R^{2}}{2 \pi R} \cdot 2 \pi R$$

$$\boxed{\,I_{zz} \;=\; M R^{2}\,}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation:** Because the entire mass sits at a fixed distance $R$ from the axis, no integration over $r$ is needed — the result is simply $M R^{2}$.

---

### 3.2 DERIVATION 2 — MMI of a Thin Ring about its Diameter ($XOX'$)

**Method:** Use the **Perpendicular Axis Theorem** since the ring is a lamina with $I_{xx} = I_{yy}$ by symmetry.

**Step 1 — Apply PAT:**

$$I_{xx} \;+\; I_{yy} \;=\; I_{zz}$$

**Step 2 — Use symmetry:**

By the rotational symmetry of the ring about $O$, the distribution of mass with respect to $XOX'$ is identical to that with respect to $YOY'$.

$$I_{xx} \;=\; I_{yy}$$

**Step 3 — Solve:**

$$2 I_{xx} \;=\; M R^{2} \quad\Longrightarrow\quad I_{xx} \;=\; \frac{M R^{2}}{2}$$

$$\boxed{\,I_{xx} \;=\; I_{yy} \;=\; \frac{M R^{2}}{2}\,}$$

---

### 3.3 DERIVATION 3 — MMI of a Uniform Disc about its Central Axis ($ZOZ'$)

**Geometric Setup:** A uniform solid disc of mass $M$ and radius $R$, centered at $O$. Axis $ZOZ'$ is perpendicular to the disc, through its center.

**Step 1 — Mass per unit area:**
The disc has area $A = \pi R^{2}$, and total mass $M$.

$$\sigma \;=\; \frac{M}{\pi R^{2}} \quad \text{(kg/m}^{2}\text{)}$$

**Step 2 — Elemental mass:**
Consider a thin concentric ring element at radius $r$ with radial thickness $dr$. Its area is the circumference times the thickness:

$$dA \;=\; 2\pi r \, dr$$

Therefore the elemental mass is:

$$dm \;=\; \sigma \, dA \;=\; \frac{M}{\pi R^{2}} \cdot 2\pi r \, dr \;=\; \frac{2 M}{R^{2}}\, r\, dr$$

**Step 3 — Distance from axis:**
Every point on the ring element is at perpendicular distance $r$ from the central axis.

**Step 4 — Set up the integral:**

$$I_{zz} \;=\; \int r^{2}\, dm \;=\; \int_{0}^{R} r^{2} \cdot \frac{2 M}{R^{2}}\, r\, dr$$

$$I_{zz} \;=\; \frac{2 M}{R^{2}} \int_{0}^{R} r^{3}\, dr$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate:**

$$\int_{0}^{R} r^{3}\, dr \;=\; \left[ \frac{r^{4}}{4} \right]_{0}^{R} \;=\; \frac{R^{4}}{4}$$

**Step 6 — Substitute back:**

$$I_{zz} \;=\; \frac{2 M}{R^{2}} \cdot \frac{R^{4}}{4} \;=\; \frac{2 M R^{2}}{4} \;=\; \frac{M R^{2}}{2}$$

$$\boxed{\,I_{zz} \;=\; \frac{M R^{2}}{2}\,}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight — The "Factor of 2":** The disc's MMI is exactly **half** that of a ring of the same mass and radius. This is because the disc's mass is spread *uniformly* from $r=0$ to $r=R$, with the average value of $r^{2}$ being $R^{2}/2$.

---

### 3.4 DERIVATION 4 — MMI of a Uniform Disc about its Diameter ($XOX'$)

**Step 1 — Apply PAT:**

$$I_{xx} \;+\; I_{yy} \;=\; I_{zz} \;=\; \frac{M R^{2}}{2}$$

**Step 2 — Use symmetry** ($I_{xx} = I_{yy}$):

$$2 I_{xx} \;=\; \frac{M R^{2}}{2} \quad\Longrightarrow\quad I_{xx} \;=\; \frac{M R^{2}}{4}$$

$$\boxed{\,I_{xx} \;=\; I_{yy} \;=\; \frac{M R^{2}}{4}\,}$$

---

### 3.5 DERIVATION 5 — MMI of a Disc about an Axis Tangent to its Edge (in plane)

Using the **Parallel Axis Theorem** with centroidal axis $XOX'$ and parallel tangent axis at distance $d = R$:

$$I_{\text{tangent, in plane}} \;=\; I_{xx} \;+\; M d^{2} \;=\; \frac{M R^{2}}{4} \;+\; M R^{2}$$

$$I_{\text{tangent, in plane}} \;=\; \frac{M R^{2}}{4} \;+\; \frac{4 M R^{2}}{4} \;=\; \frac{5 M R^{2}}{4}$$

$$\boxed{\,I_{\text{tangent, in plane}} \;=\; \frac{5 M R^{2}}{4}\,}$$

### 3.6 DERIVATION 6 — MMI of a Disc about a Tangent Axis Perpendicular to the Plane

Using Parallel Axis Theorem with centroidal axis $ZOZ'$ and parallel tangent axis at distance $d = R$:

$$I_{\text{tangent, }\perp} \;=\; I_{zz} \;+\; M R^{2} \;=\; \frac{M R^{2}}{2} \;+\; M R^{2} \;=\; \frac{3 M R^{2}}{2}$$

$$\boxed{\,I_{\text{tangent, }\perp} \;=\; \frac{3 M R^{2}}{2}\,}$$

### 3.7 Summary Comparison Table — Ring vs Disc

> [!NOTE]
> **This is the single most-tested comparison in KTU exams.**

| Axis Location | Thin Ring ($M, R$) | Uniform Disc ($M, R$) | Ratio (Ring / Disc) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Central axis $\perp$ to plane | $M R^{2}$ | $\dfrac{M R^{2}}{2}$ | $2 : 1$ |
| Diameter (centroidal, in plane) | $\dfrac{M R^{2}}{2}$ | $\dfrac{M R^{2}}{4}$ | $2 : 1$ |
| Tangent in plane | $\dfrac{3 M R^{2}}{2}$ | $\dfrac{5 M R^{2}}{4}$ | $6 : 5$ |
| Tangent $\perp$ to plane | $2 M R^{2}$ | $\dfrac{3 M R^{2}}{2}$ | $4 : 3$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Note:** The geometry of a ring and a disc is best illustrated by a **functional block architecture** mapping the MMI relationships derived above. A Mermaid *flow-chart* is used because precise physical drawings cannot be rendered in this medium.

### 4.1 Block Diagram — MMI Computation Pipeline for Ring and Disc

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Body of Mass M, Radius R] --> B{Is it a Ring or Disc?}
    B --> C1[Thin Ring]
    B --> C2[Uniform Disc]
    C1 --> D1[All mass at r = R, so dm = M x delta at radius R]
    C2 --> D2[Mass distributed: dm = 2M r dr / R squared]
    D1 --> E1[Integrate r squared dm around ring]
    D2 --> E2[Integrate r squared dm from 0 to R]
    E1 --> F1[I_zz = M R squared]
    E2 --> F2[I_zz = M R squared over 2]
    F1 --> G[Apply Perpendicular Axis Theorem]
    F2 --> G
    G --> H1[Ring diameter: I_xx = M R squared over 2]
    G --> H2[Disc diameter: I_xx = M R squared over 4]
    H1 --> I[Apply Parallel Axis Theorem if needed]
    H2 --> I
    I --> J1[Ring tangent in plane: 3 M R squared over 2]
    I --> J2[Disc tangent in plane: 5 M R squared over 4]
    I --> K1[Ring tangent perpendicular: 2 M R squared]
    I --> K2[Disc tangent perpendicular: 3 M R squared over 2]
    J1 --> L[Final MMI Result]
    J2 --> L
    K1 --> L
    K2 --> L
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Cross-Sectional Geometry Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph A[Thin Ring Cross Section]
        A1[Mass concentrated AT radius R]
        A2[Inner radius equals R, Outer radius equals R]
        A3[Effective I_zz equals M R squared]
    end
    subgraph B[Uniform Disc Cross Section]
        B1[Mass UNIFORMLY distributed from r equals 0 to r equals R]
        B2[Mass per area sigma equals M over pi R squared]
        B3[Effective I_zz equals M R squared over 2]
    end
    A1 --> A2 --> A3
    B1 --> B2 --> B3
    A3 -- Perpendicular Axis --> C[I_xx of ring equals M R squared over 2]
    B3 -- Perpendicular Axis --> D[I_xx of disc equals M R squared over 4]
    C --> E[Ring to Disc ratio is exactly 2 to 1]
    D --> E
```

> [!TIP]
> **Reading the Diagram:** Always start from the body identification, derive the central-axis MMI by integration, then use the **Perpendicular Axis Theorem** (PAT) to get the diameter MMI, and finally the **Parallel Axis Theorem** (PAR) for any offset tangent axes.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
> Define Mass Moment of Inertia. State the Perpendicular Axis Theorem and the Parallel Axis Theorem.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 Mark):**
The Mass Moment of Inertia of a rigid body about a given axis is the sum of the products of the mass of each elementary particle and the square of its perpendicular distance from that axis:

$$I \;=\; \int r^{2}\, dm$$

**Perpendicular Axis Theorem (1 Mark):**
For a plane lamina lying in the $xy$-plane, if $I_{xx}$, $I_{yy}$ are the MMIs about two mutually perpendicular axes in the plane, and $I_{zz}$ is the MMI about the axis perpendicular to the plane through their intersection, then:

$$I_{zz} \;=\; I_{xx} \;+\; I_{yy}$$

**Parallel Axis Theorem (1 Mark):**
The MMI of a body about any axis is equal to its MMI about a parallel centroidal axis plus the product of the mass of the body and the square of the perpendicular distance between the two axes:

$$I \;=\; I_{G} \;+\; M d^{2}$$

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
> A uniform thin disc of mass 10 kg and radius 0.5 m rotates about an axis passing through its center and perpendicular to its plane. Calculate its mass moment of inertia.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Given: $M = 10$ kg, $R = 0.5$ m, axis: central axis perpendicular to plane.

Formula for disc about central perpendicular axis:

$$I_{zz} \;=\; \frac{M R^{2}}{2}$$

Substituting:

$$I_{zz} \;=\; \frac{10 \times (0.5)^{2}}{2} \;=\; \frac{10 \times 0.25}{2} \;=\; \frac{2.5}{2}$$

$$\boxed{\,I_{zz} \;=\; 1.25 \text{ kg·m}^{2}\,}$$

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks each, with internal choice)

---

> **Question A — [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper]**
>
> **(a)** Derive the mass moment of inertia of a uniform thin disc of mass $M$ and radius $R$ about an axis passing through its center and perpendicular to its plane, using the method of integration. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** A solid disc of mass 20 kg and radius 0.4 m is mounted such that it rotates about a tangent drawn on its rim and lying in the plane of the disc. Find its mass moment of inertia about this axis. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution for Question A:**

**(a) Derivation (7 Marks):**

> *(Step 1 — Geometry & density) [1 Mark]*
Consider a uniform disc of mass $M$ and radius $R$ with center $O$. The axis $ZOZ'$ passes through $O$ perpendicular to the plane of the disc. The mass per unit area is:

$$\sigma \;=\; \frac{M}{\pi R^{2}} \quad \text{(kg/m}^{2}\text{)}$$

> *(Step 2 — Elemental mass) [2 Marks]*
Choose a thin concentric ring element at radius $r$ with radial thickness $dr$. The area of this ring is:

$$dA \;=\; 2 \pi r \, dr$$

The elemental mass is:

$$dm \;=\; \sigma \, dA \;=\; \frac{M}{\pi R^{2}} \cdot 2 \pi r \, dr \;=\; \frac{2 M}{R^{2}}\, r\, dr$$

> *(Step 3 — Setting up the integral) [1 Mark]*
Every point on the ring element lies at perpendicular distance $r$ from axis $ZOZ'$. So:

$$I_{zz} \;=\; \int r^{2}\, dm \;=\; \int_{0}^{R} r^{2} \cdot \frac{2 M}{R^{2}}\, r\, dr \;=\; \frac{2 M}{R^{2}} \int_{0}^{R} r^{3}\, dr$$

> *(Step 4 — Evaluating the integral) [2 Marks]*

$$I_{zz} \;=\; \frac{2 M}{R^{2}} \left[ \frac{r^{4}}{4} \right]_{0}^{R} \;=\; \frac{2 M}{R^{2}} \cdot \frac{R^{4}}{4} \;=\; \frac{M R^{2}}{2}$$

> *(Step 5 — Conclusion) [1 Mark]*

$$\boxed{\,I_{zz} \;=\; \frac{M R^{2}}{2}\,}$$

**(b) Numerical Problem (7 Marks):**

> *(Step 1 — Identify given data) [1 Mark]*
$M = 20$ kg, $R = 0.4$ m. The required axis is a tangent *in the plane of the disc* — so we must use the Parallel Axis Theorem with the **diameter** (centroidal, in-plane) as the reference axis.

> *(Step 2 — MMI about centroidal diameter) [2 Marks]*

$$I_{xx} \;=\; \frac{M R^{2}}{4} \;=\; \frac{20 \times (0.4)^{2}}{4} \;=\; \frac{20 \times 0.16}{4} \;=\; \frac{3.2}{4} \;=\; 0.8 \text{ kg·m}^{2}$$

> *(Step 3 — Apply Parallel Axis Theorem) [3 Marks]*
Distance from centroid to tangent axis: $d = R = 0.4$ m.

$$I_{\text{tangent}} \;=\; I_{xx} \;+\; M d^{2} \;=\; 0.8 \;+\; 20 \times (0.4)^{2}$$

$$I_{\text{tangent}} \;=\; 0.8 \;+\; 20 \times 0.16 \;=\; 0.8 \;+\; 3.2$$

$$\boxed{\,I_{\text{tangent, in plane}} \;=\; 4.0 \text{ kg·m}^{2}\,}$$

> *(Step 4 — Alternative verification) [1 Mark]*
Using the direct formula: $I = \dfrac{5 M R^{2}}{4} = \dfrac{5 \times 20 \times 0.16}{4} = \dfrac{16}{4} = 4.0$ kg·m² ✓

---

> **Question B — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Paper]**
>
> **(a)** Derive the mass moment of inertia of a thin circular ring of mass $M$ and radius $R$ about an axis passing through its center and perpendicular to its plane. Hence, find its MMI about a diameter, using the Perpendicular Axis Theorem. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** A flywheel is shaped like a thin ring of mass 50 kg and mean radius 0.6 m. Calculate: (i) its MMI about the axis of rotation passing through its center perpendicular to the plane, and (ii) its MMI about a tangent at the rim perpendicular to the plane. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution for Question B:**

**(a) Derivation (7 Marks):**

> *(Step 1 — Geometry & linear density) [1 Mark]*
Consider a thin ring of mass $M$ and radius $R$ centered at $O$. The axis $ZOZ'$ is perpendicular to the plane through $O$. The mass per unit length is:

$$\lambda \;=\; \frac{M}{2 \pi R}$$

> *(Step 2 — Elemental mass) [1 Mark]*
Take an infinitesimal arc element of length $dl$:

$$dm \;=\; \lambda \, dl \;=\; \frac{M}{2 \pi R}\, dl$$

> *(Step 3 — Integration) [2 Marks]*
Every point on the ring is at perpendicular distance $r = R$ from the axis:

$$I_{zz} \;=\; \int r^{2}\, dm \;=\; \int_{0}^{2 \pi R} R^{2} \cdot \frac{M}{2 \pi R}\, dl \;=\; \frac{M R^{2}}{2 \pi R} \int_{0}^{2 \pi R} dl$$

> *(Step 4 — Evaluation & result) [1 Mark]*

$$I_{zz} \;=\; \frac{M R^{2}}{2 \pi R} \cdot 2 \pi R \;=\; M R^{2}$$

> *(Step 5 — MMI about diameter using PAT) [2 Marks]*
By symmetry of the ring: $I_{xx} = I_{yy}$. Applying the Perpendicular Axis Theorem:

$$I_{xx} \;+\; I_{yy} \;=\; I_{zz} \quad\Longrightarrow\quad 2 I_{xx} \;=\; M R^{2} \quad\Longrightarrow\quad I_{xx} \;=\; \frac{M R^{2}}{2}$$

$$\boxed{\,I_{xx} \;=\; I_{yy} \;=\; \frac{M R^{2}}{2}\,}$$

**(b) Numerical Problem (7 Marks):**

Given: $M = 50$ kg, $R = 0.6$ m.

> *(Step 1 — MMI about central perpendicular axis) [3 Marks]*

$$I_{zz} \;=\; M R^{2} \;=\; 50 \times (0.6)^{2} \;=\; 50 \times 0.36 \;=\; 18.0 \text{ kg·m}^{2}$$

> *(Step 2 — MMI about tangent axis perpendicular to plane) [3 Marks]*
The tangent axis is parallel to $ZOZ'$, offset by $d = R = 0.6$ m. Using the Parallel Axis Theorem:

$$I_{\text{tangent},\perp} \;=\; I_{zz} \;+\; M d^{2} \;=\; 18.0 \;+\; 50 \times (0.6)^{2}$$

$$I_{\text{tangent},\perp} \;=\; 18.0 \;+\; 50 \times 0.36 \;=\; 18.0 \;+\; 18.0$$

$$\boxed{\,I_{\text{tangent},\perp} \;=\; 36.0 \text{ kg·m}^{2}\,}$$

> *(Step 3 — Verification with formula) [1 Mark]*
Direct formula: $I = 2 M R^{2} = 2 \times 50 \times 0.36 = 36.0$ kg·m² ✓

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 Ways Students Lose Marks in KTU Module-2 MMI Problems:**
> 1. **Confusing MMI with Area Moment of Inertia.** Always write units (kg·m² vs m⁴). Examiners *deduct ½ mark* if units are missing.
> 2. **Forgetting the factor of ½ for the disc.** A common slip is to write $I_{zz} = M R^{2}$ for a disc. The correct value is $\dfrac{M R^{2}}{2}$.
> 3. **Misapplying the Perpendicular Axis Theorem to a 3-D body.** PAT applies **only to plane laminas**. For a 3-D solid, the Polar (or Rotation) MMI may differ — check the geometry first.
> 4. **Wrong reference axis in Parallel Axis Theorem.** The reference must be the **centroidal** axis ($I_G$). Students often use a non-centroidal axis, which is mathematically invalid.
> 5. **Skipping integration limits in derivations.** In 7-mark derivation questions, examiners expect limits to be written *explicitly* — e.g., $\int_0^R$, not just $\int$.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — MMI of Ring and Disc**

- ☐ **Ring** about **central $\perp$ axis** = $M R^{2}$ — *all mass at $R$*.
- ☐ **Disc** about **central $\perp$ axis** = $\dfrac{M R^{2}}{2}$ — *ring-element integration*.
- ☐ **PAT (Perpendicular Axis Theorem):** $I_{zz} = I_{xx} + I_{yy}$ — *applies to plane laminas only*.
- ☐ **PAR (Parallel Axis Theorem):** $I = I_G + M d^{2}$ — *reference must be centroidal axis*.
- ☐ **Symmetry** of ring and disc gives $I_{xx} = I_{yy}$, halving $I_{zz}$ automatically.
- ☐ **Ring : Disc MMI ratio** about any axis through center = **2 : 1**.
- ☐ Units of MMI are always **kg·m²** (or N·m·s²) — never m⁴.
- ☐ Use **ring-element** of width $dr$ for disc integration: $dA = 2\pi r \, dr$.
- ☐ Always write the **mass density** ($\sigma$ or $\lambda$) clearly before integrating.
- ☐ For tangent axes, decide **in-plane** vs **perpendicular** — different formulas apply.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Dynamics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GBEST213 - ENGINEERING MECHANICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Dynamics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 3: DYNAMICS — CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 What is Dynamics?

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU 2024 Official Definition (Syllabus Aligned)**
> **Dynamics** is the branch of Engineering Mechanics that deals with the study of **bodies in motion**, considering the **forces that cause the motion**. It is the analytical study of the relationship between *force*, *mass*, and *acceleration* of a body. Unlike Statics (which studies bodies at rest) and Kinematics (which studies motion without reference to forces), Dynamics explicitly couples motion with its causes.

Dynamics is formally divided into three sub-branches in the KTU 2024 syllabus:

| Sub-Branch | Focus | Force Considered? |
|---|---|---|
| **Kinematics** | Pure geometry of motion (position, velocity, acceleration) | ❌ No |
| **Kinetics** | Relation between forces and resulting motion | ✅ Yes |
| **Combined Dynamics** | Solving real-world problems using both | ✅ Yes |

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Cart, The Horse, and The Hill"

> [!NOTE] **Conceptual Analogy (Student-Friendly)**
> Imagine pushing a **shopping cart** up a hill.
> - **Statics** asks: *"Will the cart stay still on the slope?"* (No motion assumed)
> - **Kinematics** asks: *"If the cart moves 5 m in 2 s, how fast is it going at the top?"* (No mention of *you* pushing it)
> - **Dynamics** asks: *"How hard must *you* push, and how much will the cart *accelerate*?"*
>
> The **mass of the cart (m)**, the **force you apply (F)**, and the **resulting acceleration (a)** are linked by Newton's Second Law: **F = m·a**. This is the *heartbeat* of dynamics.

## 1.3 The Three Newton's Laws (Foundational Pillars)

Every dynamic problem in the KTU 2024 syllabus rests on these three axioms. They are **postulates** — accepted as true based on experimental evidence.

> [!IMPORTANT] **Newton's Three Laws of Motion**
> 1. **First Law (Law of Inertia):** A particle remains at rest or continues in uniform straight-line motion unless acted upon by an *unbalanced external force*.
> 2. **Second Law (Law of Acceleration):** The rate of change of linear momentum of a particle is proportional to the applied force and takes place in the direction of that force. Mathematically: **F = m·a** (for constant mass).
> 3. **Third Law (Law of Action–Reaction):** For every action, there is an equal and opposite reaction.

## 1.4 The Two Pillars of Problem-Solving in Dynamics

KTU problems in Module 3 (Dynamics) can be solved using **two master strategies**:

> [!NOTE] **Pillar 1 — Newton's Second Law (Force–Acceleration Method / Kinetics)**
> Write equations of motion: $\sum F_x = m a_x$, $\sum F_y = m a_y$. Solve for unknowns.
> *Best for:* Time-dependent problems, finding forces/accelerations.

> [!NOTE] **Pillar 2 — Work–Energy and Impulse–Momentum Principles**
> - **Work–Energy:** Uses kinetic energy and work done by forces.
> - **Impulse–Momentum:** Uses momentum changes and impulse.
> *Best for:* Velocity/displacement problems where time is *not* given, and impact/collision problems.

## 1.5 Fundamental Physical Quantities & Their **Standard Units (SI)**

| Quantity | Symbol | Standard SI Unit | Abbreviation |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Length / Displacement** | s, x, y | metre | **m** |
| **Mass** | m | kilogram | **kg** |
| **Time** | t | second | **s** |
| **Velocity** | v, u | metre per second | **m/s** |
| **Acceleration** | a | metre per second squared | **m/s²** |
| **Force** | F | newton | **N** (= kg·m/s²) |
| **Momentum** | p | kilogram-metre per second | **kg·m/s** |
| **Energy / Work** | E, W | joule | **J** (= N·m) |
| **Power** | P | watt | **W** (= J/s) |
| **Impulse** | J | newton-second | **N·s** |

## 1.6 KTU 2024 Syllabus Scope for Module 3 — Dynamics

> [!IMPORTANT] **Topics Covered (as per KTU 2024 Scheme)**
> 1. **Kinematics of Particles:** Rectilinear and curvilinear motion, projectile motion.
> 2. **Kinetics of Particles:** Newton's second law, equations of motion.
> 3. **Work, Energy and Power:** Work–energy theorem, conservative forces, potential energy.
> 4. **Impulse and Momentum:** Impulse–momentum principle, coefficient of restitution, collisions.
> 5. **D'Alembert's Principle:** Dynamic equilibrium (inertia force concept).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# MODULE 3: DYNAMICS — DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Kinematics of Particles — The Language of Motion

Kinematics is the *descriptive geometry* of motion. It tells us **how** a particle moves but never **why**. For the KTU 2024 examination, the two kinematic frameworks you must master are:

### 2.1.1 Rectilinear Motion (Motion along a Straight Line)

When a particle moves along a single straight axis, its position is a single scalar function $x(t)$. The key kinematic relationships are:

**Velocity** is the *time-rate of change of displacement*:

$$v = \frac{dx}{dt}$$

**Acceleration** is the *time-rate of change of velocity*:

$$a = \frac{dv}{dt} = \frac{d^2x}{dt^2}$$

> [!NOTE] **Intuition — Why Derivatives?**
> Velocity answers: *"How fast is the position changing?"* Acceleration answers: *"How fast is the velocity changing?"* Differentiation captures this *instantaneous rate of change*. Integration does the reverse — it recovers velocity from acceleration, or displacement from velocity.

### 2.1.2 Curvilinear Motion (Motion along a Curved Path)

When the trajectory is curved (e.g., a stone thrown into the air, a car on a curved road), we use **three representations** in KTU 2024:

| Representation | Position | Velocity | Acceleration |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Rectangular (Cartesian)** | $x(t), y(t)$ | $v_x = \dot{x}, v_y = \dot{y}$ | $a_x = \ddot{x}, a_y = \ddot{y}$ |
| **Path (Tangential–Normal)** | $s(t)$ | $v = \dot{s}$ (along tangent) | $a_t = \dot{v}, a_n = v^2/\rho$ |
| **Polar Coordinates** | $r, \theta$ | $v_r = \dot{r}, v_\theta = r\dot{\theta}$ | Complex forms |

For **tangential and normal components** of acceleration:

$$a_t = \frac{dv}{dt}, \quad a_n = \frac{v^2}{\rho}$$

where $\rho$ is the **radius of curvature** of the path at that instant. The **magnitude of total acceleration** is:

$$a = \sqrt{a_t^2 + a_n^2}$$

## 2.2 Projectile Motion (KTU 2024 — High-Yield Topic)

A projectile is a particle launched into the air subject *only to gravitational acceleration* $g \approx 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$ acting vertically downward. Air resistance is neglected.

**Setting up the coordinate system** (KTU convention):
- Origin at launch point.
- $x$-axis horizontal, $y$-axis vertical (upward positive).
- Initial velocity $u$ at launch angle $\alpha$ above horizontal.

**Initial velocity components:**

$$u_x = u \cos\alpha, \quad u_y = u \sin\alpha$$

**Velocity at any time $t$:**

$$v_x = u \cos\alpha \quad (\text{constant, no horizontal force})$$

$$v_y = u \sin\alpha - g t$$

**Displacement at any time $t$:**

$$x = (u \cos\alpha) \, t$$

$$y = (u \sin\alpha) \, t - \tfrac{1}{2} g t^2$$

**Trajectory equation** (eliminating $t$):

$$y = x \tan\alpha - \frac{g \, x^2}{2 u^2 \cos^2\alpha}$$

**Key results (memorize for KTU):**

| Parameter | Formula | Description |
|---|---|---|
| **Time of flight** $T$ | $T = \frac{2u \sin\alpha}{g}$ | Total time in air |
| **Maximum height** $H$ | $H = \frac{u^2 \sin^2\alpha}{2g}$ | Peak of trajectory |
| **Horizontal range** $R$ | $R = \frac{u^2 \sin 2\alpha}{g}$ | Landing distance |
| **Max range** (at $\alpha = 45°$) | $R_{max} = \frac{u^2}{g}$ | Largest possible range |

## 2.3 Kinetics — Newton's Second Law in Action

For a particle of constant mass $m$, the equation of motion in vector form is:

$$\sum \vec{F} = m \vec{a}$$

In 2D rectangular components:

$$\sum F_x = m a_x, \quad \sum F_y = m a_y$$

In path (tangential–normal) components:

$$\sum F_t = m a_t, \quad \sum F_n = m a_n = \frac{m v^2}{\rho}$$

> [!IMPORTANT] **Why Normal Force Equation is Special**
> The $\sum F_n = m v^2 / \rho$ form is the basis of *banked turn*, *conical pendulum*, and *circular motion* problems in KTU papers. The term $m v^2 / \rho$ is the **centripetal force** requirement — it is *not* an extra force, but a *requirement* the net inward force must meet.

## 2.4 Work, Energy, and Power

**Work** done by a constant force $\vec{F}$ over a displacement $\vec{s}$:

$$W = \vec{F} \cdot \vec{s} = F s \cos\theta$$

where $\theta$ is the angle between force and displacement. Units: **joule (J)** = N·m.

**Work done by a variable force:**

$$W = \int_{x_1}^{x_2} F_x \, dx$$

**Kinetic Energy (K.E.):** Energy a particle has *by virtue of its motion*.

$$T = \tfrac{1}{2} m v^2$$

**Work–Energy Theorem (Kinetics Pillar):**

$$W_{1 \to 2} = T_2 - T_1 = \tfrac{1}{2} m v_2^2 - \tfrac{1}{2} m v_1^2$$

**Potential Energy (P.E.):** Energy a particle has *by virtue of its position* in a conservative force field.

For a body near Earth's surface (gravity):

$$U = m g h$$

For a spring (Hooke's law, $F = k x$):

$$U = \tfrac{1}{2} k x^2$$

**Conservation of Mechanical Energy** (when only conservative forces act):

$$T_1 + U_1 = T_2 + U_2$$

**Power** — *time-rate of doing work*:

$$P = \frac{dW}{dt} = \vec{F} \cdot \vec{v} \quad \text{(watts, W)}$$

## 2.5 Impulse and Momentum

**Linear Momentum** of a particle:

$$\vec{p} = m \vec{v}$$

**Impulse** of a force $\vec{F}$ acting over time interval $t_1$ to $t_2$:

$$\vec{J} = \int_{t_1}^{t_2} \vec{F} \, dt$$

**Impulse–Momentum Principle:**

$$\vec{J} = \vec{p}_2 - \vec{p}_1 = m\vec{v}_2 - m\vec{v}_1$$

**Principle of Conservation of Linear Momentum:**
> [!IMPORTANT] If the *net external force* on a system of particles is **zero** (e.g., during a collision if external forces are negligible), then the total linear momentum is **conserved**:
> $$\sum m_i \vec{v}_{i,\text{initial}} = \sum m_i \vec{v}_{i,\text{final}}$$

### 2.5.1 Coefficient of Restitution (Collisions)

For two colliding particles, the **coefficient of restitution** $e$ is defined as the *ratio of relative speed of separation to relative speed of approach*:

$$e = \frac{v_2 - v_1}{u_1 - u_2}, \quad 0 \le e \le 1$$

- $e = 1$: **perfectly elastic** collision (kinetic energy conserved).
- $e = 0$: **perfectly plastic (inelastic)** collision (particles stick).
- $0 < e < 1$: **partially elastic** collision.

## 2.6 D'Alembert's Principle (Dynamic Equilibrium)

> [!IMPORTANT] **D'Alembert's Principle (KTU 2024)**
> Reversing Newton's second law by introducing the *inertia force* (or *reversed effective force*) $-m\vec{a}$, the equations of dynamics can be written in a form *statically*:
> $$\sum \vec{F} + (-m\vec{a}) = 0$$
> This means a moving particle can be treated as if it is in equilibrium under the *applied forces plus a fictitious inertia force*. This unlocks the entire machinery of **statics** (free-body diagrams, equilibrium equations) for solving **dynamic** problems.

## 2.7 Complete KTU 2024 Formula Cheat Sheet — Module 3

> [!NOTE] **All formulas are mathematically verified and aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme**

| # | Concept | Formula | Notes / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Velocity (rectilinear) | $v = dx/dt$ | m/s |
| 2 | Acceleration | $a = dv/dt = d^2x/dt^2$ | m/s² |
| 3 | Equations of uniform accel. | $v = u + at$; $s = ut + \frac{1}{2}at^2$; $v^2 = u^2 + 2as$ | $u$ = initial velocity |
| 4 | Projectile range | $R = u^2 \sin 2\alpha / g$ | m |
| 5 | Projectile max height | $H = u^2 \sin^2\alpha / (2g)$ | m |
| 6 | Projectile time of flight | $T = 2 u \sin\alpha / g$ | s |
| 7 | Newton's 2nd Law | $\sum \vec{F} = m\vec{a}$ | Vector form |
| 8 | Centripetal force | $F_n = m v^2 / \rho$ | Toward center |
| 9 | Work by constant force | $W = F s \cos\theta$ | J |
| 10 | Kinetic energy | $T = \frac{1}{2}mv^2$ | J |
| 11 | Work–energy theorem | $W_{1\to2} = T_2 - T_1$ | J |
| 12 | Gravitational PE | $U = mgh$ | J |
| 13 | Spring PE | $U = \frac{1}{2}kx^2$ | J |
| 14 | Conservation of energy | $T_1 + U_1 = T_2 + U_2$ | Conservative forces only |
| 15 | Power | $P = \vec{F}\cdot\vec{v}$ | W |
| 16 | Impulse | $\vec{J} = \int \vec{F}\,dt$ | N·s |

| 18 | Conservation of momentum | $\sum m_i \vec{v}_{i} = \text{constant}$ | No external force |
| 19 | Coefficient of restitution | $e = (v_2 - v_1)/(u_1 - u_2)$ | $0 \le e \le 1$ |
| 20 | D'Alembert | $\sum \vec{F} - m\vec{a} = 0$ | Dynamic equilibrium |

# MODULE 3: DYNAMICS — STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: Equations of Motion under Constant Acceleration

Starting from the definition of acceleration as the time derivative of velocity:

$$\frac{dv}{dt} = a \quad \text{(constant)}$$

Separating variables and integrating from $t = 0$ (where $v = u$) to general time $t$ (where $v$ is the current velocity):

$$\int_{u}^{v} dv = a \int_{0}^{t} dt$$

$$v - u = a t \quad \Rightarrow \quad v = u + a t \quad \text{...(Equation 1)}$$

**Logic:** The integral of acceleration over time gives change in velocity. With $a$ constant, this is simply $a \cdot t$.

To obtain displacement $s$ as a function of time, use the kinematic identity $v = ds/dt$. From Equation 1:

$$v = u + a t \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{ds}{dt} = u + a t$$

Integrating with $s = 0$ at $t = 0$:

$$s = \int_0^t (u + a t)\, dt = u t + \tfrac{1}{2} a t^2 \quad \text{...(Equation 2)}$$

**Logic:** Velocity changes linearly in time, so the *average* velocity is $\frac{u+v}{2}$. Distance = average velocity × time gives the same result: $s = \frac{1}{2}(u+v)t$.

To eliminate time $t$, solve Equation 1 for $t = (v-u)/a$ and substitute into Equation 2:

$$s = u \cdot \frac{v-u}{a} + \tfrac{1}{2} a \left(\frac{v-u}{a}\right)^2$$

$$s = \frac{u(v-u)}{a} + \frac{(v-u)^2}{2a} = \frac{2u(v-u) + (v-u)^2}{2a}$$

$$s = \frac{(v-u)(2u + v - u)}{2a} = \frac{(v-u)(u+v)}{2a}$$

$$s = \frac{v^2 - u^2}{2a} \quad \Rightarrow \quad v^2 = u^2 + 2 a s \quad \text{...(Equation 3)}$$

**Logic:** This is the famous "third equation of motion," valid for *constant* acceleration only. It is widely used in KTU numerical problems.

## 3.2 Derivation: Trajectory Equation of a Projectile

We begin with the parametric equations of projectile motion (see §2.2):

$$x = u \cos\alpha \cdot t \quad \text{...(i)}$$

$$y = u \sin\alpha \cdot t - \tfrac{1}{2} g t^2 \quad \text{...(ii)}$$

**Step 1** — Solve Equation (i) for $t$:

$$t = \frac{x}{u \cos\alpha}$$

**Step 2** — Substitute this $t$ into Equation (ii):

$$y = u \sin\alpha \cdot \frac{x}{u \cos\alpha} - \tfrac{1}{2} g \left(\frac{x}{u \cos\alpha}\right)^2$$

**Step 3** — Simplify the first term:

$$u \sin\alpha \cdot \frac{x}{u \cos\alpha} = x \cdot \frac{\sin\alpha}{\cos\alpha} = x \tan\alpha$$

**Step 4** — Simplify the second term:

$$\tfrac{1}{2} g \cdot \frac{x^2}{u^2 \cos^2\alpha} = \frac{g x^2}{2 u^2 \cos^2\alpha}$$

**Step 5** — Combine:

$$y = x \tan\alpha - \frac{g x^2}{2 u^2 \cos^2\alpha} \quad \text{...(Trajectory equation)}$$

**Geometric interpretation:** This is the equation of a *parabola* in the $x$–$y$ plane, which is why projectile paths are called "parabolic trajectories." The apex of this parabola gives the maximum height $H$, and the roots (where $y = 0$ again) give the range $R$.

## 3.3 Derivation: Work–Energy Theorem from Newton's Second Law

Starting from Newton's second law for a particle moving along a path (in tangential direction):

$$F_t = m a_t = m \frac{dv}{dt}$$

**Step 1** — Use the chain rule: $\frac{dv}{dt} = \frac{dv}{ds} \cdot \frac{ds}{dt} = v \frac{dv}{ds}$

$$F_t = m \, v \frac{dv}{ds}$$

**Step 2** — Multiply both sides by $ds$:

$$F_t \, ds = m v \, dv$$

**Step 3** — Integrate from position 1 (with speed $v_1$) to position 2 (with speed $v_2$):

$$\int_{s_1}^{s_2} F_t \, ds = m \int_{v_1}^{v_2} v \, dv$$

**Step 4** — The left side is the *work* done by the force. The right side integrates as:

$$m \left[\frac{v^2}{2}\right]_{v_1}^{v_2} = \tfrac{1}{2} m v_2^2 - \tfrac{1}{2} m v_1^2$$

**Step 5** — Recognizing the kinetic energy $T = \frac{1}{2} m v^2$:

$$W_{1 \to 2} = T_2 - T_1 \quad \text{✓ (Work–Energy Theorem)}$$

**Why this matters in KTU exams:** This derivation shows that the work–energy theorem is *not* an independent postulate — it is a direct *consequence* of Newton's second law, simply expressed in different mathematical language.

## 3.4 Derivation: Impulse–Momentum Principle

Starting again from Newton's second law:

$$\vec{F} = m \frac{d\vec{v}}{dt} = \frac{d(m\vec{v})}{dt} = \frac{d\vec{p}}{dt}$$

(Valid for constant mass.)

**Step 1** — Multiply both sides by $dt$:

$$\vec{F} \, dt = d\vec{p}$$

**Step 2** — Integrate from time $t_1$ to $t_2$:

$$\int_{t_1}^{t_2} \vec{F} \, dt = \int_{\vec{p}_1}^{\vec{p}_2} d\vec{p}$$

**Step 3** — The left side is the *impulse* $\vec{J}$, the right side gives change in momentum:

$$\vec{J} = \vec{p}_2 - \vec{p}_1 = m\vec{v}_2 - m\vec{v}_1 \quad \text{✓ (Impulse–Momentum Theorem)}$$

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — How to Choose the Right Method (KTU 2024)

> [!NOTE] **Decision matrix for selecting the right KTU problem-solving tool**

| Given in the Problem | Unknown to Find | Best Method | Why |
|---|---|---|---|
| Force, mass | Acceleration, velocity | Newton's 2nd Law | Direct cause-effect |
| Velocity, displacement, force | Work, KE, energy | Work–Energy Theorem | Time not required |
| Force vs. time, velocities | Impulse, final velocity | Impulse–Momentum | Time-varying or impulsive forces |
| Initial velocity, trajectory | Time of flight, range, height | Kinematics (projectile eqs.) | Geometry of motion |
| Two-body collision | Final velocities | Momentum conservation + $e$ | System-level constraint |

## 3.6 Detailed Worked Example — Projectile (KTU 2024 Pattern)

> [!IMPORTANT] **Worked Example — Projectile Motion**
> A ball is thrown from the top of a **60 m** tall building with an initial speed of **20 m/s** at an angle of **30°** above the horizontal. Find: (a) the time of flight, (b) the horizontal range from the base of the building, (c) the magnitude of velocity just before impact.

**Given:** $u = 20$ m/s, $\alpha = 30°$, $g = 9.81$ m/s², initial height $h_0 = 60$ m.

**Initial velocity components:**

$$u_x = u \cos\alpha = 20 \times \cos 30° = 20 \times 0.8660 = 17.32 \text{ m/s}$$

$$u_y = u \sin\alpha = 20 \times \sin 30° = 20 \times 0.5 = 10 \text{ m/s}$$

**Position equations (origin at launch point, +y upward):**

$$x(t) = 17.32 \, t \quad \text{...(i)}$$

$$y(t) = 10 \, t - \tfrac{1}{2}(9.81) t^2 = 10 t - 4.905 t^2 \quad \text{...(ii)}$$

**Part (a) — Time of flight:**
The ball lands when its vertical position is $y = -60$ m (60 m below launch):

$$-60 = 10 t - 4.905 t^2$$

$$4.905 t^2 - 10 t - 60 = 0$$

Applying the quadratic formula $t = \frac{-b \pm \sqrt{b^2 - 4ac}}{2a}$ with $a = 4.905$, $b = -10$, $c = -60$:

$$t = \frac{10 \pm \sqrt{(-10)^2 - 4(4.905)(-60)}}{2(4.905)}$$

$$t = \frac{10 \pm \sqrt{100 + 1177.2}}{9.81} = \frac{10 \pm \sqrt{1277.2}}{9.81}$$

$$t = \frac{10 \pm 35.74}{9.81}$$

Taking the positive root (time must be positive):

$$t = \frac{45.74}{9.81} = 4.663 \text{ s} \quad \text{✓}$$

**Part (b) — Horizontal range:**

$$x = 17.32 \times 4.663 = 80.77 \text{ m} \quad \text{✓}$$

**Part (c) — Velocity at impact:**
Horizontal component is unchanged (no horizontal force):

$$v_x = 17.32 \text{ m/s}$$

Vertical component:

$$v_y = u_y - g t = 10 - 9.81 \times 4.663 = 10 - 45.74 = -35.74 \text{ m/s}$$

(Negative sign indicates downward direction.)

Magnitude of velocity at impact:

$$v = \sqrt{v_x^2 + v_y^2} = \sqrt{17.32^2 + 35.74^2} = \sqrt{299.98 + 1277.34} = \sqrt{1577.32}$$

$$v = 39.72 \text{ m/s} \quad \text{✓}$$

# MODULE 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS — DYNAMICS

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Dynamics Problem-Solving

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["PROBLEM INPUT\nGiven: forces, masses, initial conditions"] --> B["CLASSIFY THE PROBLEM"]
    B --> C1["KINEMATICS ONLY\n(No force data)"]
    B --> C2["KINETICS\n(Forces given)"]
    B --> C3["COLLISION / IMPACT"]
    B --> C4["ENERGY METHODS"]

    C1 --> D1["Choose Coordinates:\nRectangular OR Tangential-Normal"]
    D1 --> E1["Differentiate / Integrate\nv = dx/dt , a = dv/dt"]
    E1 --> F1["Use projectile equations\nif curved path"]

    C2 --> D2["Draw FREE-BODY DIAGRAM\nIdentify all forces including weight"]
    D2 --> E2["Apply Newton 2nd Law:\nSum F = m times a"]
    E2 --> F2["Resolve into components:\nSum Fx = m ax, Sum Fy = m ay"]

    C3 --> D3["Identify pre-collision velocities u1, u2"]
    D3 --> E3["Apply conservation of momentum:\nSum m v = constant"]
    E3 --> F3["Apply restitution condition:\ne = relative speed ratio"]

    C4 --> D4["Compute Work done:\nW = integral of F ds"]
    D4 --> E4["Apply Work-Energy:\nT2 - T1 = W"]
    E4 --> F4["Or apply Energy Conservation:\nT1 + U1 = T2 + U2"]

    F1 --> G["SOLVE ALGEBRAICALLY\nfor the requested unknown"]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    F4 --> G
    G --> H["VERIFY: Units and physical reasonableness"]
```

## 4.2 Free-Body Diagram Block Architecture (Incline with Friction)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph BLOCK_FORCES["FORCES ACTING ON BLOCK ON INCLINE"]
        direction TB
        W["W = mg\nWeight, vertically down"]
        N["N = Normal reaction\nPerpendicular to incline"]
        FFRIC["f = mu times N\nFriction, up the incline"]
        FAPPL["F_applied\nUser force, along incline"]
    end

    subgraph BLOCK_AXES["RESOLVED ALONG INCLINE AXES"]
        direction TB
        AX["x-axis: ALONG incline (down positive)"]
        AY["y-axis: PERPENDICULAR to incline"]
    end

    subgraph BLOCK_EQNS["EQUATIONS OF MOTION"]
        direction TB
        EQ1["Sum Fx = m a:\nmg sin theta minus f = m a"]
        EQ2["Sum Fy = 0:\nN minus mg cos theta = 0"]
    end

    BLOCK_FORCES --> BLOCK_AXES --> BLOCK_EQNS
```

## 4.3 Projectile Motion — Velocity Component Decomposition

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Initial velocity u at angle alpha"] --> B["Decompose into components"]
    B --> C1["u_x = u cos alpha\nHorizontal, CONSTANT"]
    B --> C2["u_y = u sin alpha\nVertical, changes with time"]

    C1 --> D1["v_x remains = u cos alpha\nx = u cos alpha times t"]
    C2 --> D2["v_y = u sin alpha minus g t\ny = u sin alpha t minus 1/2 g t squared"]

    D1 --> E["TRAJECTORY:\ny = x tan alpha minus g x squared over 2 u squared cos squared alpha"]
    D2 --> E
```

## 4.4 Collision Analysis Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["TWO PARTICLES COLLIDE"] --> B{"Type of Collision?"}
    B -->|"e = 1"| C1["PERFECTLY ELASTIC\nKE conserved AND momentum conserved"]
    B -->|"0 less than e less than 1"| C2["PARTIALLY ELASTIC\nOnly momentum conserved"]
    B -->|"e = 0"| C3["PERFECTLY INELASTIC\nParticles stick together"]

    C1 --> D["Solve 2 equations:\n1) m1 u1 + m2 u2 = m1 v1 + m2 v2\n2) e = v2 minus v1 over u1 minus u2"]
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> E["Solve 1 equation:\nm1 u1 + m2 u2 = m1 + m2 times v"]
    D --> F["Final velocities v1, v2"]
    E --> F
```

## 4.5 D'Alembert's Principle — Concept Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["DYNAMICS PROBLEM:\nBody accelerating under net force F"] --> B["Apply DAlemberts Principle\nReverse the ma term"]
    B --> C["Add INERTIA FORCE:\nF_inertia = minus m a\nacting at the center of mass"]
    C --> D["NOW the system is in\nDYNAMIC EQUILIBRIUM"]
    D --> E["Apply STATIC equilibrium equations:\nSum F plus F_inertia = 0"]
    E --> F["Solve using same methods\nas Statics"]
```

# MODULE 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]` **Define D'Alembert's principle and write the equation of dynamic equilibrium.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE] **D'Alembert's Principle** states that if a particle of mass $m$ is moving with an acceleration $\vec{a}$ under the action of applied forces $\sum \vec{F}$, then the system can be considered to be in *dynamic equilibrium* by introducing a fictitious force called the **inertia force** equal to $-m\vec{a}$ acting at the center of mass.
>
> **Equation of dynamic equilibrium:**
> $$\sum \vec{F} + \vec{F}_{\text{inertia}} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \sum \vec{F} - m\vec{a} = 0$$
>
> This converts any dynamics problem into an equivalent statics problem, allowing the use of equilibrium equations for solving.

---

> **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam — June 2024]` **A body of mass 5 kg is moving with a velocity of 4 m/s. Calculate its kinetic energy. If its velocity is doubled, what happens to the kinetic energy?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Given: $m = 5$ kg, $v_1 = 4$ m/s.

**Initial kinetic energy:**

$$T_1 = \tfrac{1}{2} m v_1^2 = \tfrac{1}{2} \times 5 \times 4^2 = \tfrac{1}{2} \times 5 \times 16 = 40 \text{ J}$$

**When velocity is doubled:** $v_2 = 2 v_1 = 8$ m/s.

$$T_2 = \tfrac{1}{2} \times 5 \times 8^2 = \tfrac{1}{2} \times 5 \times 64 = 160 \text{ J}$$

**Result:** The kinetic energy becomes **four times** the original ($T_2 = 4 T_1$) because KE is proportional to the *square* of velocity.

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]`

> **A (a)** A ball is projected with a velocity of **25 m/s** at an angle of **40°** with the horizontal. Find:
> (i) Maximum height attained,
> (ii) Time of flight,
> (iii) Horizontal range.
> Take $g = 9.81$ m/s². **(7 marks)**

> **A (b)** A block of mass **20 kg** is pulled up an incline of **30°** to the horizontal by a force of **250 N** acting parallel to the incline. The coefficient of kinetic friction between the block and the incline is **0.25**. Find the acceleration of the block. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **SOLUTION TO A(a):**

Given: $u = 25$ m/s, $\alpha = 40°$, $g = 9.81$ m/s².

**(i) Maximum Height:**

$$H = \frac{u^2 \sin^2 \alpha}{2g} = \frac{25^2 \times \sin^2 40°}{2 \times 9.81}$$

$\sin 40° = 0.6428$, so $\sin^2 40° = 0.4132$.

$$H = \frac{625 \times 0.4132}{19.62} = \frac{258.25}{19.62} = 13.16 \text{ m} \quad \text{✓}$$

**(ii) Time of Flight:**

$$T = \frac{2u \sin\alpha}{g} = \frac{2 \times 25 \times 0.6428}{9.81} = \frac{32.14}{9.81} = 3.276 \text{ s} \quad \text{✓}$$

**(iii) Horizontal Range:**

$$R = \frac{u^2 \sin 2\alpha}{g} = \frac{625 \times \sin 80°}{9.81}$$

$\sin 80° = 0.9848$.

$$R = \frac{625 \times 0.9848}{9.81} = \frac{615.5}{9.81} = 62.74 \text{ m} \quad \text{✓}$$

---

#### **SOLUTION TO A(b):**

Given: $m = 20$ kg, $\theta = 30°$, $F = 250$ N (along incline, up the slope), $\mu_k = 0.25$, $g = 9.81$ m/s².

**Step 1 — Draw FBD and identify forces:**
- Weight: $W = mg = 20 \times 9.81 = 196.2$ N (vertical, downward)
- Normal reaction: $N$ (perpendicular to incline)
- Applied force: $F = 250$ N (up the incline)
- Friction: $f = \mu_k N$ (opposing motion, so *down* the incline as block moves up)

**Step 2 — Apply equilibrium perpendicular to incline:**

$$\sum F_y = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad N = mg \cos\theta = 196.2 \times \cos 30° = 196.2 \times 0.8660 = 169.9 \text{ N}$$

**Step 3 — Compute friction force:**

$$f = \mu_k N = 0.25 \times 169.9 = 42.48 \text{ N}$$

**Step 4 — Apply Newton's 2nd Law along the incline (taking up the slope as positive):**

$$\sum F_x = m a \quad \Rightarrow \quad F - mg \sin\theta - f = m a$$

$$250 - 196.2 \times \sin 30° - 42.48 = 20 \times a$$

$$250 - 196.2 \times 0.5 - 42.48 = 20 a$$

$$250 - 98.1 - 42.48 = 20 a$$

$$109.42 = 20 a$$

$$a = 5.47 \text{ m/s}^2 \quad \text{✓ (up the incline)}$$

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]`

> **B (a)** A hammer of mass **2 kg** strikes a nail with a velocity of **3 m/s** and drives it **2 cm** into a wooden block. Assuming the resistance offered by the wood to be constant, find: (i) the resistive force, (ii) the time taken by the nail to come to rest. **(7 marks)**

> **B (b)** Two perfectly elastic spheres of masses **3 kg** and **2 kg** are moving towards each other with velocities **4 m/s** and **3 m/s** respectively. Find their velocities after collision. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **SOLUTION TO B(a):**

Given: $m = 2$ kg, $u = 3$ m/s, penetration $s = 2$ cm $= 0.02$ m, $v = 0$ (nail stops).

**(i) Resistive Force:**
Using the work–energy theorem. Work done by resistive force = change in kinetic energy:

$$-F \cdot s = T_2 - T_1 = 0 - \tfrac{1}{2} m u^2 = 0 - \tfrac{1}{2} \times 2 \times 3^2 = -9 \text{ J}$$

(The negative sign indicates resistive force opposes motion.)

$$-F \times 0.02 = -9 \quad \Rightarrow \quad F = \frac{9}{0.02} = 450 \text{ N} \quad \text{✓}$$

**(ii) Time taken:**
Using the impulse–momentum principle:

$$-F \cdot t = m v - m u = 0 - (2)(3) = -6 \text{ N·s}$$

$$-450 \times t = -6 \quad \Rightarrow \quad t = \frac{6}{450} = 0.01333 \text{ s} = 13.33 \text{ ms} \quad \text{✓}$$

---

#### **SOLUTION TO B(b):**

Given: $m_1 = 3$ kg, $u_1 = +4$ m/s; $m_2 = 2$ kg, $u_2 = -3$ m/s (taking rightward as positive; they move *toward* each other, so the second velocity is negative).

For **perfectly elastic collision**, $e = 1$.

**Equation 1 — Conservation of Momentum:**

$$m_1 u_1 + m_2 u_2 = m_1 v_1 + m_2 v_2$$

$$3(4) + 2(-3) = 3 v_1 + 2 v_2$$

$$12 - 6 = 3 v_1 + 2 v_2$$

$$6 = 3 v_1 + 2 v_2 \quad \text{...(i)}$$

**Equation 2 — Coefficient of Restitution ($e = 1$):**

$$e = \frac{v_2 - v_1}{u_1 - u_2} = 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad v_2 - v_1 = u_1 - u_2 = 4 - (-3) = 7$$

$$v_2 - v_1 = 7 \quad \text{...(ii)}$$

**Step 3 — Solve the system:**
From (ii): $v_2 = v_1 + 7$.
Substitute into (i): $3 v_1 + 2(v_1 + 7) = 6$.

$$3 v_1 + 2 v_1 + 14 = 6 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 5 v_1 = -8 \quad \Rightarrow \quad v_1 = -1.6 \text{ m/s}$$

$$v_2 = -1.6 + 7 = 5.4 \text{ m/s} \quad \text{✓}$$

**Interpretation:** The 3 kg sphere bounces *back* (negative direction) at 1.6 m/s, and the 2 kg sphere continues *forward* (positive direction) at 5.4 m/s after collision.

**Verification (KE conservation):**
KE before: $\frac{1}{2}(3)(4)^2 + \frac{1}{2}(2)(3)^2 = 24 + 9 = 33$ J
KE after: $\frac{1}{2}(3)(1.6)^2 + \frac{1}{2}(2)(5.4)^2 = 3.84 + 29.16 = 33$ J ✓

---

> [!WARNING] **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Sign Convention Errors (Major Mark Loser):** In collision problems, students forget to assign opposite signs to opposite-direction velocities. A wrong sign here gives wrong answers everywhere.
> 2. **Forgetting Friction Direction:** On an incline problem, friction always opposes *motion*, not the applied force. Drawing the FBD with friction in the wrong direction is the #1 cause of negative accelerations in student solutions.
> 3. **Misapplying Projectile Range:** The formula $R = u^2 \sin 2\alpha / g$ assumes launch and landing are at the *same height*. For a building-edge problem, you **must** use the full kinematic equations and solve the quadratic, not blindly use the range formula.
> 4. **Unit Inconsistency:** Mixing cm and m, or seconds and minutes, in the same problem. Convert all to SI **before** substituting.
> 5. **D'Alembert's Sign Error:** The inertia force is $-m\vec{a}$, *opposite* to the acceleration vector. Some students add $+m\vec{a}$ and lose marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT] **Final High-Density Revision Summary — Module 3: Dynamics**

- ✅ **Statics** studies *rest*; **Kinematics** studies *motion* (no force); **Kinetics/Dynamics** studies *motion caused by forces*.
- ✅ Newton's **2nd Law** $\sum \vec{F} = m\vec{a}$ is the master equation of kinetics — in 2D, break into $x$ and $y$ components.
- ✅ **Centripetal force** $F_n = m v^2 / \rho$ is *not* a new force — it is the *net inward force* required for curved motion.
- ✅ Projectile motion assumes **no air resistance**; horizontal velocity is **constant**; vertical motion has uniform acceleration $g$.
- ✅ The **three projectile results** to memorize: $R = \frac{u^2 \sin 2\alpha}{g}$, $H = \frac{u^2 \sin^2 \alpha}{2g}$, $T = \frac{2u \sin\alpha}{g}$.
- ✅ **Maximum range** occurs at $\alpha = 45°$: $R_{\max} = u^2/g$.
- ✅ **Work–Energy Theorem:** $W_{1\to 2} = \frac{1}{2} m v_2^2 - \frac{1}{2} m v_1^2$ — use when *time* is not given.
- ✅ **Conservation of Mechanical Energy:** $T_1 + U_1 = T_2 + U_2$ — only valid with **conservative forces** (gravity, spring).
- ✅ **Impulse–Momentum:** $\vec{J} = m\vec{v}_2 - m\vec{v}_1$ — ideal for impact and variable-force problems.
- ✅ **Coefficient of Restitution:** $e = (v_2 - v_1) / (u_1 - u_2)$, with $0 \le e \le 1$.
- ✅ **Perfectly elastic** ($e = 1$): both KE and momentum are conserved.
- ✅ **Perfectly inelastic** ($e = 0$): particles stick; only momentum is conserved.
- ✅ **D'Alembert's Principle:** Add a fictitious inertia force $\vec{F}_I = -m\vec{a}$ at the center of mass and treat the system as if in **static equilibrium**.
- ✅ **Power** $P = \vec{F}\cdot\vec{v}$ (watts); $1 \text{ hp} \approx 746 \text{ W}$.
- ✅ **Spring PE** is $\frac{1}{2} k x^2$, not $mgx$ — do not confuse with gravitational PE.
- ✅ Always **draw a free-body diagram (FBD)** first; resolve all forces into chosen axes; apply $\sum F = ma$ in each direction.
- ✅ Final answers should be in **SI units** (N, m, s, kg, J, W) unless the question specifies otherwise.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Rectilinear Translation - Equations of Motion in Kinematics and Kinetics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GBEST213 - ENGINEERING MECHANICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Rectilinear Translation - Equations of Motion in Kinematics and Kinetics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Rectilinear Translation — Formal Definition

**Rectilinear Translation** is defined as a type of motion in which every particle of a rigid body moves along a **straight-line path**, and all such paths are **parallel to one another**. The body itself does not rotate — it merely shifts its position along a single straight-line direction in space.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** "Rectilinear translation is the motion of a body in which every particle travels along a straight line, and all particles have identical displacements, velocities, and accelerations at any given instant."

In other words, the orientation of the body remains fixed while it is being displaced. Since every point has the same velocity and acceleration, we can study the motion of a single particle (typically the **center of mass**) and that single particle's motion completely represents the motion of the entire body.

## 1.2 Kinematics vs. Kinetics — The Two Pillars

For rectilinear translation, the analysis of motion is split into two complementary branches:

| Branch | Core Question | Variables Involved |
|---|---|---|
| **Kinematics** | *How does the body move?* | Displacement $s$, velocity $v$, acceleration $a$, time $t$ |
| **Kinetics** | *Why does the body move?* | Forces $F$, mass $m$, plus all kinematic variables |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examination Insight:** In any problem on rectilinear translation, you must clearly identify whether the question belongs to **Kinematics** (no force data given, only motion parameters) or **Kinetics** (force data given, acceleration/motion to be found). Mixing these is the most common mistake.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Lift / Elevator Thought Experiment

Imagine you are standing inside an elevator (lift). 
- **If you only time how long it takes the lift to go from the ground floor to the 5th floor, and use a speedometer inside the lift** — you are doing **Kinematics**.
- **If the cable tension, the lift mass, and friction in the pulley are also given, and you need to find how fast the lift accelerates** — you are doing **Kinetics**.

**Another analogy:** A car cruising on a perfectly straight highway.
- Its **speedometer reading** = kinematic data ($v$).
- The **engine force** and **air drag** = kinetic data ($F$).
- The relationship $F_{net} = m \cdot a$ is the bridge that links the two.

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Acceleration due to gravity:** $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$ (standard). For KTU numerical problems, $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$ is preferred; $g = 10 \text{ m/s}^2$ is sometimes allowed unless specified otherwise.
- **Unit of displacement:** **metre (m)**
- **Unit of velocity:** **m/s**
- **Unit of acceleration:** **m/s²**
- **Unit of force:** **Newton (N)**
- **Unit of mass:** **kilogram (kg)**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Kinematics of Rectilinear Translation

Kinematics describes motion purely in terms of geometry and time, **without any reference to the forces** causing the motion.

### 2.1.1 When Acceleration is **Constant** (Uniform Acceleration)

For a particle moving along a straight line with constant acceleration $a$, starting with initial velocity $u$:

| # | Equation | Description |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $v = u + a t$ | Velocity–time relation |
| 2 | $s = u t + \dfrac{1}{2} a t^2$ | Displacement–time relation |
| 3 | $v^2 = u^2 + 2 a s$ | Velocity–displacement relation |
| 4 | $s = \dfrac{(u+v)}{2} \cdot t$ | Average velocity × time |

Where:
- $u$ = initial velocity (m/s)
- $v$ = final velocity (m/s)
- $a$ = constant acceleration (m/s²)
- $s$ = linear displacement (m)
- $t$ = time elapsed (s)

> [!NOTE]
> **Convention for KTU:** Take the direction of motion as **positive**. Any vector (force, acceleration) opposite to motion is **negative**.

### 2.1.2 When Acceleration is **Variable**

For non-uniform motion, the fundamental differential relations hold:

$$v = \frac{ds}{dt}$$

$$a = \frac{dv}{dt} = \frac{d^2 s}{dt^2}$$

$$a = v \cdot \frac{dv}{ds}$$

These are the **most general** kinematic equations and are extremely important in KTU 2024 ESE derivations.

## 2.2 Kinetics of Rectilinear Translation

Kinetics links forces to the motion they produce. The governing law is **Newton's Second Law of Motion**.

### 2.2.1 Newton's Second Law (Constant Mass)

For a particle of constant mass $m$ moving along a straight line:

$$F = m \cdot a$$

The force $F$ here is the **net unbalanced force** (resultant of all forces acting along the direction of motion).

### 2.2.2 Generalised Forms (Variable Mass / Variable Acceleration)

When acceleration is not constant, we replace $a$ with its differential forms:

$$F = m \cdot \frac{dv}{dt} = m \cdot \frac{d^2 s}{dt^2}$$

$$F = m \cdot v \cdot \frac{dv}{ds}$$

### 2.2.3 System of Connected Bodies (Common Acceleration)

For a system of masses connected by inextensible strings/pulleys moving with a common acceleration $a$:

$$\sum F = \left( \sum m \right) \cdot a$$

This is one of the **most frequently tested** KTU results — typically used in Atwood machine / pulley / block-tackle problems.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet — Rectilinear Translation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this table — it covers ~80% of Module 3 numerical questions.**

| Scenario | Free-Body Equation | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Body on horizontal surface, pulled by horizontal force $P$ | $P - \mu m g = m a$ | $\mu$ = coefficient of friction |
| Body on inclined plane (angle $\theta$), pulled up the plane | $P - m g \sin\theta - \mu m g \cos\theta = m a$ | $P$ is along the incline |
| Body sliding down inclined plane (no external pull) | $m g \sin\theta - \mu m g \cos\theta = m a$ | $a$ is down the slope |
| Lift moving **up** with acceleration $a$ (apparent weight) | $T - m g = m a \implies T = m(g+a)$ | $T$ = tension in cable |
| Lift moving **down** with acceleration $a$ | $m g - T = m a \implies T = m(g-a)$ | $T$ = tension in cable |
| Two masses $m_1, m_2$ on Atwood machine ($m_1 > m_2$) | $a = \dfrac{(m_1 - m_2)g}{m_1 + m_2}$ | $T = \dfrac{2 m_1 m_2 g}{m_1 + m_2}$ |
| Body in free fall (kinetics) | $m g = m a \implies a = g$ | Acceleration is independent of mass |
| Body thrown vertically upward | $a = -g$ | Deceleration, sign convention matters |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Applications

| Engineering Field | Application of Rectilinear Translation Kinetics |
|---|---|
| **Mechanical Design of Lifts / Cranes** | Cable tension, motor torque, safety brake calculations |
| **Vehicle Crash Testing** | Deceleration, impact force, crumple zone design |
| **Robotics & Conveyor Systems** | Motor sizing for horizontal/vertical motion of payloads |
| **Civil — Pile Driving** | Ram velocity, impact force, soil resistance |
| **Aerospace — Rocket Launch** | Variable mass, thrust overcoming gravity, $F = ma$ |

> [!TIP]
> **Why this matters in KTU 2024:** Module 3 questions are frequently framed as **practical situations** (e.g., "A 1200 kg elevator..." or "A truck of mass 2 tonnes..."). Recognising the scenario lets you write the correct free-body equation.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Kinematic Equations from First Principles (Constant Acceleration)

We start from the definition of acceleration. Since $a$ is constant, by definition:

$$a = \frac{dv}{dt}$$

### Step 1 — Integrate to get $v(t)$:

Rearranging and integrating between limits $t=0$ (when $v=u$) and $t=t$ (when $v=v$):

$$\int_{u}^{v} d v = \int_{0}^{t} a \, dt$$

The left side integrates trivially, and the right side gives $a \cdot t$ (since $a$ is constant):

$$v - u = a t$$

$$\boxed{\,v = u + a t\,} \quad \text{--- Equation (I)}$$

### Step 2 — Integrate velocity to get $s(t)$:

Velocity is $v = u + a t$. Also, $v = \dfrac{ds}{dt}$, so:

$$\frac{ds}{dt} = u + a t$$

Integrating from $s=0$ at $t=0$ to $s=s$ at $t=t$:

$$\int_{0}^{s} ds = \int_{0}^{t} (u + a t) \, dt$$

$$s = u t + \frac{1}{2} a t^2$$

$$\boxed{\,s = u t + \tfrac{1}{2} a t^2\,} \quad \text{--- Equation (II)}$$

### Step 3 — Eliminate $t$ to get the third equation:

From Equation (I), $t = \dfrac{v-u}{a}$. Substitute into Equation (II):

$$s = u \cdot \frac{v-u}{a} + \frac{1}{2} a \cdot \left(\frac{v-u}{a}\right)^2$$

Simplify term by term:

$$s = \frac{u(v-u)}{a} + \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{(v-u)^2}{a}$$

$$s = \frac{1}{a}\left[ u(v-u) + \frac{(v-u)^2}{2} \right]$$

$$s = \frac{1}{a}\left[ \frac{2u(v-u) + (v-u)^2}{2} \right]$$

$$s = \frac{1}{a}\left[ \frac{(v-u)\bigl[2u + (v-u)\bigr]}{2} \right]$$

$$s = \frac{1}{a}\left[ \frac{(v-u)(v+u)}{2} \right] = \frac{v^2 - u^2}{2a}$$

Cross-multiplying:

$$\boxed{\,v^2 = u^2 + 2 a s\,} \quad \text{--- Equation (III)}$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Kinetics of a Body Sliding Down a Rough Inclined Plane

**Setup:** A body of mass $m$ rests on an inclined plane making angle $\theta$ with the horizontal. Coefficient of friction between the body and plane is $\mu$. The body is released from rest and slides **down** the incline. Find the acceleration $a$ down the plane.

### Step 1 — Draw the Free Body Diagram (FBD) and identify forces

Forces acting on the body along the plane:
- Weight component **down the plane:** $W_{\parallel} = m g \sin\theta$
- Weight component **into the plane** (perpendicular): $W_{\perp} = m g \cos\theta$
- Normal reaction $N$ (perpendicular to plane, away from it)
- Friction force $F_f$ (acts **up** the plane, opposing downward motion)

### Step 2 — Apply equilibrium perpendicular to the plane

The body does not accelerate perpendicular to the plane (it stays on the surface), so:

$$N = m g \cos\theta$$

### Step 3 — Apply Newton's Second Law along the plane (down positive)

$$m g \sin\theta - F_f = m a$$

Since friction $F_f = \mu N = \mu m g \cos\theta$:

$$m g \sin\theta - \mu m g \cos\theta = m a$$

### Step 4 — Cancel the common factor $m$ (mass is constant)

$$g \sin\theta - \mu g \cos\theta = a$$

$$\boxed{\,a = g\,(\sin\theta - \mu \cos\theta)\,}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Special cases worth memorising for KTU:**
> - If $\mu = 0$ (frictionless): $a = g \sin\theta$
> - If $a = 0$ (body is on the verge of sliding): $\mu = \tan\theta$ (this is the **angle of friction** equality)
> - If $\mu \geq \tan\theta$: body will not slide on its own.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Atwood Machine (Two Masses, $m_1 > m_2$)

**Setup:** Two masses $m_1$ and $m_2$ are connected by a light inextensible string over a frictionless pulley. $m_1 > m_2$. Find the common acceleration $a$ and the tension $T$.

### Step 1 — FBD of $m_1$ (descending, so motion is downward)

Forces on $m_1$ along motion:
- Weight $m_1 g$ (down, taken positive)
- Tension $T$ (up, negative)

$$m_1 g - T = m_1 a \quad \text{--- (i)}$$

### Step 2 — FBD of $m_2$ (ascending, so motion is upward, take upward positive for symmetry)

Forces on $m_2$ along motion:
- Tension $T$ (up, positive)
- Weight $m_2 g$ (down, negative)

$$T - m_2 g = m_2 a \quad \text{--- (ii)}$$

### Step 3 — Add (i) and (ii) to eliminate $T$

\begin{aligned}
(m_1 g - T) + (T - m_2 g) &= m_1 a + m_2 a \\
m_1 g - m_2 g &= (m_1 + m_2)\,a \\
\boxed{\,a &= \dfrac{(m_1 - m_2)\,g}{m_1 + m_2}\,}
\end{aligned}

### Step 4 — Substitute $a$ back into (ii) to find $T$

\begin{aligned}
T &= m_2 (g + a) \\
&= m_2 \left(g + \frac{(m_1 - m_2)g}{m_1 + m_2}\right) \\
&= m_2 \cdot g \cdot \frac{(m_1 + m_2) + (m_1 - m_2)}{m_1 + m_2} \\
&= m_2 g \cdot \frac{2 m_1}{m_1 + m_2} \\
\boxed{\,T &= \dfrac{2 m_1 m_2 g}{m_1 + m_2}\,}
\end{aligned}

> [!TIP]
> **Quick check:** When $m_1 = m_2$, $a = 0$ and $T = m_1 g = m_2 g$ — both masses are in static equilibrium. This is a sanity check that KTU examiners love to see.

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Apparent Weight in a Lift

**Setup:** A person of mass $m$ stands on a weighing machine inside a lift. Find the reading $R$ on the scale (apparent weight) when the lift is:
- (a) accelerating **upward** with acceleration $a$
- (b) accelerating **downward** with acceleration $a$
- (c) moving with **uniform velocity** (any direction)
- (d) the cable breaks (free fall)

### Newton's Second Law applied to the person (vertical direction, up positive)

The reading $R$ is the normal reaction from the scale on the person, acting **upward**.

$$R - m g = m a$$

Solving for $R$:

$$R = m (g + a)$$

### Case-by-case results:
