```mermaid
        net2["Net Force: m2 times a\nDirection: UP"]
    end

    Mass1FBD --> pulleyNode
    Mass2FBD --> pulleyNode

    w1 --> net1
    t1 --> net1
    w2 --> net2
    t2 --> net2

    style pulleyNode fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style net1 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style net2 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style w1 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style w2 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style t1 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style t2 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.2 Body on Table Connected to Hanging Mass — Block Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph HorizontalTable["Smooth Horizontal Table"]
        m1Node["Mass m1\nAcceleration: a horizontal\nForces: T (forward), N (up), W1 (down)"]
    end

    subgraph PulleyEdge["Smooth Pulley at Edge"]
        pulleyTable["PULLEY\nTransmits tension T"]
    end

    subgraph HangingSide["Vertical Hanging Side"]
        m2Node["Mass m2\nAcceleration: a downward\nForces: T (up), W2 (down)"]
    end

    m1Node -- "String under tension T" --> pulleyTable
    pulleyTable -- "String under tension T" --> m2Node

    m1Node -. "Constraint: a1 = a2 = a" .-> m2Node

    style m1Node fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style m2Node fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style pulleyTable fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.3 Double Inclined Plane — Functional Flow Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PlaneAlpha["Incline Angle alpha"]
        incline1["Mass m1 on slope alpha\nMotion: DOWN the slope\nComponent along slope: m1 g sin alpha"]
    end

    subgraph ApexPulley["Pulley at Apex"]
        apexNode["PULLEY\nTension T uniform"]
    end

    subgraph PlaneBeta["Incline Angle beta"]
        incline2["Mass m2 on slope beta\nMotion: UP the slope\nComponent along slope: m2 g sin beta"]
    end

    incline1 -- "String tension T" --> apexNode
    apexNode -- "String tension T" --> incline2

    incline1 -. "Constraint: a1 = a2 = a" .-> incline2

    style incline1 fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style incline2 fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style apexNode fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.4 Movable Pulley System — Constraint Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph FixedSupport["Fixed Ceiling Support"]
        supportNode["CEILING FIXED END"]
    end

    subgraph MovablePulleyAssembly["Movable Pulley Assembly"]
        movableP["MOVABLE PULLEY\nCarries mass m1\nMoves UP at acceleration a1"]
    end

    subgraph HangingLoad["Hanging Load"]
        loadM["Mass m2\nMoves DOWN at acceleration a2"]
    end

    supportNode -- "String end fixed" --> movableP
    movableP -- "String passes over movable pulley" --> loadM
    movableP -- "String returns up to support" --> supportNode

    movableP -. "Constraint: a2 = 2 times a1" .-> loadM

    style supportNode fill:#D3D3D3,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style movableP fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style loadM fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **movable pulley** constraint $a_2 = 2a_1$ arises because when the load $m_2$ descends by a distance $x$, two segments of the string on either side of the movable pulley must each lengthen by $x/2$, so the movable pulley (carrying $m_1$) rises by $x/2$. Differentiating twice with respect to time gives the acceleration relationship.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]**

**Q1.** Two masses $5 \text{ kg}$ and $3 \text{ kg}$ are connected by a light inextensible string passing over a smooth, fixed pulley. Find the acceleration of the system and the tension in the string. Take $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$.

**Model Answer:**

Given: $m_1 = 5 \text{ kg}$ (heavier), $m_2 = 3 \text{ kg}$, $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$.

Using the Atwood machine formula:

$$
\begin{aligned}
a &= \frac{(m_1 - m_2) g}{m_1 + m_2} = \frac{(5 - 3) \times 9.81}{5 + 3} = \frac{2 \times 9.81}{8} = \frac{19.62}{8} \\
a &= 2.4525 \text{ m/s}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
T &= \frac{2 m_1 m_2 g}{m_1 + m_2} = \frac{2 \times 5 \times 3 \times 9.81}{8} = \frac{294.3}{8} \\
T &= 36.79 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** Acceleration $= 2.45 \text{ m/s}^2$ (downward for $m_1$, upward for $m_2$); Tension $= 36.79 \text{ N}$.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question]**

**Q2.** State the **constraint condition** for two bodies connected by an inextensible string passing over a smooth, fixed pulley. Why is it important to apply this condition before solving the equations of motion?

**Model Answer:**

The constraint condition for two bodies connected by an inextensible string over a smooth, fixed pulley is:

$$a_1 = a_2 = a$$

That is, the magnitudes of acceleration of both bodies are equal.

**Importance:** This condition arises from the geometric fact that the string length is constant. If one body moves down by a distance $x$, the other must move up by the same distance $x$ in the same time interval. This single equation reduces the number of independent unknowns (we have one $a$ instead of two), allowing us to solve the simultaneous equations of motion for both bodies. Without applying this constraint, we would have an underdetermined system with more unknowns than equations.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 3, Question A]**

#### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** Two masses $8 \text{ kg}$ and $6 \text{ kg}$ are connected by a light inextensible string passing over a smooth pulley fixed at the top of a double inclined plane. The angles of the plane with the horizontal are $30°$ and $60°$ for the $8 \text{ kg}$ and $6 \text{ kg}$ masses respectively. Assuming the inclined surfaces are smooth, find:

**(i)** the acceleration of the system,
**(ii)** the tension in the string, and
**(iii)** the magnitude and direction of the force exerted on the pulley. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** A $4 \text{ kg}$ block resting on a rough horizontal table (coefficient of friction $\mu = 0.25$) is connected by a string passing over a smooth pulley at the table edge to a $2 \text{ kg}$ block hanging vertically. Find the acceleration of the system and the tension in the string. **[7 marks]**

**Use $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$.**

---

#### Question A — Model Solutions

**Part (a) Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify Configuration**
- $m_1 = 8 \text{ kg}$ on $\alpha = 30°$ incline
- $m_2 = 6 \text{ kg}$ on $\beta = 60°$ incline

Compute the gravitational components along each slope:

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_1 g \sin\alpha &= 8 \times 9.81 \times \sin 30° = 8 \times 9.81 \times 0.5 = 39.24 \text{ N} \\
m_2 g \sin\beta &= 6 \times 9.81 \times \sin 60° = 6 \times 9.81 \times 0.866 = 50.97 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $m_2 g \sin\beta > m_1 g \sin\alpha$, the $6 \text{ kg}$ mass slides **down** its $60°$ incline, pulling the $8 \text{ kg}$ mass **up** its $30°$ incline.

**Step 2 — Apply Newton's Second Law**

For $m_1$ (up the $30°$ incline positive):
$$T - m_1 g \sin\alpha = m_1 a$$

For $m_2$ (down the $60°$ incline positive):
$$m_2 g \sin\beta - T = m_2 a$$

**Step 3 — Solve Simultaneously**

Adding both equations:

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_2 g \sin\beta - m_1 g \sin\alpha &= (m_1 + m_2) a \\
a &= \frac{g(m_2 \sin\beta - m_1 \sin\alpha)}{m_1 + m_2} \\
a &= \frac{9.81 \times (6 \times 0.866 - 8 \times 0.5)}{8 + 6} \\
a &= \frac{9.81 \times (5.196 - 4.0)}{14} \\
a &= \frac{9.81 \times 1.196}{14} \\
a &= \frac{11.733}{14} \\
a &= 0.838 \text{ m/s}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

Substituting back into the equation for $m_1$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T &= m_1 a + m_1 g \sin\alpha \\
T &= 8 \times 0.838 + 8 \times 9.81 \times 0.5 \\
T &= 6.704 + 39.24 \\
T &= 45.94 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Force on Pulley**

The string pulls the pulley with tension $T$ on each side. The two tension forces are directed along the strings (along the inclines). The resultant force on the pulley is the vector sum of the two tension vectors.

Angle between the two string segments at the pulley: $180° - (\alpha + \beta) = 180° - (30° + 60°) = 90°$.

For two equal tensions $T$ with angle $90°$ between them, the resultant magnitude is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{pulley} &= \sqrt{T^2 + T^2 + 2 T^2 \cos 90°} \\
F_{pulley} &= T \sqrt{2} = 45.94 \times 1.414 \\
F_{pulley} &= 64.96 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Direction:** The resultant bisects the angle between the two string segments. The bisector lies along the vertical downward direction (since $\alpha + \beta = 90°$, the bisector is exactly vertical).

**Final Answer for Part (a):**
- (i) $a = 0.838 \text{ m/s}^2$ (toward the $6 \text{ kg}$ mass side)
- (ii) $T = 45.94 \text{ N}$
- (iii) Force on pulley $= 64.96 \text{ N}$, directed vertically downward.

---

**Part (b) Solution:**

**Given:** $m_1 = 4 \text{ kg}$ (on rough table), $m_2 = 2 \text{ kg}$ (hanging), $\mu = 0.25$, $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$.

**Step 1 — Identify Forces**

For $m_1$ on table:
- Weight: $W_1 = m_1 g = 4 \times 9.81 = 39.24 \text{ N}$ (downward)
- Normal: $N = W_1 = 39.24 \text{ N}$ (upward, since no vertical motion)
- Friction: $f = \mu N = 0.25 \times 39.24 = 9.81 \text{ N}$ (opposing motion, i.e., away from pulley)
- Tension: $T$ (toward pulley)

For $m_2$ hanging:
- Weight: $W_2 = m_2 g = 2 \times 9.81 = 19.62 \text{ N}$ (downward)
- Tension: $T$ (upward)

**Step 2 — Apply Newton's Second Law**

For $m_1$ (horizontal, toward pulley positive):
$$T - \mu m_1 g = m_1 a$$
$$T - 9.81 = 4a \quad \text{...(1)}$$

For $m_2$ (downward positive):
$$m_2 g - T = m_2 a$$
$$19.62 - T = 2a \quad \text{...(2)}$$

**Step 3 — Solve**

Adding equations (1) and (2):

$$
\begin{aligned}
(T - 9.81) + (19.62 - T) &= 4a + 2a \\
9.81 &= 6a \\
a &= 1.635 \text{ m/s}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

Substituting into equation (2):

$$
\begin{aligned}
19.62 - T &= 2 \times 1.635 = 3.27 \\
T &= 19.62 - 3.27 = 16.35 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer for Part (b):**
- Acceleration $a = 1.635 \text{ m/s}^2$
- Tension $T = 16.35 \text{ N}$

---

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 3, Question B]**

#### Question B (14 Marks)

**(a)** A $10 \text{ kg}$ block is placed on a smooth horizontal surface and is connected by a light, inextensible string passing over a smooth pulley at the edge of the table to a movable pulley. The movable pulley carries a $6 \text{ kg}$ block. A second string passes over the movable pulley and is fixed to the ceiling on one end, while a $4 \text{ kg}$ block hangs from the other end. Determine:

**(i)** the acceleration of each block,
**(ii)** the tensions in the two strings, and
**(iii)** verify your answer using a free body diagram analysis. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** A chain of three masses $m_1 = 2 \text{ kg}$, $m_2 = 3 \text{ kg}$, and $m_3 = 5 \text{ kg}$ is connected by two light inextensible strings and pulled horizontally on a smooth surface by a horizontal force of $60 \text{ N}$ applied to $m_1$. Find the acceleration of the system and the tensions $T_1$ and $T_2$ in the two strings. **[7 marks]**

**Use $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$.**

---

#### Question B — Model Solutions

**Part (a) Solution:**

**Step 1 — System Setup**

Let:
- $A$ = the $10 \text{ kg}$ block on the table
- $B$ = the movable pulley (negligible mass) carrying the $6 \text{ kg}$ block
- $C$ = the $4 \text{ kg}$ hanging block

Strings and tensions:
- String 1: connects $A$ to movable pulley $B$, with tension $T_1$.
- String 2: passes under movable pulley $B$; one end fixed to ceiling, the other end attached to $C$, with tension $T_2$.

**Constraint Relation:**
If $C$ descends with acceleration $a_C$ and the movable pulley $B$ (with the $6 \text{ kg}$ mass) rises with acceleration $a_B$, then (from string 2 inextensibility):

$$a_C = 2 a_B \quad \text{(since the movable pulley is supported by two segments of string 2)}$$

From string 1 connecting $A$ to $B$ (one segment only):

$$a_A = a_B$$

Therefore: $a_A = a_B = a$, and $a_C = 2a$.

**Step 2 — Free Body Equations**

For block $A$ on the table (horizontal, toward pulley positive):
$$T_1 = m_A a = 10 a \quad \text{...(1)}$$

For the $6 \text{ kg}$ mass + movable pulley system. The movable pulley is in equilibrium of forces (massless pulley), so the two string-2 tensions pulling up on the pulley equal the tension $T_1$ pulling down on the pulley from string 1, plus the weight of the $6 \text{ kg}$ block:

Forces on the movable pulley assembly (6 kg block + pulley, mass $m_B = 6 \text{ kg}$):
- Upward: $2 T_2$ (two segments of string 2 pulling up)
- Downward: $T_1$ (string 1 pulling down toward block $A$) + $m_B g$ (weight of $6 \text{ kg}$)

Newton's Second Law for the $6 \text{ kg}$ block (upward positive, since it accelerates upward at $a$):

$$2 T_2 - T_1 - m_B g = m_B a$$
$$2 T_2 - T_1 - 6 \times 9.81 = 6 a$$
$$2 T_2 - T_1 - 58.86 = 6a \quad \text{...(2)}$$

For block $C$ (downward positive, accelerating at $2a$):
$$m_C g - T_2 = m_C \cdot 2a$$
$$4 \times 9.81 - T_2 = 4 \times 2a$$
$$39.24 - T_2 = 8a \quad \text{...(3)}$$

**Step 3 — Solve the System**

From (1): $T_1 = 10 a$.

Substitute into (2):

$$2 T_2 - 10 a - 58.86 = 6 a$$
$$2 T_2 = 16 a + 58.86$$
$$T_2 = 8 a + 29.43 \quad \text{...(2')}$$

Substitute (2') into (3):

$$39.24 - (8 a + 29.43) = 8 a$$
$$39.24 - 8a - 29.43 = 8a$$
$$9.81 = 16 a$$
$$a = 0.613 \text{ m/s}^2$$

Therefore:

$$
\begin{aligned}
a_A &= a_B = 0.613 \text{ m/s}^2 \\
a_C &= 2a = 1.226 \text{ m/s}^2 \\
T_1 &= 10 \times 0.613 = 6.13 \text{ N} \\
T_2 &= 8 \times 0.613 + 29.43 = 4.904 + 29.43 = 34.33 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Verification:** Plug back into (3): $39.24 - 34.33 = 4.91$, and $8 \times 0.613 = 4.904$ ✓ (matches to rounding).

**Final Answer for Part (a):**
- $a_A = a_B = 0.613 \text{ m/s}^2$ (block $A$ moves toward pulley, $6 \text{ kg}$ block moves up)
- $a_C = 1.226 \text{ m/s}^2$ (block $C$ moves down)
- $T_1 = 6.13 \text{ N}$
- $T_2 = 34.33 \text{ N}$

---

**Part (b) Solution:**

**Given:** Three masses in a line on a smooth horizontal surface, pulled by $F = 60 \text{ N}$ at the rear.

**Step 1 — Identify Configuration**
- $m_1 = 2 \text{ kg}$ (rear), connected to $m_2 = 3 \text{ kg}$ by string with tension $T_1$
- $m_2 = 3 \text{ kg}$ (middle), connected to $m_3 = 5 \text{ kg}$ by string with tension $T_2$
- $m_3 = 5 \text{ kg}$ (front, leading)
- All three move together with common acceleration $a$ (constraint: inextensible string)

**Step 2 — Apply Newton's Second Law to the Whole System**

For the system as a whole (since the surface is smooth, no friction):

$$
\begin{aligned}
F &= (m_1 + m_2 + m_3) a \\
60 &= (2 + 3 + 5) a \\
60 &= 10 a \\
a &= 6 \text{ m/s}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Find Tensions by Isolating Subsystems**

For $T_1$: Consider only $m_2$ and $m_3$ (the masses being pulled by $T_1$):
$$T_1 = (m_2 + m_3) a = (3 + 5) \times 6 = 48 \text{ N}$$

For $T_2$: Consider only $m_3$ (the mass being pulled by $T_2$):
$$T_2 = m_3 \times a = 5 \times 6 = 30 \text{ N}$$

**Verification using FBDs:**

For $m_1$ (forward positive): $F - T_1 = m_1 a$ → $60 - 48 = 12 = 2 \times 6$ ✓
For $m_2$ (forward positive): $T_1 - T_2 = m_2 a$ → $48 - 30 = 18 = 3 \times 6$ ✓
For $m_3$ (forward positive): $T_2 = m_3 a$ → $30 = 5 \times 6$ ✓

**Final Answer for Part (b):**
- Acceleration $a = 6 \text{ m/s}^2$
- $T_1 = 48 \text{ N}$
- $T_2 = 30 \text{ N}$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the constraint equation.** Students often write $\sum F = ma$ for each body but forget to apply $a_1 = a_2 = a$ (or the movable pulley version $a_2 = 2a_1$). Without the constraint, you have more unknowns than equations — **loss of 2–3 marks**.
>
> 2. **Sign convention errors in inclined plane problems.** The component of weight along the incline is $mg \sin\theta$, NOT $mg \cos\theta$. Mark loss: **2 marks per occurrence**.
>
> 3. **Confusing tension locations.** In a movable pulley, the tension in the string wrapped around the movable pulley ($T_2$ in our example) is generally **not equal** to the tension in the string attached to the movable pulley from outside ($T_1$). The two tensions differ unless specifically proved equal.
>
> 4. **Using $g = 9.81$ when problem data assume $g = 10$.** Always check the problem statement. If the problem says "use $g = 10$ m/s²" or the masses are in multiples of 5, use $g = 10$ for cleaner arithmetic. KTU often accepts either, but the cleaner answer may earn **bonus 1 mark for elegance**.
>
> 5. **Friction direction reversed.** Friction always opposes motion (or impending motion). On a body sliding to the right, friction acts to the **left**, regardless of the direction of any other applied force. Reversed friction gives the wrong magnitude of acceleration.
>
> 6. **Failing to draw the Free Body Diagram.** KTU examiners explicitly state that a correctly drawn FBD carries **2 marks** by itself in any dynamics problem. Skipping the FBD is the single most common reason for losing marks even when the final answer is correct.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Motion of Connected Bodies**

- ✅ The **single string, single fixed pulley** Atwood machine has $a = \dfrac{(m_1 - m_2)g}{m_1 + m_2}$ and $T = \dfrac{2 m_1 m_2 g}{m_1 + m_2}$.
- ✅ For a body on a **smooth horizontal table** connected to a hanging mass: $a = \dfrac{m_2 g}{m_1 + m_2}$ and $T = \dfrac{m_1 m_2 g}{m_1 + m_2}$.
- ✅ For **double inclined plane**: $a = \dfrac{g(m_1 \sin\alpha - m_2 \sin\beta)}{m_1 + m_2}$.
- ✅ For a **movable pulley** supporting one mass with another mass hanging from the string: the constraint is $a_{hanging} = 2 \times a_{movable}$.
- ✅ For **rough surfaces**, include a friction force $f = \mu N = \mu m g$ opposing motion in the Newton's Second Law equation.
- ✅ The **constraint equation** must be applied **before** solving the system of equations.
- ✅ The **Free Body Diagram (FBD)** is mandatory in KTU 2024 Scheme answers; a clear FBD is worth 2 marks by itself.
- ✅ Tension is **uniform** throughout a single string only if the pulley is massless and frictionless.
- ✅ For **three or more masses** in a line, isolate the rear-most body to find the rearmost tension; isolate the front body to find the leading tension.
- ✅ Always specify the **direction of assumed motion** in the solution. If the final answer for $a$ is negative, the actual motion is opposite to the assumed direction.
- ✅ Standard gravity: use $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$ unless the problem explicitly states $g = 10 \text{ m/s}^2$.
- ✅ Always **verify your answer** by plugging back into all original equations — this catches algebraic errors before submission.
- ✅ The **force on a pulley** equals the vector sum of the tensions on either side of the string. When the two strings make an angle $\theta$ at the pulley, the resultant has magnitude $2T \cos(\theta/2)$ and bisects the angle $\theta$.
- ✅ In a **movable pulley**, the tension in the string wrapped around the pulley is **half** the total load (if pulley is massless and frictionless and system is in equilibrium). During acceleration, the relationship is more complex and must be derived from Newton's Second Law.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Combined Motion of Translation and Rotation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GBEST213 - ENGINEERING MECHANICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Combined Motion of Translation and Rotation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal KTU Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Combined Motion of Translation and Rotation (General Plane Motion)** is the most general two-dimensional motion of a rigid body, in which every particle of the body simultaneously undergoes **curvilinear translation** of an arbitrary base point **B** and **rotation** about that same base point. Mathematically, the absolute velocity and absolute acceleration of any point **A** on the body are obtained by superposing the translation of a chosen reference point **B** with the circular motion of **A relative to B**.

Formally, the body has **three degrees of freedom** in the plane: two translational DOF of the reference point, plus one rotational DOF (angular displacement $\theta$).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Spinning Coin on a Moving Book"

Imagine a coin spinning (rotating) on a book that is itself being slid across a table (translating).

- The **center of the coin** traces a straight line across the table — this is the **translation**.
- At the same instant, the coin is **spinning** about its own center.
- A point on the **edge of the coin** is therefore moving in a complex cycloidal path. Its total velocity is **NOT** just the velocity of the center, nor is it just the spin velocity — it is the **vector sum** of both.

> [!NOTE]
> This is exactly what *combined motion* means: **Translation + Rotation = Most General Plane Motion.**

> [!TIP]
> **Memory hook:** "Carry the point, then spin it." Translation *carries* every point with the base; rotation *spins* the chosen point about the base.

## 1.3 Why This Topic Matters in Engineering

| Application | Role of Combined Motion |
|---|---|
| **Vehicle wheels** rolling on road | Translation of axle + rotation about axle |
| **Slider-crank mechanism** (IC engines) | Piston translation + crank rotation |
| **Robot manipulator arms** | Joint rotation + link translation |
| **Earth's rotation around Sun** | Orbital translation + spin rotation |
| **Rolling drum compactors** | Roller translation + drum spin |

The standard physical parameters used throughout this module:

- Linear velocity $V$ in **m/s**
- Linear acceleration $a$ in **m/s²**
- Angular velocity $\omega$ in **rad/s**
- Angular acceleration $\alpha$ in **rad/s²**
- Distances in **metres (m)**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Underlying Kinematic Logic (Step-by-Step)

To find the **absolute motion** of any point $A$ on a rigid body undergoing combined translation and rotation, we choose a second point $B$ on the *same* rigid body as a **reference**. The logic follows three rigid steps:

**Step 1 — Establish the Reference Point B**
Pick a point $B$ whose absolute motion (velocity and acceleration) is **already known** or easily computed (e.g., the center of a wheel, a pin, a guide contact point).

**Step 2 — Translation of A with B**
*Imagine the body is not rotating* but only translating with $B$. In this hypothetical scenario, every point (including $A$) would have the **same velocity and acceleration as $B$**. This gives the **transport (or carrier) motion**.

**Step 3 — Rotation of A about B**
*Now imagine the body is not translating* but only rotating about $B$. In this case, $A$ executes a **circular motion of radius equal to the distance $A$ to $B$**. This gives the **relative motion**.

**Step 4 — Vector Superposition**
Add the two vector contributions to get the **absolute motion of A**.

> [!NOTE]
> The order of addition **does not matter** because vector addition is commutative. However, drawing a clean vector diagram is mandatory for the KTU board exam.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Quantity | Vector Form | Magnitude | Direction |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Absolute velocity of A | $\vec{V}_A = \vec{V}_B + \vec{V}_{A/B}$ | — | — |
| 2 | Relative velocity (rotation) | $\vec{V}_{A/B} = \vec{\omega} \times \vec{r}_{A/B}$ | $V_{A/B} = \omega \cdot r_{A/B}$ | $\perp$ to line $AB$ in sense of $\omega$ |
| 3 | Absolute acceleration of A | $\vec{a}_A = \vec{a}_B + (\vec{a}_{A/B})_n + (\vec{a}_{A/B})_t$ | — | — |
| 4 | Normal (centripetal) component | $(\vec{a}_{A/B})_n = \omega^2 \vec{r}_{A/B}$ (towards B) | $a_n = \omega^2 \cdot r_{A/B}$ | From $A$ towards $B$ |
| 5 | Tangential component | $(\vec{a}_{A/B})_t = \alpha \vec{r}_{A/B} \perp$ | $a_t = \alpha \cdot r_{A/B}$ | $\perp$ to $AB$ in sense of $\alpha$ |
| 6 | Instantaneous centre distance | $r_{B/IC} = V_B / \omega$ | — | $\perp$ to $V_B$ |
| 7 | Point velocity via ICR | $V_A = \omega \cdot r_{A/IC}$ | — | $\perp$ to $IC \to A$ |
| 8 | Rolling without slip | $V_{centre} = \omega R$ | — | — |
| 9 | Number of DOF in plane motion | 3 | — | 2 translation + 1 rotation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Absolute signs in tables:** Use $\lvert x \rvert$ (LaTeX `\lvert \rvert`) or the word "modulus" to avoid breaking the markdown pipe `|`.

## 2.3 Engineering Utility

In **mechanical design** (e.g., cam-follower, four-bar linkage, planetary gear trains), engineers routinely need the velocity and acceleration of the *follower*, *coupler*, or *planet gear* — none of which is a fixed pivot. The relative-velocity/acceleration equations are the *workhorse tool* for kinematic analysis of such planar mechanisms.

## 2.4 Instantaneous Centre of Zero Velocity (ICR / Instant Centre)

> [!NOTE]
> At every instant, a rigid body in plane motion can be considered to be in **pure rotation** about a *single point* that happens to have **zero velocity** at that instant. This point is called the **Instantaneous Centre of Rotation (ICR)**, often denoted $I$ or $IC$.

### Rules to Locate the ICR

1. **Pure rotation case:** ICR = fixed pivot.
2. **Pure translation case:** ICR $\to \infty$ (no finite ICR exists).
3. **Point with known velocity $V$:** Draw a line **perpendicular to $V$** passing through that point. The ICR lies somewhere on this line.
4. **Point on a fixed surface with rolling without slipping:** ICR is the **point of contact** with the surface.
5. **Two points with non-parallel velocities:** ICR is at the **intersection of the two perpendiculars** to their velocity vectors.

> [!TIP]
> Once the ICR is located, *every* point of the body has velocity magnitude $V_P = \omega \cdot r_{P/IC}$, directed perpendicular to the line joining $P$ to ICR.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Relative Velocity Equation

**Statement:** For a rigid body, the absolute velocity of point $A$ equals the absolute velocity of point $B$ plus the velocity of $A$ relative to $B$ caused by the body's rotation.

**Starting point — Position vectors in a fixed (inertial) frame:**

$$
\vec{r}_A = \vec{r}_B + \vec{r}_{A/B}
$$

where $\vec{r}_{A/B}$ is the position of $A$ measured *from* $B$ inside the rotating body.

**Differentiate once with respect to time:**

$$
\frac{d\vec{r}_A}{dt} = \frac{d\vec{r}_B}{dt} + \frac{d\vec{r}_{A/B}}{dt}
$$

**Left side:** By definition, $\dfrac{d\vec{r}_A}{dt} = \vec{V}_A$ (absolute velocity of $A$).

**First term on right:** $\dfrac{d\vec{r}_B}{dt} = \vec{V}_B$ (absolute velocity of $B$).

**Second term on right:** Since $\vec{r}_{A/B}$ is a vector of **fixed length** (rigid body) that *rotates* with the body, its time derivative is purely a rotation effect. If the body rotates with angular velocity $\vec{\omega}$ about $B$, then:

$$
\frac{d\vec{r}_{A/B}}{dt} = \vec{\omega} \times \vec{r}_{A/B} = \vec{V}_{A/B}
$$

**Combine the three pieces:**

$$
\vec{V}_A = \vec{V}_B + \vec{\omega} \times \vec{r}_{A/B}
$$

$$
\boxed{\vec{V}_A = \vec{V}_B + \vec{V}_{A/B}}
$$

**Magnitude form:**

$$
\lvert V_{A/B} \rvert = \omega \cdot \lvert r_{A/B} \rvert
$$

with $\vec{V}_{A/B}$ directed **perpendicular** to $\vec{r}_{A/B}$ in the sense of $\vec{\omega}$ (right-hand rule).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Relative Acceleration Equation

**Statement:** The absolute acceleration of $A$ equals the absolute acceleration of $B$ plus two perpendicular components: a centripetal (normal) term and a tangential term caused by the body's rotation.

**Differentiate the velocity equation with respect to time:**

$$
\frac{d\vec{V}_A}{dt} = \frac{d\vec{V}_B}{dt} + \frac{d}{dt}\bigl(\vec{\omega} \times \vec{r}_{A/B}\bigr)
$$

**Left side:** $\vec{a}_A$ (absolute acceleration of $A$).

**First right term:** $\vec{a}_B$ (absolute acceleration of $B$).

**Second right term — use the product rule for cross product:**

$$
\frac{d}{dt}\bigl(\vec{\omega} \times \vec{r}_{A/B}\bigr) = \frac{d\vec{\omega}}{dt} \times \vec{r}_{A/B} + \vec{\omega} \times \frac{d\vec{r}_{A/B}}{dt}
$$

**Substitute $\vec{\alpha} = d\vec{\omega}/dt$ and $\vec{\omega} \times \vec{r}_{A/B}$ for the second inner term:**

$$
\frac{d}{dt}\bigl(\vec{\omega} \times \vec{r}_{A/B}\bigr) = \vec{\alpha} \times \vec{r}_{A/B} + \vec{\omega} \times \bigl(\vec{\omega} \times \vec{r}_{A/B}\bigr)
$$

**Apply the BAC–CAB identity** $\vec{\omega} \times (\vec{\omega} \times \vec{r}_{A/B}) = \vec{\omega}(\vec{\omega}\cdot\vec{r}_{A/B}) - \vec{r}_{A/B}\omega^2$. Since $\vec{r}_{A/B} \perp \vec{\omega}$ in plane motion, $\vec{\omega}\cdot\vec{r}_{A/B} = 0$, leaving $-\omega^2 \vec{r}_{A/B}$.

$$
\frac{d}{dt}\bigl(\vec{\omega} \times \vec{r}_{A/B}\bigr) = \vec{\alpha} \times \vec{r}_{A/B} - \omega^2 \vec{r}_{A/B}
$$

**Recognise the two physical terms:**

- $(\vec{a}_{A/B})_t = \vec{\alpha} \times \vec{r}_{A/B}$ — tangential acceleration, magnitude $\alpha r$, perpendicular to $AB$.
- $(\vec{a}_{A/B})_n = -\omega^2 \vec{r}_{A/B}$ — centripetal acceleration, magnitude $\omega^2 r$, directed **from $A$ towards $B$**.

**Combine everything:**

$$
\vec{a}_A = \vec{a}_B + (\vec{a}_{A/B})_n + (\vec{a}_{A/B})_t
$$

$$
\boxed{\vec{a}_A = \vec{a}_B + \omega^2 \vec{r}_{A/B}\ (\text{towards } B) + \alpha \vec{r}_{A/B}\ (\text{tangential})}
$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Instantaneous Centre of Rotation (ICR)

**Claim:** For every rigid body in plane motion, at every instant there exists a unique point $I$ of the body (extended in space) with zero velocity.

**Proof:** Let $B$ be any known point of the body with velocity $\vec{V}_B$. The velocity of another point $P$ is:

$$
\vec{V}_P = \vec{V}_B + \vec{\omega} \times \vec{r}_{P/B}
$$

We seek $P = I$ such that $\vec{V}_I = 0$:

$$
0 = \vec{V}_B + \vec{\omega} \times \vec{r}_{I/B}
$$

$$
\vec{V}_B = -\vec{\omega} \times \vec{r}_{I/B} = \vec{\omega} \times \vec{r}_{B/I}
$$

Take magnitudes:

$$
\lvert V_B \rvert = \omega \cdot \lvert r_{B/I} \rvert
$$

$$
\boxed{r_{B/IC} = \frac{V_B}{\omega}}
$$

and the direction of $\vec{r}_{B/I}$ is perpendicular to $\vec{V}_B$ (and $\vec{r}_{I/B}$ is perpendicular in the opposite sense, so that $\vec{\omega}\times\vec{r}_{I/B}$ cancels $\vec{V}_B$).

**Geometric interpretation:** The ICR lies on a line drawn through $B$ **perpendicular to $\vec{V}_B$**, at a distance $V_B/\omega$ from $B$, on the side such that $\vec{V}_B$ is the velocity that $B$ would have if rotating about $I$ with angular velocity $\omega$.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Quick Decision Tree for KTU Problems

| Given in problem | Strategy |
|---|---|
| $V_B$ and $\omega$ known, need $V_A$ | Use $\vec{V}_A = \vec{V}_B + \vec{\omega}\times\vec{r}_{A/B}$ |
| Two known point velocities | Locate ICR by intersecting perpendiculars; then $V_{\text{any}} = \omega r$ |
| Wheel rolling on ground, $V_C$ given, $R$ given | $\omega = V_C / R$; ICR is contact point |
| Crank–slider mechanism | Take crank pin as $B$ and slider as $A$; $V_B = \omega_1 r$, direction $\perp$ crank; $V_{A/B} \perp$ connecting rod |
| Need accelerations of $A$ when $\omega$ and $\alpha$ both exist | Use full 3-term acceleration equation |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Velocity & Acceleration Vector Breakdown (Block Functional Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Rigid body in plane motion] --> B[Choose reference point B on same body]
    B --> C[B has known absolute motion VaB and AaB]
    C --> D[Step 1: Transport A with B - pure translation]
    D --> E[Contribution 1: V transports = V of B]
    C --> F[Step 2: Rotate A about B - pure rotation]
    F --> G[Contribution 2: V rotates = omega cross r AB]
    F --> H[Contribution 3: a normal = omega squared r towards B]
    F --> I[Contribution 4: a tangential = alpha r perpendicular to AB]
    E --> J[Vector superposition - Tip to tail]
    G --> J
    H --> K[Acceleration superposition]
    I --> K
    J --> L[Result: Absolute velocity V of A]
    K --> M[Result: Absolute acceleration a of A]
    L --> N[Optional: Locate ICR by drawing perpendiculars]
    M --> N
    N --> O[End: Full kinematic state of point A]
```

## 4.2 Instantaneous Centre Construction

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Point P1 with known velocity V1] --> X1[Draw line L1 perpendicular to V1 through P1]
    P2[Point P2 with known velocity V2] --> X2[Draw line L2 perpendicular to V2 through P2]
    X1 --> Y[Intersection of L1 and L2 = ICR]
    X2 --> Y
    Y --> Z[Distance r1 from ICR to P1 and r2 from ICR to P2]
    Z --> W[omega = V1 slash r1 = V2 slash r2]
    W --> V[Any other point P: V of P = omega times r P to ICR]
```

## 4.3 Rolling Wheel Kinematics Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R[Wheel of radius R rolling on flat ground] --> A1[Contact point C: Vc = 0 - this is ICR]
    R --> A2[Centre O: VO = omega R - pure horizontal]
    R --> A3[Top point T: VT = 2 omega R - horizontal same direction as VO]
    R --> A4[Side point S: VS = omega R times sqrt 2 - at 45 degrees]
    A4 --> A5[Direction is tangent to circle centred at ICR passing through S]
    R --> A6[Acceleration of O: aO = alpha R horizontal]
    R --> A7[Acceleration of T: aT has component alpha R horizontal and omega squared R downward towards O]
```

## 4.4 Crank–Slider Mechanism Block View

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    O[Fixed pivot O] --> Q[Crank OA of length r rotating with omega]
    Q --> A[Crank pin A - velocity V of A = omega r perpendicular to OA]
    A --> B[Connecting rod AB of length L]
    B --> C[Slider pin B moves on horizontal guide]
    C --> D[V of B is purely horizontal]
    A --> E[Vector triangle: V of A = V of B + V of A on B]
    E --> F[V of A on B perpendicular to AB]
    F --> G[Unknowns: V of B and omega of connecting rod - solved by geometry]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define combined motion of translation and rotation. Why is it called the most general plane motion of a rigid body?**

**Model Answer:**

Combined motion of translation and rotation is a planar motion of a rigid body in which a chosen reference point $B$ on the body translates along an arbitrary (curvilinear) path, while the rest of the body simultaneously rotates about $B$.

It is the *most general* plane motion because both **pure translation** (with $\omega = 0$) and **pure rotation** (with $V_B = 0$) emerge as special cases:

- Setting $\omega = 0$ gives pure translation of the entire body.
- Setting $V_B = 0$ gives pure rotation about the fixed point $B$.

Therefore every conceivable planar rigid-body motion is contained within this single framework.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What is the instantaneous centre of rotation? How is it located when velocities of two points of a rigid body are known?**

**Model Answer:**

The **Instantaneous Centre of Rotation (ICR)** is a point of a rigid body (or its extension in space) that has **zero velocity** at a given instant. The entire body may, at that instant, be treated as undergoing pure rotation about this point.

**Location procedure when velocities of two points $P_1$ and $P_2$ are known and are not parallel:**

1. Through $P_1$, draw a line $L_1$ perpendicular to the velocity $\vec{V}_{P1}$.
2. Through $P_2$, draw a line $L_2$ perpendicular to the velocity $\vec{V}_{P2}$.
3. The intersection of $L_1$ and $L_2$ is the ICR.

Once located, the angular velocity is $\omega = \dfrac{V_{P1}}{r_{P1/IC}} = \dfrac{V_{P2}}{r_{P2/IC}}$ and the velocity of any other point $Q$ is $V_Q = \omega \cdot r_{Q/IC}$ directed perpendicular to $Q$–IC.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often draw the lines *parallel* to the velocities instead of *perpendicular*. This costs full marks. Always remember: **the ICR lies on a line perpendicular to the velocity vector of the point.**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

A wheel of radius **0.5 m** is rolling without slipping on a horizontal surface. At the instant shown, the centre $O$ has a velocity of **4 m/s** to the right and an acceleration of **2 m/s²** to the right. Determine:
**(a)** The angular velocity $\omega$ and angular acceleration $\alpha$ of the wheel, and the velocity of the top point $T$ and the side point $P$ (90° from top, in direction of motion).
**(b)** The acceleration of the top point $T$ and the side point $P$.

---

#### Part (a) — Solution [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Use rolling constraint to find $\omega$ and $\alpha$.**

For pure rolling, the contact point $C$ is the instantaneous centre of zero velocity.

$$
V_O = \omega R \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; 4 = \omega \times 0.5 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \boxed{\omega = 8 \text{ rad/s}}
$$

$$
a_O = \alpha R \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; 2 = \alpha \times 0.5 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \boxed{\alpha = 4 \text{ rad/s}^2}
$$

**Step 2 — Velocity of top point $T$ using ICR.**

Since $C$ is the ICR, the distance from $C$ to $T$ is the diameter $= 2R = 1$ m.

$$
V_T = \omega \cdot (2R) = 8 \times 1 = \boxed{8 \text{ m/s}}\ \text{(horizontally, same direction as } V_O)
$$

**Step 3 — Velocity of side point $P$.**

Point $P$ is located 90° from the top, i.e., on the leading edge of the wheel. Distance from $C$ to $P$:

$$
r_{P/C} = \sqrt{R^2 + R^2} = R\sqrt{2} = 0.5\sqrt{2} \approx 0.7071 \text{ m}
$$

$$
V_P = \omega \cdot R\sqrt{2} = 8 \times 0.5\sqrt{2} = 4\sqrt{2} \approx \boxed{5.657 \text{ m/s}}
$$

Direction of $\vec{V}_P$ is perpendicular to line $CP$, i.e., at 45° above the horizontal, slanted forward and upward.

---

#### Part (b) — Solution [7 marks]

**Step 4 — Acceleration of top point $T$.**

Treating $O$ as the reference point $B$ and $T$ as point $A$, the distance $r_{T/O} = R = 0.5$ m, with $T$ directly above $O$.

Components of acceleration of $T$ relative to $O$:

- Normal (centripetal, directed from $T$ towards $O$, i.e., **downward**):

$$
(a_{T/O})_n = \omega^2 R = 8^2 \times 0.5 = 64 \times 0.5 = 32 \text{ m/s}^2 \ \text{(downward)}
$$

- Tangential (perpendicular to $OT$, i.e., **horizontal**). For a wheel rolling to the right and speeding up, the tangential component points to the **right**:

$$
(a_{T/O})_t = \alpha R = 4 \times 0.5 = 2 \text{ m/s}^2 \ \text{(rightward)}
$$

**Add the absolute acceleration of $O$** ($a_O = 2$ m/s² rightward):

- Horizontal component: $a_O + (a_{T/O})_t = 2 + 2 = 4$ m/s²
- Vertical component: $(a_{T/O})_n = 32$ m/s² downward

$$
\boxed{a_T = \sqrt{4^2 + 32^2} = \sqrt{16 + 1024} = \sqrt{1040} \approx 32.25 \text{ m/s}^2}
$$

Direction: $\theta = \tan^{-1}(32/4) = \tan^{-1}(8) \approx 82.87°$ below the horizontal.

---

**Step 5 — Acceleration of side point $P$.**

Point $P$ is on the leading edge (90° from top, in direction of motion). $r_{P/O} = R = 0.5$ m, with $P$ horizontally forward of $O$.

Relative components of $P$ w.r.t. $O$:

- Normal (centripetal, from $P$ towards $O$, i.e., **leftward / horizontal backward**):

$$
(a_{P/O})_n = \omega^2 R = 32 \text{ m/s}^2 \ \text{(leftward)}
$$

- Tangential (perpendicular to $OP$, i.e., **vertical upward**, since $\alpha$ is in the rolling-forward sense, but for the leading point the tangential is upward when the wheel speeds up rotating clockwise as seen from the right):

Wait — careful sign check. The wheel rolls to the right and speeds up. Viewed in standard orientation, it rotates **clockwise**. For a point at the rightmost (3 o'clock) position:

- The centripetal direction points inward toward the centre, i.e., to the left.
- The tangential direction is perpendicular to $OP$, in the direction of the angular acceleration. For clockwise rotation speeding up, the tangential acceleration at the 3 o'clock position points **downward**.

Therefore:

$$
(a_{P/O})_t = \alpha R = 2 \text{ m/s}^2 \ \text{(downward)}
$$

Add $a_O = 2$ m/s² rightward:

- Horizontal: $a_O - (a_{P/O})_n = 2 - 32 = -30$ m/s² → **30 m/s² leftward**
- Vertical: $(a_{P/O})_t = 2$ m/s² **downward**

$$
\boxed{a_P = \sqrt{30^2 + 2^2} = \sqrt{904} \approx 30.07 \text{ m/s}^2}
$$

Direction: nearly horizontal, slightly downward.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mistakes in Rolling-Wheel Problems:**
> 1. Forgetting that the contact point $C$ is the ICR (only true for *rolling without slipping*). If slipping exists, $C$ is **not** the ICR.
> 2. Mixing up the sign/direction of the tangential component — always draw a free-body-like vector diagram of $A$ relative to $B$ before substituting magnitudes.
> 3. At the top point $T$, students often forget that the centripetal term acts **towards the centre $O$**, which means **downward**, not upward.
> 4. For the acceleration of *any* point off-centre, both $\omega^2$ and $\alpha$ terms appear — do NOT drop the $\alpha$ term even if the problem says "instant shown, no angular acceleration" — re-read the question.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

The crank $OA$ of a slider-crank mechanism rotates with constant angular velocity of **20 rad/s** (counter-clockwise) about the fixed pivot $O$. Length $OA = 0.2$ m, and the connecting rod $AB$ has length **0.6 m**. The slider $B$ moves along a horizontal guide through $O$. At the instant when $OA$ is perpendicular to the connecting rod $AB$ (i.e., the crank and the connecting rod are at right angles), determine:
**(a)** The velocity of the slider $B$ and the angular velocity of the connecting rod $AB$.
**(b)** The acceleration of the slider $B$ and the angular acceleration of the connecting rod $AB$.

---

#### Part (a) — Solution [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Velocity of crank pin $A$.**

Since $OA$ rotates with $\omega_1 = 20$ rad/s,

$$
V_A = \omega_1 \times OA = 20 \times 0.2 = 4 \text{ m/s}
$$

Direction: perpendicular to $OA$. With $OA$ horizontal (let's say) and rotation counter-clockwise, $V_A$ points vertically upward.

**Step 2 — Apply the relative velocity equation at $B$ taking $A$ as reference.**

$$
\vec{V}_B = \vec{V}_A + \vec{V}_{B/A}
$$

- $\vec{V}_B$ is **purely horizontal** (slider constraint).
- $\vec{V}_{B/A}$ is **perpendicular to $AB$** (rotation about $A$).
- $AB$ is vertical (since $OA \perp AB$ and $OA$ is horizontal), so $\vec{V}_{B/A}$ is **horizontal**.

**Step 3 — Vector diagram.**

Take $V_A$ vertical upward $= 4$ m/s. $\vec{V}_B$ is horizontal. $\vec{V}_{B/A}$ is horizontal (perpendicular to vertical $AB$). For closure:

- Horizontal: $V_B$ (right or left) must equal $V_{B/A}$ in magnitude but with the right sign to close the triangle.
- Vertical: must be zero, so $V_{B/A}$ has no vertical component, but the $V_A$ vertical component must be cancelled by... wait, the equation is $\vec{V}_B = \vec{V}_A + \vec{V}_{B/A}$.

If $V_A$ is purely vertical and $V_B$ is purely horizontal, $V_{B/A}$ must have both a vertical and horizontal component to satisfy the equation. But $V_{B/A}$ is purely perpendicular to $AB$, and $AB$ is vertical, so $V_{B/A}$ is purely horizontal. This is a contradiction unless $V_A$ has no vertical component.

**Re-interpretation:** The configuration "OA is perpendicular to AB" can have $OA$ vertical and $AB$ horizontal, OR $OA$ horizontal and $AB$ vertical. Let's choose $OA$ vertical and $AB$ horizontal (slider moves horizontally through $O$).

With $OA$ vertical, $V_A$ is **horizontal** (perpendicular to vertical $OA$). $AB$ is horizontal, so $V_{B/A}$ is **vertical**. Then $\vec{V}_B = \vec{V}_A + \vec{V}_{B/A}$ has:

- Horizontal component: $V_B = V_A = 4$ m/s
- Vertical component: $0 = V_{B/A}$, so $V_{B/A} = 0$? That can't be right either.

Let me re-choose: Put $OA$ **horizontal** (along the slider's guide direction), and $AB$ **vertical** (perpendicular to slider guide). The slider moves along the horizontal through $O$.

- $OA$ horizontal: $V_A$ is vertical, magnitude 4 m/s.
- $AB$ vertical: $V_{B/A}$ is horizontal.
- $V_B$ is horizontal (slider).

Equation $\vec{V}_B = \vec{V}_A + \vec{V}_{B/A}$:
- Vertical: $0 = 4 + 0$ → impossible.

The configuration must be the one where $OA$ and $AB$ are perpendicular but the slider constraint is consistent. Let me reconsider: at the **TDC / BDC position** of the slider-crank, the crank and connecting rod are collinear, not perpendicular. The "perpendicular" position is the **mid-stroke** position where the crank is at 90° to the line of stroke.

Let me set up: slider line is the x-axis. $O$ at origin. At the mid-stroke instant, $OA$ is along the y-axis (perpendicular to slider), and $AB$ goes from the top of the crank to the slider, which is on the x-axis. So $B$ is on the x-axis. $A$ is at $(0, 0.2)$. $B$ is somewhere on the x-axis such that $|AB| = 0.6$. Then $B = (\pm\sqrt{0.36 - 0.04}, 0) = (\pm\sqrt{0.32}, 0) = (\pm 0.5657, 0)$.

So $AB$ is the line from $(0, 0.2)$ to $(\pm 0.5657, 0)$ — this is **not** horizontal and **not** vertical; it is at an angle.

The problem statement says $OA \perp AB$. With $OA$ vertical (along y), $AB$ must be **horizontal** — but that contradicts $|AB| = 0.6$ and $A = (0, 0.2)$, because then $B$ would be at $(\pm 0.6, 0.2)$, not on the x-axis.

**Resolution:** The instant "when $OA$ is perpendicular to $AB$" is geometrically possible only if the slider is not on the y-axis. Let me adopt: at this instant, $OA$ makes some angle, and $AB$ is perpendicular to it, with $B$ on the x-axis.

Let $OA$ make angle $\theta$ with the x-axis, so $A = (0.2\cos\theta, 0.2\sin\theta)$. $AB$ is perpendicular to $OA$, so direction of $AB$ is $(-\sin\theta, \cos\theta)$. $B = A + 0.6 \cdot (-\sin\theta, \cos\theta)$ must satisfy $B_y = 0$:

$$
0.2\sin\theta + 0.6\cos\theta = 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \tan\theta = -3
$$

Take $\theta = 180° - 71.565° = 108.435°$ (so that $\sin\theta > 0$ and $\cos\theta < 0$, giving a positive $A_y$ and slightly negative $A_x$).

Then $A = (0.2\cos\theta, 0.2\sin\theta) \approx (-0.0632, 0.1897)$.

Direction of $OA$ (unit vector): $(-0.316, 0.949)$. Direction of $AB$ (unit, perp): $(-0.949, -0.316)$ (towards negative x to reach x-axis at B).

$B = A + 0.6 \cdot (-0.949, -0.316) \approx (-0.0632 - 0.569, 0.1897 - 0.190) \approx (-0.632, 0)$. Good — $B$ is on the x-axis.

**Step 1 — $\vec{V}_A$:**

$\vec{V}_A = \vec{\omega}_1 \times \vec{r}_{A/O}$. With $\vec{\omega}_1 = 20\,\hat{k}$ (CCW out of page) and $\vec{r}_{A/O} = (-0.0632, 0.1897, 0)$:

$$
\vec{V}_A = 20\,\hat{k} \times (-0.0632\,\hat{i} + 0.1897\,\hat{j}) = 20(-0.0632\,\hat{j} - 0.1897\,\hat{i}) = (-3.794, -1.264) \text{ m/s}
$$

(Or magnitude check: $|V_A| = 20 \times 0.2 = 4$ m/s, direction $\perp OA$, in CCW sense.)

**Step 2 — Apply $\vec{V}_B = \vec{V}_A + \vec{V}_{B/A}$.**

$\vec{V}_B$ is along x-axis: $(V_B, 0)$.

$\vec{V}_{B/A} = \vec{\omega}_2 \times \vec{r}_{B/A}$, with $\vec{\omega}_2 = \omega_2 \hat{k}$ and $\vec{r}_{B/A} = -0.6 \cdot (-0.949, -0.316, 0) = (0.5694, 0.1896, 0)$. Wait, let me recompute $\vec{r}_{B/A}$:

$\vec{r}_{B/A} = B - A = (-0.632 - (-0.0632), 0 - 0.1897) = (-0.569, -0.190)$. Magnitude $\sqrt{0.569^2 + 0.190^2} = \sqrt{0.3237 + 0.0361} = \sqrt{0.3598} \approx 0.6$. Good.

$$
\vec{V}_{B/A} = \omega_2 \hat{k} \times (-0.569\,\hat{i} - 0.190\,\hat{j}) = \omega_2(-0.569\,\hat{j} + 0.190\,\hat{i}) = (0.190\omega_2, -0.569\omega_2)
$$

Equation:
- x: $V_B = -3.794 + 0.190\omega_2$
- y: $0 = -1.264 - 0.569\omega_2$

From y-equation:
$$
\omega_2 = \frac{-1.264}{0.569} = -2.221 \text{ rad/s}
$$

(Negative sign means clockwise rotation of the connecting rod.)

From x-equation:
$$
V_B = -3.794 + 0.190 \times (-2.221) = -3.794 - 0.422 = -4.216 \text{ m/s}
$$

$$
\boxed{V_B \approx 4.22 \text{ m/s (in the negative x direction, i.e., to the left)}} \quad \boxed{\omega_2 \approx 2.22 \text{ rad/s (clockwise)}}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Many students blindly write $V_B = V_A = 4$ m/s assuming some "right-angle" simplification. This is **wrong** unless $AB$ is itself perpendicular to the slider direction. Always do a full vector resolution.

---

#### Part (b) — Solution [7 marks]

Since the crank rotates with **constant** $\omega_1 = 20$ rad/s, $\alpha_1 = 0$.

**Step 3 — Acceleration of $A$:**

$\vec{a}_A$ has only the centripetal component (since $\alpha_1 = 0$):

$$
\vec{a}_A = -\omega_1^2 \vec{r}_{A/O} = -400 \times (-0.0632, 0.1897) = (25.28, -75.88) \text{ m/s}^2
$$

Magnitude: $a_A = \omega_1^2 \cdot OA = 400 \times 0.2 = 80$ m/s², directed from $A$ towards $O$.

**Step 4 — Apply acceleration equation at $B$ taking $A$ as reference:**

$$
\vec{a}_B = \vec{a}_A + (\vec{a}_{B/A})_n + (\vec{a}_{B/A})_t
$$

- $\vec{a}_B$ is purely along x-axis: $(a_B, 0)$.
- $(\vec{a}_{B/A})_n = -\omega_2^2 \vec{r}_{B/A} = -(-2.221)^2 \times (-0.569, -0.190) = -4.933 \times (-0.569, -0.190) = (2.807, 0.937)$ m/s².
- $(\vec{a}_{B/A})_t = \vec{\alpha}_2 \times \vec{r}_{B/A} = \alpha_2 \hat{k} \times (-0.569\,\hat{i} - 0.190\,\hat{j}) = (0.190\alpha_2, -0.569\alpha_2)$.

**Step 5 — Solve component-wise.**

x-equation:
$$
a_B = 25.28 + 2.807 + 0.190\alpha_2 = 28.09 + 0.190\alpha_2
$$

y-equation:
$$
0 = -75.88 + 0.937 - 0.569\alpha_2 = -74.94 - 0.569\alpha_2
$$

From y:
$$
\alpha_2 = \frac{-74.94}{0.569} = -131.7 \text{ rad/s}^2
$$

(Negative → angular acceleration is clockwise.)

From x:
$$
a_B = 28.09 + 0.190 \times (-131.7) = 28.09 - 25.02 = 3.07 \text{ m/s}^2
$$

$$
\boxed{a_B \approx 3.07 \text{ m/s}^2 \ (\text{positive x direction})} \quad \boxed{\alpha_2 \approx 131.7 \text{ rad/s}^2 \ (\text{clockwise})}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — Crank–Slider Problems:**
> 1. Don't assume $\alpha_1 = 0$ means the entire mechanism has no acceleration. The *connecting rod* can still have non-zero $\alpha_2$ if the geometry is changing.
> 2. The direction of the centripetal term $(\vec{a}_{B/A})_n$ is *from B towards A*, not the other way around. Sign error here is the #1 reason students lose 3-4 marks.
> 3. At mid-stroke position, $\omega_2$ is at its **maximum** — this is an excellent cross-check, since $\omega_2^2$ should be the largest in the cycle.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ Combined motion = **Translation of a base point + Rotation of body about that base point**. Together, they describe the **most general plane motion** of a rigid body.
- ✅ The fundamental velocity equation is $\vec{V}_A = \vec{V}_B + \vec{V}_{A/B}$, where $\vec{V}_{A/B} = \vec{\omega} \times \vec{r}_{A/B}$ and is **perpendicular** to the line $AB$ in the sense of $\vec{\omega}$.
- ✅ The fundamental acceleration equation has **two** relative terms: a **normal (centripetal)** component $\omega^2 r$ pointing *towards the reference point* and a **tangential** component $\alpha r$ perpendicular to the line $AB$ in the sense of $\vec{\alpha}$.
- ✅ The **Instantaneous Centre of Rotation (ICR)** is the point of zero velocity at a given instant. Locate it by drawing **perpendiculars** to the velocities of two points and finding their intersection. For a wheel rolling without slipping, the ICR is the **contact point with the ground**.
- ✅ Always draw a **vector polygon** for $\vec{V}_A$ or $\vec{a}_A$ showing the addition tip-to-tail. This single drawing earns easy method-marks in KTU evaluation.
- ✅ Sign conventions matter: use a chosen positive sense (e.g., CCW = +ve) and stick to it consistently; otherwise signs in the tangential term will confuse you.
- ✅ For wheels, remember: $V_{\text{top}} = 2V_{\text{centre}}$, $V_{\text{bottom}} = 0$, and the centripetal acceleration of the top point is directed **downward** toward the centre.
- ✅ Common unit check: $V$ in m/s, $\omega$ in rad/s, $r$ in m, $a$ in m/s², $\alpha$ in rad/s². Note that **rad is dimensionless** but written for clarity.
- ✅ The relative-velocity method requires **two scalar equations** (x and y components) for **two unknowns** — usually the unknown velocity magnitude and the unknown angular velocity.
- ✅ In KTU board papers, expect either (i) a rolling-wheel problem or (ii) a slider-crank / four-bar linkage problem for the 14-mark question. Practice both.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Mechanical Vibration - Free and Forced Vibration
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GBEST213 - ENGINEERING MECHANICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Mechanical Vibration - Free and Forced Vibration

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Mechanical Vibration** is defined as the oscillatory motion of a particle, body, or system of bodies about an equilibrium position. In the context of the KTU 2024 scheme for *Engineering Mechanics* (GBEST213), vibration analysis is restricted primarily to **Single Degree of Freedom (SDOF) systems** undergoing translation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Degree of Freedom (DOF):** The minimum number of independent coordinates required to completely define the configuration of a vibrating system at any instant of time. A simple mass-spring-damper system possesses **one (1) DOF** when only vertical translation is permitted.

### Classification of Vibration (As per KTU Module 4)

| Classification | Description |
|---|---|
| **Free Vibration** | Oscillation of a system occurring *without* any external force after an initial disturbance. |
| **Forced Vibration** | Oscillation sustained by a *continuous* time-dependent external force (harmonic excitation). |
| **Damped Vibration** | Vibration wherein energy is progressively dissipated by a damping element. |
| **Undamped Vibration** | An ideal vibration with no energy loss (theoretical construct). |
| **Torsional Vibration** | Angular oscillation about the axis of a shaft. |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Analogy:** Imagine pulling a child on a swing to one side and letting go. The swing oscillates back and forth across the resting position, gradually slowing down until it stops. The *back-and-forth motion without you pushing* is **Free Vibration**. Now imagine you keep giving a small periodic push to the swing at the right moment — the swing's motion is then sustained by your *periodic pushes*. This is **Forced Vibration**.

**Engineer's Intuition Box:**
- **Mass ($m$)** = The inertia of the body (resistance to change in motion).
- **Spring ($k$)** = The restoring element that pulls/pushes the body back to equilibrium.
- **Damper ($c$)** = The energy-dissipating element (like a shock absorber in a car or a door closer).
- **Force ($F$)** = The external agent exciting the system.

The interplay of these four components determines whether a structure vibrates mildly, resonates catastrophically, or settles to rest quickly. This is why mechanical vibration is one of the most critical topics in machine design and structural engineering.

## 1.3 Standard Physical Constants & Units

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Mass ($m$):** kg (in SI) | slug (in FPS)
> - **Stiffness ($k$):** N/m
> - **Damping coefficient ($c$):** N·s/m
> - **Natural angular frequency ($\omega_n$):** rad/s
> - **Frequency ($f$):** Hz (cycles per second)
> - **Period ($T$):** seconds (s)
> - **Gravitational acceleration ($g$):** **9.81 m/s²** (standard value used universally in KTU problems)

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 SDOF System Components

A single degree of freedom vibrating system is idealized as a lumped mass $m$ connected to a fixed support via a spring of stiffness $k$ and a viscous damper of damping coefficient $c$, with displacement $x(t)$ measured from the static equilibrium.

### 2.1.1 Logical Steps to Formulate a Vibration Problem

1. **Identify the mass** $m$ that is in motion.
2. **Identify the spring constant** $k$ (force per unit deflection).
3. **Identify the damping coefficient** $c$ (force per unit velocity).
4. **Draw the Free Body Diagram (FBD)** with all forces acting on $m$.
5. **Apply Newton's Second Law** $\sum F = m\ddot{x}$ in the direction of motion.
6. **Solve the resulting differential equation** with appropriate initial conditions.
7. **Extract** natural frequency, amplitude, and phase information.

## 2.2 Case 1: Free Undamped Vibration (FUDS)

The system is displaced by an initial amount $x_0$ and released, or given an initial velocity $v_0$ with no external force and no damper.

**Equation of Motion (EOM):**

$$m\ddot{x} + kx = 0$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is a **second-order linear homogeneous ordinary differential equation (ODE)** with constant coefficients. Its characteristic equation yields purely imaginary roots, producing sinusoidal motion.

**Key Relationships:**

$$\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}} \quad \text{(Natural circular frequency, rad/s)}$$

$$f_n = \frac{\omega_n}{2\pi} = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{k}{m}} \quad \text{(Natural frequency, Hz)}$$

$$T_n = 2\pi\sqrt{\frac{m}{k}} \quad \text{(Natural period, s)}$$

**General Solution:**

$$x(t) = A\cos(\omega_n t) + B\sin(\omega_n t) = X\sin(\omega_n t + \phi)$$

where $X = \sqrt{A^2 + B^2}$ is the amplitude and $\phi = \tan^{-1}(A/B)$ is the phase angle.

## 2.3 Case 2: Free Damped Vibration (FDVS)

When a viscous damper $c$ is present, energy is dissipated as heat and the vibration amplitude decays exponentially.

**Equation of Motion:**

$$m\ddot{x} + c\dot{x} + kx = 0$$

**Critical Damping Coefficient:**

$$c_c = 2m\omega_n = 2\sqrt{mk}$$

**Damping Ratio (Dimensionless):**

$$\zeta = \frac{c}{c_c} = \frac{c}{2m\omega_n} = \frac{c}{2\sqrt{mk}}$$

**Damped Natural Frequency:**

$$\omega_d = \omega_n\sqrt{1 - \zeta^2}$$

### Sub-Cases Based on $\zeta$:

| Condition | Regime | Motion Type |
|---|---|---|
| $\zeta = 0$ | Undamped | Pure sinusoidal, perpetual oscillation |
| $0 < \zeta < 1$ | Underdamped | Decaying sinusoidal oscillation |
| $\zeta = 1$ | Critically damped | Returns to equilibrium in minimum time *without* oscillation |
| $\zeta > 1$ | Overdamped | Returns to equilibrium slowly *without* oscillation |

**General Solution (Underdamped, $0 < \zeta < 1$):**

$$x(t) = e^{-\zeta\omega_n t}\left[A\cos(\omega_d t) + B\sin(\omega_d t)\right]$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Logarithmic Decrement ($\delta$):** A practical method to measure damping experimentally.
> $$\delta = \ln\left(\frac{x_1}{x_2}\right) = \frac{2\pi\zeta}{\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}} \approx 2\pi\zeta \text{ (for small }\zeta\text{)}$$

## 2.4 Case 3: Forced Undamped Vibration (FUVS)

The system is excited by a harmonic force $F(t) = F_0\sin(\omega_f t)$ with no damping.

**Equation of Motion:**

$$m\ddot{x} + kx = F_0\sin(\omega_f t)$$

**Total Solution (Particular + Complementary):**

$$x(t) = A\cos(\omega_n t) + B\sin(\omega_n t) + \frac{F_0/m}{\omega_n^2 - \omega_f^2}\sin(\omega_f t)$$

> [!WARNING]
> When the excitation frequency $\omega_f$ approaches the natural frequency $\omega_n$, the steady-state amplitude tends to **infinity**. This phenomenon is called **Resonance**, and is the most critical failure mode in mechanical design.

**Steady-State Amplitude (Particular solution only):**

$$X = \frac{F_0}{\vert k - m\omega_f^2 \vert} = \frac{F_0/m}{\vert \omega_n^2 - \omega_f^2 \vert}$$

## 2.5 Case 4: Forced Damped Vibration (FDVS)

**Equation of Motion:**

$$m\ddot{x} + c\dot{x} + kx = F_0\sin(\omega_f t)$$

**Steady-State Amplitude:**

$$X = \frac{F_0}{\sqrt{(k - m\omega_f^2)^2 + (c\omega_f)^2}}$$

**Magnification Factor (MF) or Dynamic Amplification Factor (DAF):**

$$\text{DAF} = \frac{X}{X_{static}} = \frac{X}{F_0/k} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\left(1 - r^2\right)^2 + \left(2\zeta r\right)^2}}$$

where $r = \omega_f / \omega_n$ is the **frequency ratio**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Resonance Condition (with damping):** The amplitude peak does not occur exactly at $r = 1$, but at:
> $$r_{peak} = \sqrt{1 - 2\zeta^2} \quad \text{(for } \zeta < 1/\sqrt{2}\text{)}$$
> and the **maximum magnification factor** is:
> $$\text{DAF}_{max} = \frac{1}{2\zeta\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}} \approx \frac{1}{2\zeta} \text{ (for small }\zeta\text{)}$$

## 2.6 Transmissibility (Isolation)

When the base of the system is excited, the force transmitted to the foundation is $F_T = kx + c\dot{x}$.

**Transmissibility Ratio:**

$$\text{TR} = \frac{F_T}{F_0} = \sqrt{\frac{1 + (2\zeta r)^2}{(1 - r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> For $r > \sqrt{2}$, TR $< 1$, meaning the system acts as a **vibration isolator**. This principle is used in rubber mounts for engines and buildings in seismic zones.

## 2.7 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

| Formula / Parameter | Expression | Application |
|---|---|---|
| Natural frequency | $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}$ | Free undamped/damped systems |
| Natural period | $T_n = 2\pi\sqrt{m/k}$ | Time period of oscillation |
| Critical damping | $c_c = 2m\omega_n$ | Threshold for oscillation |
| Damping ratio | $\zeta = c/c_c$ | Classifies damping regime |
| Damped frequency | $\omega_d = \omega_n\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}$ | Underdamped oscillation |
| Log. decrement | $\delta = 2\pi\zeta/\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}$ | Experimental damping |
| Frequency ratio | $r = \omega_f/\omega_n$ | Forced vibration analysis |
| Steady-state amplitude | $X = F_0/\sqrt{(k-m\omega_f^2)^2 + (c\omega_f)^2}$ | Forced damped vibration |
| Magnification factor | $\text{DAF} = 1/\sqrt{(1-r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}$ | Dynamic amplification |
| Transmissibility | $\text{TR} = \sqrt{(1+(2\zeta r)^2)/((1-r^2)^2+(2\zeta r)^2)}$ | Vibration isolation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:**
> - Designing earthquake-resistant structures (avoiding resonance with seismic waves).
> - Tuning automotive suspension systems for ride comfort.
> - Mounting precision instruments on vibration isolators.
> - Balancing rotating machinery to prevent catastrophic failure.
> - Designing foundations for heavy turbines and pumps.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Equation of Motion for SDOF Free Undamped System

**Starting Point:** A mass $m$ attached to a spring of stiffness $k$, displaced by $x$ from equilibrium, and released.

**Step 1 — Identify the forces on the mass:**

Only the spring force acts on the mass. From Hooke's Law, the restoring force is:

$$F_{spring} = -kx$$

The negative sign indicates the force opposes the displacement (acts towards equilibrium).

**Step 2 — Apply Newton's Second Law ($\sum F = ma$):**

$$m\ddot{x} = -kx$$

Rearranging:

$$m\ddot{x} + kx = 0$$

This is the **governing EOM** for free undamped vibration.

**Step 3 — Recognize the ODE form:**

This is a second-order linear homogeneous ODE with constant coefficients. Dividing throughout by $m$:

$$\ddot{x} + \frac{k}{m}x = 0$$

**Step 4 — Assume a solution of the form $x = e^{st}$:**

Substituting $x = e^{st}$ gives $\dot{x} = s e^{st}$ and $\ddot{x} = s^2 e^{st}$. Plugging back:

$$s^2 e^{st} + \frac{k}{m} e^{st} = 0$$

Since $e^{st} \neq 0$:

$$s^2 + \frac{k}{m} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad s^2 = -\frac{k}{m}$$

$$s = \pm i\sqrt{\frac{k}{m}} = \pm i\omega_n$$

where $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}$ is the natural circular frequency.

**Step 5 — Form the general solution:**

With purely imaginary roots, the general solution becomes:

$$x(t) = C_1 e^{i\omega_n t} + C_2 e^{-i\omega_n t}$$

Using Euler's identity, this can be rewritten in real form:

$$x(t) = A\cos(\omega_n t) + B\sin(\omega_n t)$$

Or equivalently:

$$x(t) = X\sin(\omega_n t + \phi)$$

where $X = \sqrt{A^2 + B^2}$ is the amplitude and $\phi$ is the phase.

**Step 6 — Apply initial conditions $x(0) = x_0$ and $\dot{x}(0) = v_0$:**

$$x(0) = A = x_0$$

$$\dot{x}(0) = B\omega_n = v_0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad B = \frac{v_0}{\omega_n}$$

Therefore the **complete solution** is:

$$x(t) = x_0 \cos(\omega_n t) + \frac{v_0}{\omega_n}\sin(\omega_n t)$$

The amplitude:

$$X = \sqrt{x_0^2 + \left(\frac{v_0}{\omega_n}\right)^2}$$

The phase:

$$\phi = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{x_0 \omega_n}{v_0}\right)$$

## 3.2 Derivation: Forced Damped Vibration (Full EOM)

**Starting Point:** SDOF system with mass $m$, spring $k$, damper $c$, and external forcing $F_0\sin(\omega_f t)$.

**Step 1 — Free Body Diagram analysis:**

Forces on mass in the direction of motion $x$:
- Spring force: $-kx$ (restoring)
- Damping force: $-c\dot{x}$ (opposes velocity)
- External force: $F_0\sin(\omega_f t)$ (driving)

**Step 2 — Apply Newton's Second Law:**

$$m\ddot{x} = -kx - c\dot{x} + F_0\sin(\omega_f t)$$

Rearranging:

$$m\ddot{x} + c\dot{x} + kx = F_0\sin(\omega_f t)$$

**Step 3 — Assume a steady-state particular solution:**

For harmonic forcing, the response is also harmonic at the same frequency but lagging in phase:

$$x_p(t) = X\sin(\omega_f t - \phi)$$

Compute derivatives:

$$\dot{x}_p = X\omega_f \cos(\omega_f t - \phi)$$

$$\ddot{x}_p = -X\omega_f^2 \sin(\omega_f t - \phi)$$

**Step 4 — Substitute into the EOM:**

$$m\left(-X\omega_f^2 \sin(\omega_f t - \phi)\right) + c\left(X\omega_f \cos(\omega_f t - \phi)\right) + k\left(X\sin(\omega_f t - \phi)\right) = F_0\sin(\omega_f t)$$

**Step 5 — Express $\sin(\omega_f t)$ in terms of $(\omega_f t - \phi)$:**

$$\sin(\omega_f t) = \sin\left((\omega_f t - \phi) + \phi\right) = \sin(\omega_f t - \phi)\cos\phi + \cos(\omega_f t - \phi)\sin\phi$$

**Step 6 — Collect $\sin$ and $\cos$ coefficients:**

For the $\sin(\omega_f t - \phi)$ term:

$$X(k - m\omega_f^2) = F_0 \cos\phi$$

For the $\cos(\omega_f t - \phi)$ term:

$$X c \omega_f = F_0 \sin\phi$$

**Step 7 — Solve for $X$ and $\phi$:**

Squaring and adding both equations:

$$X^2\left[(k - m\omega_f^2)^2 + (c\omega_f)^2\right] = F_0^2$$

$$\boxed{X = \frac{F_0}{\sqrt{(k - m\omega_f^2)^2 + (c\omega_f)^2}}}$$

Dividing the two equations:

$$\tan\phi = \frac{c\omega_f}{k - m\omega_f^2}$$

**Step 8 — Express in non-dimensional form:**

Dividing numerator and denominator by $k$, and using $\zeta = c/(2\sqrt{mk})$ and $r = \omega_f/\omega_n$:

$$\boxed{X = \frac{F_0/k}{\sqrt{(1 - r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}}$$

This is the **Steady-State Amplitude** formula. The Dynamic Amplification Factor (DAF) is the ratio of dynamic amplitude to static deflection $F_0/k$:

$$\boxed{\text{DAF} = \frac{X}{F_0/k} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{(1 - r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}}$$

## 3.3 Derivation: Resonance Frequency and Maximum DAF

To find when DAF is maximum, we minimize the denominator $D(r) = (1 - r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2$ with respect to $r$.

**Step 1 — Differentiate $D$ with respect to $r$:**

$$\frac{dD}{dr} = 2(1 - r^2)(-2r) + 2(2\zeta r)(2\zeta) = 0$$

$$-4r(1 - r^2) + 8\zeta^2 r = 0$$

**Step 2 — Factor out $4r$ (assuming $r \neq 0$):**

$$-4r\left[(1 - r^2) - 2\zeta^2\right] = 0$$

$$1 - r^2 = 2\zeta^2$$

$$r_{peak}^2 = 1 - 2\zeta^2$$

$$\boxed{r_{peak} = \sqrt{1 - 2\zeta^2}}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute back to get maximum DAF:**

$$\text{DAF}_{max} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{(2\zeta^2)^2 + (2\zeta\sqrt{1-2\zeta^2})^2}}$$

$$= \frac{1}{\sqrt{4\zeta^4 + 4\zeta^2(1 - 2\zeta^2)}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{4\zeta^2}} = \frac{1}{2\zeta}$$

$$\boxed{\text{DAF}_{max} \approx \frac{1}{2\zeta} \quad \text{(for small } \zeta \text{)}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** For $\zeta \to 0$ (lightly damped), the maximum DAF tends to infinity → **resonance catastrophe**. Even a tiny undamped harmonic force can cause unbounded vibrations if $\omega_f \to \omega_n$.

## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example (Typical KTU Style)

**Problem:** A 10 kg mass is attached to a spring of stiffness 1000 N/m with a viscous damper of coefficient 50 N·s/m. The system is initially at rest and displaced 0.05 m. Find: (a) Damping ratio, (b) Damped natural frequency, (c) Time at which the mass first passes through equilibrium.

**Given:** $m = 10$ kg, $k = 1000$ N/m, $c = 50$ N·s/m, $x_0 = 0.05$ m, $v_0 = 0$

**Solution:**

(a) **Natural frequency:**

$$\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}} = \sqrt{\frac{1000}{10}} = \sqrt{100} = 10 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Critical damping coefficient:**

$$c_c = 2m\omega_n = 2(10)(10) = 200 \text{ N·s/m}$$

**Damping ratio:**

$$\zeta = \frac{c}{c_c} = \frac{50}{200} = 0.25$$

(b) **Damped natural frequency:**

$$\omega_d = \omega_n\sqrt{1 - \zeta^2} = 10\sqrt{1 - 0.0625} = 10\sqrt{0.9375}$$

$$\omega_d = 10 \times 0.9683 = 9.683 \text{ rad/s}$$

(c) **General solution (underdamped, $v_0 = 0$):**

$$x(t) = e^{-\zeta\omega_n t}\left[x_0\cos(\omega_d t) + \frac{v_0 + \zeta\omega_n x_0}{\omega_d}\sin(\omega_d t)\right]$$

$$x(t) = e^{-2.5 t}\left[0.05\cos(9.683 t) + \frac{0 + (0.25)(10)(0.05)}{9.683}\sin(9.683 t)\right]$$

$$x(t) = e^{-2.5 t}\left[0.05\cos(9.683 t) + 0.0129\sin(9.683 t)\right]$$

**Time to first pass through equilibrium (x = 0):**

Setting $x(t) = 0$ means the bracket must equal zero:

$$0.05\cos(\omega_d t) + 0.0129\sin(\omega_d t) = 0$$

$$\tan(\omega_d t) = -\frac{0.05}{0.0129} = -3.876$$

$$\omega_d t = \pi - \tan^{-1}(3.876) = \pi - 1.317 = 1.825 \text{ rad}$$

$$t = \frac{1.825}{9.683} = 0.1885 \text{ s}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification:** The period of damped oscillation is $T_d = 2\pi/\omega_d = 0.649$ s, so the mass passes through equilibrium at roughly $T_d/4 \approx 0.162$ s. The slight delay from 0.162 to 0.1885 s arises because the decay envelope shifts the effective zero crossing, which is a known feature of damped systems.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Schematic: SDOF Mass-Spring-Damper System

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Fixed Support Ceiling]:::support
    B[Damper c]:::damper
    C[Spring k]:::spring
    D[Mass m]:::mass
    E[Equilibrium Reference Line]:::ref
    F[Displacement x t]:::disp
    G[External Force F0 sin omega t]:::force

    A --- B
    B --- D
    A --- C
    C --- D
    D --- F
    E --- D
    G -.-> D

    classDef support fill:#37474F,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFFFFF
    classDef damper fill:#FFB300,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef spring fill:#43A047,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFFFFF
    classDef mass fill:#1E88E5,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFFFFF
    classDef ref fill:#9E9E9E,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:1px,color:#000000
    classDef disp fill:#FFFFFF,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:1px,color:#000000
    classDef force fill:#E53935,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFFFFF
```

**Block-Level Functional Architecture:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph InputLayer["External Excitation"]
        I1[Initial Displacement x0]
        I2[Initial Velocity v0]
        I3[Forcing Function F0 sin omega t]
    end

    subgraph SystemCore["SDOF Vibrating System"]
        S1[Mass m] --> S2[Kinetic Energy Storage]
        S3[Spring k] --> S4[Potential Energy Storage]
        S5[Damper c] --> S6[Energy Dissipation]
    end

    subgraph OutputLayer["System Response"]
        O1[Displacement x t]
        O2[Velocity xdot t]
        O3[Acceleration xddot t]
        O4[Transmitted Force FT]
    end

    I1 --> SystemCore
    I2 --> SystemCore
    I3 --> SystemCore
    SystemCore --> O1
    SystemCore --> O2
    SystemCore --> O3
    SystemCore --> O4
```

## 4.2 Decision Flow Chart: Classifying the Vibration Regime

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Identify System Parameters] --> B{Is there an external forcing?}
    B -- No --> C[Free Vibration]
    B -- Yes --> D[Forced Vibration]
    C --> E{Is there damping?}
    E -- No --> F[Undamped: m xddot + kx = 0]
    E -- Yes --> G[Compute zeta = c divide 2 sqrt mk]
    G --> H{Compare zeta with 1}
    H -- zeta equals 0 --> F
    H -- zeta less than 1 --> I[Underdamped: Decaying Oscillation]
    H -- zeta equals 1 --> J[Critically Damped: No Oscillation]
    H -- zeta greater than 1 --> K[Overdamped: Slow Return]
    D --> L{Is there damping?}
    L -- No --> M[Undamped Forced: Pure Resonance at omega = omega n]
    L -- Yes --> N[Damped Forced: Steady State Sinusoidal Response]
    N --> O{Is r greater than sqrt 2?}
    O -- Yes --> P[Vibration Isolator Region]
    O -- No --> Q[Amplification Region]
```

## 4.3 Graph: Dynamic Amplification Factor vs Frequency Ratio (Conceptual)

```mermaid
graph LR
    A["r = 0.0  DAF = 1.0"] -->|r increases| B["r = 0.5  DAF approx 1.3"]
    B -->|r approaches 1| C["r = 1.0  DAF approx 1 over 2 zeta peak"]
    C -->|r passes 1| D["r = sqrt 2  DAF = 1"]
    D -->|r increases more| E["r = 2.0  DAF approx 0.3"]
    E -->|r large| F["r approaches infinity  DAF approaches 0"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Graph Interpretation:**
> - At $r = 0$: System behaves as if statically loaded.
> - At $r \to 1$: Resonance peak (amplitude magnification).
> - At $r = \sqrt{2}$: DAF = 1, the threshold of isolation.
> - At $r > \sqrt{2}$: DAF $< 1$, the system attenuates the input — the **isolation zone**.

---
