# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Part A questions are direct, definition/conceptual based, and require concise (3–4 line) answers.

---

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`

**Define Simple Harmonic Motion. State the necessary and sufficient conditions for a body to execute SHM.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Definition:** Simple Harmonic Motion is a special type of periodic oscillation in which the **restoring force** acting on the body is **directly proportional to the displacement** from the mean position and is **always directed towards the mean position**.

> **Necessary and Sufficient Conditions:**
> 1. The body must have a **stable equilibrium position** (mean position).
> 2. The restoring force (or acceleration) must be **proportional to the displacement** from this equilibrium.
> 3. The restoring force (or acceleration) must be **always directed towards** the equilibrium position.
>
> **Mathematically:** $F = -kx$ or equivalently $\ddot{x} + \omega_n^2 x = 0$.

*(Self-Assessment: Definition = 1 mark, Two conditions = 1 mark, Mathematical form = 1 mark.)*

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]`

**A spring-mass system has a mass of 4 kg and a spring stiffness of 100 N/m. Find its natural frequency and time period.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Given:** $m = 4$ kg, $k = 100$ N/m.

**Step 1: Natural angular frequency.**

$$\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}} = \sqrt{\frac{100}{4}} = \sqrt{25} = 5 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 2: Natural frequency (Hz).**

$$f_n = \frac{\omega_n}{2\pi} = \frac{5}{2\pi} = 0.796 \text{ Hz}$$

**Step 3: Time period.**

$$T = \frac{1}{f_n} = 2\pi \sqrt{\frac{m}{k}} = 2\pi \sqrt{\frac{4}{100}} = \frac{2\pi}{5} = 1.257 \text{ s}$$

*(Self-Assessment: Formulae = 1 mark, $\omega_n$ = 1 mark, $f_n$ and $T$ = 1 mark.)*

---

## 5.2 PART B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks split into two 7-mark sub-parts: (a) and (b). The student answers **EITHER** Question A **OR** Question B in full.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - July 2024 Model]`

**(a)** Derive the equation of motion of a spring-mass system and hence obtain expressions for the natural frequency and time period of oscillation. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A mass of 2 kg is attached to a spring of stiffness 200 N/m. The mass is displaced by 0.05 m from the equilibrium position and released from rest. Determine: (i) the natural frequency, (ii) the maximum velocity, and (iii) the total energy of the system. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A:**

##### **Part (a) — Derivation (7 Marks):**

**Free Body Diagram Concept:** Consider a mass $m$ attached to a spring of stiffness $k$, free to move on a frictionless horizontal surface.

**Step 1: Apply Hooke's Law.** The spring force on the mass, when displaced by $x$ from equilibrium, is:
$$F_s = -k \cdot x \quad \text{(1 mark)}$$

**Step 2: Apply Newton's Second Law of Motion.**
$$F = m \cdot a = m \cdot \ddot{x} \quad \text{(1 mark)}$$

**Step 3: Equate the two expressions.**
$$m \cdot \ddot{x} = -k \cdot x$$

**Step 4: Rearrange into standard form.**
$$m \cdot \ddot{x} + k \cdot x = 0 \quad \text{(1 mark)}$$

**Step 5: Divide by $m$ to obtain the canonical SHM equation.**
$$\ddot{x} + \frac{k}{m} \cdot x = 0 \quad \text{(1 mark)}$$

**Step 6: Define natural angular frequency squared.**
$$\omega_n^2 = \frac{k}{m}$$

**Step 7: Write canonical SHM equation.**
$$\ddot{x} + \omega_n^2 x = 0 \quad \text{(1 mark)}$$

**Step 8: Natural Frequency (Hz) and Time Period.**

$$f_n = \frac{1}{2\pi} \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}} \quad \text{(1 mark)}$$

$$T = 2\pi \sqrt{\frac{m}{k}} \quad \text{(1 mark)}$$

---

##### **Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 Marks):**

**Given:** $m = 2$ kg, $k = 200$ N/m, $X = 0.05$ m, $v_0 = 0$ (released from rest).

**(i) Natural Frequency (2 marks):**

$$f_n = \frac{1}{2\pi} \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}} = \frac{1}{2\pi} \sqrt{\frac{200}{2}} = \frac{1}{2\pi} \sqrt{100} = \frac{10}{2\pi} = 1.592 \text{ Hz}$$

$$\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}} = \sqrt{100} = 10 \text{ rad/s}$$

**(ii) Maximum Velocity (2 marks):**

$$V_{max} = X \cdot \omega_n = 0.05 \times 10 = 0.5 \text{ m/s}$$

**(iii) Total Energy (3 marks):**

$$E = \frac{1}{2} k X^2 = \frac{1}{2} \times 200 \times (0.05)^2$$

$$E = 100 \times 0.0025 = 0.25 \text{ J}$$

**Final Answers:**
- (i) $f_n = 1.592$ Hz (or $\omega_n = 10$ rad/s)
- (ii) $V_{max} = 0.5$ m/s
- (iii) $E = 0.25$ J

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]` *(Alternative Choice)*

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the spring-mass model. Derive expressions for displacement, velocity, and acceleration of a mass executing SHM. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A body of mass 5 kg is suspended from a spring and stretches it by 0.1 m at equilibrium. If the body is pulled down by an additional 0.05 m and released, find: (i) the spring stiffness, (ii) the natural frequency of oscillation, and (iii) the maximum acceleration of the body. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B:**

##### **Part (a) — Sketch + Derivation (7 Marks):**

**Sketch Description (1 mark):** Draw a spring (zig-zag line) fixed at the left to a wall, with a mass $m$ attached to its right end on a frictionless horizontal surface. Indicate the equilibrium position and a displacement $x$ from it.

**Derivation:**

**Step 1: From the equation of motion** $\ddot{x} + \omega_n^2 x = 0$, the general solution is:

$$x(t) = A \cos(\omega_n t) + B \sin(\omega_n t) \quad \text{(1 mark)}$$

**Step 2: Apply initial conditions** $x(0) = x_0$ and $\dot{x}(0) = v_0$.

- $A = x_0$ (1 mark)
- $B = v_0 / \omega_n$ (1 mark)

**Step 3: Hence, displacement:**
$$x(t) = x_0 \cos(\omega_n t) + \frac{v_0}{\omega_n} \sin(\omega_n t) \quad \text{(0.5 mark)}$$

**Step 4: Differentiate to obtain velocity:**
$$v(t) = \dot{x}(t) = -x_0 \omega_n \sin(\omega_n t) + v_0 \cos(\omega_n t) \quad \text{(0.5 mark)}$$

**Step 5: Differentiate again to obtain acceleration:**
$$a(t) = \ddot{x}(t) = -x_0 \omega_n^2 \cos(\omega_n t) - \frac{v_0}{\omega_n} \omega_n^2 \sin(\omega_n t) = -\omega_n^2 x(t) \quad \text{(1 mark)}$$

**Step 6: Maximum values** (1 mark):
- $V_{max} = \sqrt{x_0^2 \omega_n^2 + v_0^2}$
- $a_{max} = \omega_n^2 \sqrt{x_0^2 + (v_0/\omega_n)^2} = \omega_n^2 \cdot X$

---

##### **Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 Marks):**

**Given:** $m = 5$ kg, static deflection $\delta_{st} = 0.1$ m, additional pull = 0.05 m.

**(i) Spring Stiffness (2 marks):**

From $\delta_{st} = \dfrac{mg}{k}$:
$$k = \frac{mg}{\delta_{st}} = \frac{5 \times 9.81}{0.1} = \frac{49.05}{0.1} = 490.5 \text{ N/m}$$

**(ii) Natural Frequency (2 marks):**

$$f_n = \frac{1}{2\pi} \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}} = \frac{1}{2\pi} \sqrt{\frac{490.5}{5}} = \frac{1}{2\pi} \sqrt{98.1}$$

$$f_n = \frac{1}{2\pi} \times 9.905 = 1.577 \text{ Hz}$$

$$\omega_n = 9.905 \text{ rad/s}$$

**(iii) Maximum Acceleration (3 marks):**

The amplitude of oscillation is the additional pull, i.e., $X = 0.05$ m.

$$a_{max} = \omega_n^2 \cdot X = 98.1 \times 0.05 = 4.905 \text{ m/s}^2$$

**Final Answers:**
- (i) $k = 490.5$ N/m
- (ii) $f_n = 1.577$ Hz
- (iii) $a_{max} = 4.905$ m/s²

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Sign error in Hooke's Law:** Writing $F = kx$ instead of $F = -kx$ will cost 1–2 marks. The **negative sign is mandatory**.
> 2. **Mixing up $\omega_n$ and $f_n$:** $\omega_n$ is in **rad/s** while $f_n$ is in **Hz**. Always remember the factor $2\pi$.
> 3. **Confusing Amplitude with Displacement:** Amplitude $X$ is the **maximum** value of displacement, not a generic $x(t)$.
> 4. **Vertical vs Horizontal:** In vertical spring-mass problems, gravity cancels when measuring $x$ from the **static equilibrium**, not from the natural length.
> 5. **Energy formula:** Total energy is $E = \frac{1}{2} k X^2$ (spring-based) or equivalently $E = \frac{1}{2} m V_{max}^2$ (mass-based). Using the wrong form will give wrong units.
> 6. **Initial conditions:** When the body is "released from rest," $v_0 = 0$. This directly gives the amplitude $X = x_0$.

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Bullets — Read this 30 minutes before the exam.**

- **Definition of SHM:** Restoring force is proportional to displacement and directed toward mean position. Mathematically: $\ddot{x} + \omega_n^2 x = 0$.

- **Spring-Mass Model is the prototype of SHM.** It involves a single mass $m$ and a single spring $k$ — a 1-DOF system.

- **Hooke's Law:** $F = -kx$. This is the **only source** of the restoring force.

- **Equation of Motion:** $m\ddot{x} + kx = 0$. Divide by $m$: $\ddot{x} + \dfrac{k}{m}x = 0$.

- **Natural Angular Frequency:** $\omega_n = \sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m}}$ (rad/s). This is the **inherent** rate of oscillation of the system.

- **Natural Frequency:** $f_n = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m}}$ (Hz).

- **Time Period:** $T = 2\pi\sqrt{\dfrac{m}{k}}$ (seconds). **Independent of amplitude** — a defining property of SHM.

- **Displacement:** $x(t) = X\sin(\omega_n t + \phi)$, where $X$ = amplitude, $\phi$ = phase angle.

- **Velocity:** $v(t) = X\omega_n \cos(\omega_n t + \phi)$. Maximum: $V_{max} = X\omega_n$.

- **Acceleration:** $a(t) = -\omega_n^2 x(t)$. Maximum: $a_{max} = X\omega_n^2$.

- **Total Energy:** $E = \dfrac{1}{2}kX^2 = \dfrac{1}{2}mV_{max}^2$ — **conserved** at all times.

- **Energy oscillation:** PE is max at extremes ($x = \pm X$); KE is max at mean position ($x = 0$).

- **Vertical spring-mass:** Static deflection $\delta_{st} = \dfrac{mg}{k}$. The natural frequency is the **same** as horizontal case: $\omega_n = \sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m}}$ (gravity cancels out!).

- **Phase relationships:**
  - $x$ and $v$ are **90° out of phase**.
  - $x$ and $a$ are **180° out of phase** (in anti-phase).
  - $v$ and $a$ are **90° out of phase**.

- **Springs in parallel:** $k_{eq} = k_1 + k_2 + \cdots$ (stiffer system).

- **Springs in series:** $\dfrac{1}{k_{eq}} = \dfrac{1}{k_1} + \dfrac{1}{k_2} + \cdots$ (softer system).

- **Torsional SHM (bonus):** $\omega_n = \sqrt{\dfrac{k_t}{I}}$ where $k_t$ is torsional stiffness and $I$ is mass moment of inertia.

- **Engineering Takeaway:** Always design machinery to operate **far from** $\omega_n$ to avoid resonance — a destructive phenomenon at the natural frequency.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Period
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** GBEST213 - ENGINEERING MECHANICS > - **Module:** Module Module 4 > - **Topic:** Period # 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition of "Period" In Engineering Mechanics, **Period (T)** — more precisely called the **Time Period** — is defined as the **minimum time interval after which a particle (or a rigid body) executing periodic motion repeats its position and velocity vector**. It is the fundamental scalar quantity that characterizes the temporal repetition of any cyclic mechanical phenomenon governed by a restoring force/torque. Mathematically, if a particle's displacement $x(t)$ satisfies the condition: $$x(t + T) = x(t) \quad \text{and} \quad \dot{x}(t + T) = \dot{x}(t)$$ then $T$ is the **Period** of the motion, measured in **seconds (s)**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Focus (Module 4):** The Period is studied as a derived property of **Simple Harmonic Motion (SHM)** arising in mechanical vibratory systems — primarily the **simple pendulum**, the **spring-mass system**, the **compound (physical) pendulum**, and the **torsional pendulum**. ## 1.2 Related Vibratory Terminology (Must Know) | Term | Symbol | Definition | SI Unit | |------|--------|------------|---------| | Period | $T$ | Time for one complete cycle | **s** | | Frequency | $f$ | Number of cycles per second | **Hz** | | Angular Frequency | $\omega_n$ | $2\pi f$ | **rad/s** | | Amplitude | $A$ | Maximum displacement from mean position | **m** | | Phase Angle | $\phi$ | Initial angular position of the motion | **rad** | | Natural Frequency | $f_n$ | Frequency of free undamped vibration | **Hz** | > [!NOTE] > **Fundamental Relationship (must memorize for KTU):** > $$T = \frac{1}{f} = \frac{2\pi}{\omega_n}$$ > The Period and Frequency are **reciprocally related**. Doubling the frequency halves the period. ## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy 🎯 Imagine a **child sitting on a swing** in a Kerala temple festival ground. The swing is pulled to one side and released. It moves forward, slows down, reverses, swings back through the bottom, slows down again at the other extreme, and returns — completing **one full cycle**. The **time taken for that single, complete to-and-fro journey** is the **Period (T)**. It does **not** depend on *how far* the child swung (amplitude), provided the swing is ideal — only on the **length of the rope** and the **gravity of Earth**. A **clock pendulum** is the engineering masterpiece that exploits this exact principle — its constant period is what keeps time accurate to the second. ## 1.4 Physical Constants Used - **Acceleration due to gravity:** $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$ (standard KTU value) - **A seconds pendulum** has a period of exactly **$T = 2 \text{ s}$** (one beat each second) # 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Conceptual Foundation — Why Does a "Period" Exist? A periodic motion arises whenever a particle is acted upon by a **restoring influence** (force, torque, or moment) that always tries to bring it back to an **equilibrium (mean) position**, and is proportional in magnitude to the displacement from that equilibrium. The general governing differential equation of **free, undamped simple harmonic motion** is: $$\ddot{x} + \omega_n^2 \, x = 0$$ where: - $x$ = instantaneous displacement from mean position (m) - $\ddot{x}$ = second derivative of displacement w.r.t. time = acceleration (m/s²) - $\omega_n$ = natural angular frequency (rad/s) The solution is: $$x(t) = A \cos(\omega_n t + \phi)$$ This trigonometric form clearly shows **periodicity**: cosine repeats every $2\pi$ radians, so the time for one full cycle is $T = 2\pi / \omega_n$. ## 2.2 Detailed Logic Flow of Period Derivation For every KTU-listed system, the period emerges from the **same 4-step logical flow**: 1. **Identify the restoring influence** (linear force, gravitational component, or elastic torque). 2. **Apply Newton's 2nd Law** (or D'Alembert's principle / energy method) to get an equation of motion. 3. **Compare the resulting ODE with the canonical SHM form** $\ddot{q} + \omega_n^2 q = 0$. 4. **Extract $\omega_n$**, then compute $T = 2\pi / \omega_n$. ## 2.3 ⭐ KTU Formula Sheet — All Period Equations at a Glance > [!IMPORTANT] > **CRITICAL KTU EXAM TABLE — Memorize This Before Solving Any Problem** | # | System | Period (T) | Natural Frequency ($\omega_n$) | Frequency ($f$) | KTU Marks Weight | |---|--------|-----------|-------------------------------|------------------|------------------| | 1 | **Spring-Mass System** (Linear) | $T = 2\pi \sqrt{\dfrac{m}{k}}$ | $\sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m}}$ | $\dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m}}$ | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ | | 2 | **Simple Pendulum** (small angle) | $T = 2\pi \sqrt{\dfrac{L}{g}}$ | $\sqrt{\dfrac{g}{L}}$ | $\dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{g}{L}}$ | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ | | 3 | **Compound (Physical) Pendulum** | $T = 2\pi \sqrt{\dfrac{I_O}{mgd}}$ | $\sqrt{\dfrac{mgd}{I_O}}$ | $\dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{mgd}{I_O}}$ | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ | | 4 | **Torsional Pendulum** | $T = 2\pi \sqrt{\dfrac{I}{C}}$ | $\sqrt{\dfrac{C}{I}}$ | $\dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{C}{I}}$ | ⭐⭐⭐ | | 5 | **Conical Pendulum** (full period) | $T = 2\pi \sqrt{\dfrac{L \cos\theta}{g}}$ | $\sqrt{\dfrac{g}{L \cos\theta}}$ | $\dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{g}{L \cos\theta}}$ | ⭐⭐ | | 6 | **Seconds Pendulum** (special case) | $T = 2 \text{ s}$ ⇒ $L = 0.994 \text{ m}$ | $\pi$ rad/s | $0.5$ Hz | ⭐⭐ | ### Notation Key for the Table - $m$ = mass of oscillating body (kg) - $k$ = stiffness / spring constant (N/m) - $L$ = effective length of simple pendulum (m) - $g$ = acceleration due to gravity = **9.81 m/s²** - $I_O$ = mass moment of inertia about the suspension (pivot) axis (kg·m²) - $d$ = distance from pivot to centre of gravity (m) - $C$ = torsional stiffness of the shaft (N·m/rad) - $I$ = mass moment of inertia of the disc about the axis of rotation (kg·m²) - $\theta$ = half-angle of the cone traced by the string of a conical pendulum ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility 🏗️ | System | Engineering Application | |--------|-------------------------| | Spring-Mass | Vehicle suspension design, earthquake-resistant buildings, packaging of fragile goods | | Simple Pendulum | Mechanical metronomes, inertial navigation units, seismograph calibration | | Compound Pendulum | Governor mechanisms in old steam engines, damped absorbers | | Torsional Pendulum | Watch balance wheels, gyroscopes, torsional vibration absorbers in IC engine crankshafts | | Seconds Pendulum | Wall clocks, precision time-keeping instruments | > [!NOTE] > **KTU Insight:** Many KTU problems ask — *"If the length of a seconds pendulum is doubled, what happens to its period?"* The answer: $T \to \sqrt{2} T$, so a 2-second pendulum becomes a $2\sqrt{2} \approx 2.83$ s pendulum. This is a frequently asked conceptual question. # 📙 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix > [!IMPORTANT] > **Step-by-step derivations of the period of all four standard KTU-listed systems. Each step is justified with text. No line is skipped.** --- ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Period of a Simple Pendulum **System:** A bob of mass $m$ suspended from a fixed support by a light, inextensible string of length $L$. Displaced through a small angle $\theta$ from the vertical and released. ### Step 1 — Free Body Diagram and Restoring Force For small angular displacement $\theta$, the weight $mg$ acts vertically downward at the bob. The component **tangential to the arc** (i.e., perpendicular to the string) and **opposing the motion** is: $$F_{\text{restore}} = -mg \sin\theta$$ The negative sign indicates that the force is **restoring** (directed towards the equilibrium position). ### Step 2 — Apply Newton's Second Law in the Tangential Direction The bob moves along a circular arc of radius $L$, so its tangential acceleration is $L \ddot{\theta}$. Newton's second law along the tangential direction gives: $$m L \ddot{\theta} = -mg \sin\theta$$ ### Step 3 — Apply the Small-Angle Approximation For small angles, expressed in radians: $$\sin\theta \approx \theta \quad (\text{valid for } \theta \lesssim 15°)$$ Substituting this: $$m L \ddot{\theta} = -mg \theta$$ ### Step 4 — Rearrange into Canonical SHM Form Divide both sides by $mL$: $$\ddot{\theta} + \frac{g}{L} \theta = 0$$ ### Step 5 — Compare with the Standard SHM Equation The standard form is $\ddot{q} + \omega_n^2 \, q = 0$. Comparing term-by-term: $$\omega_n^2 = \frac{g}{L} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{g}{L}}$$ ### Step 6 — Extract the Period Using the defining relation $T = 2\pi / \omega_n$: $$\boxed{T = 2\pi \sqrt{\frac{L}{g}}}$$ --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Period of a Spring-Mass System (Linear) **System:** A mass $m$ attached to a light, linear spring of stiffness $k$, free to slide on a smooth horizontal surface. The mass is pulled to extend the spring by a small distance $x$ and released. ### Step 1 — Restoring Force from Hooke's Law By Hooke's law, the spring exerts a force on the mass that is proportional to the displacement $x$ from the natural (unstretched) length and is directed **towards the mean position**: $$F_{\text{restore}} = -kx$$ ### Step 2 — Apply Newton's Second Law The only horizontal force on the mass is the spring force. Thus: $$m \ddot{x} = -kx$$ ### Step 3 — Rearrange to Canonical SHM Form Divide by $m$: $$\ddot{x} + \frac{k}{m} x = 0$$ ### Step 4 — Identify Angular Frequency Comparing with $\ddot{x} + \omega_n^2 x = 0$: $$\omega_n^2 = \frac{k}{m} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}}$$ ### Step 5 — Extract the Period $$\boxed{T = 2\pi \sqrt{\frac{m}{k}}}$$ --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Period of a Compound (Physical) Pendulum **System:** A rigid body of mass $m$ suspended from a fixed horizontal axis (the pivot) that does **not** pass through its centre of gravity. Let $O$ be the pivot, $G$ be the centre of gravity, and $d$ be the perpendicular distance between $O$ and $G$. ### Step 1 — Restoring Torque When the body is displaced by a small angle $\theta$ from the vertical, the weight $mg$ acting at $G$ produces a torque about $O$. The moment arm is $d \sin\theta$, and the torque acts to restore the body to vertical: $$\tau = -mg \, d \sin\theta$$ ### Step 2 — Apply the Rotational Equation of Motion Using Newton's second law for rotation, $\tau = I_O \ddot{\theta}$, where $I_O$ is the mass moment of inertia about the pivot axis $O$: $$I_O \ddot{\theta} = -mg \, d \sin\theta$$ ### Step 3 — Small-Angle Linearization For small $\theta$: $$I_O \ddot{\theta} = -mg \, d \, \theta$$ ### Step 4 — Canonical SHM Form Rearranging: $$\ddot{\theta} + \frac{mgd}{I_O} \theta = 0$$ ### Step 5 — Identify $\omega_n$ and Period $$\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{mgd}{I_O}}$$ $$\boxed{T = 2\pi \sqrt{\frac{I_O}{mgd}}}$$ ### Step 6 — Equivalent Length of Simple Pendulum The compound pendulum behaves exactly like a simple pendulum of an **equivalent length**: $$L_{\text{eq}} = \frac{I_O}{md}$$ This is the **length of the equivalent simple pendulum** — a high-yield KTU sub-question topic. --- ## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Period of a Torsional Pendulum **System:** A disc of mass moment of inertia $I$ suspended from a thin elastic shaft of torsional stiffness $C$. The disc is rotated by a small angle $\theta$ about the shaft axis and released. ### Step 1 — Restoring Torque The shaft resists twisting, producing a restoring torque proportional to the angular twist: $$\tau = -C \theta$$ ### Step 2 — Equation of Rotational Motion $$\tau = I \ddot{\theta} \quad \Rightarrow \quad I \ddot{\theta} = -C \theta$$ ### Step 3 — Canonical SHM Form $$\ddot{\theta} + \frac{C}{I} \theta = 0$$ ### Step 4 — Extract Period $$\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{C}{I}}$$ $$\boxed{T = 2\pi \sqrt{\frac{I}{C}}}$$ --- ## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Period Dependence on System Parameters | System | Depends on $L$? | Depends on $m$? | Depends on $g$? | Independent of Amplitude? | |--------|:---------------:|:---------------:|:---------------:|:-------------------------:| | Simple Pendulum | $\propto \sqrt{L}$ | ❌ No | $\propto 1/\sqrt{g}$ | ✅ Yes (for small $\theta$) | | Spring-Mass | ❌ No | $\propto \sqrt{m}$ | ❌ No | ✅ Yes (ideal linear spring) | | Compound Pendulum | (via $I_O$, $d$) | (via $I_O$, $d$) | $\propto 1/\sqrt{g}$ | ✅ Yes (small angle) | | Torsional Pendulum | ❌ No | (via $I$) | ❌ No | ✅ Yes (linear elastic shaft) | # 📒 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Schematic Block Diagram — All Period-Defining Systems ```mermaid flowchart TD A["PERIODIC MECHANICAL SYSTEMS
KTU Module 4"] A --> B1["LINEAR VIBRATION
Restoring Force type"] A --> B2["ANGULAR VIBRATION
Restoring Torque type"] A --> B3["GRAVITATIONAL VIBRATION
Restoring force via gravity"] B1 --> C1["SPRING MASS SYSTEM
Force = kx
Period = 2pi sqrt of m over k"] B2 --> C2["TORSIONAL PENDULUM
Torque = C theta
Period = 2pi sqrt of I over C"] B2 --> C3["COMPOUND PENDULUM
Torque = mgd sin theta
Period = 2pi sqrt of I over mgd"] B3 --> C4["SIMPLE PENDULUM
Force = mg sin theta
Period = 2pi sqrt of L over g"] B3 --> C5["CONICAL PENDULUM
3D motion, bob traces circle
Period = 2pi sqrt of L cos theta over g"] B3 --> C6["SECONDS PENDULUM
T = 2 seconds exactly
L = 0.994 metre at 9.81"] C1 --> D1["APPLICATION: Vehicle suspension
Earthquake dampers
Packaging industry"] C2 --> D2["APPLICATION: Watch balance wheel
Gyroscopes, IC engine shafts"] C3 --> D3["APPLICATION: Watt governor
Old steam engine regulators"] C4 --> D4["APPLICATION: Metronomes
Clocks, seismographs"] C5 --> D5["APPLICATION: Helicopter rotor model
Centrifugal governor"] C6 --> D6["APPLICATION: Pendulum wall clocks
Precision timekeeping"] ``` ## 4.2 Free-Body Diagram (Text Schematic) — Simple Pendulum ``` Fixed Support ●───────────── (ceiling) │ │ L (string length) │ │╲ θ (angular displacement, small) │ ╲ │ ╲ │ ● ⟵ mass m (the bob) │ ↙ │ ╱ F_t = -mg sinθ (tangential restoring force) │╱ ▼ (arc of motion, radius L) ``` **Key Annotations:** - The **string** remains taut with tension $T_{\text{string}} = mg \cos\theta$. - Only the **tangential component** of weight drives the oscillation. - The **radial direction** is in dynamic equilibrium along the string. ## 4.3 Free-Body Diagram (Text Schematic) — Spring-Mass System ``` Smooth horizontal surface ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━●━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━ │ │ k (spring, stiffness) │ ┌───┴───┐ │ m │ ⟵ mass └───┬───┘ ↔ x (displacement from mean) (1) Neutral position: ━━━━━●━━━━━━ (2) Stretched: ━━━━●┤ x→ (3) Restoring force: F = -k x (always towards neutral) ``` ## 4.4 Block Functional Flow — Why Period Depends on System Parameters ```mermaid flowchart LR P1["System Geometry
L, d, I, m"] --> M1["Mass Distribution
inertia term"] P2["Material Properties
k, C, g"] --> M2["Restoring Strength
stiffness term"] M1 --> EQ["Equation of Motion
mL theta double dot = - mg d sin theta"] M2 --> EQ EQ --> CAN["Canonical SHM form
theta double dot + omega_n squared theta = 0"] CAN --> EX["Extract omega_n
and then T = 2 pi over omega_n"] ``` # 📕 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## ✏️ PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **State the expression for the period of a simple pendulum. What is a seconds pendulum?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The **period of a simple pendulum** for small angular oscillations is given by: $$T = 2\pi \sqrt{\frac{L}{g}}$$ where $L$ is the effective length of the pendulum (m) and $g$ is the acceleration due to gravity (m/s²). A **seconds pendulum** is a simple pendulum whose period is exactly **$T = 2$ seconds** (one complete oscillation per second, with one oscillation = two beats). At $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$, the length of a seconds pendulum is: $$L = \frac{g T^2}{4\pi^2} = \frac{9.81 \times 4}{4 \times 9.8696} \approx 0.994 \text{ m} \approx 1 \text{ m}$$ --- ### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Define the term "Time Period" as applied to Simple Harmonic Motion. Is the period of SHM dependent on its amplitude?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Time Period (T)** of SHM is defined as the **smallest time interval after which the motion repeats itself** — i.e., the time taken for the particle to complete one full oscillation such that both its **displacement and velocity** are exactly as they were at the start. Mathematically, $T = 2\pi / \omega_n$, where $\omega_n$ is the angular natural frequency of the system. **Dependence on amplitude:** For an **ideal, undamped simple harmonic motion** (governed by a linear restoring force $F \propto -x$), the period is **independent of the amplitude of oscillation**. This property is known as **isochronism**, and was first observed by Galileo. The period depends only on the **system properties** ($m$, $k$, $L$, $g$, $I$, $C$) and not on the initial disturbance. > [!NOTE] > This isochronism is the physical basis of pendulum clocks. --- ## ✏️ PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!IMPORTANT] > **As per KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question has an **internal choice** (OR option). Two alternatives are provided below. --- ### 🅰️ QUESTION A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model]` **Derive an expression for the period of a compound (physical) pendulum. Hence, define the equivalent length of a simple pendulum and the centre of oscillation.** #### (a) Derivation of the period of a compound pendulum — **7 marks** A **compound pendulum** is a rigid body of mass $m$ suspended from a smooth, horizontal cylindrical axis through a point $O$ such that the body can oscillate freely in a vertical plane. Let $G$ be the centre of gravity at a distance $d$ from the suspension axis $O$, and let $I_O$ be the mass moment of inertia of the body about the axis $O$. Consider the body displaced through a small angle $\theta$ from the vertical. The only force producing a torque about $O$ is the weight $mg$ acting at $G$. The moment arm of this weight about $O$ is $d \sin\theta$, and the torque is **restoring** (opposes displacement): $$\tau = -mg \cdot d \sin\theta$$ Applying Newton's second law for rotation, $\tau = I_O \ddot{\theta}$: $$I_O \ddot{\theta} = -mg \, d \sin\theta$$ For small angles, $\sin\theta \approx \theta$: $$I_O \ddot{\theta} = -mg \, d \, \theta$$ Rearranging: $$\ddot{\theta} + \left(\frac{mgd}{I_O}\right) \theta = 0$$ This is the canonical SHM equation with: $$\omega_n^2 = \frac{mgd}{I_O} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{mgd}{I_O}}$$ Therefore, the **period of the compound pendulum** is: $$\boxed{T = 2\pi \sqrt{\frac{I_O}{mgd}}}$$ #### (b) Equivalent length and centre of oscillation — **7 marks** Comparing the period of a compound pendulum with that of a simple pendulum: $$T_{\text{compound}} = 2\pi \sqrt{\frac{I_O}{mgd}} \quad ; \quad T_{\text{simple}} = 2\pi \sqrt{\frac{L_{\text{eq}}}{g}}$$ For both to be equal: $$\frac{L_{\text{eq}}}{g} = \frac{I_O}{mgd} \quad \Rightarrow \quad L_{\text{eq}} = \frac{I_O}{md}$$ **Definition:** The **equivalent length ($L_{\text{eq}}$)** of a compound pendulum is the length of a simple pendulum that has the **same period** of oscillation as the given compound pendulum. Using the **parallel axis theorem**, $I_O = I_G + m d^2$ (where $I_G$ is the moment of inertia about an axis through $G$ parallel to $O$): $$L_{\text{eq}} = \frac{I_G + m d^2}{m d} = \frac{I_G}{m d} + d$$ **Centre of Oscillation:** The point on the compound pendulum at a distance $L_{\text{eq}} = I_O / (md)$ from the pivot $O$ is called the **centre of oscillation**. If the pendulum is suspended from this new point (i.e., from the centre of oscillation), it will have the **same period** as before. This reciprocal property is known as the **reversibility of the centre of suspension and centre of oscillation**, and is a classic KTU sub-question. --- ### 🅱️ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]` **A mass of 4 kg is attached to a spring of stiffness 100 N/m. The system is pulled down by 50 mm from its equilibrium position and released. Determine: (a) the natural frequency, the period, and the angular frequency of the system, and (b) the maximum velocity and maximum acceleration of the mass during the oscillation.** #### Given Data - Mass: $m = 4$ kg - Spring stiffness: $k = 100$ N/m - Initial displacement (amplitude): $A = 50$ mm = 0.05 m - $g = 9.81$ m/s² (not needed here, as vertical gravity is balanced by static spring extension in equilibrium) #### (a) Natural Frequency, Period, and Angular Frequency — **7 marks** **Angular natural frequency:** $$\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}} = \sqrt{\frac{100}{4}} = \sqrt{25} = 5 \text{ rad/s}$$ **Period:** $$T = \frac{2\pi}{\omega_n} = \frac{2\pi}{5} = 1.2566 \text{ s} \approx 1.257 \text{ s}$$ **Natural frequency (Hz):** $$f_n = \frac{1}{T} = \frac{\omega_n}{2\pi} = \frac{5}{2\pi} = 0.7958 \text{ Hz}$$ #### (b) Maximum Velocity and Maximum Acceleration — **7 marks** The displacement, velocity, and acceleration of the mass during SHM are: $$x(t) = A \cos(\omega_n t)$$ $$v(t) = -A \omega_n \sin(\omega_n t)$$ $$a(t) = -A \omega_n^2 \cos(\omega_n t)$$ **Maximum velocity** occurs at the mean position ($x = 0$, $\sin(\omega_n t) = \pm 1$): $$v_{\max} = A \, \omega_n = 0.05 \times 5 = 0.25 \text{ m/s}$$ **Maximum acceleration** occurs at the extreme positions ($x = \pm A$, $\cos(\omega_n t) = \pm 1$): $$a_{\max} = A \, \omega_n^2 = 0.05 \times 25 = 1.25 \text{ m/s}^2$$ **Final Results Summary:** | Quantity | Value | |----------|-------| | Angular frequency $\omega_n$ | **5 rad/s** | | Period $T$ | **1.257 s** | | Natural frequency $f_n$ | **0.796 Hz** | | Max velocity $v_{\max}$ | **0.25 m/s** | | Max acceleration $a_{\max}$ | **1.25 m/s²** | --- ## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common mistakes KTU students make — leading to 1–3 mark deductions:** > > 1. **Forgetting the small-angle approximation** in pendulum derivations. Always state $\sin\theta \approx \theta$ explicitly. > 2. **Mixing up $I_O$ and $I_G$** in compound pendulum problems. The period formula uses $I_O$ (about the pivot), **not** $I_G$. Apply the parallel axis theorem to convert if needed. > 3. **Including $g$ in spring-mass problems** unless specifically asked for static deflection. The natural frequency of vertical and horizontal spring-mass systems is **identical** because gravity is balanced by static extension. > 4. **Confusing seconds pendulum definition** — it is the pendulum with period $T = 2$ s (one full oscillation in 2 seconds), **not** period = 1 s. > 5. **Forgetting the role of amplitude** — stating that period depends on amplitude is a classic error. Period is independent of amplitude in ideal SHM. > 6. **Unit inconsistency** — use SI units throughout (m, kg, N/m, s, rad). KTU strictly enforces this. --- ## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Master Before the Exam** - ⭐ **Period $T$** is the time for one complete cycle of oscillation. **Unit: seconds (s)** - ⭐ **Inverse relationship:** $T = 1/f = 2\pi / \omega_n$ - ⭐ **Simple Pendulum:** $T = 2\pi\sqrt{L/g}$ — depends on length and gravity only. - ⭐ **Spring-Mass:** $T = 2\pi\sqrt{m/k}$ — depends on mass and stiffness only. - ⭐ **Compound Pendulum:** $T = 2\pi\sqrt{I_O / (mgd)}$ — depends on moment of inertia, mass, gravity, and distance to CG. - ⭐ **Torsional Pendulum:** $T = 2\pi\sqrt{I/C}$ — depends on moment of inertia and torsional stiffness. - ⭐ **Seconds Pendulum:** $T = 2$ s → Length $L \approx 0.994$ m at $g = 9.81$ m/s². - ⭐ **Period of SHM is independent of amplitude** (Isochronism — Galileo's discovery). - ⭐ **Small-angle approximation** $\sin\theta \approx \theta$ is mandatory for pendulum analyses. - ⭐ The **canonical SHM equation** is $\ddot{q} + \omega_n^2 q = 0$ — every derivation must reduce to this. - ⭐ **Equivalent length** of compound pendulum: $L_{\text{eq}} = I_O / (md)$. - ⭐ **Maximum velocity** $v_{\max} = A \omega_n$, **Maximum acceleration** $a_{\max} = A \omega_n^2$. - ⭐ **Standard value:** $g = 9.81$ m/s² (use 9.80665 for high-precision problems).

topics.title: Stiffness
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GBEST213 - ENGINEERING MECHANICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Stiffness

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stiffness ($k$)** is defined as the capacity of a body or structural element to resist deformation (linear displacement or angular twist) when subjected to an applied load. Mathematically, it is the ratio of the applied load (force or moment) to the resulting deformation produced in the direction of the load.

In the **linear elastic region**, stiffness is expressed as:

$$k = \frac{\text{Applied Force } (F)}{\text{Resulting Deformation } (\delta)}$$

Its SI unit is **N/m (Newton per metre)**. For rotational/twisting loads, it is termed **Torsional Stiffness** with unit **N·m/rad**.

The **inverse** of stiffness is called **Compliance (or Flexibility)**, denoted as:

$$c = \frac{1}{k}$$

Compliance has the unit **m/N**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Stiffness is most commonly introduced in the context of **springs**, which are elastic components specifically designed to store potential energy through elastic deformation. Module 4 emphasises stiffness in **linear (translational)** and **rotational (torsional)** forms, alongside combinations of springs in **series and parallel**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: A Trampoline vs. A Wooden Plank**
> Imagine pushing down on a **trampoline** versus a **thick wooden plank** with the same force. The trampoline deforms significantly (low stiffness), whereas the wooden plank barely moves (high stiffness). Stiffness is essentially a measure of *how stubbornly* a body refuses to deform.

Another way to visualise stiffness:

- A **stiffer** object → steeper load–deformation graph → larger $k$.
- A **softer (more compliant)** object → flatter load–deformation graph → smaller $k$.

A more relatable analogy is the **spring balance in a weighing scale**. The extension of the spring per unit weight tells us its stiffness. A "hard" spring gives a small extension for a large weight (high $k$), whereas a "soft" spring stretches easily (low $k$).

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Standard Units (SI System):**

| Quantity | Symbol | SI Unit |
|----------|--------|---------|
| Linear Stiffness | $k$ | **N/m** |
| Torsional Stiffness | $k_t$ | **N·m/rad** |
| Force | $F$ | **N (Newton)** |
| Linear Deformation | $\delta$ or $x$ | **m (metre)** |
| Angular Deformation | $\theta$ | **rad (radian)** |
| Compliance | $c$ | **m/N** |
| Modulus of Rigidity | $G$ | **N/m² (Pa)** |

---

## 1.4 Load–Deformation Relationship

Stiffness is the **slope of the Force–Displacement curve** in the elastic region. For a **linear (Hookean) spring**:

$$F = k \cdot \delta$$

The graph is a straight line through the origin with slope $k$. The **stiffer** the spring, the **steeper** the line.

For a **torsional spring**:

$$T = k_t \cdot \theta$$

where $T$ is the applied torque and $\theta$ is the angle of twist in radians.

---

## 1.5 Categories of Stiffness in KTU Module 4

> [!NOTE]
> Stiffness in Module 4 of Engineering Mechanics is studied under **three primary categories:**

1. **Linear (Translational) Stiffness** — resistance to axial/transverse displacement.
2. **Torsional (Rotational) Stiffness** — resistance to angular twist.
3. **Equivalent/Combined Stiffness** — behaviour of multiple springs connected in series or parallel.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Conceptual Foundation — The "Why" Behind Stiffness

Every elastic body obeys **Hooke's Law** within its elastic limit, which states that *the deformation produced is directly proportional to the applied load*. The proportionality constant between them is the **stiffness** of that body.

Mathematically, Hooke's Law in its most general form:

$$\sigma = E \cdot \epsilon$$

where:
- $\sigma$ = Stress (N/m²)
- $E$ = Young's Modulus of Elasticity (N/m²)
- $\epsilon$ = Strain (dimensionless)

When this is integrated over a structural member, the **stiffness** emerges as the constant linking the **macroscopic load** to the **macroscopic deformation**.

---

## 2.2 Linear (Translational) Stiffness of a Spring

For an ideal linear spring:

$$F = k \cdot x$$

$$\therefore k = \frac{F}{x}$$

Where:
- $F$ = Applied axial force (N)
- $x$ = Linear displacement / extension / compression (m)
- $k$ = Stiffness of the spring (N/m)

**Interpretation:** A force of $F$ Newtons produces a deformation of $x$ metres along the line of action. The ratio is the stiffness.

---

## 2.3 Torsional (Rotational) Stiffness of a Shaft

For a circular shaft subjected to torque:

$$T = \frac{G \cdot J \cdot \theta}{L}$$

Rearranging:

$$T = k_t \cdot \theta$$

$$\therefore k_t = \frac{T}{\theta} = \frac{G \cdot J}{L}$$

Where:
- $T$ = Applied torque (N·m)
- $\theta$ = Angle of twist (rad)
- $G$ = Modulus of rigidity of shaft material (N/m²)
- $J$ = Polar moment of inertia of cross-section (m⁴)
- $L$ = Length of shaft (m)
- $k_t$ = Torsional stiffness (N·m/rad)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** Torsional stiffness is critical in **propeller shafts, drive shafts, turbine rotors, and torsion bars in vehicle suspension systems**. A drive shaft with insufficient torsional stiffness will twist excessively under load, causing vibration and power loss.

---

## 2.4 Stiffness of a Helical (Coil) Spring

A helical spring is the most common form of spring in engineering practice. The standard formula for stiffness:

$$k = \frac{G \cdot d^4}{8 \cdot D^3 \cdot N}$$

Where:
- $G$ = Modulus of rigidity of wire material (N/m²)
- $d$ = Diameter of spring wire (m)
- $D$ = Mean coil diameter (m)
- $N$ = Number of active coils (dimensionless)

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters:** Doubling the wire diameter increases stiffness by **$2^4 = 16$ times**, while doubling the mean coil diameter **decreases** stiffness by **$2^3 = 8$ times**. This is a high-yield KTU exam observation.

---

## 2.5 Equivalent Stiffness — Springs in Parallel

When springs are connected such that they share the **same deformation** but **divide the load**, they are in **parallel**.

For two springs $k_1$ and $k_2$ in parallel:

$$k_{eq} = k_1 + k_2$$

For $n$ springs in parallel:

$$k_{eq} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} k_i$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Equivalent Stiffness Rule (Parallel):** Always **greater** than the individual largest stiffness. **Stiffness adds directly in parallel.**

---

## 2.6 Equivalent Stiffness — Springs in Series

When springs are connected such that they carry the **same load** but produce **cumulative deformation**, they are in **series**.

For two springs $k_1$ and $k_2$ in series:

$$\frac{1}{k_{eq}} = \frac{1}{k_1} + \frac{1}{k_2}$$

For $n$ springs in series:

$$\frac{1}{k_{eq}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{1}{k_i}$$

The equivalent stiffness is given by:

$$k_{eq} = \frac{1}{\sum \frac{1}{k_i}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Equivalent Stiffness Rule (Series):** Always **less** than the individual smallest stiffness. **Compliance (1/k) adds in series.**

---

## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **THE ULTIMATE KTU STIFFNESS FORMULA TABLE — MEMORISE THIS**

| # | Concept | Formula | SI Unit |
|---|---------|---------|---------|
| 1 | Linear Stiffness (Hooke's Law) | $k = \dfrac{F}{x}$ | **N/m** |
| 2 | Compliance | $c = \dfrac{x}{F} = \dfrac{1}{k}$ | **m/N** |
| 3 | Torsional Stiffness | $k_t = \dfrac{T}{\theta}$ | **N·m/rad** |
| 4 | Torsional Stiffness of Shaft | $k_t = \dfrac{G \cdot J}{L}$ | **N·m/rad** |
| 5 | Helical Spring Stiffness | $k = \dfrac{G \cdot d^4}{8 \cdot D^3 \cdot N}$ | **N/m** |
| 6 | Springs in Parallel (2) | $k_{eq} = k_1 + k_2$ | **N/m** |
| 7 | Springs in Series (2) | $\dfrac{1}{k_{eq}} = \dfrac{1}{k_1} + \dfrac{1}{k_2}$ | **N/m** |
| 8 | Springs in Parallel ($n$) | $k_{eq} = \sum k_i$ | **N/m** |
| 9 | Springs in Series ($n$) | $\dfrac{1}{k_{eq}} = \sum \dfrac{1}{k_i}$ | **N/m** |
| 10 | Strain Energy Stored | $U = \dfrac{1}{2} k x^2 = \dfrac{1}{2} k_t \theta^2$ | **J (Joule)** |
| 11 | Natural Frequency (SDOF) | $\omega_n = \sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m}}$ | **rad/s** |

---

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Where Stiffness Governs Design Decisions:**

- **Civil Engineering:** Stiffness of beams, columns, and trusses governs deflection limits (e.g., a floor beam must not deflect more than L/360).
- **Mechanical Engineering:** Machine tool beds require **high stiffness** to maintain dimensional accuracy during cutting.
- **Automotive Engineering:** Suspension springs have carefully tuned stiffness to balance ride comfort and handling.
- **Aerospace Engineering:** Aircraft wings must have appropriate stiffness to avoid aeroelastic flutter.
- **Biomechanics:** Spinal disc stiffness is a critical parameter in prosthesis and implant design.
- **Civil / Structural:** Earthquake-resistant buildings are designed with controlled stiffness to manage drift and dynamic response.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Equivalent Stiffness of Two Springs in Parallel

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Setup:** Two springs $k_1$ and $k_2$ are connected in **parallel** between two rigid supports. A common force $F$ is applied, producing the **same deformation $x$** in both springs.

**Step 1 — Apply the principle of superposition (load distribution):**
The total applied force $F$ is shared between the two springs:

$$F = F_1 + F_2$$

**Step 2 — Apply Hooke's Law to each spring individually:**

$$F_1 = k_1 \cdot x \quad \text{and} \quad F_2 = k_2 \cdot x$$

**Step 3 — Substitute back into the force balance:**

$$F = k_1 \cdot x + k_2 \cdot x$$

**Step 4 — Factor out the common deformation $x$:**

$$F = (k_1 + k_2) \cdot x$$

**Step 5 — Compare with the equivalent single spring relation $F = k_{eq} \cdot x$:**

$$F = k_{eq} \cdot x = (k_1 + k_2) \cdot x$$

**Step 6 — Equate the stiffness coefficients:**

$$\boxed{k_{eq} = k_1 + k_2}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation:** Springs in parallel behave like a single "thicker" spring — the effective stiffness **adds up** because both springs resist the load simultaneously.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Equivalent Stiffness of Two Springs in Series

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Setup:** Two springs $k_1$ and $k_2$ are connected **end-to-end (in series)**. A common force $F$ is transmitted through both, but the total deformation $x$ is the **sum of individual deformations**.

**Step 1 — Establish the deformation compatibility condition:**
The total extension equals the sum of extensions:

$$x = x_1 + x_2$$

**Step 2 — Apply Hooke's Law to each spring (force is common in series):**

$$x_1 = \frac{F}{k_1} \quad \text{and} \quad x_2 = \frac{F}{k_2}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into the compatibility equation:**

$$x = \frac{F}{k_1} + \frac{F}{k_2}$$

**Step 4 — Factor out the common force $F$:**

$$x = F \left( \frac{1}{k_1} + \frac{1}{k_2} \right)$$

**Step 5 — Rearrange into the form $F = k_{eq} \cdot x$:**

$$F = \frac{x}{\left( \dfrac{1}{k_1} + \dfrac{1}{k_2} \right)}$$

**Step 6 — Equate the equivalent stiffness coefficient:**

$$\boxed{\frac{1}{k_{eq}} = \frac{1}{k_1} + \frac{1}{k_2} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad k_{eq} = \frac{k_1 \cdot k_2}{k_1 + k_2}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation:** Springs in series behave like a single "longer" spring — the effective stiffness **decreases** because the same force now produces deformation across both springs.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example: Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem (KTU 2024 Pattern):** Two helical springs A and B are connected in series between a fixed support and a rigid load block. Spring A has stiffness $k_A = 4000$ N/m and Spring B has $k_B = 6000$ N/m. A weight of 100 N is hung from the free end. Find:
> (a) The equivalent stiffness of the system.
> (b) The total extension produced.
> (c) The extension of each individual spring.

**Given:**
- $k_A = 4000$ N/m
- $k_B = 6000$ N/m
- $F = 100$ N

---

**Solution (a) — Equivalent Stiffness:**

Springs are in **series**, so:

$$\frac{1}{k_{eq}} = \frac{1}{k_A} + \frac{1}{k_B}$$

$$\frac{1}{k_{eq}} = \frac{1}{4000} + \frac{1}{6000}$$

$$\frac{1}{k_{eq}} = \frac{3 + 2}{12000} = \frac{5}{12000}$$

$$k_{eq} = \frac{12000}{5} = \boxed{2400 \text{ N/m}}$$

---

**Solution (b) — Total Extension:**

$$x_{total} = \frac{F}{k_{eq}} = \frac{100}{2400}$$

$$x_{total} = 0.04167 \text{ m} = \boxed{41.67 \text{ mm}}$$

---

**Solution (c) — Individual Extensions (force in series is common = 100 N):**

$$x_A = \frac{F}{k_A} = \frac{100}{4000} = 0.025 \text{ m} = 25 \text{ mm}$$

$$x_B = \frac{F}{k_B} = \frac{100}{6000} = 0.01667 \text{ m} = 16.67 \text{ mm}$$

**Verification:**

$$x_A + x_B = 25 + 16.67 = 41.67 \text{ mm} = x_{total} \quad \checkmark$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Takeaway:** Observe that the **stiffer spring (B, 6000 N/m) deforms LESS (16.67 mm)** than the **softer spring (A, 4000 N/m) which deforms MORE (25 mm)**. In series, deformation is inversely proportional to individual stiffness.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Compound Spring Configurations

> [!NOTE]
> **Decision Table for Identifying Series vs. Parallel**

| Configuration Criterion | Series | Parallel |
|--------------------------|--------|----------|
| **Force** through each spring | Same in all | Divided among them |
| **Deformation** of each spring | Different, sum to total | Same for all |
| **Equivalent Stiffness** vs. smallest $k$ | $k_{eq} < \text{min}(k_i)$ | $k_{eq} > \text{max}(k_i)$ |
| **Adding another spring** | $k_{eq}$ **decreases** | $k_{eq}$ **increases** |
| **Analogy** | Capacitors in series (E) | Capacitors in parallel (E) |
| **Formula Type** | $\frac{1}{k_{eq}}$ adds | $k_{eq}$ adds |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow — Stiffness Relationships

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid flowchart maps the **interactions between stiffness types, governing laws, and combined configurations** studied under KTU Module 4. It serves as a high-level architecture view of the concept.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[STIFFNESS] --> B[Linear Translational k]
    A --> C[Torsional Rotational kt]
    A --> D[Combined Equivalent keq]

    B --> B1[F equals k times x]
    B1 --> B2[SI Unit N per m]
    B2 --> B3[Hookes Law in Macroscopic Form]

    C --> C1[T equals kt times theta]
    C1 --> C2[SI Unit N m per rad]
    C2 --> C3[Applies to Shafts and Torsion Bars]

    D --> D1[Springs in Parallel]
    D --> D2[Springs in Series]

    D1 --> D1A[Same Deformation]
    D1 --> D1B[Force Splits Among Springs]
    D1 --> D1C[keq equals k1 plus k2 plus ... plus kn]

    D2 --> D2A[Same Force in All Springs]
    D2 --> D2B[Deformation Adds Up]
    D2 --> D2C[One over keq equals Sum of one over ki]

    B3 --> E[Strain Energy equals half k x squared]
    C3 --> E
    D1C --> E
    D2C --> E

    E --> F[Natural Frequency omega n equals sqrt of k over m]
    F --> G[Foundation for Vibration Analysis in Higher Semesters]
```

---

## 4.2 Text-Based Schematic: Parallel vs. Series Spring Arrangement

> [!NOTE]
> The following text-art diagram clarifies the geometric difference between **parallel** and **series** spring connections. This is critical for KTU 3-mark questions.

```
        PARALLEL CONNECTION                       SERIES CONNECTION
    (Same deformation x, force divides)      (Same force F, deformation sums)

   -----▲-----------▲-------                 -----▲-----▲-----▲-------
         |           |                               |     |     |
        [k1]        [k2]                            [k1]  [k2]
         |           |                               |     |     |
   -----▼-----------▼-------                 -----▼-----▼-----▼-------
         \           /                                 |
          \         /                                  |
           \       /                                   |
            ▼     ▼                                    ▼
         F1 + F2 = F                              F passes through
         x same in both                           x1 + x2 = x
         keq = k1 + k2                            1/keq = 1/k1 + 1/k2
```

---

## 4.3 Load–Displacement Curve Interpretation

> [!NOTE]
> **Graphical interpretation of stiffness as slope:**

```
   F (Force)
   ▲
   │                              ●  Stiff Spring (k2 = high)
   │                          ●
   │                      ●
   │                  ●              Slope = k2  (steeper = stiffer)
   │              ●
   │          ●
   │      ●                    ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─
   │  ●
   │●  Softer Spring (k1 = low)
   │
   └──────────────────────────►  x (Displacement)

         Slope of line = k  (Stiffness)
         Steeper line  = Higher stiffness
         Flatter line  = Lower stiffness  (more compliant)
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> **All questions below strictly follow KTU 2024 Scheme pattern. Mark distribution is in line with the official End-Semester Examination (ESE) regulation.**

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**Define stiffness. State its SI unit and explain the difference between linear stiffness and torsional stiffness.**

#### Model Answer:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stiffness ($k$)** is defined as the ability of a body to resist deformation under an applied load. It is mathematically expressed as the ratio of the applied load to the corresponding deformation produced.

**Linear Stiffness** is for translational (linear) deformation:

$$k = \frac{F}{x} \quad \text{(Unit: N/m)}$$

**Torsional Stiffness** is for rotational (angular) deformation:

$$k_t = \frac{T}{\theta} \quad \text{(Unit: N·m/rad)}$$

The two differ in the **type of deformation** they quantify — linear stiffness measures resistance to linear displacement, while torsional stiffness measures resistance to angular twist.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**State Hooke's Law. Derive the relationship between equivalent stiffness and individual stiffnesses for two springs connected in parallel.**

#### Model Answer:

**Hooke's Law** states that within the elastic limit, the deformation produced in a body is directly proportional to the applied load. For a spring: $F \propto x \;\Rightarrow\; F = kx$.

**Derivation for two springs in parallel:**

Both springs undergo the same deformation $x$. The total force is:

$$F = F_1 + F_2 = k_1 x + k_2 x = (k_1 + k_2)x$$

Comparing with $F = k_{eq} \cdot x$:

$$\boxed{k_{eq} = k_1 + k_2}$$

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **As per KTU 2024 regulation, candidates must answer ONE full question from the choice provided. Each Part B question has two sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern]

**(a)** Two helical springs A and B are connected in **series** between a rigid ceiling and a load. The stiffnesses are $k_A = 2 \text{ kN/m}$ and $k_B = 3 \text{ kN/m}$. A mass of 50 kg is suspended from the free end. Calculate:
1. The equivalent stiffness of the system.
2. The total static deflection.
3. The deflection of each spring.

**(b)** A solid circular steel shaft of length 1.5 m and diameter 40 mm is fixed at one end. A torque of 200 N·m is applied at the free end. Given $G = 80 \text{ GPa}$, find:
1. The torsional stiffness of the shaft.
2. The angle of twist at the free end.
3. The maximum shear stress developed in the shaft.

#### Model Solution to Question A (a):

**Given:**
- $k_A = 2000$ N/m, $k_B = 3000$ N/m
- $m = 50$ kg, hence $F = mg = 50 \times 9.81 = 490.5$ N

**1. Equivalent stiffness (Series):**

$$\frac{1}{k_{eq}} = \frac{1}{k_A} + \frac{1}{k_B} = \frac{1}{2000} + \frac{1}{3000}$$

$$\frac{1}{k_{eq}} = \frac{3 + 2}{6000} = \frac{5}{6000}$$

$$k_{eq} = 1200 \text{ N/m}$$

**2. Total static deflection:**

$$x_{total} = \frac{F}{k_{eq}} = \frac{490.5}{1200} = 0.4088 \text{ m} = 408.75 \text{ mm}$$

**3. Individual deflections (force common in series = 490.5 N):**

$$x_A = \frac{F}{k_A} = \frac{490.5}{2000} = 0.2453 \text{ m} = 245.25 \text{ mm}$$

$$x_B = \frac{F}{k_B} = \frac{490.5}{3000} = 0.1635 \text{ m} = 163.5 \text{ mm}$$

**Verification:** $x_A + x_B = 245.25 + 163.5 = 408.75 \text{ mm} = x_{total}$ ✓

#### Model Solution to Question A (b):

**Given:**
- $L = 1.5$ m, $d = 40 \text{ mm} = 0.04$ m
- $T = 200$ N·m, $G = 80 \text{ GPa} = 80 \times 10^9 \text{ N/m}^2$

**1. Polar moment of inertia:**

$$J = \frac{\pi d^4}{32} = \frac{\pi \times (0.04)^4}{32}$$

$$J = \frac{\pi \times 2.56 \times 10^{-6}}{32} = 2.513 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m}^4$$

**2. Torsional stiffness:**

$$k_t = \frac{G \cdot J}{L} = \frac{80 \times 10^9 \times 2.513 \times 10^{-7}}{1.5}$$

$$k_t = \frac{20106.2}{1.5} = 13404.14 \text{ N·m/rad}$$

**3. Angle of twist:**

$$\theta = \frac{T}{k_t} = \frac{200}{13404.14} = 0.01492 \text{ rad} = 0.855°$$

**4. Maximum shear stress:**

$$\tau_{max} = \frac{T \cdot r}{J} = \frac{16 T}{\pi d^3}$$

$$\tau_{max} = \frac{16 \times 200}{\pi \times (0.04)^3} = \frac{3200}{\pi \times 6.4 \times 10^{-5}}$$

$$\tau_{max} = \frac{3200}{2.0106 \times 10^{-4}} = 15.92 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa} = 15.92 \text{ MPa}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern]

**(a)** Two springs of stiffness $k_1 = 5$ kN/m and $k_2 = 7$ kN/m are connected in **parallel** and carry a common load of 600 N. Find:
1. The equivalent stiffness.
2. The deflection of the system.
3. The load shared by each spring.

**(b)** A close-coiled helical spring has 10 active coils, mean coil diameter 60 mm, and wire diameter 8 mm. If the spring is subjected to an axial load of 200 N, determine:
1. The stiffness of the spring (take $G = 80$ GPa).
2. The deflection of the spring.
3. The maximum shear stress in the wire.

#### Model Solution to Question B (a):

**Given:**
- $k_1 = 5000$ N/m, $k_2 = 7000$ N/m
- $F = 600$ N

**1. Equivalent stiffness (Parallel):**

$$k_{eq} = k_1 + k_2 = 5000 + 7000 = 12000 \text{ N/m}$$

**2. Deflection of the system (same for both springs in parallel):**

$$x = \frac{F}{k_{eq}} = \frac{600}{12000} = 0.05 \text{ m} = 50 \text{ mm}$$

**3. Load shared by each spring:**

$$F_1 = k_1 \cdot x = 5000 \times 0.05 = 250 \text{ N}$$

$$F_2 = k_2 \cdot x = 7000 \times 0.05 = 350 \text{ N}$$

**Verification:** $F_1 + F_2 = 250 + 350 = 600$ N = $F$ ✓

#### Model Solution to Question B (b):

**Given:**
- $N = 10$ coils
- $D = 60 \text{ mm} = 0.06$ m
- $d = 8 \text{ mm} = 0.008$ m
- $F = 200$ N
- $G = 80 \times 10^9$ N/m²

**1. Stiffness of helical spring:**

$$k = \frac{G \cdot d^4}{8 \cdot D^3 \cdot N}$$

$$k = \frac{80 \times 10^9 \times (0.008)^4}{8 \times (0.06)^3 \times 10}$$

**Numerator:** $80 \times 10^9 \times 4.096 \times 10^{-9} = 327.68$ N·m

**Denominator:** $8 \times 2.16 \times 10^{-4} \times 10 = 0.01728$ m³

$$k = \frac{327.68}{0.01728} = 18963 \text{ N/m} \approx 18.96 \text{ kN/m}$$

**2. Deflection of the spring:**

$$\delta = \frac{F}{k} = \frac{200}{18963} = 0.01055 \text{ m} = 10.55 \text{ mm}$$

**3. Maximum shear stress (Wahl's correction may be applied; here using simple form):**

$$\tau_{max} = \frac{8 F D}{\pi d^3}$$

$$\tau_{max} = \frac{8 \times 200 \times 0.06}{\pi \times (0.008)^3}$$

$$\tau_{max} = \frac{96}{\pi \times 5.12 \times 10^{-7}} = \frac{96}{1.6085 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$\tau_{max} = 59.68 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa} = 59.68 \text{ MPa}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes KTU examiners penalise heavily:**
>
> 1. **Mixing up Series vs. Parallel:** Students often add stiffnesses directly even for series combinations, or add compliances for parallel. **Memory trick:** *"Force same → Series → Add compliances"*. *"Deformation same → Parallel → Add stiffnesses"*.
>
> 2. **Forgetting to convert units:** $G$ is given in GPa, lengths in mm, loads in N — must convert to **SI (N, m, Pa)** before substitution. Most calculation errors (≈ 2-3 marks loss) come from this.
>
> 3. **Using the wrong formula for helical springs:** Some students mistakenly use $k = Gd^4/(8D^3N)$ for **torsion bar** problems. Remember: the helical spring formula is **specific to coiled springs**, not straight shafts.
>
> 4. **Sign convention for deflection:** Always report deflection as a **positive magnitude** unless the question explicitly demands direction. Negative signs in static equilibrium problems lose marks.
>
> 5. **Not verifying answers:** In series/parallel problems, ALWAYS check whether the equivalent stiffness lies within the expected range. For series, $k_{eq} < \min(k_i)$; for parallel, $k_{eq} > \max(k_i)$. If it doesn't, **recheck your work** — this single check can save 5+ marks.
>
> 6. **Polar moment of inertia confusion:** For **solid** shaft, $J = \pi d^4/32$. For **hollow** shaft, $J = \pi (D^4 - d^4)/32$. Mixing these up is a common 3-mark deduction.
>
> 7. **Force vs. Weight confusion:** If a mass $m$ is given, the actual force is $F = mg$ (with $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$). Plugging in $m$ directly as the force is a frequent error.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — Revise before entering the KTU exam hall**

- [ ] **Definition of Stiffness:** $k = F/x$ (linear), $k_t = T/\theta$ (torsional). Units: **N/m** and **N·m/rad**.
- [ ] **Compliance:** $c = 1/k$ (flexibility). Unit: **m/N**.
- [ ] **Series vs. Parallel Identification:** Same force = Series; Same deformation = Parallel.
- [ ] **Series Formula:** $\frac{1}{k_{eq}} = \sum \frac{1}{k_i}$. Result is **less than smallest** $k$.
- [ ] **Parallel Formula:** $k_{eq} = \sum k_i$. Result is **greater than largest** $k$.
- [ ] **Helical Spring Formula:** $k = G d^4 / (8 D^3 N)$. Wire diameter $d$ has a $4^{th}$-power effect.
- [ ] **Torsional Stiffness of Shaft:** $k_t = GJ/L$. Depends on material, geometry, and length.
- [ ] **Polar moment of inertia:** Solid $J = \pi d^4/32$, Hollow $J = \pi(D^4 - d^4)/32$.
- [ ] **Strain Energy:** $U = \frac{1}{2} k x^2 = \frac{1}{2} k_t \theta^2$.
- [ ] **Natural Frequency (foundation concept):** $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}$ (rad/s). This links stiffness to **vibrations** in higher semesters.
- [ ] **Always convert to SI units** before substituting. GPa → Pa, mm → m, kN → N.
- [ ] **Force in series is common.** Deformation in parallel is common.
- [ ] **Verify** that $k_{eq}$ lies in the expected range (min < series < parallel, max).
- [ ] **Hooke's Law** is valid only in the **elastic region**. Stiffness changes beyond the yield point.
- [ ] **Slope of F–x graph** = stiffness. Steeper slope = stiffer spring.
- [ ] **Mechanical analogy:** Springs-in-series ↔ Capacitors-in-series; Springs-in-parallel ↔ Capacitors-in-parallel. This analogy is sometimes asked as a 2-mark conceptual question.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> - **Topic:** Frequency

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Frequency (f):** In engineering mechanics, frequency is defined as the number of complete cycles, oscillations, or revolutions executed by a system per unit time. It is the reciprocal of the **time period (T)** and is measured in **Hertz (Hz)**, where **1 Hz = 1 cycle per second = 1 s⁻¹**.

Mathematically, the **fundamental frequency equation** is:

$$f = \frac{1}{T}$$

Where:
- $f$ = Frequency in **Hertz (Hz)**
- $T$ = Time Period in **seconds (s)**

In rotational and oscillatory systems, we often encounter the **Angular Frequency (ω)**, which represents the rate of change of phase angle per unit time:

$$\omega = 2\pi f = \frac{2\pi}{T}$$

Units of angular frequency: **radians per second (rad/s)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** In Module 4 (Dynamics/Kinetics), frequency appears as a critical parameter when analyzing **Simple Harmonic Motion (SHM)** of pendulums, spring-mass systems, and torsional systems. The natural frequency governs the dynamic response of mechanical structures.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuition

Imagine you are sitting beside an old grandfather clock. The pendulum inside swings back and forth — completing **one full cycle** (left → right → left) every **2 seconds**. If you count how many complete swings it makes in **1 minute (60 seconds)**, you get **30 oscillations**.

- **Time Period (T) = 2 seconds** → "How long does one cycle take?"
- **Frequency (f) = 1/2 = 0.5 Hz** → "How many cycles per second?"

Another everyday analogy: **Your heartbeat**. A healthy resting heart beats about **72 times per minute**.
- Frequency = 72 / 60 = **1.2 Hz**
- Period = 60 / 72 ≈ **0.833 seconds**

In engineering, frequency is omnipresent:
- A **car engine** idling at 800 RPM → frequency = 800/60 ≈ **13.33 Hz**
- A **seismic wave** from an earthquake might be **0.1 Hz** (very slow)
- A **smartphone vibration motor** operates at **150–200 Hz**

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Standard gravity | g | **9.81 m/s²** |
| Pi constant | π | **3.14159...** |
| 2π (radians per cycle) | 2π | **6.28318... rad** |
| Frequency unit (SI) | Hz | **s⁻¹** |
| Angular frequency unit | rad/s | **s⁻¹** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Breakdown of Frequency Concepts in Engineering Mechanics

### A. Linear Frequency vs. Angular Frequency

- **Linear frequency (f)** counts complete cycles per second.
- **Angular frequency (ω)** counts radians traversed per second.
- Since one full cycle = $2\pi$ radians, the conversion is always $\omega = 2\pi f$.

### B. Natural Frequency in Simple Harmonic Motion (SHM)

When a system is displaced from its equilibrium position and released, it oscillates at its **natural frequency (fₙ or ωₙ)**. This is determined purely by the system's **mass** and **stiffness**, not by the amplitude.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Concept:** Natural frequency is the *signature* of a mechanical system. Every structure has one — a building, a bridge, a pendulum, a tuning fork. If an external periodic force matches this frequency, **resonance** occurs, leading to catastrophic failure (e.g., Tacoma Narrows Bridge, 1940).

### C. Free vs. Forced Vibration

- **Free vibration frequency** = Natural frequency (ωₙ) — the system oscillates on its own after an initial disturbance.
- **Forced vibration frequency** = Driving frequency (ω) — imposed by an external force.
- When $\omega = \omega_n$ → **Resonance** (dangerous in real systems).

## 2.2 The 'Why' and 'How' Behind Each Formula

**Why does a pendulum have a fixed frequency?**
Because gravity ($g$) and length ($L$) are the only parameters governing its motion, and energy conservation forces it to complete each cycle in the same time — independent of swing amplitude (for small angles, this is *isochronism*, discovered by Galileo).

**Why does a spring-mass system oscillate?**
The spring stores **potential energy** when stretched/compressed, and the mass possesses **kinetic energy** at equilibrium. The continuous interchange between PE and KE produces periodic motion.

**Why is ωₙ called "natural"?**
Because it is intrinsic to the system — it is the *preferred* frequency at which the system "wants" to oscillate. No external forcing is required.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table contains **all high-yield formulas** for frequency-related problems in the KTU 2024 Engineering Mechanics syllabus.

| # | System / Concept | Frequency Formula | Angular Frequency Formula | Key Variables | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | General Periodic Motion | $f = \dfrac{1}{T}$ | $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi}{T}$ | T = period | Hz, rad/s |
| 2 | Simple Pendulum (small angle) | $f = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{g}{L}}$ | $\omega = \sqrt{\dfrac{g}{L}}$ | g = 9.81 m/s², L = length | Hz, rad/s |
| 3 | Spring-Mass System | $f = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m}}$ | $\omega = \sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m}}$ | k = stiffness, m = mass | Hz, rad/s |
| 4 | Torsional Pendulum | $f = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{c}{I}}$ | $\omega = \sqrt{\dfrac{c}{I}}$ | c = torsional stiffness, I = mass moment of inertia | Hz, rad/s |
| 5 | Conical Pendulum (vertical circle) | $f = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{g}{L\cos\theta}}$ | $\omega = \sqrt{\dfrac{g}{L\cos\theta}}$ | L = string length, θ = half-angle of cone | Hz, rad/s |
| 6 | Rotating Mass (RPM to Hz) | $f = \dfrac{N}{60}$ | $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$ | N = revolutions per minute | Hz, rad/s |
| 7 | U-Tube Liquid Oscillation | $f = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{2g}{h}}$ | $\omega = \sqrt{\dfrac{2g}{h}}$ | h = height of liquid column | Hz, rad/s |
| 8 | Compound Pendulum | $f = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{mgL}{I_O}}$ | $\omega = \sqrt{\dfrac{mgL}{I_O}}$ | L = CG distance from pivot, $I_O$ = MI about pivot | Hz, rad/s |
| 9 | Stretched String (transverse wave) | $f = \dfrac{1}{2L}\sqrt{\dfrac{T}{\mu}}$ | — | T = tension, μ = linear density | Hz |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** These frequencies dictate the design of **seismic-resistant buildings**, **vehicle suspension tuning**, **machinery isolation**, **clock mechanisms**, and **structural health monitoring** systems. KTU examiners frequently test these formulas with numerical substitution.

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following derivations are **exhaustive, step-by-step** and aligned with the **KTU board examiner's expected solution style**. Every algebraic transition is explicitly stated.

---

## 3.1 Derivation: Frequency of a Simple Pendulum

**Setup:** A point mass $m$ suspended from a fixed pivot by a light, inextensible string of length $L$, displaced by a small angle $\theta$ and released.

**Step 1 — Force Analysis:**
The weight $mg$ acts vertically downward. The component of weight **tangential** to the arc of motion is:

$$F_t = -mg\sin\theta$$

The negative sign indicates the force is **restoring** (directed toward equilibrium).

**Step 2 — Apply Newton's Second Law (Tangential Direction):**
Tangential acceleration is $a_t = L\dfrac{d^2\theta}{dt^2}$. By Newton's law:

$$mL\frac{d^2\theta}{dt^2} = -mg\sin\theta$$

**Step 3 — Simplify:**

$$\frac{d^2\theta}{dt^2} = -\frac{g}{L}\sin\theta$$

**Step 4 — Apply Small-Angle Approximation:**
For small angles (in radians), $\sin\theta \approx \theta$. This is the **isochronous approximation** discovered by Galileo.

$$\frac{d^2\theta}{dt^2} = -\frac{g}{L}\theta$$

**Step 5 — Identify as the Standard SHM Equation:**
The standard SHM differential equation is:

$$\frac{d^2\theta}{dt^2} + \omega^2\theta = 0$$

Comparing, we obtain:

$$\omega^2 = \frac{g}{L}$$

**Step 6 — Solve for Angular Frequency:**

$$\omega = \sqrt{\frac{g}{L}} \quad \text{(rad/s)}$$

**Step 7 — Convert to Linear Frequency using $\omega = 2\pi f$:**

$$f = \frac{\omega}{2\pi} = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{g}{L}}$$

**Step 8 — Time Period:**

$$T = \frac{1}{f} = 2\pi\sqrt{\frac{L}{g}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The frequency of a simple pendulum depends **only on g and L**, not on the mass $m$ or the amplitude (for small angles). This is why pendulum clocks are remarkably stable.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Frequency of a Spring-Mass System

**Setup:** A mass $m$ attached to a spring of stiffness $k$ on a frictionless surface, displaced by $x$ from equilibrium.

**Step 1 — Hooke's Law (Restoring Force):**

$$F = -kx$$

The negative sign indicates the force always pulls the mass back toward the equilibrium position.

**Step 2 — Apply Newton's Second Law:**

$$m\frac{d^2x}{dt^2} = -kx$$

**Step 3 — Rearrange to Standard SHM Form:**

$$\frac{d^2x}{dt^2} + \frac{k}{m}x = 0$$

**Step 4 — Identify the SHM Equation:**
Comparing with $\dfrac{d^2x}{dt^2} + \omega^2 x = 0$:

$$\omega^2 = \frac{k}{m}$$

**Step 5 — Solve for Angular and Linear Frequency:**

$$\omega = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}} \quad \text{(rad/s)}$$

$$f = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{k}{m}} \quad \text{(Hz)}$$

$$T = 2\pi\sqrt{\frac{m}{k}} \quad \text{(s)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stiffness vs. Mass:** Increasing stiffness $k$ **raises** the frequency; increasing mass $m$ **lowers** the frequency. This is the engineering principle behind **tuned mass dampers** in skyscrapers.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Frequency of a Torsional Pendulum

**Setup:** A rigid disk with mass moment of inertia $I$ about the central axis, suspended by a wire of torsional stiffness $c$ (units: N·m/rad), twisted by angle $\theta$.

**Step 1 — Torsional Restoring Torque:**

$$\tau = -c\theta$$

**Step 2 — Newton's Second Law for Rotation:**

$$I\frac{d^2\theta}{dt^2} = -c\theta$$

**Step 3 — Rearrange to SHM Form:**

$$\frac{d^2\theta}{dt^2} + \frac{c}{I}\theta = 0$$

**Step 4 — Identify Frequency:**

$$\omega = \sqrt{\frac{c}{I}} \quad \text{(rad/s)}$$

$$f = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{c}{I}} \quad \text{(Hz)}$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Frequency of a Compound Pendulum

**Setup:** A rigid body of mass $m$, pivoted at point $O$, with center of gravity $G$ at distance $L$ from the pivot, and mass moment of inertia $I_O$ about the pivot.

**Step 1 — Restoring Torque (Small Angle):**
Weight $mg$ acts at G. The moment arm about O is $L\sin\theta \approx L\theta$.

$$\tau = -mgL\theta$$

**Step 2 — Newton's Second Law for Rotation:**

$$I_O \frac{d^2\theta}{dt^2} = -mgL\theta$$

**Step 3 — Rearrange to SHM Form:**

$$\frac{d^2\theta}{dt^2} + \frac{mgL}{I_O}\theta = 0$$

**Step 4 — Identify Frequency:**

$$\omega = \sqrt{\frac{mgL}{I_O}} \quad \text{(rad/s)}$$

$$f = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{mgL}{I_O}} \quad \text{(Hz)}$$

**Step 5 — Equivalent Length of Simple Pendulum:**
Setting the simple pendulum and compound pendulum frequencies equal yields the **equivalent length**:

$$L_{eq} = \frac{I_O}{mL}$$

This is the length of a simple pendulum that has the same period as the compound pendulum. The point at this distance from the pivot is called the **center of oscillation**.

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Pattern)

**Problem:** A mass of 2 kg attached to a spring of stiffness 200 N/m is displaced 50 mm from equilibrium and released. Find (a) the natural frequency, (b) the angular frequency, and (c) the maximum velocity.

**Given:** $m = 2$ kg, $k = 200$ N/m

**Part (a) — Natural Frequency:**

$$f = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{k}{m}} = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{200}{2}} = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{100} = \frac{10}{2\pi} = 1.5915 \text{ Hz}$$

**Part (b) — Angular Frequency:**

$$\omega = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}} = \sqrt{100} = 10 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Part (c) — Maximum Velocity:**
For SHM, $v_{max} = \omega \cdot A$, where amplitude $A = 50$ mm $= 0.05$ m.

$$v_{max} = 10 \times 0.05 = 0.5 \text{ m/s}$$
