# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define frequency and angular frequency. Establish the relationship between them.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Frequency (f)** is defined as the number of complete cycles or oscillations of a periodic motion per unit time. Its SI unit is **Hertz (Hz)**.

> **Angular frequency (ω)** is defined as the rate of change of angular displacement in a periodic (rotational/oscillatory) motion per unit time. Its SI unit is **radians per second (rad/s)**.

> **Relationship:** Since one complete cycle corresponds to an angular change of $2\pi$ radians, the relationship is:

$$\omega = 2\pi f = \frac{2\pi}{T}$$

> Numerically, if $f = 50$ Hz, then $\omega = 2\pi \times 50 = 314.16$ rad/s.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition of f, 1 for definition of ω, 1 for relationship)*

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**A simple pendulum has a length of 1 m. Find its frequency of oscillation on Earth.**

**Model Answer:**

> Given: $L = 1$ m, $g = 9.81$ m/s².

> Using the simple pendulum frequency formula:

$$f = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{g}{L}} = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{9.81}{1}}$$

$$f = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{9.81} = \frac{3.132}{6.283} = 0.4985 \text{ Hz}$$

> **Frequency ≈ 0.5 Hz** (i.e., approximately 1 cycle every 2 seconds).

*(3 marks: 1 for formula, 1 for substitution, 1 for final answer with unit)*

---

## 5.2 Part B: Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Alert:**
> - Students often **forget units** in the final answer (always write Hz or rad/s).
> - Many write $g = 10$ m/s² without specifying — for a 14-mark problem, use $g = 9.81$ m/s² unless the problem states otherwise.
> - The **most common error** is mixing up linear and angular frequency. KTU examiners specifically look for the correct formula based on what the question asks.
> - Always show the **SHM differential equation** step — it fetches 2–3 easy marks even if the rest is incomplete.

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Derive the expression for the frequency of oscillation of a simple pendulum undergoing small angular oscillations. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A spring-mass system has a mass of 5 kg and a spring stiffness of 1250 N/m. If the mass is displaced 80 mm from equilibrium and released, determine:
- (i) the natural frequency,
- (ii) the angular frequency,
- (iii) the maximum velocity,
- (iv) the maximum acceleration of the mass. **(7 marks)**

---

### **SOLUTION TO QUESTION A:**

#### Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Setup:** Consider a bob of mass $m$ attached to an inextensible string of length $L$, pivoted at point O. When displaced by a small angle $\theta$ from the vertical, the restoring force is the tangential component of weight.

**Step 2 — Force Equation:**
Tangential restoring force:
$$F_t = -mg\sin\theta$$

**Step 3 — Newton's Second Law (Tangential):** Tangential acceleration is $L\dfrac{d^2\theta}{dt^2}$:
$$mL\frac{d^2\theta}{dt^2} = -mg\sin\theta$$

**Step 4 — Simplify:**
$$\frac{d^2\theta}{dt^2} = -\frac{g}{L}\sin\theta$$

**Step 5 — Small-Angle Approximation:** For small $\theta$ (in radians), $\sin\theta \approx \theta$:
$$\frac{d^2\theta}{dt^2} + \frac{g}{L}\theta = 0$$

**Step 6 — Identify SHM:** Comparing with $\dfrac{d^2\theta}{dt^2} + \omega^2\theta = 0$:
$$\omega^2 = \frac{g}{L} \implies \omega = \sqrt{\frac{g}{L}} \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 7 — Linear Frequency:**
$$f = \frac{\omega}{2\pi} = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{g}{L}} \text{ Hz}$$

**Step 8 — Period:**
$$T = 2\pi\sqrt{\frac{L}{g}} \text{ seconds}$$

*(Mark split: 2 for SHM equation derivation, 2 for solving, 2 for f and T expressions, 1 for conclusion)*

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 marks)

**Given:** $m = 5$ kg, $k = 1250$ N/m, $A = 80$ mm $= 0.08$ m

**(i) Natural Frequency (2 marks):**
$$f = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{k}{m}} = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{1250}{5}} = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{250} = \frac{15.811}{6.283} = 2.516 \text{ Hz}$$

**(ii) Angular Frequency (1 mark):**
$$\omega = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}} = \sqrt{250} = 15.811 \text{ rad/s}$$

**(iii) Maximum Velocity (2 marks):**
For SHM, $v_{max} = \omega \cdot A$:
$$v_{max} = 15.811 \times 0.08 = 1.265 \text{ m/s}$$

**(iv) Maximum Acceleration (2 marks):**
For SHM, $a_{max} = \omega^2 \cdot A$:
$$a_{max} = 250 \times 0.08 = 20 \text{ m/s}^2$$

**Final Answers:**
- $f = 2.516$ Hz
- $\omega = 15.811$ rad/s
- $v_{max} = 1.265$ m/s
- $a_{max} = 20$ m/s²

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Derive the expression for the frequency of oscillation of a compound pendulum. Define the term "center of oscillation." **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A compound pendulum consists of a uniform rod of length 1.2 m and mass 3 kg, pivoted at one end. Determine:
- (i) the mass moment of inertia about the pivot,
- (ii) the distance of the center of gravity from the pivot,
- (iii) the natural frequency of oscillation. **(7 marks)**

---

### **SOLUTION TO QUESTION B:**

#### Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Setup:** A rigid body of mass $m$ pivoted at point O, with center of gravity G at perpendicular distance $L$ from O. Mass moment of inertia about O is $I_O$.

**Step 2 — Restoring Torque:** When displaced by a small angle $\theta$, the weight $mg$ creates a restoring moment:
$$\tau = -mgL\sin\theta$$

**Step 3 — Small-Angle Approximation:** $\sin\theta \approx \theta$:
$$\tau = -mgL\theta$$

**Step 4 — Newton's Second Law (Rotation):**
$$I_O\frac{d^2\theta}{dt^2} = -mgL\theta$$

**Step 5 — SHM Form:**
$$\frac{d^2\theta}{dt^2} + \frac{mgL}{I_O}\theta = 0$$

**Step 6 — Identify Frequency:**
$$\omega = \sqrt{\frac{mgL}{I_O}} \text{ rad/s}, \quad f = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{mgL}{I_O}} \text{ Hz}$$

**Step 7 — Center of Oscillation Definition (2 marks):**
The **center of oscillation** is a point on the extended pendulum at a distance $L_{eq} = \dfrac{I_O}{mL}$ from the pivot, such that a simple pendulum of length $L_{eq}$ would have the same period. The center of suspension and center of oscillation are interchangeable (reversibility property).

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 marks)

**Given:** Uniform rod, $L_{rod} = 1.2$ m, $m = 3$ kg, pivoted at one end.

**(i) Mass Moment of Inertia about Pivot End (3 marks):**
For a uniform rod pivoted at one end, the standard formula is:
$$I_O = \frac{mL^2}{3} = \frac{3 \times (1.2)^2}{3} = \frac{3 \times 1.44}{3} = 1.44 \text{ kg·m}^2$$

**(ii) Distance of CG from Pivot (1 mark):**
For a uniform rod, the CG lies at the geometric center:
$$L = \frac{L_{rod}}{2} = \frac{1.2}{2} = 0.6 \text{ m}$$

**(iii) Natural Frequency (3 marks):**
$$f = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{mgL}{I_O}} = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{3 \times 9.81 \times 0.6}{1.44}}$$

$$f = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{17.658}{1.44}} = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{12.263} = \frac{3.502}{6.283} = 0.5575 \text{ Hz}$$

**Final Answers:**
- $I_O = 1.44$ kg·m²
- $L = 0.6$ m
- $f = 0.5575$ Hz (≈ 0.558 Hz)

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Frequency (KTU Module 4):**

- ✅ **Definition (must-memorize):** $f = \dfrac{1}{T}$, units = **Hz**; $\omega = 2\pi f$, units = **rad/s**
- ✅ **Always write the SHM differential equation** $\dfrac{d^2x}{dt^2} + \omega^2 x = 0$ in derivations — it is worth **2–3 marks by itself**.
- ✅ **Simple Pendulum:** $f = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{g}{L}}$ — independent of mass and amplitude (small angle).
- ✅ **Spring-Mass:** $f = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m}}$ — increase $k$ → higher $f$; increase $m$ → lower $f$.
- ✅ **Torsional Pendulum:** $f = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{c}{I}}$ — $c$ in N·m/rad, $I$ in kg·m².
- ✅ **Compound Pendulum:** $f = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{mgL}{I_O}}$ — remember the equivalent length $L_{eq} = I_O / mL$.
- ✅ **Use $g = 9.81$ m/s²** unless the problem explicitly says otherwise.
- ✅ **Conversion to watch:** RPM to Hz: $f = N/60$; Hz to rad/s: multiply by $2\pi$.
- ✅ **Resonance:** occurs when driving frequency $\omega = $ natural frequency $\omega_n$ — leads to dangerous amplitude growth.
- ✅ **Always quote the unit** (Hz or rad/s) — losing the unit costs **½ mark** in valuation.
- ✅ **SHM velocity max** = $\omega A$; **SHM acceleration max** = $\omega^2 A$ — these are very common follow-up numerical sub-parts.
- ✅ **Conceptual key:** Natural frequency is a *property of the system*, not the motion.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

## 1. Core Technical Definition and Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Single Degree of Freedom (SDOF)** system in engineering mechanics is a dynamic system whose instantaneous configuration can be completely specified by **one independent generalized coordinate**. In physical terms, only a single scalar displacement variable — either linear or angular — is needed to describe the position of every moving mass in the system at any instant of time.

The classical representative of an SDOF system is the idealized **spring-mass-damper assembly**, whose motion is governed by the second-order linear ordinary differential equation:

$$m\ddot{x}(t) + c\dot{x}(t) + kx(t) = F(t)$$

Symbol legend:

- **m** — equivalent mass of the system (**kg**)
- **c** — equivalent viscous damping coefficient (**N·s/m**)
- **k** — equivalent stiffness of the restoring element (**N/m**)
- **x(t)** — instantaneous displacement measured from the static equilibrium position (**m**)
- **ẋ(t)** — velocity (**m/s**)
- **ẍ(t)** — acceleration (**m/s²**)
- **F(t)** — externally applied excitation force (**N**)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Within GBEST213 Module 4, the SDOF treatment is restricted to simple undamped and lightly-damped systems. Students are expected to derive the equation of motion, compute the natural frequency and time period, and evaluate the time-domain response under standard initial conditions.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy and Physical Intuition

Imagine a small block of mass **m** tied to a perfectly elastic spring resting on a frictionless horizontal table. You pull the block slightly to the right and release it. The spring immediately drags it back. As the block crosses the original position, the spring exerts no force, but the block carries kinetic energy, which carries it to the left, compressing the spring. The compressed spring then pushes the block back to the right, and the cycle repeats forever in the absence of friction.

This is the **canonical SDOF oscillator**. The system "remembers" only one fact: *how far is it from the equilibrium position right now?* That single scalar is enough to predict the entire future motion — hence the term "single degree of freedom."

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-world SDOF examples in engineering practice:**
> - A vehicle body bouncing on its suspension after striking a road bump.
> - A building floor vibrating due to a rotating motor mounted on it.
> - The diaphragm of a loudspeaker driven by the voice coil.
> - A small weight hanging from a slender vertical coil spring (vertical SDOF).
> - A torsional pendulum: a disc attached to a thin shaft twisting about its axis.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Physical Constants

| Parameter | Symbol | SI Unit | Typical Engineering Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| Equivalent mass | m | **kg** | 10⁻³ to 10⁶ |
| Equivalent stiffness | k | **N/m** | 10 to 10⁷ |
| Damping coefficient | c | **N·s/m** | 0 to 10⁵ |
| Natural angular frequency | ωₙ | **rad/s** | 0.1 to 10⁴ |
| Natural cyclic frequency | fₙ | **Hz** | 0.02 to 10³ |
| Time period | T | **s** | 10⁻³ to 60 |
| Gravitational acceleration | g | **m/s²** | **9.81** (standard) |

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis and KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of SDOF Vibration Problems

The dynamic response of an SDOF system depends on two distinct aspects of the loading history.

**A. By the presence of an external time-varying force:**

1. **Free vibration** — no external force after the initial disturbance; the system oscillates using its own stored strain and kinetic energy.
   - *Undamped free vibration* — motion continues indefinitely at constant amplitude.
   - *Damped free vibration* — amplitude decays exponentially with time.

2. **Forced vibration** — a time-varying external force $F(t)$ continuously supplies energy to the system.
   - *Steady-state response* — persists as long as the force is applied.
   - *Transient response* — present only briefly after the force is applied or removed.

**B. By the nature of the restoring mechanism:**

1. **Translational SDOF** — displacement is linear (mass on a linear spring).
2. **Torsional SDOF** — displacement is angular (rigid disc on an elastic shaft).
3. **Pendulum-type SDOF** — small-angle linearisation makes the geometric system linear.

### 2.2 Underlying Assumptions of the Linear SDOF Model

For the standard second-order model to apply, KTU examiners expect the following idealisations to be stated explicitly:

- The spring is **massless, linear, and obeys Hooke's law** ($F_s = kx$).
- The damping force is **viscous**, i.e. linearly proportional to velocity ($F_d = c\dot{x}$).
- Motion is confined to **small displacements** so that geometric non-linearities can be ignored.
- The mass is treated as a **rigid body** so that all parts move together with a single coordinate.
- The system is **time-invariant** — m, c, and k do not change with time.
- The support is **rigid** and does not itself move.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Tip:** Always list these assumptions explicitly in a boxed preamble at the start of your KTU answer. A 1-mark "design credit" is routinely awarded for this practice.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet and Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Equation of motion (undamped) | $m\ddot{x} + kx = 0$ | N (force balance) | Free vibration, no damping |
| 2 | Natural angular frequency | $\omega_n = \sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m}}$ | rad/s | All free-vibration problems |
| 3 | Natural cyclic frequency | $f_n = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m}}$ | Hz | Time-domain response |
| 4 | Time period | $T = 2\pi\sqrt{\dfrac{m}{k}}$ | s | Periodic time of oscillation |
| 5 | Stiffness from frequency | $k = m\omega_n^2 = 4\pi^2 m f_n^2$ | N/m | Reverse-type problem |
| 6 | Mass from frequency | $m = \dfrac{k}{\omega_n^2}$ | kg | Reverse-type problem |
| 7 | Static deflection | $\delta_{st} = \dfrac{mg}{k}$ | m | Spring supporting weight |
| 8 | Natural frequency from $\delta_{st}$ | $\omega_n = \sqrt{\dfrac{g}{\delta_{st}}}$ | rad/s | Vertical hanging system |
| 9 | Critical damping | $c_c = 2m\omega_n = 2\sqrt{mk}$ | N·s/m | Damping benchmark |
| 10 | Damping ratio | $\zeta = \dfrac{c}{c_c}$ | dimensionless | Classifies damping regime |
| 11 | Damped natural frequency | $\omega_d = \omega_n\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}$ | rad/s | Underdamped response |
| 12 | Logarithmic decrement | $\delta_d = \dfrac{2\pi\zeta}{\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}}$ | dimensionless | Experimental damping measurement |
| 13 | Steady-state amplitude (harmonic) | $X = \dfrac{F_0}{\sqrt{(k-m\omega^2)^2 + (c\omega)^2}}$ | m | Forced vibration |
| 14 | Magnification factor | $M = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{(1-r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}$ | dimensionless | Resonance design |
| 15 | Phase lag | $\psi = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{c\omega}{k-m\omega^2}\right)$ | rad | Forced response timing |
| 16 | Springs in series | $\dfrac{1}{k_{eq}} = \sum \dfrac{1}{k_i}$ | N/m | Composite spring system |
| 17 | Springs in parallel | $k_{eq} = \sum k_i$ | N/m | Composite spring system |
| 18 | Torsional SDOF | $\omega_n = \sqrt{\dfrac{K_t}{I}}$ | rad/s | Rotational systems |
| 19 | Simple pendulum (small angle) | $\omega_n = \sqrt{\dfrac{g}{L}}$ | rad/s | Pendulum SDOF |
| 20 | Resonance frequency | $\omega_r = \omega_n\sqrt{1 - 2\zeta^2}$ | rad/s | Damped resonance peak |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on Symbols:** Throughout this note, any vertical bar denoting magnitude is rendered as $\vert \cdot \vert$ to preserve table integrity.

### 2.4 Engineering Utility of SDOF Analysis

SDOF modelling forms the analytical backbone of:

- **Structural earthquake engineering** — buildings idealised as SDOF to estimate seismic demand.
- **Machine design** — sizing shafts, springs, and isolators to avoid resonance.
- **Vehicle suspension** — ride-comfort and road-holding optimisation.
- **Instrumentation** — seismographs, accelerometers, and force-balance transducers.
- **Rotating machinery** — balancing of rotors and torsional critical-speed prediction.
- **Aerospace** — fuel slosh in tanks and panel flutter analysis.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations and Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Equation of Motion (Undamped SDOF)

**System considered:** A rigid block of mass **m** resting on a smooth horizontal surface, connected to a fixed wall by a linear spring of stiffness **k**.

**Step 1 — Choose the coordinate.**
Let **x** denote the displacement of the mass measured from the **static equilibrium position**. Equilibrium is the configuration where the spring force balances any constant gravitational or pre-load force.

**Step 2 — Identify the forces in the x-direction.**
The free-body diagram shows:
- Spring restoring force: $-kx$ (opposing displacement).
- Applied external force: $F(t)$.
- Weight and normal reaction cancel vertically.

**Step 3 — Apply Newton's second law of motion.**
The net force in the x-direction equals mass times acceleration:

$$\sum F_x = m \ddot{x}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the force terms.**

$$-kx + F(t) = m\ddot{x}$$

**Step 5 — Rearrange into the canonical second-order form.**

$$m\ddot{x} + kx = F(t)$$

For the **free-vibration** case, $F(t) = 0$:

$$\boxed{m\ddot{x} + kx = 0}$$

### 3.2 Solution of the Undamped Free Vibration Equation

We seek a solution of the form $x(t) = A e^{st}$, where A and s are constants to be determined.

**Step 1 — Substitute the trial solution into the governing ODE.**

$$m(A s^2 e^{st}) + k(A e^{st}) = 0$$

**Step 2 — Factor out the common non-zero term $A e^{st}$.**

$$A e^{st}\left(ms^2 + k\right) = 0$$

**Step 3 — Identify the characteristic equation.**
Since $A e^{st} \neq 0$ for a non-trivial solution, the term in parentheses must vanish:

$$ms^2 + k = 0$$

**Step 4 — Solve for s.**

$$s^2 = -\frac{k}{m}$$

$$s = \pm j\sqrt{\frac{k}{m}} = \pm j\omega_n$$

**Step 5 — Define the natural angular frequency.**

$$\boxed{\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}} \quad \text{rad/s}}$$

**Step 6 — Write the general solution using Euler's identity.**
The two roots are $s_1 = +j\omega_n$ and $s_2 = -j\omega_n$. The general solution is a linear combination of the two exponential modes:

$$x(t) = C_1 e^{j\omega_n t} + C_2 e^{-j\omega_n t}$$

Applying Euler's formula $e^{\pm j\theta} = \cos\theta \pm j\sin\theta$:

$$x(t) = (C_1 + C_2)\cos(\omega_n t) + j(C_1 - C_2)\sin(\omega_n t)$$

Renaming $A = C_1 + C_2$ and $B = j(C_1 - C_2)$, both real when the initial conditions are real:

$$\boxed{x(t) = A\cos(\omega_n t) + B\sin(\omega_n t)}$$

**Step 7 — Apply the initial conditions.**
Let $x(0) = x_0$ and $\dot{x}(0) = v_0$. At $t = 0$:

$$x(0) = A\cos(0) + B\sin(0) = A = x_0$$

Differentiating $x(t)$ gives $\dot{x}(t) = -A\omega_n\sin(\omega_n t) + B\omega_n\cos(\omega_n t)$. At $t = 0$:

$$\dot{x}(0) = B\omega_n = v_0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad B = \frac{v_0}{\omega_n}$$

**Step 8 — Final time-domain form.**

$$\boxed{x(t) = x_0\cos(\omega_n t) + \frac{v_0}{\omega_n}\sin(\omega_n t)}$$

**Step 9 — Equivalent amplitude–phase form.**
Define $X = \sqrt{x_0^2 + (v_0/\omega_n)^2}$ and $\phi = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{v_0}{x_0\omega_n}\right)$:

$$x(t) = X\cos(\omega_n t - \phi)$$

This is the canonical **simple harmonic motion** representation. The amplitude X is constant in time for the undamped case.

### 3.3 Derivation of Natural Frequency from Static Deflection

For a body hanging vertically from a coil spring under gravity, the static equilibrium condition gives:

$$k \delta_{st} = mg \quad \Rightarrow \quad \delta_{st} = \frac{mg}{k}$$

Rearranging for k:

$$k = \frac{mg}{\delta_{st}}$$

Substituting into the natural-frequency formula:

$$\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}} = \sqrt{\frac{mg/\delta_{st}}{m}} = \sqrt{\frac{g}{\delta_{st}}}$$

$$\boxed{\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{g}{\delta_{st}}}}$$

This compact shortcut is extremely valuable in KTU problems where the spring stiffness is not given directly but the static stretch is.

### 3.4 Derivation of Critical Damping Coefficient

The damped equation of motion is:

$$m\ddot{x} + c\dot{x} + kx = 0$$

Assuming an exponential trial solution $x(t) = A e^{st}$ leads to the characteristic equation:

$$ms^2 + cs + k = 0$$

The discriminant of this quadratic in s is:

$$\Delta = c^2 - 4mk$$

For the system to be **critically damped**, the response must be non-oscillatory with the fastest possible decay, which requires a repeated root, i.e. $\Delta = 0$:

$$c_c^2 - 4mk = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad c_c = \sqrt{4mk} = 2\sqrt{mk}$$

Since $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}$, we have $mk = m^2 \omega_n^2$, hence:

$$\boxed{c_c = 2m\omega_n = 2\sqrt{mk}}$$

### 3.5 Derivation of Magnification Factor (Forced Vibration)

For an SDOF system under harmonic excitation $F(t) = F_0 \sin(\omega t)$:

$$m\ddot{x} + c\dot{x} + kx = F_0\sin(\omega t)$$

**Step 1 — Assume a steady-state particular solution** of the form $x_p(t) = X\sin(\omega t - \psi)$.

**Step 2 — Substitute and differentiate.**

$$\dot{x}_p = X\omega\cos(\omega t - \psi)$$

$$\ddot{x}_p = -X\omega^2\sin(\omega t - \psi)$$

**Step 3 — Insert into the governing ODE.**

$$-mX\omega^2\sin(\omega t - \psi) + cX\omega\cos(\omega t - \psi) + kX\sin(\omega t - \psi) = F_0\sin(\omega t)$$

**Step 4 — Group the sine and cosine terms.**

$$X(k - m\omega^2)\sin(\omega t - \psi) + c\omega X\cos(\omega t - \psi) = F_0\sin(\omega t)$$

**Step 5 — Expand using the identity** $\sin(A - B) = \sin A \cos B - \cos A \sin B$:

$$X(k - m\omega^2)\big[\sin(\omega t)\cos\psi - \cos(\omega t)\sin\psi\big] + c\omega X\cos(\omega t - \psi) = F_0\sin(\omega t)$$

Matching the coefficients of $\sin(\omega t)$ and $\cos(\omega t)$ on both sides yields two scalar equations:

$$X(k - m\omega^2)\cos\psi = F_0$$

$$X(k - m\omega^2)\sin\psi + c\omega X\cos\psi = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \tan\psi = \frac{c\omega}{k - m\omega^2}$$

**Step 6 — Square and add the two magnitude equations.**

$$X^2\left[(k - m\omega^2)^2\cos^2\psi + (k - m\omega^2)^2\sin^2\psi\right] = F_0^2$$

Using $\cos^2\psi + \sin^2\psi = 1$:

$$X^2(k - m\omega^2)^2 = F_0^2\cos^2\psi$$

Combining with the second equation similarly gives:

$$X^2\left[(k - m\omega^2)^2 + (c\omega)^2\right] = F_0^2$$

$$\boxed{X = \frac{F_0}{\sqrt{(k - m\omega^2)^2 + (c\omega)^2}}}$$

**Step 7 — Define the static deflection** $X_{static} = F_0/k$ and the **frequency ratio** $r = \omega/\omega_n$. Substituting $k = m\omega_n^2$:

$$M = \frac{X}{X_{static}} = \frac{k}{\sqrt{(k - m\omega^2)^2 + (c\omega)^2}} = \frac{\omega_n^2}{\sqrt{(\omega_n^2 - \omega^2)^2 + (c\omega/m)^2}}$$

Using $c/c_c = \zeta$ and $c_c = 2m\omega_n$:

$$\boxed{M = \frac{1}{\sqrt{(1 - r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}}$$

The magnification factor diverges at resonance when $\zeta \to 0$ and $r \to 1$, confirming the unbounded amplitude growth in the undamped case.

### 3.6 Worked Numerical Patterns (Analytical Matrix)

| Pattern | Given Quantities | To Find | Governing Equation | Final Computation |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| A | m, k | $\omega_n$, $T$, $f_n$ | $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}$ | $T = 2\pi/\omega_n$, $f_n = 1/T$ |
| B | m, $\delta_{st}$ (vertical) | $\omega_n$, $T$ | $\omega_n = \sqrt{g/\delta_{st}}$ | $T = 2\pi\sqrt{\delta_{st}/g}$ |
| C | $f_n$, m | k | $k = 4\pi^2 m f_n^2$ | numeric evaluation |
| D | Two springs in series/parallel | $k_{eq}$, $T$ | $1/k_{eq} = \sum 1/k_i$ or $k_{eq} = \sum k_i$ | numeric evaluation |
| E | $\zeta$, $x_1$, $x_{n+1}$ | $\delta_d$ | $\delta_d = \ln(x_1/x_{n+1})/n$ | experimental damping |
| F | Torsional SDOF ($K_t$, $I$) | $\omega_n$ | $\omega_n = \sqrt{K_t/I}$ | numeric evaluation |
| G | Forced vibration ($F_0$, $\omega$, $c$, $k$, $m$) | $X$, $\psi$ | $X = F_0/\sqrt{(k-m\omega^2)^2+(c\omega)^2}$ | numeric evaluation |

## 4. Structural Diagrams and Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an SDOF Vibration System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Excitation Source F of t] --> B[Spring Element Stiffness k]
    A --> C[Damping Element Coefficient c]
    A --> D[Mass Element m]
    B --> E[Restoring Force Fk equal minus k times x]
    C --> F[Dissipative Force Fc equal minus c times x dot]
    D --> G[Inertial Force m times x double dot]
    E --> H[Equation of Motion Force Balance]
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H --> I[Displacement Response x of t]
    H --> J[Natural Frequency omega n]
    H --> K[Time Period T]
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank and Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** *[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Module 4 Pattern]*

Define a Single Degree of Freedom (SDOF) system. Give **two** engineering examples.

**Model Answer:**

A Single Degree of Freedom (SDOF) system is a dynamic mechanical system whose instantaneous configuration can be completely specified by a **single** independent generalised coordinate. Only one scalar displacement variable (linear or angular) is sufficient to describe the position of every moving mass in the system at any instant of time.

**Two engineering examples:**

1. A vehicle body bouncing vertically on its suspension after striking a road bump, idealised as a mass on a coil spring.
2. A tall building modelled as a vertical cantilever beam with its mass lumped at the top, undergoing lateral sway during an earthquake.

---

**Q2.** *[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Module 4 Pattern]*

State the significance of the natural frequency in the design of mechanical systems.

**Model Answer:**

The natural angular frequency $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}$ is the frequency at which a system oscillates freely when disturbed from equilibrium and released. Its significance in design is threefold:

1. **Resonance avoidance.** If the frequency of an external excitation coincides with $\omega_n$, the response amplitude grows without bound in an undamped system, leading to catastrophic failure. Designers therefore ensure operating frequencies stay well clear of $\omega_n$.
2. **Vibration isolation.** Equipment mounts use soft springs so that their $\omega_n$ is much lower than the disturbing frequency, thereby attenuating the transmitted force.
3. **Performance tuning.** Sensors, filters, and structural elements are deliberately tuned to a target $\omega_n$ for correct functional behaviour (e.g. accelerometers tuned to be well below $\omega_n$ for faithful measurement).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

**Question A** *[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Module 4]*

**(a)** A mass of **5 kg** is attached to a spring of stiffness **2000 N/m**. The mass is displaced **0.02 m** from equilibrium and released from rest. Determine:
   (i) the natural angular frequency, natural cyclic frequency, and time period,
   (ii) the maximum velocity and maximum acceleration of the mass,
   (iii) the displacement, velocity, and acceleration at $t = 0.1$ s. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Two springs of stiffness **3 kN/m** and **6 kN/m** are connected (i) in **series** and (ii) in **parallel**. A **4 kg** mass is attached to the combination. Determine the time period in each case. **(7 marks)**

---

**Solution to Question A(a):**

**Given:** $m = 5$ kg, $k = 2000$ N/m, $x_0 = 0.02$ m, $v_0 = 0$.

**Step 1 — Natural angular frequency.**

$$\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}} = \sqrt{\frac{2000}{5}} = \sqrt{400} = 20 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 2 — Natural cyclic frequency.**

$$f_n = \frac{\omega_n}{2\pi} = \frac{20}{2\pi} = 3.183 \text{ Hz}$$

**Step 3 — Time period.**

$$T = \frac{2\pi}{\omega_n} = \frac{2\pi}{20} = 0.3142 \text{ s}$$

**Step 4 — Maximum velocity (SHM property $v_{max} = x_0 \omega_n$).**

$$v_{max} = 0.02 \times 20 = 0.40 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 5 — Maximum acceleration (SHM property $a_{max} = x_0 \omega_n^2$).**

$$a_{max} = 0.02 \times 400 = 8.0 \text{ m/s}^2$$

**Step 6 — Response equations.**
With $x_0 = 0.02$ m and $v_0 = 0$:

$$x(t) = x_0 \cos(\omega_n t) = 0.02 \cos(20t) \text{ m}$$

$$\dot{x}(t) = -x_0 \omega_n \sin(\omega_n t) = -0.40 \sin(20t) \text{ m/s}$$

$$\ddot{x}(t) = -x_0 \omega_n^2 \cos(\omega_n t) = -8.0 \cos(20t) \text{ m/s}^2$$

**Step 7 — Evaluate at $t = 0.1$ s.**
The argument is $\omega_n t = 20 \times 0.1 = 2.0$ rad.
Using $\cos 2 = -0.4161$ and $\sin 2 = 0.9093$:

$$x(0.1) = 0.02 \times (-0.4161) = -8.32 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m} = -8.32 \text{ mm}$$

$$\dot{x}(0.1) = -0.40 \times 0.9093 = -0.3637 \text{ m/s}$$

$$\ddot{x}(0.1) = -8.0 \times (-0.4161) = 3.329 \text{ m/s}^2$$

---

**Solution to Question A(b):**

**Case (i) — Series combination.**
Reciprocals add:

$$\frac{1}{k_{eq,s}} = \frac{1}{k_1} + \frac{1}{k_2} = \frac{1}{3000} + \frac{1}{6000} = \frac{2 + 1}{6000} = \frac{3}{6000} = \frac{1}{2000}$$

$$k_{eq,s} = 2000 \text{ N/m}$$

Time period:

$$T_s = 2\pi\sqrt{\frac{m}{k_{eq,s}}} = 2\pi\sqrt{\frac{4}{2000}} = 2\pi \times 0.04472 = 0.2810 \text{ s}$$

**Case (ii) — Parallel combination.**
Stiffnesses add:

$$k_{eq,p} = k_1 + k_2 = 3000 + 6000 = 9000 \text{ N/m}$$

Time period:

$$T_p = 2\pi\sqrt{\frac{m}{k_{eq,p}}} = 2\pi\sqrt{\frac{4}{9000}} = 2\pi \times 0.02108 = 0.1325 \text{ s}$$

**Observation:** Series combination yields a longer period (softer system), parallel combination yields a shorter period (stiffer system), consistent with physical intuition.

---

**Question B** *[KTU University Exam — Alternative Choice, Module 4]*

**(a)** A machine of mass **50 kg** is mounted on **four identical springs in parallel**. The machine vibrates at a natural frequency of **4 Hz**. Determine:
   (i) the stiffness of each spring,
   (ii) the static deflection of the system under its own weight,
   (iii) if a damping coefficient of **200 N·s/m** is now introduced, compute the damping ratio and comment on the type of damping. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A uniform rigid bar of mass **6 kg** and length **1.2 m** is pinned at one end and a spring of stiffness **800 N/m** is attached at the other end in the horizontal plane. Find the natural frequency of small angular oscillations about the pin. **(7 marks)**

---

**Solution to Question B(a):**

**Given:** $m = 50$ kg, 4 springs in parallel, $f_n = 4$ Hz, $c = 200$ N·s/m (post-introduction).

**Step 1 — Compute the angular natural frequency.**

$$\omega_n = 2\pi f_n = 2\pi \times 4 = 8\pi = 25.133 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the total equivalent stiffness.**

$$k_{eq} = m \omega_n^2 = 50 \times (8\pi)^2 = 50 \times 64\pi^2 = 3200\pi^2$$

$$k_{eq} = 3200 \times 9.8696 = 31582.7 \text{ N/m}$$

**Step 3 — Stiffness of each spring.**
Four springs in parallel: $k_{eq} = 4 k_s$, hence:

$$k_s = \frac{k_{eq}}{4} = \frac{31582.7}{4} = 7895.7 \text{ N/m} \approx 7.90 \text{ kN/m}$$

**Step 4 — Static deflection under self-weight.**

$$\delta_{st} = \frac{mg}{k_{eq}} = \frac{50 \times 9.81}{31582.7} = \frac{490.5}{31582.7} = 1.553 \times 10^{-2} \text{ m} = 15.53 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 5 — Critical damping coefficient.**

$$c_c = 2 m \omega_n = 2 \times 50 \times 25.133 = 2513.3 \text{ N·s/m}$$

**Step 6 — Damping ratio.**

$$\zeta = \frac{c}{c_c} = \frac{200}{2513.3} = 0.0796$$

**Step 7 — Comment.**
Since $0 < \zeta < 1$, the system is **underdamped**. It will oscillate with exponentially decaying amplitude at the damped natural frequency:

$$\omega_d = \omega_n\sqrt{1 - \zeta^2} = 25.133 \times \sqrt{1 - 0.00634} \approx 25.05 \text{ rad/s}$$

The amplitude decay is slow because $\zeta$ is small.

---

**Solution to Question B(b):**

**Given:** uniform rigid bar, $m = 6$ kg, $L = 1.2$ m, pinned at one end, spring $k = 800$ N/m at the other end (horizontal).

**Step 1 — Identify the coordinate.**
Let $\theta$ be the small angular displacement of the bar from the horizontal equilibrium position.

**Step 2 — Mass moment of inertia about the pin.**
For a uniform rod pivoted at one end:

$$I_O = \frac{mL^2}{3} = \frac{6 \times (1.2)^2}{3} = \frac{6 \times 1.44}{3} = 2.88 \text{ kg·m}^2$$

**Step 3 — Spring torque about the pin.**
The linear displacement at the spring location is $x = L\theta$. The spring force is $F_s = -kx = -kL\theta$. The torque about the pin is:

$$\tau = -F_s \cdot L = -kL^2 \theta$$

The equivalent **torsional stiffness** is therefore:

$$K_t = kL^2 = 800 \times (1.2)^2 = 800 \times 1.44 = 1152 \text{ N·m/rad}$$

**Step 4 — Angular equation of motion.**

$$I_O \ddot{\theta} + K_t \theta = 0$$

$$2.88 \ddot{\theta} + 1152 \theta = 0$$

**Step 5 — Natural angular frequency.**

$$\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{K_t}{I_O}} = \sqrt{\frac{1152}{2.88}} = \sqrt{400} = 20 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 6 — Natural cyclic frequency.**

$$f_n = \frac{\omega_n}{2\pi} = \frac{20}{2\pi} = 3.183 \text{ Hz}$$

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in SDOF Numerical Problems — Read Carefully:**
> - **Unit mismatches.** Converting mm to m *before* substituting into $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}$ is mandatory. A stiffness expressed in N/mm combined with mass in kg gives an $\omega_n$ that is **1000 times too large**. Examiners routinely deduct 2 marks here.
> - **Forgetting the parallel factor.** Four springs in parallel means $k_{eq} = 4 k$, **not** $k$. A single arithmetic slip here costs 2–3 marks.
> - **Misapplying the static-deflection shortcut.** Use $\omega_n = \sqrt{g/\delta_{st}}$ **only** when the spring supports the weight vertically. For horizontal springs on a frictionless table, always revert to $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}$.
> - **Omitting the phase lag in forced vibration.** Many students quote the magnitude $X$ and forget the phase angle $\psi = \tan^{-1}\!\big(c\omega/(k - m\omega^2)\big)$. Examiners expect both.
> - **Sign convention in SHM.** A negative displacement simply means the mass is on the opposite side of equilibrium. Write the sign explicitly.
> - **Truncating intermediate steps.** Always show the substitution of numerical values into the formula. Never jump from a symbolic expression to a final numeric answer — examiners award partial marks only for visible steps.
> - **Wrong moment of inertia in torsional SDOF.** For a uniform rod pivoted at one end, $I = mL^2/3$; pivoted at the centre, $I = mL^2/12$. Mixing these up is a guaranteed mark-loser.

### 5.4 Topic Recap and Important Things to Remember

- An **SDOF system** is fully described by a **single** generalised coordinate.
- The **undamped equation of motion** is $m\ddot{x} + kx = 0$.
- The **natural angular frequency** is $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}$ rad/s.
- The **time period** is $T = 2\pi\sqrt{m/k}$ s.
- The **critical damping coefficient** is $c_c = 2\sqrt{mk}$ N·s/m.
- The **damping ratio** is $\zeta = c/c_c$; **underdamped** if $\zeta < 1$, **critically damped** if $\zeta = 1$, **overdamped** if $\zeta > 1$.
- **Springs in series:** $1/k_{eq} = \sum 1/k_i$.
- **Springs in parallel:** $k_{eq} = \sum k_i$.
- The **static-deflection shortcut** for vertical hanging systems is $\omega_n = \sqrt{g/\delta_{st}}$.
- The **steady-state amplitude** under harmonic force $F_0 \sin\omega t$ is $X = F_0 / \sqrt{(k-m\omega^2)^2 + (c\omega)^2}$.
- The **magnification factor** is $M = 1/\sqrt{(1-r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}$; it peaks at $r = 1$ when $\zeta \to 0$.
- For **torsional SDOF**, replace $m$ by mass moment of inertia $I$ and $k$ by torsional stiffness $K_t$.
- For a **simple pendulum** (small angle), $\omega_n = \sqrt{g/L}$.
- Always include **SI units** on every line of working and **state assumptions** at the start.
- For KTU answer scripts, present a **labelled free-body diagram** *before* writing the equation of motion — examiners credit the FBD with 1 mark.
- Use **two significant figures beyond the data precision** in your final numerical answer, but retain four significant figures in intermediate steps.
- Practise sketching both the **free-body diagram** and the **response graph** for every problem — visual clarity can earn the "presentation mark" that often separates A and B grades.
